title: [testing-metamorphic] Series::str.find/rfind/index_of/rindex_of return BYTE position, not CHAR position (pandas divergence on multi-byte UTF-8)
description:
pandas semantics: Series.str.find/rfind/index/rindex return CHAR-based positions (Python 3 strings are char-indexed).

fp-frame Series.str.find/rfind/index_of/rindex_of (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14214,14227,14241,14255) return BYTE positions from Rust str::find/rfind. ASCII inputs are correct; multi-byte UTF-8 inputs diverge.

Repro:
  pandas:    pd.Series(["héllo"]).str.find("lo") -> 2  (char index)
  fp-frame:  s.str.find("lo") -> 3                       (byte index, é is 2 bytes)

Skill: /testing-metamorphic — cross-implementation oracle property: s.chars().nth(find_pos) starts the matched substring.

Fix: convert byte position to char position via &s[..byte_pos].chars().count().

title: [test] metamorphic: Series.corr shift-invariance (guards two-pass cov_components)

Seeded LCG: correlated Float64 x,y; corr(x,y) ~= corr(x+1e9, y+1e9) within 1e-6 (a one-pass sum-of-products formula would diverge via catastrophic cancellation). Also assert |corr|<=1. Test-only; fp-frame.

title: [testing-conformance-harnesses] Live pandas oracle missing op_series_str_find/op_series_str_rfind — recently-fixed char-position bug has no live-oracle regression net
description:
Skill: /testing-conformance-harnesses.

br-frankenpandas-02ae2b just fixed Series.str.find/rfind to return CHAR (not BYTE) positions, matching pandas. The fix has 6 in-crate regression tests but no live-pandas oracle coverage. The conformance harness already supports SeriesStrFind/SeriesStrRfind FixtureOperations (lib.rs:676, 678, 15974, 15981) and the fixture struct has str_sub field, but the python oracle pandas_oracle.py has NO op_series_str_find or op_series_str_rfind handlers — dispatch() falls through to OracleError(unsupported operation).

Effect: any future regression that re-introduces byte/char divergence would only be caught by the in-crate tests with hand-coded expected values. A live oracle test would compare against the running pandas installation, the most authoritative parity check.

Fix:
1. Add op_series_str_find(pd, payload) and op_series_str_rfind(pd, payload) to pandas_oracle.py
2. Wire into dispatch()
3. Add live_oracle_series_str_find_char_position_matches_pandas + rfind variant in fp-conformance tests, with multi-byte UTF-8 inputs (héllo, café_lo, etc.) so a byte-position regression would diverge from pandas char-position output.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::from_values has 66 caller sites that drop index name (umbrella for systematic preservation)
description:
After shipping lxot8/um15v/43269/a1sfw/0trpd/5cc2t (Series-side index name preservation) and z5qu1/lhzot/789xi/omjaw (DataFrame-side), 66 remaining `Self::from_values(self.name.clone(), ...)` call sites in fp-frame still drop the index name through Series::from_values's internal `Index::new(...)`.

The pattern: Series transforms that build a new index manually (rather than via reorder_by_positions or similar helpers) lose the source index name.

Recommended fix approach (avoid 60+ separate beads):

Option A — add a helper:
```rust
impl Series {
    fn from_values_preserving_index_name(&self, labels: Vec<IndexLabel>, values: Vec<Scalar>) -> Result<Self, FrameError> {
        let index = Index::new(labels).rename_index(self.index.name());
        let column = Column::from_values(values)?;
        Self::new(self.name.clone(), index, column)
    }
}
```
Then convert each `Self::from_values(self.name.clone(), labels, values)` to `self.from_values_preserving_index_name(labels, values)`.

Option B — modify Series::from_values signature to take an Option<&str> for index name. Breaking API change; less attractive.

Tests: parametric — for each affected method, exercise with a `rename_axis("idx")` setup and assert result.index().name() preserved.

Recommend doing this in waves of 10-15 sites per commit to keep diffs reviewable. Estimated effort: 2-3 hours.

Sister beads already shipped (mark superseded once this lands):
- lxot8 (dropna), um15v (drop), 43269 (reorder/head/tail/sort),
  a1sfw (add_prefix/suffix), 0trpd (nlargest/nsmallest), 5cc2t (truncate),
  lhzot (DataFrame take_rows/head/tail), z5qu1 (filter_rows), 789xi (rename_index_with),
  omjaw (sample), 6xd34 (squeeze axis=0), nvglo (value_counts).
close_reason: Added Series::with_labels_and_values_preserving_name helper and converted 36 transform call sites (fillna/ffill/bfill/interpolate/abs/round/clip/shift/diff/str.*/etc). Commit fe61bbd3. 1923 fp-frame tests pass.

description:
## Background
README claims Python bindings via PyO3 are "planned" but no PyO3 code exists in the project.
Users cannot currently `import frankenpandas` from Python.

## Justification  
Python bindings are critical for adoption - pandas users won't switch without Python interop.

- Write Python-side tests
- Add pip-installable package

## Acceptance Criteria
- `import frankenpandas` works from Python
- Basic DataFrame operations accessible
- Type stubs for IDE support

title: Regenerate hardened-null fixtures: code returns Int64 (nullable Int64 model), fixtures expect Float64 (numpy promotion)
description:
Several packet_filter_runs hardened-null cases fail with the same pattern:
  actual=Int64(N), expected=Float64(N.0)

Affected (from latest workspace test run, post-br-jnsax / br-vpeoh / br-764ys):
- FP-P2C-011 groupby_first_multikey_null_hardened
- FP-P2C-011 groupby_last_multikey_null_hardened
- FP-P2C-011 groupby_min_multikey_null_hardened
- FP-P2C-011 groupby_max_multikey_null_hardened
- FP-P2D-064 series_abs_with_nulls_hardened
- (likely the other 4 idx_extrema / dataframe_numeric_transform cases — same shape)

Root cause: the fixture oracle was generated against pandas's default (numpy-backed) int64 dtype, which promotes Int+NaN → Float64. Our codebase has explicitly chosen the nullable-Int64 model (Scalar::Null(NullKind::Null) markers inside DType::Int64 columns) — see br-jnsax (concat axis=1), br-vpeoh (DataFrame::reduce_numeric Timedelta), br-764ys (groupby first/last Int64 preservation), and this batch's fp-groupby Min/Max Int64 preservation. Code is now internally consistent: any Int64 column with nulls stays Int64.

Fix: regenerate the affected fixtures with an oracle that uses nullable Int64 (pandas Int64 extension dtype or convert_dtypes()) for outputs where the input is integer-with-nulls. This matches the ctmet regen WhiteHeron just shipped for cumsum/cumprod/cummin/cummax/describe/round.

Filed by cc_1 after observing the pattern in workspace tests; cannot regenerate fixtures from this agent (requires the pandas oracle Python environment).
close_reason: Regenerated nullable-Int64 groupby/series_abs hardened-null fixtures and added oracle dispatch coverage; cargo validation blocked because rch status is not_ready

title: [perf] Cache-friendly inversion counter for DataFrame.corr(kendall) (random-access Fenwick wall)
description: PROFILE (df_kendall n=50000, 32 cols, 496 pairs): 145ms, the LARGEST single perf gap in the suite. complete_kendall_no_tie_parallel_matrix already parallel + Knight's O(n log n); 77.75% self is count_ordered_rank_inversions — a u32 Fenwick/BIT over a ~200KB array with 2 random-access tree walks (query+update) per element = cache-miss bound. NEUTRAL/rejected so far: (a) per-worker buffer reuse to kill per-pair vec![0u32;n] alloc — NEUTRAL because calloc gives free kernel zero-pages, so reuse just trades calloc for explicit memset; (b) merge-sort inversions (prior, rejected, slower than Fenwick). NEXT alien primitive: cache-friendly order-statistics — sqrt-decomposition / blocked counting (sequential block scans replacing log-depth random Fenwick walks), or a SIMD-friendly two-level structure. Bit-identical (exact inversion count). Target ~1.5-2x on the biggest gap. Alternatively gated on the FMA build decision is NOT applicable here (integer counting, not f64 Gram).
notes: RECONFIRMED NOT VIABLE (BlackThrush): merge-sort inversion count is bit-identical (goldens 031978ba/acf366c2/f164fa86 unchanged) but REGRESSES df_kendall 200k 617->685ms (~1.11x slower). Root cause: the u32 Fenwick (322yd) is ~800KB at n=200k = L3-RESIDENT, so its random accesses are L3 hits (~12cyc) not memory misses — there is NO cache wall. Merge-sort does O(n log n)=3.6M sequential writes/pair, far more total work; sqrt-decomposition would do ~2*sqrt(n)=900 L1 ops/row vs Fenwick's ~36 L3-hit ops — also slower. Kendall is compute-bound at the Fenwick's O(n log n) with cache-resident state. Cache-layout swaps cannot beat it; only algorithmic work-reduction (sub-O(n log n)/pair or cross-pair sharing) could. See tests/artifacts/perf/0dm7c_rejected_kendall_inversion_cache_layout.md
close_reason: Rejected cache-friendly inversion-counter family. Current-head RCH baseline: df_kendall 50000x1 154.5 ms +/- 5.4, 200000x1 698.3 ms +/- 6.6; goldens acf366c2/031978ba/f164fa86 unchanged. Prior merge-sort/sqrt/buffer/cache-layout variants do not clear Score>=2.0; route to exact all-pairs Kendall rank-signature/offline dominance work sharing instead.

title: fp-columnar: add numeric checking (isfinite/isinf/isnan)
[perf][ARCH] Datetime64 typed column backing (parse-once) to unblock dt accessors
description: fp-frame stores datetimes as Utf8 ISO-8601 STRINGS and dt.year/month/day/hour/etc RE-PARSE every call (~12-19ms/200k, perf_profile + gap_hunt confirmed). Micro-levers REJECTED (wvpxz): the per-element trim+split+parse+Scalar-output is irreducible while the data stays strings. THE FIX is architectural: a typed Datetime64 column backing (i64 nanos/days since epoch, parsed ONCE at construction/CSV-read), so dt.<field> becomes a cheap civil-from-days/SWAR extraction over an i64 slice (>=5-10x, and dt becomes O(n) arithmetic not O(n) string parsing). SCOPE (multi-session): new ColumnData::Datetime64 backing + materialization arms + CSV/from-string datetime parsing path + reroute all DatetimeAccessor methods + parity goldens. Big but high-value (datetimes are ubiquitous). CONTEXT: filed after 5 consecutive single-column micro-lever rejects (spearman/sem/value_counts/dt-materialization/dt-fastparse) conclusively exhausted the materialization-removal lever; remaining >=2x paths are ALL architectural (this, rcvpj nullable-bool backing) or FMA-gated (jawxr corr/spearman).
notes: dt floor/ceil/round/normalize typed-routing landed @3b4a67e7 (BlackThrush): round_to_freq typed branch snaps nanos via snap_datetime_ns and keeps the column TYPED Datetime64 (pandas returns datetime64; NaT->NaT). THIS COMPLETES dt-accessor-on-Datetime64 routing — every dt accessor that previously ERRORED on Datetime64 now works: year..dayofyear/quarter/is_*(bool)/month_name/day_name/date/time/strftime(str)/floor/ceil/round/normalize. The remaining 0ezw7 ARCH scope (typed Datetime64 column backing from CSV/to_datetime parse-once + perf) is the BIG multi-session piece still open; the accessor-correctness layer is now DONE. 113 dt/floor tests + clippy green.

title: [perf] String groupby-sum 2x slower than pandas (factorize-hashmap throughput)
description: PROFILE-BACKED (1M, release-perf, new strings bench de207731): df.groupby('key')['val'].sum() over a ~1000-distinct ~5-byte string key is 2.01x@1M / 1.97x@100k (fp=53529us vs pandas=26659us) — CONSISTENT across scale = real, not fixed overhead. (str_sort 0.23x and str_value_counts 0.21x: fp WINS 4-5x.) Root cause: aggregate_str_dense (~54539) factorize phase — 1M pack_short_utf8_span + 1M FxHashMap<u64,usize> lookups into a ~1000-entry table. The accumulation (acc[gid]+=v, ~54694) is already optimal; the grouping/factorize hashmap is the bottleneck. SHARED PRIMITIVE: this is the SAME safe-Rust hashbrown-vs-pandas-khash factorize/tally throughput gap as value_counts nan50 (~2.2x, s2i37) and drop_duplicates bucketing. khash (simple open-addressing, 1-probe at low load) beats hashbrown SwissTable (SIMD-group setup overhead) for low-cardinality u64 keys; matching it needs a custom open-addressing u64->usize table, but #![forbid(unsafe_code)] makes per-probe bounds-check elision hard, so a naive safe table likely won't beat hashbrown. NEXT ALIEN SWING (shared by value_counts/drop_duplicates/str-groupby): a safe-Rust open-addressing u64 table where the power-of-2 mask provably elides bounds checks (e.g. via slice windows / careful indexing), OR a SWAR/radix factorize for fixed-width-short packed keys. Target 2x -> <=1x. Big uncertain swing.
notes: ROOT CAUSE FOUND (refined): the 2x is NOT the factorize hashmap (custom open-addressing table was perf-neutral this turn). It is the TWO-PASS structure vs pandas' FUSED single pass. fp aggregate_str_dense: pass 1 factorize writes gid_per_row (Vec<usize> = 8MB for 1M rows), pass 2 accumulate reads gid_per_row (8MB) + vals (8MB). pandas cython groupby fuses factorize+sum in ONE pass (no gid_per_row materialization). Eliminating the gid_per_row round-trip (8MB write + 8MB read) cuts memory traffic from ~29MB to ~13MB (~2.2x) — matches the 2x. THE LEVER: a FUSED single-pass fast path for (sum/mean/count, single-or-few f64 value cols, hash-group branch): in the factorize loop, accumulate acc[g]+=vals[row] inline and DO NOT materialize gid_per_row; keep reps for the output keys; sort distinct keys; emit order.iter().map(acc[g]). Bit-identical (same operands/order, just fused). Narrower secondary lever: gid_per_row Vec<usize>->u32 codes halves its traffic (~1.4x, broad, sub-2x). The fused path is the >=2x swing; the factorize hashmap and the per-group accumulator are both already optimal. Same fused-vs-2-pass pattern likely applies to the Int64-key dense groupby in aggregate_named_func.
close_reason: PHANTOM (bench bug, not an fp gap). Fixed in f14086c1. The str_groupby_sum workload (de207731) summed frame.groupby(key).sum() over key+name+val, so fp aggregated BOTH non-key cols — including 'name', a ~1M-unique string it concatenates per group — while pandas did groupby(key)['val'].sum() (val only). The extra string-concat work was the entire '2x'. After dropping 'name' to match pandas: str_groupby_sum 2.07x -> 0.24x@1M = fp ~4x FASTER. Both attempted levers (custom open-addressing factorize table last turn; fused single-pass this turn) were chasing the phantom and were correctly reverted (neutral / never-hit). fp string groupby was never slow. Same bench-methodology class as iloc f597a3c6 and groupby_agg_multi.

title: perf: khash-class open-addressing primitive hash table (FrankenHash) for tally/setop/dedup
description: ALIEN-ARTIFACT deep lever. Multiple fp-SLOWER-than-pandas ops are bottlenecked on FxHashMap of enum/bits keys where pandas wins on khash constants: value_counts all-distinct float (0.62x, hash tally ~70% — see bench-frontier memory), Index unsorted set-ops/get_indexer (FxHashMap<IndexLabel> enum hashing, position_map_first_ref), drop_duplicates, unsorted groupby. Build a cache-friendly open-addressing (swiss/khash-style) hash table specialized for primitive keys (i64/u64/f64-bits) with inlined counts/positions (one cache line per probe vs FxHashMap's separate value Vec), then integrate into the hot tally/dedup/setop paths. Target: beat pandas khash >=1.5-2x on those ops. 100% safe Rust. Spans fp-columnar + fp-index + fp-frame (currently contended). Per NO-CEILING addendum this is the fundamentally-different primitive replacing per-op micro-tuning.
close_reason: Rejected residual open-address Float64 value_counts variants: golden SHA unchanged (2e0567f681bdacae8d0b5f59c542582b185b64186ada5bf4352741272dd0afb6), but candidate4 was only 1.04x forward and 1.01x reversed on value_counts_nan50 1000000x10; candidate2 regressed and candidate3 lacked a reversed keep. No source hunk retained; proof in tests/artifacts/perf/proof_lavender_0mtyz_open_address_residual_reject.md.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::nlargest/nsmallest lose index name (Self::from_values without rename_index)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:6499 (nlargest) and :6527 (nsmallest) — both call `Self::from_values(self.name.clone(), labels, values)`. Self::from_values builds an unnamed Index, dropping the source index name. Same root cause as br-lxot8/um15v/43269/a1sfw family.

Pandas preserves index name through nlargest/nsmallest.

Surgical fix: replace each Self::from_values call with:
  let index = Index::new(labels).rename_index(self.index.name());
  let column = Column::from_values(values)?;
  Self::new(self.name.clone(), index, column)

Tests: Series with named index → nlargest/nsmallest → result.index().name() preserved.

Also check nlargest_keep / nsmallest_keep / nlargest_smallest_impl for the same pattern.
close_reason: Applied rename_index in nlargest, nsmallest, and nlargest_smallest_impl. Commit 23965238. 1921 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [testing-metamorphic] No proptest asserting cumsum/diff round-trip property
description:
Skill: /testing-metamorphic.

Series::cumsum and Series::diff are inverses (modulo the first-position
NaN that diff inserts). For any null-free numeric series s of length n:

  cumsum(s).diff(1)[i] == s[i]  for i in [1, n)
  cumsum(s).diff(1)[0] == NaN

Existing proptests cover scaling linearity (prop_series_cumsum_scales_linearly
at line 9275 + diff variants near 6053) but no round-trip property test
asserts the cumsum/diff inverse relationship.

A regression that broke either op (e.g. diff drift on Float64 promotion,
cumsum off-by-one, mishandled boundary) would silently produce wrong
intermediate values that the existing scaling tests might not catch on
specific f64 inputs.

Add proptest with random null-free Float64 series of length 2..=12,
restricted to bounded magnitudes to avoid f64 catastrophic cancellation
near zero crossings.

title: [perf] replace quadratic Kendall tau pair counting
description: Profile-backed optimization pass candidate for DataFrame.corr_method("kendall") / Series::corr_kendall. Current implementation counts every pair in O(n^2); perf_profile explicitly keeps df_kendall n small because of that cost. Baseline first via rch release-perf + hyperfine and golden sha256, then apply one algorithmic lever only: safe-Rust Knight/Fenwick-style Kendall tau-b inversion counting with exact tie accounting. Isomorphism obligations: preserve aligned numeric-pair filtering, exact f64 equality tie behavior, ordering-insensitive pair counts, NaN result for degenerate denominators, deterministic output ordering, no RNG, and golden-output sha256 before/after. Retain only if Score>=2.0 after rch re-benchmark.
close_reason: Completed: Kendall no-tie inversion-count fast path retained. RCH df_kendall 512x5 baseline 1.8219s +/-0.0977 -> explicit candidate 0.1593s +/-0.0038 (11.44x). Golden sha256 unchanged dcce8a4e1f13d887361650ec24fcfee13c6c4c3954936fd124a1feb53cf125ce. Validation: RCH fp-frame kendall tests/check/clippy, cargo fmt --check; UBS timed out on large fp-frame file.

title: [perf] Series::is_unique null_count via validity mask (skip extra Vec<Scalar> pass)
description: Series::is_unique (fp-frame/src/lib.rs ~7968) computes null_count = self.column.values().iter().filter(is_missing).count() — a full Vec<Scalar> materialization pass IN ADDITION to the self.nunique() it already calls. For dtypes whose missingness is captured entirely by the validity mask (Int64/Bool/Utf8 — no in-band NaN/NaT/sentinel), null_count = len - validity.count_valid() is O(1). FIX: dtype-guarded null_count (Int64/Bool/Utf8 => len-count_valid; else the values() scan, preserving Float64 NaN / Datetime64 NaT / Categorical / Sparse / Interval / Period semantics). Bit-identical; removes one of the two materialization passes for the common typed case. Same validity vein as br-frankenpandas-2jibm. Add equivalence test vs the values() reference across dtypes.
close_reason: Series::is_unique null_count now derives from the validity mask (len-count_valid) for Int64/Bool/Utf8 instead of a second Vec<Scalar> materialization pass on top of nunique(); Float64/Datetime64/Timedelta64/Categorical/Sparse/Interval/Period defer to the Scalar scan (sacred NaN/NaT). Bit-identical (no in-band missing for the guarded dtypes); halves materialization in the common typed case. Test series_is_unique_null_count_validity_path_160km covers unique/dup/one-null/two-nulls for Int64/Bool/Utf8 + deferred Float64 NaN/Datetime64 NaT. Verified: is_unique tests 8 passed, full fp-frame 2897 passed/0 failed. Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/160km_series_is_unique_null_count_validity.txt

title: perf(fp-frame): rolling cov/corr O(n*w) -> O(n) (pandas windowed-mean form; goldens trivial-exact)
description: Rolling::cov (~17285) and Rolling::corr (~17230) re-fold each window (two-pass means + cross/var sums) — O(n*w). KEY FINDING: the only rolling cov/corr assert_text_golden tests (rolling_dataframe_cov_with/corr_with_basic.txt) are TRIVIAL-EXACT (cov=2, corr=1 on perfectly-correlated data) so they survive ANY correct algorithm — NOT golden-gated (unlike skew/kurt g2veb). Two O(n) routes: (A) mirror the proven slt1p RollingVarianceState add/remove/compensation pattern bivariately (RollingCovarianceState tracking mean_a/mean_b/m2a/m2b/cab with compensated add+remove over window_bounds); cov=cab/(n-ddof), corr=cab/sqrt(m2a*m2b). (B) pandas-faithful: pandas Rolling.cov computes (roll_mean(X*Y) - roll_mean(X)*roll_mean(Y))*count/(count-ddof) via O(n) rolling means — reuse rolling_online_sum_mean x3, but needs pairwise-present masking (NaN-out both series where EITHER is missing) to match FP's current pairwise semantics. Route (A) is more accurate (matches current two-pass closer); (B) matches pandas exactly. Conformance is tolerance-based either way. min_periods.max(2) + center via window_bounds + std==0->NaN guard must carry through. PROOF: tolerance tests + trivial goldens green; rch bench Score>=2.0.
notes: GOTCHA for route A: with REMOVES, a window sliding INTO a constant region leaves m2a/m2b tiny-nonzero (residual from earlier removed varied values) not EXACTLY 0, so corr's std==0->NaN guard misfires. pandas fixes via num_consecutive_same_value; RollingVarianceState already tracks consecutive_same (remove() doesn't touch it; output forces 0 when consecutive_same>=nobs) — replicate per-variable. Route B (pandas-exact, simpler): cov=(roll_mean(XY)-roll_mean(X)roll_mean(Y))*count/(count-ddof) via rolling_online_sum_mean x3 + pairwise NaN-mask. Goldens trivial-exact (cov=2,corr=1) so NOT golden-gated. Validate with isomorphism vs two-pass over a constant-region-transition window. Not bench-flagged; do in focused session w/ agent-mail up.
close_reason: Completed by LavenderStone: shipped O(n) rolling pairwise cov/corr using a sliding pairwise-valid bivariate sum state. Paired hyperfine bench_rolling_cov_corr 228.4ms +-5.7ms -> 10.4ms +-1.2ms, 22.06x +-2.56 faster. Existing live-pandas rolling_cov_corr_match_pandas tolerance cases pass; exact rolling DataFrame cov/corr text goldens pass with SHA-256 d6eb07074bcdb96c9497f2fa615e90c47890775f7a49d4570dea4d4b3261e512 and af7e35dc95668e96a0e33759788e10f9699d5a43d3118b2bdf0bf3c09ecc91b1. fp-frame check/clippy pass remotely; UBS timed out at 180s with no findings emitted.

title: [testing-golden-artifacts] DataFrame::to_markdown formatting has no golden-byte regression coverage
description:
Skill: /testing-golden-artifacts.

DataFrame::to_markdown (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:25954) has rich formatting:
- Per-column width based on max(header, max(cell)) clamped to >= 3
- Float rendering: whole numbers get .1 padding (2.0 not 2), large/non-whole
  use Float64::to_string default
- NaN/null → "NaN" string
- Bool → "True"/"False"
- Index column inserted with empty header when include_index=true

Existing goldens cover only Series::Display and DataFrame::Display (basic
tabular). to_markdown's formatting could regress silently — e.g., width
clamping changing from 3 to something else, or float formatting drifting.

title: [perf] CSV token-table numeric parser for residual csv_read
description:
Profile-backed target: residual `csv_read 100000 20` after pass 36/37 remains the top measured hotspot around 0.75-0.77s on release-perf rch runs. The simple-header admission trial was rejected because paired A/B was only 1.04x; the next lever must be a fundamentally different CSV primitive, not another small admission tweak.

Attack: split simple numeric CSV parsing into a framing/token-table pass (row offsets + field spans) followed by typed column materialization from spans, with deterministic row/column order and strict fallback for quotes, CR, ragged rows, comments, skip rows, explicit NA/default-NA semantics, bool/object promotion, or any unsupported option.

Proof obligations: rch hyperfine/criterion baseline before edit; one lever only; golden-output sha256 before/after for representative csv_read fixture; preserve header semantics, duplicate-name errors, dtype promotion, null/NaN handling, floating-point bit results for accepted simple numeric inputs, ordering/tie-breaking, and RNG-free determinism; re-benchmark via rch and keep only Score>=2.0. Target ratio: at least 1.15x on `csv_read 100000 20`.
close_reason: Rejected: token-table CSV parser regressed profile target; baseline 509.5 ms, clean token-table 563.0 ms, paired old 514.1 ms vs candidate 571.8 ms; golden sha256 unchanged; no source retained; next attack is SWAR byte-classified single-pass clean numeric parser.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::reindex/DataFrame::reindex lose index name (pandas preserves it)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:3205 (Series::reindex) builds new_index = Index::new(new_labels) without rename_index. Same root cause as the broader index-name preservation family.

Pandas:
  >>> pd.Series([1,2,3], index=pd.Index(['a','b','c'], name='idx')).reindex(['a','c']).index.name
  'idx'

Surgical fix: change line 3205 to:
  let new_index = Index::new(new_labels).rename_index(self.index.name());

Same fix likely needed in DataFrame::reindex.

Tests: Series with named index → reindex → result.index().name() preserved.

title: [perf] typed-comparison multi-column sort_values_multi (skip Scalar materialization + enum dispatch)
description: DataFrame::sort_values_multi (multi-key df.sort_values([..])) still uses the generic Scalar comparison sort: order.sort_by closure does col.values()[a] (materializes a Vec<Scalar> per sort column) + is_missing()x2 + compare_scalars_with_na_position enum dispatch, per comparison per by-column = O(n log n * k) Scalar ops. Single-column sort already got radix; multi-column never did. Lever: pre-extract each by-column as a typed key once (SortKey enum: I64(&[i64]) for all-valid Int64, F64(&[f64]) for all-valid no-NaN Float64, else Scalar fallback) and compare typed values directly in the closure — no Vec<Scalar> materialization, no enum dispatch, no missing branch for the typed cases. Bit-identical: Int64 typed cmp == compare_non_missing Int64 branch; no-NaN Float64 partial_cmp == the Float64 branch; all-valid => no missing, matching the (false,false) arm; same stable sort_by => same tie-break => same order. Per-column fallback to Scalar for Bool/Utf8/nullable/NaN-f64. Golden sha256 on a multi-key sort; Score>=2.0. NOTE: a stable radix LSD lexsort is the bigger follow-up but has NaN/na_position bit-identicality risk; typed-compare is the safe first lever.
close_reason: Shipped abaad0a4: typed SortKey (I64/F64/Scalar) in sort_values_multi — skips Vec<Scalar> materialization of sort columns + enum dispatch + missing branch for all-valid Int64/no-NaN-Float64 columns. Bit-identical (golden sort_multi a56322a7 before==after; 82 fp-frame sort tests green). n=500k 2-key (Int64+Float64), min-of-runs: 380->242 ms = 1.57x. BELOW 2.0: typed compare removes Scalar overhead but the O(n log n) comparison-sort machinery is the floor. The >=2x lever (stable LSD radix lexsort, O(n), reusing single-column radix) filed as br-frankenpandas-lnsu6.

title: proptest: combine_first associativity fails on mixed dtypes
description:
## Summary
Property test `prop_dataframe_combine_first_associative` fails when combining DataFrames with mixed Int64/Float64 dtypes and null values.

## Regression Seeds
Two failing seeds recorded in `proptest_properties.proptest-regressions`:
1. cc 78f62b29ce... - Three DataFrames with mixed Int64/Float64 columns and different column sets  
2. cc 0beb5bc7d2... - DataFrame with nullable Float64 columns

## Root Cause (Suspected)
Related to DISC-011 (no nullable Int64 extension dtype). When `combine_first` fills nulls from another DataFrame, dtype promotion paths may differ depending on operand order:
- `(left.combine_first(middle)).combine_first(right)` may promote differently than
- `left.combine_first(middle.combine_first(right))`

The test asserts these should be equal for unique-label inputs, but dtype coercion asymmetry breaks this.

## Evidence
- proptest_properties.rs:2626
- Regression file: crates/fp-conformance/tests/proptest_properties.proptest-regressions

## Fix Strategy
Either:
1. Ensure combine_first dtype promotion is symmetric/associative
2. Mark test as XFAIL until DISC-011 (nullable Int64) lands
close_reason: done

Bit-identical (floor/ceil are exact, no NaN created from finite input; from_f64_values handles any). Add bench + golden.

title: perf(fp-frame): expanding cov/corr O(n^2) -> O(n) online bivariate Welford
description: Expanding::cov (fp-frame ~18287) and Expanding::corr (~18223) re-fold the entire prefix [0,i] every step: collect pairs, two-pass mean_a/mean_b then Σ(a-ma)(b-mb) (+ per-var for corr). O(n^2) (n=100k => 1e10 ops). Expanding is add-only, so a single online bivariate Welford pass (running mean_a,mean_b,M2a,M2b,Cab) yields cov=Cab/(nobs-1), corr=Cab/sqrt(M2a*M2b) in O(n). BIT-SAFE: expanding cov/corr have NO assert_text_golden tests (only tolerance tests expanding_cov_matches_sample_cov / expanding_corr_* — rolling cov/corr are the golden-gated ones and are NOT touched). Constant-series -> M2=0 exactly under Welford (da=0) so std==0 NaN guard preserved. pairwise missing skip + min_periods.max(2) preserved. PROOF: tolerance tests green; rch bench before/after Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped @7082c772 (main+master). Expanding cov/corr -> O(n) online bivariate Welford via shared expanding_bivariate helper. Bench n=10k: cov 156->0.84ms (187x), corr 250->0.69ms (361x); scales ~10x more at 100k. Bit-safe (tolerance-tested, no goldens; rolling cov/corr untouched). fp-frame 2889/0 incl. isomorphism cross-check. Rolling cov/corr remain O(n*w) but golden-gated -> separate decision.

title: Add DType type checking helper methods
Reject zero repair-symbol encoded artifacts in fp-runtime codec
description: Fallback scan after an empty ready queue. PassthroughCodec::encode rejects max_repair_symbols == 0, but PassthroughCodec::decode accepts externally supplied EncodedArtifact values with repair_symbols == 0 as long as source_len <= encoded_bytes.len(). That creates an inconsistent repair-symbol manifest boundary for Asupersync/RaptorQ sidecar durability: artifacts the local encoder would never emit are accepted during decode/recovery. Fix by making decode fail closed when encoded.repair_symbols == 0, preserving the existing source_len guard, and add focused fp-runtime tests covering encode rejection, decode rejection, and a valid round trip.
close_reason: Added fail-closed PassthroughCodec::decode guard for zero repair-symbol encoded artifacts, replaced guarded direct slicing with checked range access, and added focused asupersync feature tests for encode rejection, decode rejection, and valid round trip.

title: Add fp-io read_fwf fixed-width file reader (explicit colspecs/widths)
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): IOWrappers is missing read_fwf without an open child bead.

Pandas signature:
  pd.read_fwf(filepath_or_buffer, colspecs='infer', widths=None, infer_nrows=100, ...)

Phase A scope: explicit colspecs OR widths, has_headers control, and the same NA / dtype / index_col options as read_csv. Auto-inference (colspecs='infer') is a follow-up.

Add:
- struct FwfReadOptions { colspecs: Option<Vec<(usize, usize)>>, widths: Option<Vec<usize>>, has_headers: bool, na_values, keep_default_na, na_filter, index_col, ... }
- read_fwf_str(input, options) parses each line into fixed-width slices, trims trailing whitespace, runs the same scalar-coercion path as read_csv.
- read_fwf(path, options) and read_fwf_str (no-options) convenience wrappers.
- Reject when both colspecs and widths are missing (Phase A defers infer mode); reject when both are provided to mirror pandas TypeError.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering colspecs parse, widths-derived colspecs, header inference, NA handling parity, and the deferred-infer rejection.
close_reason: Shipped in 94f3a50: fp-io exposes read_fwf_str/read_fwf with FwfReadOptions (colspecs|widths, has_headers, na_values, keep_default_na, na_filter, index_col, usecols, nrows, skiprows, dtype, parse_dates, true/false_values, decimal, thousands, skipfooter). IoError::Fwf added. Auto-infer mode deferred. 6 regression tests pass; 523 fp-io tests total.

title: [bug] Timestamp isoformat pre-epoch subsecond day floor
description: Fix fp-types Timestamp::isoformat for pre-epoch subsecond timestamps. Current formatting derives days from truncating total seconds, so values like -1 ns can land on 1970-01-01 instead of the floor-calendar day 1969-12-31. Use Euclidean day/nanos-of-day arithmetic like the component accessors, preserve timezone formatting and NaT behavior, and add no-mock seeded coverage across negative and positive subday cases. Code-first lane; batch tests pending.

Surgical (one-file edit; matches the existing dtype-branch pattern at line 5363).

title: [perf] fp-frame::take_rows_by_positions_unchecked:26427 + fp-columnar::Column::new:1149 — gather fast-path; filter_bool 6.81ms vs pandas 0.38ms (17.9x)
description:
MEASUREMENT BY RubyGoose profiling pass 2026-06-01. See tests/artifacts/perf/HOTSPOT_TABLE.md.

SCENARIO: DataFrame::iloc_bool(mask), 100k rows x 10 float cols, mask=every other row. Also the cross-cutting materialization path for sort/drop_duplicates/take/head/tail/reindex/groupby-selection.

BASELINE (100k, 20 hyperfine runs, release-perf + frame pointers):
- FP filter_bool: 6.81 ms/iter (sigma 0.30); CPU-bound (User 710ms / Sys 36ms)
- pandas 2.2.3: p50 0.38ms, p95 0.40ms => 17.9x slower.

PROFILER EVIDENCE (samply, symbolicated; samply_filter.json):
- 34.5% self  fp_frame::DataFrame::take_rows_by_positions_unchecked
- ~11% self   fp_columnar::Column::new
- 6.0% self   drop_in_place BTreeMap<String,Column> (intermediate frame free)
- 3.1% self   fp_columnar::ValidityMask::from_values

ROOT CAUSE: take_rows_by_positions_unchecked clones each Scalar per position into a Vec, then calls Column::new, which runs ~5 O(n) passes over an ALREADY-valid source column (utf8-bucket x2, needs_coercion scan, normalize map, ValidityMask::from_values rescan). Pure overhead when reindexing.

HYPOTHESIZED FIX (H1, highest leverage): add a Column gather/from_validated_parts fast-path that copies selected values + validity bits in ONE pass with no coercion/revalidation (source dtype already known-valid). Have take_rows_by_positions_unchecked call it per column. This simultaneously speeds filter, sort, drop_duplicates, take, head, tail, reindex, groupby row-selection. For filter specifically, prefer a vectorized mask-gather over position materialization.

DO NOT optimize yet without re-baselining against HOTSPOT_TABLE.md. Owner: optimizer agent.
close_reason: Completed gather fast path. Added Column::take_positions and routed DataFrame row materialization through it. RCH baseline/filter_bool artifact: 748.7 ms mean / 749.9 ms median per 110 iters (6.81 ms/iter). RCH after rerun: 533.8 ms mean / 534.4 ms median per 110 iters (4.85 ms/iter). Golden checksum ledger unchanged at sha256 66c1bd6cf3cfbba251180c267575b88981999e03130e4fc83faa96d080bb4a54. Validation: rch cargo build -p fp-conformance --profile release-perf --example perf_profile; rch cargo test -p fp-columnar take_positions_matches_validated_materialization; rch cargo test -p fp-frame --lib dataframe_take_paths_preserve_index_name_lhzot; rch cargo test -p fp-frame --lib dataframe_drop_duplicates_keep_first_preserves_index_order; rch cargo fmt -p fp-columnar -p fp-frame -p fp-conformance -- --check; rch cargo clippy -p fp-columnar -p fp-frame -p fp-conformance --all-targets -- -D warnings. UBS on changed Rust files was interrupted after several minutes with no findings emitted because it stalled in ast-grep on fp-frame.

These return new indexes / grouping maps and were fast-pathed for lazy Int64 backings but lack a cross-cutting differential test (the predicate family is covered by yfyb9, lookups by q70pn). Add a deterministic (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks) harness comparing a lazy Int64 index (from_i64_values => fast path) against the materialized equivalent (Index::new => fallback): assert insert(loc,item).labels(), delete(loc).labels(), take(positions).labels(), and groupby() agree for random valid locs/positions/items (incl Int64 and a non-Int64 item to exercise dtype transition). Validates insert (uza04.152), delete (uza04.150), groupby (xk18v). Result: PASS, no divergence. Completes differential coverage of the recent fp-index Int64 fast-path wave (with yfyb9 predicates + q70pn lookups). Verified: targeted 1 passed, full fp-index 422 passed/0 failed, ubs 0 critical. title: [mock-code-finder] DataFrameGroupBy::filter passes group DataFrame missing the groupby key columns (pandas includes them)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:36340 — DataFrameGroupBy::filter builds the group DataFrame from `value_cols`, which is filtered to exclude every column listed in `self.by`:

```rust
let value_cols: Vec<String> = self
    .df
    .column_order
    .iter()
    .filter(|c| !self.by.contains(c))
    .cloned()
    .collect();
```

So the predicate receives a DataFrame that lacks the groupby key columns. Pandas passes the full group including the key columns, so a predicate like `lambda g: g["key"].iloc[0] == "x"` works in pandas but cannot work here.

Surgical fix: drop the `.filter(...)` and use `self.df.column_order.clone()` so the group DataFrame contains every column. The downstream `keep_indices.extend(row_indices)` is unchanged.

Tests: `df.groupby("k").filter(|g| g.column("k").is_some())` — verify the key column is present in the group passed to the predicate.
close_reason: Dropped key-column filter from DataFrameGroupBy::filter so the predicate sees the full group. Commit 33be72c4. 1906 fp-frame tests pass (1 new regression).

title: [perf] Series::hasnans materializes Vec<Scalar> (validity-mask short-circuit)
description: Series::hasnans (fp-frame/src/lib.rs ~13419) does self.column.values().iter().any(|v| v.is_missing()) — materializing the whole Vec<Scalar> + per-element is_missing dispatch. Missing-ness for many dtypes is captured entirely by the validity mask. FIX: (1) if !validity.all() => return true immediately (a null slot is always missing; no materialization); (2) all-valid AND dtype in {Int64,Bool,Utf8} => false (these have NO in-band NaN/NaT/sentinel, so all-valid means no missing); (3) all other dtypes (Float64 NaN, Datetime64/Timedelta64 NaT sentinel, Categorical/Sparse/Interval/Period) DEFER to the exact Scalar path to preserve sacred NaN/NaT semantics. Bit-identical. Avoids Vec<Scalar> materialization for the common all-valid integer/bool/string case and for any null-bearing column. Add test: equivalence vs Scalar path across dtypes incl Float64 NaN + null-bearing.
close_reason: Series::hasnans validity-mask short-circuit: !validity.all()=>true (no materialization for any null-bearing column); all-valid Int64/Bool/Utf8=>false (no in-band missing); Float64/Datetime64/Timedelta64/Categorical/Sparse/Interval/Period defer to the Scalar path (sacred NaN/NaT). Bit-identical, avoids Vec<Scalar> materialization for the common case. Test series_hasnans_validity_short_circuit_equivalence_2jibm asserts hasnans()==values().any(is_missing) across 9 dtype cases incl Float64 NaN + Datetime64 NaT + nulls. Verified: hasnans tests 4 passed, full fp-frame 2896 passed/0 failed. Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/2jibm_series_hasnans_validity_shortcircuit.txt

title: [perf][epic-opener] str-op typed sweep: extend apply_str_bool pattern to apply_str_int + string-OUTPUT ops; THEN evaluate contiguous Utf8 (offsets+bytes) layout with measured numbers
description: Campaign state after 42da7556 (str_contains 86.9->28.2 ms/iter 3.06x, FP now ~3.4x ahead of pandas; profile artifact tests/artifacts/perf/fp_ab_str_bool_typed_output.json): the allocation walls, not the search, dominated. NEXT CONTAINED LEVERS (same pattern, measure each): (1) apply_str_int (len/find/count family) -> from_i64_values + Arc index clone — identical shape to apply_str_bool, but NOTE its null-promotion contract (any null -> Float64 column) only matters for nullable inputs, all-valid path is clean Int64; (2) string-OUTPUT ops (lower/upper/strip/slice — the bulk of apply_str users) still pay Vec<Scalar::Utf8> + per-row String alloc; no typed Utf8 buffer exists -> leads to (3); (3) THE REPRESENTATION DECISION: contiguous Utf8 layout (arrow-style offsets[] + bytes[], lazy like LazyAllValidInt64) would let string-output ops write into one rolling byte buffer (no per-row String) and unlock genuine SIMD scans (memchr/SWAR over the contiguous bytes ignoring row boundaries, then bound-check hits). Sizing: str ops are 2822-test-covered; the variant addition is exhaustive-match-bounded like IndexLabel::Null was (37 sites then). Profile str_lower/str_len FIRST with new scenarios (mirror str_contains) to get the before numbers. pandas comparison baseline: object-dtype pandas is Python-loop bound (~97ms/1M) so every lever extends a LEAD (no-gaps already satisfied) — prioritize accordingly vs other subsystems.
close_reason: Str typed-output campaign complete: contains 3.06x (+SIMD whole-buffer stage ~3x), len 3.36x, lower 1.52x (pandas gap closed) + ASCII-fast, strip zero-alloc, chains 1.42x via end-to-end contiguous reads. Two final rungs (anchored starts/ends, ASCII-case) measured sub-bar — apply-loop alloc no longer the wall, per NO-CEILING pivoting. Deeper algorithmic surface (contiguous keys in sort/groupby) routed as new bead.

title: [perf] iloc_slice lazy index/materialized row slice keep
description: After the shipped iloc(list) affine fix (12d7af50, 2.15x), iloc is still ~11.7x vs pandas (canonical fp-bench, 100k). Profiled the residual: iloc per-call is SUPER-linear (100k=0.24ms, 1M=11.4ms) i.e. O(n)+ work per call, NOT constant frame-assembly. ATTEMPTED this turn: route DataFrame::iloc_slice(start,stop) directly through take_rows_by_affine_certificate_unchecked with a known certificate (no position Vec, no O(n) detect scan) + make fp-bench/perf_profile iloc_slice scenarios faithfully call the slice API (pandas iloc[a:b] is a SLICE, not a materialized position list). RESULT: golden sha256 IDENTICAL before/after at n=5000 and n=100000, but interleaved hyperfine = 1.07x (perf-NEUTRAL, before even slightly faster); optimistic non-interleaved 1.42x, still <2.0. REVERTED. ROOT CAUSE: the affine 'lazy strided' path is O(1) to CONSTRUCT (Arc-clone views) yet measures ~0.8ms/iter same as the take_rows_by_positions gather — the O(n) cost is common to BOTH paths and is NOT the column gather. Candidates: int64 view materialization (should be OnceLock-cached across iters but verify), per-call ValidityMask::all_valid(n) x ncols, or BTreeMap/String frame reassembly. KNOWN DEAD END: Index::new unit-range canonicalization = uza04.122 REJECTED (regressed series_add_align 69ms->1.13s). NEXT DEEP LEVER: a truly-lazy O(1) index-slice backing (Arc-slice over the parent's materialized Arc<Vec<IndexLabel>>, start+len) so iloc[a:b] index is zero-copy like pandas RangeIndex slice — needs new fp-index IndexLabels variant; target ~10x. BLOCKED: heavy LavenderStone contention on fp-index/fp-frame (uza04139/140 in flight). Profile with perf/cachegrind to localize the O(n) before implementing.
notes: ACCEPTED 2026-06-15 (LavenderStone): replaced the rejected affine-routing attempt with the deeper lazy index/materialized row-slice primitive. title: [perf] Branchless all-finite corr/cov pairwise moments
description:
Profile-backed target: after Spearman pass, frame reprofile on RCH-built release-perf perf_profile shows df_corr 5000x10 mean about 111 ms and df_cov about 109 ms; perf/gdb are restricted on this host, but the harness isolates the frame corr/cov workload and pairwise_stat_matrix remains the inner O(cols^2 * rows) moment kernel. Previous dot-marginal precompute lever was rejected as slower, so do not repeat that family.

Alien primitive: branchless dense numeric kernel with semantic witness. One lever only: when every extracted f64 column is finite, run pairwise_stat_matrix through a no-missing branchless loop with count=len, preserving the exact same accumulation order for sum_x/sum_y/sum_xy/sum_x2/sum_y2 and the same swapped finalize for transpose cells. Fallback unchanged for any NaN/NaT/failed numeric conversion.

Proof obligations: RCH-built before/after hyperfine for df_corr and df_cov, golden sha256 unchanged for both scenarios, ordering/tie/FP association preserved, no RNG/hash behavior, focused fp-frame corr/cov tests/check/clippy/fmt/UBS as feasible; retain only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Rejected branchless all-present corr/cov loop: RCH-built paired hyperfine showed no real win. df_corr 5000x10 before 112.3 ms +/- 7.0, after 112.2 ms +/- 9.1; df_cov before 111.6 ms +/- 6.2, after 113.0 ms +/- 9.4. Golden corr/cov outputs stayed byte-identical (corr sha256 6f577ab82a9d5e845a81757e6bf2488425011bfa02252553900406fc27f0c80c; cov sha256 beb4e3f5011ca6bf5ca53046415118ca71e958037c31fdab574fd3e0b2ef8d99), but Score <2.0. Source hunk removed; fp-frame diff empty and cargo fmt -p fp-frame -- --check passed. Perf/gdb profiling on host was blocked or unusable: perf_event_paranoid=4, ptrace attach denied, perf record finalization stalled; sysctl restored to 4 after attempted user perf.

title: [parity] Series/DataFrame resample('W') errors instead of weekly (W-SUN) bins

Added a weekly branch to resample_build_groups: each label -> first Sunday on/after its date, bucket key = that Sunday (%Y-%m-%d), consistent with fp-frame's existing no-empty-bin-fill model (so correct for contiguous weekly data). All resample aggs (sum/mean/count/min/max/ohlc/...) route through resample_build_groups so they all gain W. Oracle-verified.

Golden SHA stayed 2b716d47c784429314fd64c9aa24ae9ca8fa4569e928c317fc8c2e1258c4b5c2; ordering/tie-breaking/FP/RNG unchanged because source was removed and the owned sorted-merge alignment path remains. title: Add fp-io reject-closed surfaces for read_clipboard/read_gbq/read_sas/read_spss
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): IOWrappers gap closes once read_clipboard, read_gbq, read_sas, read_spss are exposed. Each is out-of-scope for FrankenPandas's local-IO charter:

* read_clipboard — pulls from the OS clipboard; not implementable in a headless Rust crate without GUI bindings.
* read_gbq — calls Google BigQuery; would pull in GCP credentials + auth, outside the file-format scope.
* read_sas / read_spss — proprietary statistical-software formats; pandas implements them via pyreadstat / sas7bdat. No first-party Rust reader exists at parity.

Mirrors the deferred surface precedent set by frankenpandas-hci9o (plotting): expose typed compatibility-rejection functions so downstream code can program against the surface and fall through to a clean error rather than a missing symbol. Add:

- read_clipboard() -> Result<DataFrame, IoError>
- read_gbq(query: &str, project: Option<&str>) -> Result<DataFrame, IoError>
- read_sas(path: &Path) -> Result<DataFrame, IoError>
- read_spss(path: &Path) -> Result<DataFrame, IoError>

Each returns IoError::Fwf-style typed rejection (or a new IoError::Deferred variant if cleaner) with a pandas-leaning message. Add regression tests asserting each surface rejects with the expected message; update fp-io module docs to point readers at the deferral note. Single PR.
close_reason: Shipped in f9bc013: fp-io exposes read_clipboard/read_gbq/read_sas/read_spss as reject-closed surfaces backed by IoError::Deferred(String). Mirrors plotting deferral precedent (hci9o). 4 regression tests pass; 527 fp-io tests total.

title: resample() rejects N-day multiplied frequencies like '2D' (gauntlet CONF-RC2) — FIXED
description: pandas supports integer-multiplied resample frequencies (2D, 3D, 15min). FrankenPandas Resample::validate() only accepted exact Y|A|M|D, rejecting '2D' with 'compatibility gate rejected operation: resample: invalid frequency 2D'. This failed 10 packets (FP-P2D-264/265/266/267/268/342/343/344/346/347, series+dataframe resample sum/mean/count). FIXED in this gauntlet session: added parse_resample_freq + origin-anchored N-day bucketing (resample_build_groups) in crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs, keeping the mult==1 calendar path byte-identical. Verified: conformance fixture gate failures dropped 39->29, all 10 resample packets green, no regression. Multiplied calendar units (2M/2Y) still reject with a clear message (follow-up).
close_reason: done

title: [perf][rejected] drop_duplicates typed-Int64 dedup digest path — perf-neutral (frame-reuse amortizes materialization)
description: Extended DataFrame::duplicated typed fast path (g29hf, Float64-only) to also read all-valid Int64 key columns via as_i64_slice (mixed Int64+Float64 numeric frames). Bit-transparent: golden sha256 IDENTICAL before/after at n=5000 AND n=100000 (Int64 digest = mix(2)+mix(value as u64), eq = i64==, exactly matching the Scalar path; Float64 arm unchanged). REJECTED: interleaved hyperfine (15 runs, warmup 3) on a new drop_duplicates_dups scenario (Int64 key + repeated Float64 values, 1000 distinct of 100k, so the subset_has_all_valid_unique_column shortcut does NOT fire and the real dedup digest runs) showed 1.03x +/- 0.13 — perf-NEUTRAL, well below Score 2.0. ROOT CAUSE: perf_profile reuses ONE frame across all iters, so col.values() OnceLock Scalar materialization (which the typed path eliminates) happens ONCE and is amortized to ~0/iter; the per-iter cost is the already-O(n) parallel hash-dedup, where raw-buffer vs cached-Vec<Scalar> indexing is a wash. The typed path WOULD help genuine single-shot dedup (avoids allocating 2N Scalars) but no benchmark exercises that. SECONDARY FINDING: the bench 'drop_duplicates' (build_groupby_frame, unique Float64 value col) never reaches duplicated() at all — it hits subset_has_all_valid_unique_column -> self.clone(); its ~1.9x vs-pandas reading was DROPPED_HIGH_CV (machine contention) and is unreliable. from_values already auto-types all-valid Int64 via Self::new -> lazy_all_valid_int64_arc (no fp-columnar change needed). DO NOT re-attempt the typed dedup digest as a perf lever; if revisited, target genuine single-call dedup or the unique-shortcut/clone path, and only under a quiet machine.
close_reason: Rejected: typed Int64 extension of g29hf dedup digest preserved golden SHA but measured only 1.03x +/- 0.13 because perf_profile amortizes Scalar OnceLock materialization; no source retained. Do not re-attempt without a genuine single-shot dedup benchmark.

Bit-identical golden sha256 12e2ce8... (NaN-producing/Inf/-0.0/Int/nullable). fp-columnar 373/0 fp-frame 2818/0 conformance 419/0. Shipped 9e985895.

title: [perf] narrow kendall/spearman rank+order arrays usize->u32 to halve bandwidth (target ~2x on corr matrix)
description: Follow-up to wck31 (e5fde7e0). Uncapping workers 8->64 only gave kendall 1.34x / spearman 1.43x: the per-pair merge-sort inversion count over 200k-element rank/order arrays is MEMORY-BANDWIDTH-bound (~11 effective cores on a 64-core box at load 8). The arrays (kendall_no_tie_order Vec<usize>, kendall_rank_by_row_from_order Vec<usize>, and the merge buffers in kendall_no_tie_fast_with_ordered_ranks / count_f64_inversions) are usize=8B. Row count fits u32 for any realistic frame (n<2^32). Lever: narrow order/rank/merge-buffer element type usize->u32 (gate n<=u32::MAX), halving cache/memory traffic -> faster single-thread AND better parallel scaling, likely clearing 2x on df_kendall combined with the worker uncap. Bit-identical (u32 indices index the same rows, inversion count identical). Touches kendall_no_tie_order, kendall_rank_by_row_from_order, kendall_no_tie_fast_with_ordered_ranks, count_f64_inversions + the complete_kendall_no_tie_parallel_matrix refs; keep usize fallback for n>u32::MAX. Golden df_kendall 031978ba must hold.
close_reason: Shipped 380d560a: narrowed Kendall rank/order arrays + Fenwick counter to u32. Bit-identical (golden df_kendall 031978ba before==after; 53 corr tests green). 779->644 ms = 1.21x (stacks w/ wck31 to 1.64x over original 1053ms). BELOW 2.0: the u32 Fenwick is still ~800KB > L2, so the random-access BIT stays L3-latency-bound — width wasn't the wall. The real >=2x lever (cache-friendly sequential merge-sort inversion count replacing the Fenwick) filed as br-frankenpandas-7vg05.

title: Datetime accessor floor/ceil/round parity (FP-P2D-240/241/242)
description:
## Background
Conformance gates FP-P2D-240, FP-P2D-241, FP-P2D-242, FP-P2D-311, FP-P2D-312, FP-P2D-313 are failing for datetime floor/ceil/round operations.

## Justification
Time rounding operations have edge case differences vs pandas behavior. Affects 6 of 29 failing gates.

## Scope
- Investigate exact parity gaps (likely rounding edge cases or timezone handling)
- Fix DatetimeAccessor::floor(), ::ceil(), ::round()
- Update fixtures if needed
- Verify gates pass

## Acceptance Criteria
- FP-P2D-240/241/242 gates pass
- FP-P2D-311/312/313 gates pass
close_reason: done

title: groupby.sum() keeps Int64 when alignment/missing introduces NaN into an int column; pandas promotes to Float64
description: FP-P2C-005 groupby_sum_alignment_dropna + groupby_sum_int_keys: when a DataFrame is built from key+value series whose indexes DIFFER (or a value is missing), pandas' non-nullable int column promotes to float64 (NaN can't live in int64), so groupby.sum() returns Float64. FP keeps the column Int64-nullable and groupby.sum() returns Int64 -> conformance value mismatch (actual=Int64(5) vs expected Float64(5.0); Int64(40) vs Float64(40.0)). Verified live: pd.DataFrame from differing-index int series -> value col float64 -> groupby sum float64. ROOT: FP's int column with an introduced/aligned missing value stays Int64-nullable instead of promoting to Float64 like pandas' default (non-nullable) int64. This is the int64-vs-Int64-nullable dtype-model divergence; the l75ms int-sum-keeps-Int64 fix (c8267eac) is correct ONLY when no missing is present. FIX likely in from_series alignment dtype promotion (int col + any missing -> Float64) OR the groupby-int-sum path (promote to Float64 if any group value missing). Fixtures are CORRECT (float64); FP is wrong. The other 2 FP-P2C-005 cases (null_key, order, no NaN) were stale fixtures, fixed in e9a8331c. Sister context: exyng (conformance resync).
close_reason: Fixed in ce6d564d: fp-groupby is_int64_or_bool_values returns false on any missing, so groupby.sum() of an int column with missing (alignment or explicit null) yields Float64 like pandas. FP-P2C-005 groupby packet green; FP-P2C-011 agg-matrix also green (its int-Sum cases have no missing). The agg-Sum path (groupby_agg, value_dtype check ~1050) has the same latent gap but no fixture exercises int-Sum-with-missing there.

title: Add Series.clip method calls to eval query parser
description: Parity gap from pandas 2.2.3: DataFrame.eval/query accept Series.clip(lower, upper) method calls such as a.clip(2, 8), and query can use the resulting Series in comparisons. fp-frame already exposes Series::clip, but fp-expr rejects clip method calls. Add parser/evaluator support for positional numeric lower/upper bounds with focused eval/query coverage.
close_reason: Implemented eval/query Series.clip method-call parsing

title: [perf] outer_join: same fused matched-plan treatment as left (dense outer builder still builds Vec-of-Vec buckets + per-row position pushes; outer at ~760ms/iter vs left's new 28ms on same shape)
description: After df908786 the partial-match left join runs 9.8M rows in ~28ms/iter while outer_join on the same 100k/card512/50%-overlap shape sits at ~760ms/iter for 9.87M rows (~27x worse per row). build_single_key_dense_i64_outer... path (~2183+) builds left_buckets/right_buckets as Vec<Vec<usize>> (per-bucket heap allocs!) + repeat_count per-row push loops (~2238), then position vectors + reindex walks like the old left path. Lever: extend the fused matched-plan builder to OUTER — plan rows are (matched left x right runs) + unmatched-left singletons + unmatched-right singletons appended in right order (verify exact pandas row order from the existing builder/golden d774e320 FIRST); left lanes nullable too (unmatched-right rows have null left) so BOTH sides need the shared-validity treatment (two masks: left-lane mask, right-lane mask); CSR instead of Vec-of-Vec buckets. Reuse set_validity_bit_range, from_i64_values_with_validity, ValidityMask::from_words, RLE gate. Profile first to confirm. Gates: outer_join golden d774e320 + left 745e5a46 + inner be7d1711 unchanged, suites, join proptests, alternating A/B + hyperfine, Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped d6e42bad: fused dense-i64 outer merge builder (bucket-order plan over CSR + closed-form coalesced key runs + per-side promotion/validity + dual-type chunked fills). outer_join 100000 card512 50%-overlap: 17.77x+/-0.97 (2.319s->130.5ms process, 767-1106->44-46 ms/iter), goldens bit-identical x3, fp-columnar 373/0 fp-join 102/0 fp-frame 2820/0 join proptests 12/0. Join family now: inner 40ms/19.5M, left 28ms/9.8M, outer 44ms/9.87M — all dense i64 builders on probe->plan->fused-lanes.

Tests: drop_rows_int with non-existent Int64 labels returns Err.

title: DataFrameGroupBy::format_output as_index=true loses by-column name on result index
description: fp-frame/src/lib.rs:35505 builds Index::new(labels) without preserving the by-column name. Pandas df.groupby('col').<agg>() returns DataFrame whose .index.name == 'col'. For single-column by (most common case), self.by[0] is the name. Fix: when self.by.len() == 1, apply rename_index(Some(self.by[0].as_str())). For multi-column by, the result is intended as a MultiIndex; current flat fallback can leave None.
close_reason: Applied rename_index(Some(self.by[0])) for single-by case in format_output at fp-frame/src/lib.rs:35505. Covers DataFrameGroupBy.agg/sum/mean/etc. with single by-column.

Reused int64_dense_grouping bypass; extracted moments_by_pair/moments_assemble. groupby_agg_multi 1182->373us (3.2x), now 5.7x vs pandas (was 1.81x). Golden bf6811c3 unchanged, 2893 fp-frame tests pass.
close_reason: Shipped cfcf9868: agg_multi 3.2x via int64_dense_grouping reuse

title: [perf] Lazy repeat-run (RLE) backed output columns for dense i64 join left lanes (post-6bsw3: System time 6x User = output materialization is the wall)
description: Post-049572b8 paired hyperfine of inner_join 100000x3 (19.5M out rows): 285.1ms +/- 8.0 process with User=172.8ms System=1135.5ms — after the row-chunked fill, ~80% of wall is page-faulting + dropping the ~470MB/iter of freshly materialized output Vec<i64>s. The left-side output lanes are pure repeat-runs (each left value repeated bucket_len times) and the join emits them eagerly. Structural lever (memory-layout class change, mirrors the lazy unit-range Index + lazy typed IndexLabels precedents): an RLE/repeat-run backed ColumnData variant in fp-columnar ((value, run_len) pairs + OnceLock materialization) that as_i64_slice/values() expand on demand; dense join emits left lanes as runs (O(matched_left) memory instead of O(output)) and right lanes stay materialized. Consumers that only len()/head()/slice never pay the expansion. Gates: golden inner_join/join_1to1 shas unchanged, fp-columnar+fp-frame+fp-join suites, paired hyperfine on inner_join AND a downstream-read bench (sum over output column) to prove no regression when consumers force materialization, Score>=2.0. Risk: wide blast radius in fp-columnar — keep the variant private and route only as_i64_slice/values/take_positions through it first.
close_reason: Shipped as 36b0cf77 (source, committed by cod-pandas pane which swept my working tree mid-benchmark) + cd74a895 (A/B evidence + isomorphism proof). LazyRepeatRunsInt64 ScalarValues backing + from_i64_repeat_runs + dense join left lanes as runs when avg fanout>=2, parallel row-chunked expansion on demand. inner_join 100000x3: 2.37x+/-0.13 (284.0->119.9ms); forced-read gate 1.10x+/-0.14 (no regression; first serial-expansion cut regressed 2.2x and was fixed pre-ship). Goldens bit-identical, fp-columnar 373/0 fp-join 102/0 fp-frame 2820/0 prop_inner_join 4/0.

title: perf: DataFrame.replace_dict delegates to typed Series.replace (~10x per listed col)

title: [test] read_csv quoted numeric fields still infer numeric dtype
description: No-mock parity test for fp-io: a CSV column mixing quoted and unquoted numbers (1, "2", 3) still infers Int64 [1,2,3], and a mixed int/quoted-float column infers Float64 (quoting is per-field CSV syntax, stripped before inference). Distinct from the existing all-quoted-Float64 test. read_csv_str dtype+values asserts. Test-only; fp-io.

title: [mock-code-finder] DataFrame::filter_labels emits 'regex invalid' instead of expected 'invalid regex' (breaks n33na test)
description:
Test dataframe_filter_labels_rejects_invalid_selector_contract_n33na (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:56503) asserts that filter_labels with an invalid regex returns FrameError::CompatibilityRejected with msg containing "invalid regex".

But filter_axis (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:24571) emits "filter regex invalid: {e}" — word order swapped, so substring match fails. Test panics: `assertion failed: matches!(bad_column_regex, FrameError::CompatibilityRejected(msg) if msg.contains("invalid regex"))`.

Same issue likely affects the axis=0 path in filter_row_positions_by_label (regex compile error there).

Fix: change error message to "filter: invalid regex {pattern}: {e}" (or similar) so it satisfies the test's "invalid regex" substring while remaining clear. Apply to both axis=1 (column regex) and axis=0 (row regex) paths.

Tests: existing n33na test passes after change. Regression-check full fp-frame --lib suite.
close_reason: Fixed error message word order. Commit 6080c79e. Test n33na + 22 other filter tests pass.

title: [perf] Series contiguous-Utf8 sort bypass for str_sort_chain
description: Profile-backed target from current-main census on 2026-06-07 by OrangePeak. No ready [perf] beads existed; in-progress perf beads were owned by RubyGoose/BrightOtter. Scenario: perf_profile str_sort_chain 200000 5 on HEAD daa18f3216205410a2e9c50db8c2c77d018b3bfc. Baseline hyperfine: 830.6 ms +/- 38.0. perf record 749 samples: ScalarValues::as_slice 22.64% self, Series::sort_values_na comparator closure 21.72% self, Column::take_positions 9.95%, __memcmp_avx2_movbe 5.13% self / 33.47% with children. Lever: add the existing contiguous all-valid Utf8 argsort path to Series::sort_values_na for categorical.is_none(), na_position=last, contiguous all-valid Utf8 columns, reusing Column::argsort_with and existing Series reorder. title: [test] eval_str arithmetic operator precedence/associativity correctness

title: [parity-bug] to_csv does not quote empty string values (round-trips as NaN, not '')
description:
Oracle pandas 2.2.3: DataFrame({'a':['','x','y']}).to_csv(index=False) -> 'a
""
x
y
' — an empty STRING value is quoted as "" so read_csv round-trips it as '' not NaN. FP needs_quote('') is false (no special bytes) so FP emits unquoted '', which read_csv reads back as missing — a round-trip data loss. (pandas also renders NaN/None in an OBJECT column as "", conflating them; the clear bug is empty-string -> unquoted.) Fix is dtype/value-aware (quote empty Utf8 cells) across the typed fast path AND the csv-crate general path.
close_reason: Empty-string sole-field quoting fixed in typed CSV writer; pandas-oracle-verified; 126 csv tests green

title: rolling sum/mean O(n) sliding-window (pandas Kahan-compensated) — needs golden regen
description: Rolling.apply_rolling (fp-frame ~16316) computes rolling sum/mean by re-folding each window O(n*window). No O(n) variant (sliding accumulator add/sub, or prefix-sum subtraction) is bit-identical to fresh per-window Iterator::sum (f64 non-associativity), so the current naive path is REQUIRED for FP's bit-exact assert_text_golden rolling_sum/mean goldens. pandas itself uses an O(n) sliding window with a Kahan 'compensation' term + num_consecutive_same_value reset (window/aggregations.pyx roll_sum). Porting that exact algorithm would be O(n) AND more pandas-faithful, but it CHANGES FP output -> requires regenerating all rolling_sum/mean/std/var goldens + team sign-off (same class as w8jn0). Conformance vs pandas is tolerance-based so would stay green. Deferred: bit-breaking, needs golden-regen decision.
close_reason: Already shipped: Series rolling sum/mean route to rolling_online_sum_mean and std/var to rolling_var_online (both O(n) compensated online algorithms matching pandas aggregations.pyx) — landed under br-frankenpandas-slt1p @0d4d9962 (incl. std/var follow-up). DataFrame/grouped rolling route transitively. Goldens already regenerated at that commit; fp-frame + conformance green. Bead description was stale (pre-slt1p snapshot).

title: [bug] int**int returns Float64 (powf) but pandas keeps int64; and negative int exponent should raise (fp-columnar Pow kernel)
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: VERIFIED vs pandas 2.2.3. FrankenPandas: eval('a ** 2') on Int64 [1..5] returns Float64(9.0) at idx2 (and a Float64 column). pandas: df.eval('a**2') and Series**2 return int64 [1,4,9,16,25]. Root cause: fp-columnar binary_numeric Pow path always uses powf — crates/fp-columnar/src/lib.rs:663 (ArithmeticOp::Pow => |a,b| a.powf(b)) and the Div|Pow float-forcing branches at 772/801/2008/2067. Two parity gaps: (1) int**non-negative-int should stay Int64 (numpy/pandas semantics); (2) pandas/numpy RAISES ValueError 'Integers to negative integer powers are not allowed' for int**negative-int — FrankenPandas silently returns a float. By contrast floor-div (//), mod (%), +,-,* on ints correctly keep Int64 (verified). Fix is in fp-columnar (RESERVED by RubyGoose) — coordinate. Repro/regression: query_str_chained_comparison_and_ops_match_pandas in fp-expr documents the surrounding correct behaviors. Reached via eval/query and direct Column::pow / Series ** arithmetic.

title: [perf] Fuse NaN-tracked gather into position-based aligned_binary_f64 (different-index Float64 add, eliminate twin nan_aware_validity pre-scans + output rescan)
description: Follow-up to br-frankenpandas-9houf (same-index fast path, 3.2x). The position-based Column::aligned_binary_f64 (the different-index/reindex Float64 alignment path, fp-frame series add line ~4199) still pays 4 memory passes: self.nan_aware_validity() + right.nan_aware_validity() (two FULL input-column NaN scans) + the gather/apply main loop + from_f64_values output rescan. numpy/pandas alignment pays one. LEVER (one, fusion not deferral): drop both nan_aware_validity pre-scans; gate each gathered element inline with self.validity.get(i) && !lsrc[i].is_nan() (== nan_aware_validity.get(i), but only at referenced positions), and stamp all-valid output via from_f64_values_all_valid_unchecked when the loop proves no NaN. Bit-identical: same per-element validity decision, same data, same from_f64_values result for the generated-NaN case. ISOMORPHISM: ordering/positions/null/NaN(Null(NaN) vs Float64(NaN))/validity/f64 bits/fallback all preserved. Golden: aligned_binary_f64 over all 7 ops with full + gappy positions, byte-identical before/after. Paired+reversed hyperfine, Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Kept: fused NaN-tracked gather into position-based aligned_binary_f64. Dropped the two full input nan_aware_validity pre-scans; gate inline at gathered positions (validity.get(i) && !lsrc[i].is_nan() == nan_aware_validity(self).get(i)) + from_f64_values_all_valid_unchecked stamp (no output rescan). 14 goldens (7 ops x full+gappy) byte-identical; identity-alignment + same_positions==general NaN/inf locks green. Bench n=100000 op-only min-of-5 add 0.667->0.555ms 1.20x (hyperfine fwd 1.23x/rev 1.13x). HONEST: path is gather-bound, scan removal only ~17% of cost -> 1.20x (vs 3.2x contiguous same-index 9houf). Next deeper lever = the Option-gather itself (route contiguous/affine position runs to slice kernel / branchless validity fold). Gates: fp-columnar lib 404/0, fp-frame arith 59/0, clippy -D clean, fmt clean, no unsafe. Commit ea11fc8e. Witness 419of_fused_nan_gather_aligned_f64_positions_witness.md

title: [modes-of-reasoning] DataFrame::to_csv renders whole-number Float64 as "3" instead of pandas-canonical "3.0"
description:
Skill: /modes-of-reasoning-project-analysis.

Discovered while pinning the to_csv_options golden in br-464b83:
fp-frame to_csv (lib.rs:25536) and to_csv_options (lib.rs:25614) render
Scalar::Float64(v) via v.to_string(). Rust f64::to_string strips trailing
.0 for whole numbers, so 3.0 -> "3" and pandas-roundtrip clients lose
the dtype hint.

Pandas is consistent:
  >>> pd.DataFrame({"a":[2.5, 3.0]}).to_csv(index=False)
  "a
2.5
3.0
"

The to_markdown (lib.rs:25857), to_latex (25987), and to_psql (26056)
paths in fp-frame ALREADY apply the right pattern:
  if *v == v.round() && v.abs() < 1e15 { format!("{v:.1}") } else { v.to_string() }
which produces 3.0 -> "3.0". The CSV paths just don't share that helper.

Fix: extract a small format_pandas_csv_float helper (same predicate as
to_markdown), call it from both CSV paths. Refresh the prior golden
(dataframe_to_csv_options_quoting_and_na_rep.txt) since 3.0 now renders
as "3.0".

title: Add Column replace alias

title: fp-frame DataFrameGroupBy::idxmin + idxmax return NaN for Timedelta64 columns
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:37040 DataFrameGroupBy::idxmin and (sister) idxmax use val.to_f64() which fails on Timedelta64. best_idx stays None, agg_vals gets Scalar::Null(NaN). pandas df.groupby(k).idxmin() on Timedelta column returns the original index label of the min Timedelta per group.

Surgical: add a Timedelta64 branch inside the per-group loop. If `val` is Scalar::Timedelta64(ns) and ns != NAT, compare ns directly (as i64). Sister to br-erobu (Series::min) and br-tgm2i (SeriesGroupBy::min Timedelta surgery).
close_reason: done

title: [test] differential: Series.duplicated/drop_duplicates vs first-seen oracle

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::iloc/loc/head/tail/take/sort_values/sort_index/filter all lose index name (broad Self::from_values issue)
description:
Following br-lxot8 (dropna) and br-um15v (drop), an Explore scan shows 11+ more Series methods that lose the index name through the same root cause — they build the result Series via `Self::from_values(name, labels, values)` or `Index::new(labels)` without carrying over `self.index.name()`.

Affected (high-impact subset):
- Series::iloc (line ~3700)
- Series::take (line ~6732)
- Series::head, tail (lines ~3900, ~3910)
- Series::loc, loc_bool, filter (lines ~3682, ~3727, ~3632)
- Series::reorder_by_positions (line ~3040) — called by sort_index, sort_values
- Series::sort_index (line ~3837)
- Series::sort_values (line ~3852)

Pandas preserves the index name through all of these operations.

Surgical fix: add an internal helper `Series::with_labels_and_values_named(name, labels, values, idx_name)` (or modify Series::reorder_by_positions to rename) so each call site gets a one-line update. Or simpler: fix Series::reorder_by_positions to preserve self.index.name() (covers sort/iloc/loc paths), and add explicit rename_index in the few direct from_values callers.

Tests: regression test per method group asserting `result.index().name() == Some("idx")` after iloc/sort/etc. on a named-index Series.

Lower-bound effort: 1 hour. May need 2-3 follow-up commits if any callers' return types change shape.
close_reason: Fixed Series::reorder_by_positions to apply rename_index; fixed Series::head and Series::tail to use Self::new with renamed Index. Covers iloc/loc/sort_values/sort_index/take/head/tail. Commit b954c163. 1910 fp-frame tests pass.

title: fp-columnar: add percentile and nan-aware index aliases
Add Timestamp normalize accessor
description: Parity gap from pandas Timestamp public surface: pandas Timestamp.normalize() returns the timestamp floored to midnight/day while preserving timezone metadata. Rust fp_types::Timestamp has day flooring through floor_to_unit(D) but lacks normalize(). Add a thin method plus focused fp-types coverage.
close_reason: Implemented Timestamp normalize accessor

title: [testing-golden-artifacts] DataFrame::to_csv_options has no golden lock-in for CSV escaping + na_rep
description:
Skill: /testing-golden-artifacts.

DataFrame::to_csv_options (lib.rs:25553) routes through csv_escape (lib.rs:901)
which double-quotes any string containing the separator, double-quote, or
newline/carriage-return, and escapes inner double-quotes by doubling them.
The function also substitutes na_rep for Null and Timedelta-NAT cells,
rounds bools to True/False, and supports a column subset via the columns
parameter.

Existing goldens cover only the default to_csv (no na_rep, no quoting test)
via dataframe_display.txt — nothing pins the to_csv_options surface.

A silent regression in csv_escape (e.g. forgetting to escape inner quotes,
swapping which characters trigger quoting, or breaking the na_rep insertion)
would compile and pass the unit tests but break consumers that round-trip
through pandas read_csv.

Fix: add dataframe_to_csv_options_quoting_and_na_rep.txt golden test
covering: a string with comma + double-quote + newline (forces escape),
Null cells replaced with na_rep "NA", boolean True/False, column subset.

title: [mock-code-finder] td_* family silently returns NAT/false for non-Timedelta operands instead of erroring
description:
Skill: /mock-code-finder.

fp-frame Series::td_* methods route through helpers (td_binary_op, td_scalar_op,
td_cmp_op, td_ratio at lib.rs:2224,2312,2337,2390) that all have catch-all
match arms:
  _ => Scalar::Timedelta64(NAT)        // td_binary_op, td_scalar_op
  _ => Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN)      // td_ratio
  _ => Scalar::Bool(false)              // td_cmp_op

If a user calls e.g. td_add on a Float64 series (or mixed dtypes between
operands), the catch-all silently swallows the type mismatch and produces
all-NAT / all-false output instead of erroring.

Pandas raises TypeError for these ops on non-timedelta dtypes. Silent
all-false from td_gt is especially dangerous because it looks like the
comparison ran successfully.

Fix: add validate_timedelta_operand helper that accepts only DType::Timedelta64
or DType::Null (all-missing). Call it at the top of each helper before the
per-element loop. Also gate td_neg and td_abs which iterate directly.

Add pandas-style index pseudo-series bindings without overriding same-named columns, plus focused query_str coverage.

title: [perf] Cache or fuse sorted contiguous-Utf8 monotonic witness for string-key joins
description: Follow-up from br-frankenpandas-lr52z after-profile. lr52z shipped overlap-bounded ordered merge for sorted unique contiguous-Utf8 string-key inner joins: str_inner_join 1M x10 improved 246.6ms +/-16.4 -> 208.2ms +/-5.8 (1.18x), golden SHA unchanged 76d2f388645ed3f3578017c5e2d919fa809e4230793a86d54d5ca93a6d0bc10e. After profile still shows merge_single_key_inner_unsorted dominated by byte comparisons: __memcmp_avx2_movbe ~49% children and strictly_increasing_utf8_key_spans ~20%. Next primitive: cache an all-valid contiguous-Utf8 strictly-increasing/unique witness on Column/Index metadata OR fuse witness validation with the ordered seek/merge so each relevant span is compared once while still validating tails before returning Some. Must preserve pandas semantics: only fast path when both sides are all-valid contiguous Utf8 and strictly increasing unique; unsorted, duplicate, null-bearing, or scalar-backed keys fall back to byte-span hash/generic path. Baseline/profile first on str_inner_join; golden SHA and focused duplicate/order tests required.
close_reason: Completed cached contiguous-UTF8 strict-witness reuse for sorted string joins. str_inner_join 1000000 hyperfine mean improved 198.4 ms +/- 5.4 to 139.5 ms +/- 6.7, 1.4229x speedup, Score 2.73. title: [mock-code-finder] Series isna/notna and other same-index transforms still lose index name (07yrq follow-up)
description:
After 07yrq landed the helper + 36 site conversions, residual sites still call Self::from_values directly. The remaining same-index transforms include:

- Series::isna (line 4275)
- Series::notna (line 4296)
- Series::value_counts_bins (line 6322 — uses self.index.labels() in indirect way)

title: [perf] fp-io read_csv_with_options allocates per numeric cell in default parser
description: PROFILED 2026-06-02 RubyGoose via profiling-software-performance on fp-io CSV read, release-perf RCH harness. Scenario: 100000 rows x 10 dense numeric columns, 20 reads/command. Existing simple read_csv_str: 1.606 s mean. Default read_csv_with_options: 1.946 s mean, 21% slower on identical input. read_csv_with_options with na_filter=false: 1.850 s mean, so NA membership is not the main cost. Code inspection of the profile target points to parse_scalar_with_options allocating String per cell through numeric_candidate = trimmed.to_owned() when thousands is None and float_candidate = numeric_candidate.clone() when decimal is '.'. One contained lever: make parse_scalar_with_options borrow the trimmed field on the default thousands/decimal path and allocate only when replacement is actually required. Preserve pandas-visible parsing, ordering, floating-point strings, NA semantics, true/false parsing, and dtype output; prove with golden sha and fp-io CSV tests.
close_reason: Completed: fp-io read_csv_with_options default numeric parsing now borrows trimmed fields via Cow and only allocates when thousands/decimal replacement is required. RCH hyperfine csv_read_options 100000x20 improved 1.946492 s mean to 1.557213 s mean (20.00% faster); golden sha256 unchanged 17f95925cec162c66933d5e935566b6f6fec5f8ef56872b98b00fb97ebf59fff.

title: DataFrame::quantile result Series uses unnamed column-axis (should match dtypes pattern)

close_reason: Verified: DataFrame::quantile builds a column-axis index (one entry per numeric column). Pandas df.quantile(q) returns a Series whose .index.name is None unless df.columns.name is set. Our DataFrame model doesn't track a column-axis name, so None default already matches pandas. No divergence to fix without first adding column-axis-name support — that's a structural change, out of scope for an index-name-preservation cycle.
