title: [deadlock-finder] subprocess pipe deadlock pattern in 6 fp-conformance test sites (sweep follow-up to br-92df89)
description:
Skill: /deadlock-finder-and-fixer.

br-92df89 fixed the synchronous-stdin-write-before-wait_with_output pattern
in capture_live_oracle_expected (lib.rs:12603). The same anti-pattern
exists in 6 conformance test sites:

  conformance_io.rs:187-198 (read_csv oracle)
  conformance_io_formats.rs (csv-formats oracle)
  conformance_groupby_apply.rs:129-145 (GroupBy.apply oracle)
  conformance_tseries.rs:154-170 (pandas function oracle, 1st)
  conformance_tseries.rs:285-301 (pandas offset oracle)
  conformance_tseries.rs:376+    (4th tseries oracle)

Each does:
  let mut stdin = child.stdin.take().ok_or_else(...)?;
  stdin.write_all(payload)?;
  drop(stdin);
  let output = child.wait_with_output()?;

For a payload exceeding the OS stdin pipe buffer (~64 KB) AND a child
that emits any output before reading all input filling its stdout pipe
buffer (~64 KB), parent and child deadlock on opposite-direction writes.

Test fixtures are mostly small but CSV roundtrip + scale fixtures can
hit it. Sweep all 6 sites with the same threaded-write fix.

title: [perf] byte-radix sort-merge OR parallel hash-partition for string-key inner join (target >=2x; FxHashMap is the wall)
description: Follow-up to i388q (9cb0fa23). The byte-span build+probe eliminated ~4M String clones (1.30x) but the FxHashMap build+probe over n keys is now the dominant cost and is unchanged. To clear 2x on string-key joins, REPLACE the hash join: (a) byte-radix MSD sort both key columns' spans (the project already has FP byte-radix MSD argsort, memory vecff) then merge — O(n) radix + O(n) merge, no hashing/collisions, and the sort is parallelizable; or (b) parallel hash-partition: split keys into P partitions by hash prefix across a thread::scope pool, build+probe each partition independently, concatenate position pairs preserving left-ascending order (needs a stable merge of per-partition (left_pos,right_pos) by left_pos). Both build on contiguous_utf8_inner_positions' byte-span keys. Bit-identical: same match set + same (left asc, right bucket-order) pairing. Bench str_inner_join (golden 55882afc must hold). Profile first to pick (a) vs (b): for near-unique 1:1 sort-merge likely wins; for high-fanout hash-partition. Extend to left/right/outer after inner.
close_reason: Shipped ordered strictly-unique contiguous-Utf8 byte-span merge in fp-join. str_inner_join 1M x10: 2.360s +/-0.102 -> 256.0ms +/-11.1 (9.22x); golden SHA before==after 76d2f388645ed3f3578017c5e2d919fa809e4230793a86d54d5ca93a6d0bc10e; fp-join tests/check/clippy/fmt pass; proof tests/artifacts/perf/proof_53lat_str_inner_join_orangepeak.md; follow-up br-frankenpandas-lr52z for shifted byte-comparison wall.

title: [perf] dense Int64 bucket outer merge positions
description:
Profile-backed target after 9f478b95: RCH release-perf join-bench non-unique hash joins rows=50000/right_rows=50000/key_cardinality=10000 measured inner 0.541 ms, left 35.837 ms, right 37.856 ms, outer 59.107 ms (artifact tests/artifacts/perf/fp_before_hash_join_profile_blackthrush_pass3.txt). The generic single-key outer path materializes CompositeJoinKey rows, builds both maps, records per-output keys, then stable-sorts 250000 output rows by key.

Alien primitive: dense-domain counting bucket / radix-style join emission for all-valid Int64 keys. One lever only: when single-key Int64 outer merge has all-valid keys, no indicator, no validate, and the min..max span is bounded relative to row count, bucket left/right row positions by key and emit output positions in ascending-key stable order, preserving the generic outer sorted row order without hashing or per-row key cloning. Fallback for nulls, non-Int64, sparse huge domains, multi-key, indicator, validate, and all ambiguous semantics.

Proof obligations: RCH baseline+after, golden sha256 unchanged for deterministic non-unique outer_join fixture, preserve key sort order, equal-key left/right row order, unmatched-side null/NaN promotion, shared key values, suffix behavior, RangeIndex, no FP/RNG/tie/hash-order observable behavior; RCH fp-join focused test/check/clippy, fmt, UBS. Retain only Score>=2.0.

Baseline/profile RCH release-perf join-bench non-unique outer rows=50000/right_rows=50000/key_cardinality=10000 mean/p50/p95/p99 59.107/58.534/65.904/66.984 ms, checksum 49997155700.000. After RCH-built release-perf fast mean/p50/p95/p99 30.184/29.564/33.559/36.643 ms vs forced generic 59.668/57.945/67.991/68.838 ms; checksum unchanged 49997155700.000. title: fix: .div/.floordiv(fill_value) by zero gave NaN not +/-inf; .mod(fill_value) used dividend sign
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed in 457ad27b. binary_op_fill (the .add/.div/.mod/... fill_value family) had 2 bugs in all 3 align branches: (1) div/floordiv by zero special-cased to NaN; pandas gives +/-inf (numerator sign), only 0/0=NaN. Dropped the guard -> Rust IEEE division. (2) mod used Rust % (dividend sign); pandas floored mod takes divisor sign (3%-2=-1). Used floored identity l-(l/r).floor()*r. Verified vs live pandas 2.2.3. Tests series_div_fill_by_zero_is_inf_not_nan + series_mod_fill_takes_divisor_sign. lib 2777/0. NOTE: worth checking the REGULAR (non-fill) div/mod path in fp-columnar binary_numeric for the same /0 and mod-sign handling (reserved by cod).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: RESOLVED the follow-up: verified the REGULAR (non-fill) path is CORRECT — fp-columnar binary_numeric Div = native a/b (inf), Mod = python_mod_f64 (floored/divisor-sign), FloorDiv = python_floor_div_f64. The bug was isolated to fp-frame binary_op_fill's hand-rolled arithmetic, which diverged from the correct kernels; this fix realigns it. No fp-columnar change needed.

title: [test] value-multiset oracle: fp-join inner join matches correct rows (not just count)
description: ya1po validated join ROW COUNTS but not WHICH rows are matched — a join can have correct cardinality yet join wrong rows. Add a deterministic (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks) value-level oracle for INNER join: random many-to-many Int64-key left/right DataFrames each with a distinct payload column; assert the multiset of output (key, left_payload, right_payload) triples equals the independently-computed cross-product { (k, lp, rp) : k in both, lp in left[k], rp in right[k] } (sorted-vector comparison => ordering-independent). Catches wrong-match / mis-paired-row bugs in the dense/direct-address inner join fast path (d45a291c) that cardinality alone misses. Test-only; fp-join.
close_reason: Added value-level oracle inner_join_value_multiset_matches_crossproduct_oracle_5foyp (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks): 1500 iters of many-to-many Int64-key joins with distinct per-row payloads, asserting the multiset of output (key,left_payload,right_payload) triples == the independent per-key cross product. Goes deeper than ya1po (cardinality) by verifying WHICH rows are matched — catches wrong-match/mis-pairing bugs in the dense inner join fast path (d45a291c). Result: PASS. Verified: targeted 1 passed, full fp-join lib suite 118 passed/0 failed. Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/5foyp_join_value_multiset_oracle.txt

Inconsistent and the success path diverges from pandas.

title: Series/DataFrame.pct_change() default does not forward-fill NaN; pandas 2.2.3 default fill_method='pad' does (oracle contract)
description: pandas 2.2.3 Series.pct_change()/DataFrame.pct_change() with NO fill_method uses default fill_method='pad' (forward-fill NaN BEFORE computing), emitting a FutureWarning (removed in 3.0). The conformance oracle (pandas_oracle.py op_series_pct_change line 2927 / op_dataframe_pct_change 2914) calls series.pct_change(periods=int(periods)) with NO fill_method -> so the parity contract IS ffill-default. Verified: pd.Series([10,nan,12,12,6]).pct_change() = [NaN,0.0,0.2,0.0,-0.5] (NaN ffilled to 10 first). FP Series::pct_change (fp-frame 10404) and Column::pct_change (fp-columnar 4690) do NOT fill -> propagate NaN -> would give [NaN,NaN,NaN,0.0,-0.5]. DIVERGENCE untested by conformance (pct_change fixtures avoid NaN, suite green). Also DOC BUG: both pct_change_with_fill docs (fp-frame 10449, fp-columnar) claim 'fill_method=None preserves pandas 2.2 default behavior (no fill)' — WRONG, that's the pandas 3.0 default; 2.2 default is 'pad'. FIX: Series::pct_change + DataFrame::pct_change(+axis1) default should ffill (delegate to pct_change_with_fill(periods, Some('pad'), None)); fix the docs. BLOCKED: fp-frame reserved by RubyGoose. NOTE deprecation ambiguity — FP adopted the post-deprecation (3.0) behavior, but the 2.2.3 oracle contract is ffill. Sister to interpolate 8ic7c (limit_direction default).
close_reason: Completed in 58cc82c4 co-land: Series::pct_change now uses pandas 2.2.3 default fill_method='pad' via pct_change_with_fill, pct_change_core preserves explicit no-fill behavior, and focused fp-frame tests passed through RCH.

title: [parity-bug] interval[float64] renders as '(0, 5]' instead of pandas '(0.0, 5.0]' (Rust Display drops .0)

FP's Interval stores f64 (interval[float64], no int subtype), so it should match the FLOAT form '(0.0, 5.0]' — but Interval Display (fp-types ~4198) uses Rust '{}' which drops the .0 on whole floats, giving '(0, 5]'. The existing test pins the int-subtype string, which is wrong for FP's float dtype. FIX: format endpoints with Python-str(float) semantics (keep .0 on whole numbers, signed 2-digit sci notation) in Interval Display. Ripples to csv/json/html/repr + the existing '(0,5]' test. fp-types core change.

title: [perf] Knight O(n log n) Kendall tau-b for tied data (replaces O(n^2) loop)
description: Series::corr_kendall (and DataFrame.corr(method=kendall), which falls back through it) had an O(n log n) no-tie fast path but reverted to an O(n^2) all-pairs loop whenever x OR y had ties (common: integer/categorical/repeated data). Added Knight's algorithm: lexsort by (x,y), tie-group counts (xtie/ytie/ntie via run-length), discordant pairs via the existing mergesort inversion counter -> tau-b = (n0-xtie-ytie+ntie-2*dis)/sqrt((n0-xtie)(n0-ytie)). Bit-identical to the loop (numerator C-D and denom are the same integer-exact f64 operands); gated to defer to the loop on non-finite or epsilon-near-but-not-exact ties it cannot reproduce. Proof: golden sha256 aaceeee4 unchanged (5 cases incl NaN) + 4000/4000 randomized bit-for-bit cross-check vs a verbatim copy of the old loop. Bench n=40k tied: 1910->17.8ms = 107x. fp-frame 2818 pass.
close_reason: done

title: [mock-code-finder] fp-types nansum/nanmean silently drop Timedelta64 (returns 0.0 Float64 instead of summing/averaging deltas)
description:
fp-types/src/lib.rs:1165 collect_finite uses `.filter_map(|v| v.to_f64().ok())`, which drops every Timedelta64 value because Timedelta64.to_f64() errors. Consequently:

- nansum on a Timedelta64 column returns Scalar::Float64(0.0) (empty fallback at line 1176)
- nanmean on a Timedelta64 column returns Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN) (empty fallback at line 1184)

Pandas:
- `td_series.sum()` returns Timedelta64 (sum of ns)
- `td_series.mean()` returns Timedelta64 (mean of ns, integer-truncated or rounded)

Fix: add a Timedelta64 fast path BEFORE collect_finite. Detect when at least one non-missing value is Timedelta64 (and all others are Timedelta64 or NAT). Sum ns directly in i64 (or i128 to avoid overflow). For nansum, return Scalar::Timedelta64(sum). For nanmean, return Scalar::Timedelta64(sum / count).

Tests: nansum on Timedelta64 returns Timedelta64 sum; nanmean returns Timedelta64 mean; mixing Timedelta64 with other types should NaN (current catch-all behavior preserved); NAT values skipped.

Related: br-yic5m (nanmin/nanmax fixed). br-qrn2w (groupby_sum has similar issue, more invasive).
close_reason: Added Timedelta64 fast path via collect_timedelta_ns helper. nansum/nanmean preserve Timedelta64 dtype. Commit 5231c6ff. 164 fp-types + 296 fp-columnar + 1901 fp-frame tests pass.

title: Add DatetimeIndex date/time accessors
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): DatetimeIndex still misses date/time accessors that decompose timestamps to chrono::NaiveDate/NaiveTime.

Add two methods on DatetimeIndex:
- date() -> Vec<Option<chrono::NaiveDate>> — extracts the calendar date part of each label; NAT propagates as None.
- time() -> Vec<Option<chrono::NaiveTime>> — extracts the within-day clock time of each label; NAT propagates as None.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: known timestamp → matching NaiveDate (year-month-day) and NaiveTime (hh:mm:ss.nnnnnnnnn), midnight → 00:00:00.000000000 NaiveTime, NAT propagation.

title: perf(fp-frame): O(n) online rolling std/var (Welford) — pandas ~40x faster
description: Follow-up to slt1p (mean/sum landed @0d4d9962). Rolling::std/var still recompute each window (two-pass mean+SS, O(n*w)); bench_runner rolling/std 100k = 64ms vs pandas 1.61ms (~40x). Replace with pandas roll_var online algorithm (aggregations.pyx): Welford-style running mean + M2 via add_var/remove_var with Kahan compensation_add/compensation_remove + the num_consecutive_same_value==nobs -> variance 0 exactness, ddof handling. Two-pointer sweep over window_bounds (same structure as the landed roll_sum/roll_mean in rolling_online_sum_mean). CAUTION: rolling std/var text goldens (series_rolling_var_basic.txt, dataframe_rolling_std/var_basic.txt) contain ULP-sensitive decimals — verify they survive the reassociation or regenerate vs live pandas; live-oracle uses semantic_eq 1e-14 rel tolerance. Add an online-vs-naive cross-check mirroring rolling_online_sum_mean_matches_naive_reference. Develop in a clean worktree at >= origin/main 0d4d9962 (shared perf-loop-noguard tree is contaminated w/ root-owned files).
close_reason: Implemented pandas-style online rolling std/var. Same-worker vmi1227854 Criterion rolling/std_w50 rows/10000: 3.6612 ms -> 267.15 us (13.70x); rows/100000: 37.830 ms -> 2.8082 ms (13.47x). Validation: rolling_ tests, check --lib --tests, clippy --lib --tests, golden SHA witness.

title: [test] property test: cast_scalar identity + numeric/bool coercion rules
description: cast_scalar/cast_scalar_owned (fp-types) is the scalar dtype-coercion path used by astype and dtype promotion (sacred coercion semantics). Add a deterministic (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks) property test over random Int64/Bool/Float64 scalars asserting the confirmed rules: identity cast(v,v.dtype())==v; Int64(n)->Bool == Bool(n!=0); Int64(n)->Float64 == Float64(n as f64); Bool(b)->Int64 == Int64(b as i64); Bool(b)->Float64 == Float64(0/1); finite Float64(x)->Int64 == Int64(x as i64); Int64<->Int64Nullable and Bool<->BoolNullable are representation-preserving identities. Test-only; fp-types.
close_reason: batch-verified-green: fp-types suite 242 passed/0 failed (1ews0 test corrected: cast-of-missing->Utf8 yields a string per pandas astype(str), not missing)

title: [testing-conformance-harnesses] Live pandas oracle missing Series.str.removeprefix Unicode exactness

Add a strict live oracle case for series_str_removeprefix with prefix 'é' covering exact composed prefix removal, decomposed non-match, repeated composed prefix, and no-match baseline. Enable system pandas fallback so rch/CI hosts without legacy_pandas_code still execute the reference comparison instead of pass-by-skip.

title: [perf] Optimize dense i64 inner left repeat-fill and output memory lifecycle after muis1
description: Post-br-frankenpandas-muis1 reprofile: right-side bucket gather was replaced by a bucket-value tape and paired inner_join 100000x3 improved 592.5 ms +/- 40.7 -> 446.8 ms +/- 25.7 (1.33x), with exact golden sha256 unchanged. After profile is 138.690 ms/iter and the remaining samples stay in the dense output worker closure plus bulk copy/drop/materialization memory lifecycle. Next pass must baseline via RCH from current main, use one lever only, and preserve the dense join isomorphism: left probe order, right bucket insertion order, output column names/order/dtypes, null/mixed/sorted/indicator/validate fallbacks, no FP/RNG. Candidate deeper primitives: safe-Rust left repeat-fill disjoint write plan, lazy/RLE-safe output column representation for repeated dense key/value lanes, or output-column memory lifecycle reduction. Golden gates: inner_join sha256 494106fca6e3310a318f1685c74041a2788089a4d2409107d4eef4a00c7a0764 and join_1to1 sha256 102690aa39952cc2d13bcc41547aacdeac1946113e43d62472fdb93440bc56a7 unchanged; fp-join + focused fp-conformance prop_inner_join; paired hyperfine; keep only Score>=2.0.

title: [mock-code-finder] Index::difference uses shared-name logic instead of preserving self.name (pandas convention)
description:
fp-index/src/lib.rs has Index::difference implementations for DatetimeIndex (3347), TimedeltaIndex (5643), RangeIndex (via set_op_via_int 7908), and CategoricalIndex (via set_op_via_string 8824) that use shared-name preservation (only keep name when both indices match):

```rust
if let Some(name) = self.name().filter(|_| self.name() == other.name()) {
    out = out.set_name(name);
}
```

The base Index::difference (line 696) correctly uses `self.propagate_name(...)` which always preserves self.name. The typed index variants are inconsistent.

Pandas convention:
- union/intersection: shared_name (only when both match)
- difference: always preserves self.name (asymmetric op)
- symmetric_difference: shared_name

Surgical fix: in each typed difference, always copy self.name(), not shared_name. For helper-based ones (set_op_via_int used by both union AND difference), need a parameter or split paths.

Simple approach: inline the name-preservation in each `pub fn difference` so it bypasses the shared-name helper.

Tests: DatetimeIndex.difference where self has name "a" and other has name "b" → result.name() == Some("a") (currently None).
close_reason: Fixed difference name preservation across DatetimeIndex/TimedeltaIndex/RangeIndex/CategoricalIndex to match pandas asymmetric self.name preservation. Commit 55847618. 368 fp-index + 1933 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [mock-code-finder] DataFrame::squeeze_to_series axis=0 hardcodes name '0' instead of using the row label
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:30677 — squeeze_to_series(axis=0) on a single-row DataFrame returns `Series::from_values("0".to_owned(), labels, values)`. The hardcoded "0" diverges from pandas, which uses the row's index label as the Series name:

```python
>>> df = pd.DataFrame({'a': [1], 'b': [2]}, index=['row1'])
>>> df.squeeze(axis=0).name
'row1'
```

Our code always names it "0".

Surgical fix: replace
  Series::from_values("0".to_owned(), labels, values)
with
  let series_name = self.index.labels()[0].to_string();
  Series::from_values(series_name, labels, values)

Tests: DataFrame with named row index → squeeze(axis=0) → result.name() == that row label. Existing axis=1 path (uses col_name) unaffected.
close_reason: Replaced hardcoded '0' with row label via self.index.labels()[0].to_string(). Commit 5ab46d16. 1916 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [perf] Typed Float64 fast path for DataFrame frame-vs-frame arithmetic (add/sub/mul/div_df) — Scalar-path ~8x slow
description: eydcr 4th-wave probe: add 59/sub 60/mul 61/div 67ms. binary_df_op already skips align for same-shape (b0cab) but still ran a per-element Scalar to_f64 loop + from_values. FIX: typed sub-branch — both columns contiguous Float64 -> apply op over f64+validity slices into from_f64_values_with_validity. CRUX: Scalar::to_f64 is Ok for Float64(NaN), Err only for Null, so 'Ok' = validity||is_nan (NOT is_missing); NaN op result -> Float64(NaN) missing-bit. Serves all 4 ops. ~8x bit-identical (golden f83f2582, incl 0/0=NaN). DONE.

CRUX: Ok=validity||is_nan (to_f64 semantics, not is_missing). fp-frame 2830 + conformance 0 failures.

title: [mock-code-finder] fp-groupby first/last promote Int64 → Float64 (pandas preserves Int64)
description:
fp-groupby/src/lib.rs:730-750 (or near; verify exact location) — AggFunc::First and AggFunc::Last:
```rust
AggFunc::First => match &vals[0] {
    Scalar::Int64(v) => Scalar::Float64(*v as f64),
    other => other.clone(),
}
```

Int64 scalar values are promoted to Float64. Pandas: groupby().first()/.last() preserve Int64 dtype on Int64 columns.

Sum/mean need Float64 promotion (for NaN compat), but first/last don't — they just select a row's value.

Surgical fix: drop the Int64-specific arm; clone the scalar as-is regardless of type. This will also fix the Timedelta64/Bool case if those have promotion arms.

Tests: groupby_first on Int64 series returns Int64 (not Float64); groupby_last same. Float64 column unchanged. Timedelta64 preserved.
close_reason: Removed Int64→Float64 promotion in AggFunc::First/Last; clone scalar as-is. Updated 2 regression tests. Commit aa3e464c. 66 fp-groupby + 1901 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [test] property test: semantic_cmp NAT degeneracy + ordering for Timedelta64/Datetime64
description: ay8o9 pinned Float64 NaN degeneracy in semantic_cmp; extend to the temporal sentinels. Deterministic test (no rand, no mocks): Scalar::Timedelta64(i64::MIN) and Datetime64(i64::MIN) are NAT and compare Equal to everything same-dtype (and to themselves) per semantic_cmp's NAT guard; non-NAT Timedelta64/Datetime64 order normally (a<b => Less, antisymmetric, reflexive). Documents why temporal ordering ops must treat NAT as missing. Test-only; fp-types.
close_reason: batch-verified-green: fp-types suite 242 passed/0 failed (1ews0 test corrected: cast-of-missing->Utf8 yields a string per pandas astype(str), not missing)

title: [mock-code-finder] DataFrame::rename_index_with loses index name (impacts add_prefix_axis(0)/add_suffix_axis(0)/rename_index)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:24978 — DataFrame::rename_index_with helper:
```rust
index: Index::new(new_labels),
```
Builds a fresh unnamed Index. Same root cause as br-a1sfw (Series add_prefix/add_suffix) but in DataFrame.

Reached by DataFrame::add_prefix_axis(0), DataFrame::add_suffix_axis(0), and DataFrame::rename_index (callers via rename_index_with).

Pandas preserves the index name through these label-transforming operations.

Surgical fix: change line 24978 to:
  index: Index::new(new_labels).rename_index(self.index.name()),

Tests: DataFrame with named index → add_prefix_axis(0) / rename_index_with → result.index().name() preserved.
close_reason: Applied rename_index in rename_index_with helper. Covers add_prefix_axis(0)/add_suffix_axis(0). Commit 906fd895. 1919 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [testing-metamorphic] No proptest for Series::shift inner-overlap-preservation invariant
description:
Skill: /testing-metamorphic.

fp-frame Series::shift (lib.rs:5110) inserts NaN at boundaries and
shifts the rest. Existing crate-level tests cover specific cases
(shift_basic, shift_negative, shift_zero, shift_with_nulls, etc.) but
no property test on random inputs.

Metamorphic invariant: for any Series s of length n and periods p
where -n < p < n:
  if p >= 0: shifted[i] == s[i - p] for i in [p, n)
  if p <  0: shifted[i] == s[i + |p|] for i in [0, n - |p|)
The rest of shifted is NaN.

Random inputs with mixed Int64/Float64/Bool/Null and varying length
will exercise corner cases the hand-written tests do not. A regression
that off-by-ones the boundary or scrambles the slice would surface.

Add an explicit Rust API for this option while preserving existing to_dict(orient) behavior and focused tests for accepted/rejected orientations.

title: Add DataFrame to_json index option parity

FrankenPandas currently always emits split/table index metadata and has no explicit index option. Add a Rust API preserving existing to_json(orient) behavior plus focused tests for accepted/rejected orientations.

comments.author: ubuntu

title: [perf] FxHash + partitioned-parallel + typed output for DataFrame.duplicated/drop_duplicates
description: gap_hunt 200k x8: duplicated(all cols) 30ms, drop_duplicates 33ms (biggest remaining). 3 levers: (1) seen map std HashMap(SipHash) keyed on the already-mixed row_digest u64 -> FxHashMap (SipHash was pure waste; digest already a hash); (2) Vec<Scalar::Bool> output -> typed from_bool_values; (3) partitioned PARALLEL dedup for keep=First: bucket rows by digest%P (equal rows share digest => same partition), each partition dedups ascending into a cache-resident local FxHashMap, parallelizing the dominant hash-insert cost. Bit-identical (rows_equal verifies collisions; first-seen preserved). duplicated 2.7x, drop_duplicates 2.1x. golden 1326d096 unchanged. NOTE: parallel-digest-precompute alone was NEUTRAL (digest cheap; the HashMap inserts were the wall) -> partitioning was the real lever.
notes: DONE (3f4a88bb): duplicated 30->11ms 2.7x, drop_duplicates 33->15.5ms 2.1x, bit-identical golden 1326d096. FxHashMap (over already-mixed digest) + from_bool_values + partitioned-parallel keep=First (bucket by digest%P -> per-partition cache-resident local FxHashMap). 78 unit + 54 conformance tests green. KEY LESSON: parallel-digest-precompute was NEUTRAL — the 200k sequential HashMap INSERTS were the wall, not the digest; PARTITIONING (parallelize the inserts) was the real lever. keep=Last/None still sequential (benched path is First); could partition them too if needed.
close_reason: done

title: [perf] replace Kendall Fenwick inversion count with cache-friendly merge-sort (target >=2x on corr matrix)
description: Follow-up to 322yd (380d560a). The u32 narrowing only gave 1.21x because the Kendall discordant-pair count uses a Fenwick (BIT) tree (count_ordered_rank_inversions) whose ~800KB ranks_seen array (n=200k, even at u32) exceeds L2 -> the ~2*log(n) random-access prefix-query/update probes per row are L3-LATENCY-bound, not width-bound. Lever: replace the Fenwick with the existing merge-sort inversion counter (count_f64_inversions has the pattern) operating on u32: per pair, gather perm[k]=y_rank_by_row[x_order[k]] (one random gather/elem), then count inversions via sequential merge passes (cache-friendly streaming, ~log(n) sequential touches/elem instead of ~2log(n) random). Reuse per-worker scratch buffers (perm + merge scratch, 2*n*u32) threaded into complete_kendall_no_tie_parallel_matrix workers to avoid per-pair alloc churn (the join levers showed alloc/page-fault dominance). Bit-identical: inversion count is a structure-independent integer; tau identical (golden df_kendall 031978ba must hold). Expect the sequential merge to clear 2x AND scale better with cores (less memory-bandwidth pressure). Add a count_u32_inversions mirroring count_f64_inversions.
close_reason: REJECTED (not shipped, reverted to stash). Implemented + golden-verified the cache-friendly merge-sort discordant count (count_u32_inversions, gather perm in x-order then sequential merge, per-worker scratch reuse) replacing the Fenwick. Bit-identical (golden df_kendall 031978ba held). BUT it was SLOWER: merge-sort ~745ms vs the u32 Fenwick 644ms at n=200k/32cols. WHY: the merge sort does log(n) FULL-array copy-backs (values.copy_from_slice at every recursion level) = n*log(n) sequential u32 writes + the random gather, which is MORE total data movement than the Fenwick's cheap random increments — sequential-but-more beats random-but-less only when the random structure misses cache HARDER than this does. The u32 Fenwick (380d560a) stands as the best version. LESSON: count the TOTAL bytes moved, not just access-pattern — log(n) full passes can lose to a cache-missing BIT. Kendall corr-matrix is at its safe-Rust practical floor (Fenwick); next ambition should target a DIFFERENT subsystem.

title: [parity-bug] rolling().kurt() returns 0.0 for constant windows but pandas returns -3.0; skew/kurt miss 1e-14 NaN guard
description: rolling().kurt() special-cased a constant (zero-variance) window to 0.0, but pandas returns excess kurtosis -3.0 (verified pd 2.2.3: [5,5,5,5].rolling(4).kurt()=-3.0). rolling().skew() correctly returns 0.0 for constant. Both also lacked pandas' 'biased variance <= 1e-14 -> NaN' guard. Underlying skew/kurt formulas are mathematically equivalent to pandas (proven). Two identical copies (Series + windowed rolling impl). Verified vs 8-case oracle battery.
close_reason: Fixed: rolling kurt constant-window now -3.0 (was 0.0) matching pandas; added biased-variance<=1e-14 -> NaN guard to skew AND kurt; constant skew stays 0.0. Both rolling impls (Series + windowed). Formulas already pandas-equivalent. 8-case oracle battery (rolling_skew_kurt_match_pandas) + 81 rolling tests green, clippy clean.

title: [perf] dense_int64_left_positions builds Vec<Option<usize>> via un-reserved push — 18% of left_join in realloc growth; pre-size via counted pass or chunked fill
description: perf record on left_join (875f5fb3 era, 100k card512 50% overlap): fp_join::dense_int64_left_positions 18.03% self+children, ~14% inside Vec::push (realloc growth chain visible in the call graph — no with_capacity for the fanout output). Output size is computable the same way the inner builder counts (bucket-length sum over left probe). Lever: count pass -> Vec::with_capacity (or reuse the inner builder's matched-plan + row-chunked parallel fill from 6bsw3, writing (Some(l),Some(r)/None) position pairs into pre-sized buffers). Also applies to dense_int64_right_positions and dense_int64_outer_positions (same push pattern, lines ~1107-1341). After lt5qx the left_join profile should be re-taken FIRST to confirm this is now the top cost. Gates: left/outer goldens 745e5a46/d774e320, fp-join suite, join proptests, alternating A/B under load, Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped df908786: fused dense-i64 left merge builder (matched plan + muis1 tapes + 6bsw3 chunked fill + 3ad4n RLE left lanes + one shared word-fill validity mask per nullable right lane) replaces the 627MB Vec<Option<usize>> intermediate + per-column reindex walks. left_join 100000 card512 50%-overlap: 17.65x+/-0.95 (1.485s->84.2ms), goldens bit-identical, fp-columnar 373/0 fp-join 102/0 fp-frame 2820/0 join proptests 12/0. Note: bead's realloc hypothesis was wrong (vecs were pre-sized) — the intermediate REPRESENTATION was the cost; profile-first caught it.

title: [bug] DataFrame.<reducer>(axis=1) ignores skipna for stored Float64(NaN)
description: reduce_rows (axis=1 sum/mean/min/max/std/var/median/skew/kurt/sem/prod) filtered cells via to_f64().ok() which only drops Null — a stored Float64(NaN) (validity-set NaN, e.g. from 0.0/0.0 or explicit np.nan) returns Ok(NaN) and poisoned the whole row to NaN. pandas df.<reducer>(axis=1) defaults skipna=True => drops all NaN: [1.0,NaN,2.0].sum(axis=1)==3.0 (FP gave NaN). FIX: gate on is_missing() (true for Null AND Float64(NaN)). Also added all-NaN-row identity via empty param: sum->0, prod->1 (pandas min_count=0), all others->NaN. Found by Phase B inspection (no live oracle); verified vs known pandas semantics. Regression test df_axis1_reductions_skip_nan_skipna_default. 76 axis1 unit + 20 conformance green.
notes: FIXED (0b76c334): reduce_rows now filters on is_missing() (skips Float64(NaN) not just Null) + empty-row identity param (sum 0/prod 1/rest NaN). 76 axis1 unit + 20 conformance green. Regression test added.
close_reason: done

title: Add Datetime/Timedelta/Period/Range Index append/delete forwarders
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): the typed Index variants still miss append/delete. Mirrors the take/repeat/isin pattern from br-bbgg3.

Add two methods on each of DatetimeIndex / TimedeltaIndex / PeriodIndex / RangeIndex:
- append(other) -> Self — concatenates the underlying values; index name is preserved if both sides match (else dropped, matching pandas).
- delete(loc) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — removes the value at position loc; OOB raises IndexError::OutOfBounds.

RangeIndex.delete returns Index because the residual values may not form a contiguous range.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: append concatenation + name reconciliation, delete removing the middle / first / last position, and OOB rejection.
close_reason: Shipped in 7cc4384: Datetime/Timedelta/Period/Range Index now expose pandas-parity append/delete with name reconciliation and OOB rejection. 4 regression tests pass; 285 fp-index tests total.

title: [perf] dense Int64 bucket left merge positions
description:
Profile-backed target after ddc7b754: RCH release-perf join-bench non-unique hash joins rows=50000/right_rows=50000/key_cardinality=10000 measured left mean/p50/p95/p99 35.837/34.870/43.780/43.804 ms, checksum 49997155700.000 (artifact tests/artifacts/perf/fp_before_hash_join_profile_blackthrush_pass3.txt). The generic single-key left path still builds an FxHashMap of right CompositeJoinKey buckets, then probes every left row.

Alien primitive: dense-domain counting bucket / direct-address join probe for all-valid Int64 keys. One lever only: when single-key Int64 left merge has all-valid keys, no sort, no indicator, no validate, and the right-key min..max span is bounded relative to row count, bucket right positions by key and emit output in original left-row order with right insertion order per equal key, avoiding key materialization and hashing. Fallback for nulls, non-Int64, sparse huge domains, multi-key, sort/indicator/validate, and all ambiguous semantics.

Proof obligations: RCH baseline+after, golden sha256 unchanged for deterministic non-unique left_join fixture, preserve left-row order, equal-key right order, unmatched-right missing values/dtypes, shared key values, suffix behavior, RangeIndex, no FP/RNG/tie/hash-order observable behavior; RCH fp-join focused test/check/clippy, fmt, UBS. Retain only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Completed: dense all-valid Int64 left merge counted-bucket path. Profile baseline tests/artifacts/perf/fp_before_hash_join_profile_blackthrush_pass3.txt left mean/p50/p95/p99 35.837/34.870/43.780/43.804 ms, checksum 49997155700.000. After tests/artifacts/perf/fp_rch_left_join_89t1y.txt fast mean/p50/p95/p99 29.738/29.824/31.681/37.147 ms, checksum unchanged; forced-generic 40.025/38.630/46.986/48.838 ms. Hyperfine fast 48.1 ms vs forced-generic 57.3 ms. Golden SHA unchanged 203c8fbb6659841e12bbe46ed678df5dcae04404303fe38183148202769e359b. Score=2.4 (Impact 1.20 x Confidence 4 / Effort 2). RCH focused test/check/clippy, fmt, sha, UBS passed.

title: [perf] exact cached Index equality for groupby fast-path guard
description:
Profile-backed target: Round4/Round5 artifacts rank repeated full index equality in the groupby fast-path guard as a residual hotspot after identity alignment and dense groupby paths. The current guard at fp-groupby lines 169/1039 still compares full label vectors before using the duplicate cache. Kernel perf/samply are blocked locally by perf_event_paranoid=4, so this bead uses the repo's existing Round4/Round5 flamegraph-derived profile evidence plus fresh RCH-built hyperfine baselines.

Alien primitive: exact bounded memoization of immutable label-vector equality, not hash-only semantic fingerprints. One lever only: assign each Index label vector a runtime-only immutable label identity, preserve IDs across clone, allocate new IDs for new label vectors/deserialization, and cache exact equality results for identity pairs in a bounded safe-Rust global cache. Fallback/full compare remains the source of truth on cache miss.

Proof obligations: baseline current RCH-built groupby-bench before edit; after RCH build/hyperfine; golden sha256 unchanged; preserve label-only PartialEq semantics, name-insensitive equals, name-sensitive identical, serde output unaffected, duplicate cache unaffected, ordering/tie-breaking/FP/RNG/hash-order unchanged; validate fp-index equality/cache tests and fp-groupby focused tests/check/clippy/fmt/UBS; keep only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Completed retained exact Index label equality cache. Implementation landed in d1f44e63 and evidence bundle in f56c92dc. Baseline/A-B: groupby-bench rows=500000 key_cardinality=512 iters=200 before 1.123s +/-0.050 -> after 0.804s +/-0.123 (1.40x mean, Score 3.0). Golden before/after sha256 byte-identical 6c6e9286d3a46cff54ac1b621abd4697dd535305597ab8fd9f1d1cf840896ec4. Isomorphism: PartialEq remains label-only and name-insensitive; identical() name-sensitive behavior unchanged; clone preserves runtime label identity; deserialization/new labels allocate fresh identity; cache miss falls back to structural Vec<IndexLabel> equality before bounded memo insert; ordering/tie-breaking/FP/RNG/hash-order output unaffected. Current validation: RCH fp-index identity test, RCH fp-groupby tests, RCH check -p fp-index -p fp-groupby --all-targets, RCH clippy -p fp-index -p fp-groupby --all-targets --no-deps -D warnings, UBS scoped scan. Current clean HEAD after timing artifact: 1.023s +/-0.052; current golden digest captured after benchmark output-format change.

title: Column::interpolate_linear leaves trailing NaN as NaN; pandas (limit_direction='forward' default) forward-fills trailing with last valid value
description: fp-columnar Column::interpolate_linear/interpolate (lib ~5126) only fills interior gaps; leading AND trailing nulls stay null. But pandas Series.interpolate(method='linear') with default limit_direction='forward' FORWARD-FILLS trailing nulls with the last valid value: [2,4,nan,nan]->[2,4,4,4], [5,nan,nan]->[5,5,5], [nan,1,nan,nan,4,nan]->[nan,1,2,3,4,4]. Leading nulls DO stay null (correct). The kernel's doc comment wrongly claims trailing-stays-null matches limit_direction='forward'. fp-frame Series::interpolate (lib 5148-5156) ALREADY implements the correct trailing ffill, so the Column kernel diverges from both pandas and FP's own Series API. Public API + only self-tested (no non-test callers; Series.interpolate has its own impl). Sister to jyhf7/qcutc public-kernel footguns. FIX: after interior loop, forward-fill positions after last valid index with floats[last]; update codifying test interpolate_leaves_leading_and_trailing_nulls_null + doc. Verified vs system pandas 2.2.3.
close_reason: Fixed in b9634583: Column::interpolate_linear now forward-fills trailing NaN with the last valid value (pandas default limit_direction='forward'), matching system pandas 2.2.3 and FP's own Series::interpolate. Leading nulls correctly stay null; no extrapolation. Doc corrected, codifying test replaced + no-extrapolation test added. fp-columnar 359/0, clippy clean.

title: [perf] dense-gid typed groupby.transform (mean/count/min/max) — skip build_groups hash + Scalar broadcast
description: DataFrameGroupBy::transform builds the generic SipHash groups map, clones each group's values into Vec<Scalar>, folds via fp_types::nan*, and broadcasts via Vec<Scalar>+Column::from_values. groupby().transform('mean') (normalization) is extremely common. Add try_transform_dense: all-valid bounded-Int64 key cols => direct-address gid_per_row (no build_groups, no go_gid needed since we broadcast by gid not group_order), typed f64/i64 accumulators per gid (mirror aggregate_named_func dense: mean=sum/count, count=group size, min/max=seed-first strict, dtype-preserving), broadcast out[row]=agg[gid[row]] via from_f64_values/from_i64_values. Scope mean/count/min/max (sum has i64-overflow per-group dtype edge; var/std/median/first/last/prod -> general path). Bit-identical: dense accumulators == nanmean/nancount/nanmin/nanmax in row order (proven in str-groupby dense); all-valid keys => every row grouped (no stray nulls); typed output == Scalar broadcast observable. Golden transform sha256; Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped dec12e18: dense-gid typed groupby.transform (mean/count/min/max) — direct-address gid per row + typed f64/i64 accumulators + from_f64/i64_values broadcast, skipping build_groups hash + per-group Vec<Scalar> + Scalar output. Bit-identical (golden groupby_transform_mean 8815c71a before==after; 193 transform/groupby tests green). n=500k Int64 key card 100, 4 Float64 cols: 117->14 ms = 8.4x. Clean algorithm replacement (single dominant cost = the whole hash+Scalar pipeline). Follow-up: extend to sum (i64-overflow per-group dtype) + var/std/median/first/last/prod; and string-key transform (byte-span gid).

title: Use pandas canonical intraday PeriodFreq aliases
description: Parity gap from pandas Period/PeriodIndex freqstr behavior: pandas 2.2 reports canonical intraday period aliases as h, min, and s while accepting deprecated H/T/S inputs. title: DataFrame(dtype='int64') constructor truncates lossy floats; pandas ERRORS (distinct from .astype which truncates)
description: VERIFIED vs pandas 2.2.3: pd.DataFrame({'x':[1.5]}, dtype='int64') raises ValueError 'Trying to coerce float values to integers' — the DataFrame constructor's dtype coercion is STRICT and rejects non-integer floats. This DIFFERS from pd.Series([1.5]).astype('int64') which TRUNCATES to 1 (qcutc/254013dd). FP made BOTH truncate (the qcutc cast_scalar_owned truncation is reused by the DataFrame-constructor-with-dtype path), so FP wrongly SUCCEEDS where the constructor should error. Conformance FP-P2D-023 dataframe_constructor_list_like_dtype_int64_lossy_float_error_hardened expects the error (fixture CORRECT, FP wrong). FIX: the DataFrame/Series constructor dtype-coercion path must reject finite-non-integer float->int (ValueError-equiv), while .astype keeps truncating — i.e. thread a 'strict-construct vs astype' flag through the cast, OR add a pre-check in the constructor coercion. NOTE the SISTER stale fixtures in the same packet are the OPPOSITE: dtype=bool from int 2 / from_dict bool-invalid expect FP to ERROR but pandas SUCCEEDS (True, since astype bool nonzero e426cd40) -> those fixtures are STALE and must be regenerated to success frames. So FP-P2D-023 has BOTH a real FP bug (int64-lossy constructor) AND stale bool fixtures. Part of exyng.
close_reason: Fixed in 14cf5229: Column::new (the explicit-dtype constructor coercion) now raises LossyFloatToInt on a finite non-integer float->Int64, matching pandas DataFrame(dtype='int64') strictness, while astype keeps truncating (it pre-truncates via cast_scalar so Column::new sees Int64 values). fp-columnar 361/0, fp-frame 2800/0, fp-io 568/0. FP-P2D-023 lossy-float constructor case green. Regression test added.

title: [perf] String groupby sorts all N rows (O(n log n)); hash-group + sort K distinct keys to close vs-pandas low-cardinality 3-4x gap
description: VS-PANDAS GAP (BlackThrush 2026-06-12 sweep): DataFrame.groupby(str_key).{sum,mean,count,min,max,...} is FLAT ~13ms at 100k rows REGARDLESS of group count, while pandas scales with cardinality. At LOW cardinality (64 groups, the COMMON case) FP 13ms vs pandas ~4ms = 3-4x SLOWER. At all-unique (100k groups) FP 13ms beats pandas 82ms (pandas per-group overhead). perf record: aggregate_str_dense @fp-frame:50784 sort=True branch MSD-radix-sorts ALL n key spans (utf8_msd_argsort_bytes over n spans -> memcmp 19% + utf8_msd_sort 24% + memmove 25%). LEVER: hash-group all rows (FxHashMap<&[u8],gid>, O(n), already the sort=False path) to get gid_per_row + K distinct reps, THEN MSD-argsort only the K DISTINCT group keys and remap gids to ascending order. O(n) + O(K log K) replaces O(n log n) full-row sort. Cheap prefix-sample cardinality probe keeps near-all-distinct keys on the existing direct-sort path (no regression on the pathological all-unique benchmark). ISOMORPHISM: both yield groups by exact key span in ascending key order; per-gid accumulators order-independent; first/last iterate original row order in both; output key bytes identical (all rows in a group share key bytes). Golden: str_groupby_count/mean (64-group _repeated builder = low-card path) + str_groupby_sum (4096/all-unique = direct-sort path) sha256 unchanged. Add low-card timed scenario for Score>=2.0. One lever fixes the whole str-groupby reducer family.
close_reason: Shipped: hash-group + sort-distinct for low-cardinality string groupby. Score 3.70x (count) / 4.29x (sum), goldens byte-identical, no high-card regression. FP now ~3.6x ahead of pandas at low card.

title: [deadlock-finder] capture_live_oracle_expected can deadlock on >64KB payload due to synchronous stdin write before wait_with_output
description:
Skill: /deadlock-finder-and-fixer.

Pattern in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:12603-12609 (capture_live_oracle_expected):
  .spawn()
  .and_then(|mut child| {
      if let Some(mut stdin) = child.stdin.take() {
          stdin.write_all(&input)?;     // <-- synchronous, blocks if pipe buffer fills
      }
      child.wait_with_output()           // <-- only here are stdout/stderr drained
  })?

Classic subprocess pipe deadlock:
1. input is JSON-serialized fixture payload — for wide DataFrames or
   long fixtures, can exceed the OS pipe buffer (~64 KB on Linux).
2. write_all blocks once the stdin pipe buffer fills, waiting for child
   to read more.
3. If the child has already started emitting output (stdout/stderr) and
   fills its own pipe buffer (also ~64 KB), the child blocks on write
   until parent drains it.
4. Parent has not started draining yet (still in step 2). Both sides
   are blocked on write -> deadlock.

Practical risk: capture_live_oracle_expected is the production path
called from every live_oracle_* test. Currently most fixtures are
small, but large dataframes (e.g., scale fixtures, get_dummies on
wide tables) will hit the deadlock.

Fix: spawn a thread to drain stdin (write input) concurrently with
the wait_with_output call. wait_with_output already uses internal
threads to drain stdout and stderr concurrently. Pattern is the
standard idiom for unknown-size piped IO.

title: [perf] Typed Int64 fast path for DataFrame frame-vs-frame arithmetic (add/sub/mul/div) — ~20x slow
description: Int64 vein. binary_df_op typed path (73cu1) was Float64-only; all-valid Int64 columns fell to the Scalar to_f64 loop (i64.add_df 47.7ms). Int64 arithmetic -> Float64 output (general: Float64(op(to_f64,to_f64))). FIX: as_i64_slice branch applying op(l as f64, r as f64) into from_f64_values_with_validity; NaN result (0/0) -> Null(NaN). Serves add/sub/mul/div. 47.7->2.38ms=20x bit-identical (golden 7022267d). DONE. 5th Int64 win.

title: [porting-to-rust] Series::cov missing min_periods + ddof parameters
description:
Skill: /porting-to-rust.

pandas:
  pd.Series([1,2,3,4]).cov(other, min_periods=None, ddof=1)
  - ddof=0: population covariance (divisor n)
  - ddof=1: sample covariance (divisor n-1, default)
  - ddof=2: divisor n-2 (rarer)
  - min_periods: minimum non-null pair count required; otherwise NaN

fp-frame Series::cov(other) (lib.rs:8238) -> Series::cov_with(other) ->
cov_components hardcodes (n - 1.0) at lib.rs:8276 and has no min_periods
threshold (only the implicit count < 2 -> NaN check).

DataFrame::corr already exposes min_periods (corr_min_periods at line 23601);
the Series-level cov surface is missing the equivalent.

Port: add Series::cov_with_options(other, min_periods, ddof) -> Result<f64>.
Validate ddof < count to avoid divide-by-zero. Apply min_periods threshold.
Series::cov_with(other) becomes a thin wrapper passing default ddof=1, no
min_periods. Existing cov_components keeps its computation; add a thin
ddof-aware wrapper for the divisor.

title: Add Series.mad method for mean absolute deviation
[perf][ARCH] Deferred/lazy Float64 validity to make elementwise arithmetic single-pass (close ~2.5x series-add + many vs-pandas elementwise gaps)
description: ROOT CAUSE (from br-frankenpandas-d3mfh vs-pandas reality check): FP's EAGER per-op validity mask forces an extra NaN-scan pass that numpy never pays, so identical-index Float64 Series add is ~2.5x slower than pandas (FP 0.236ms vs pandas 0.096ms at 100k) even though the add itself autovectorizes. aligned_binary_f64* does add (1 pass) + from_f64_values NaN-scan (1 pass) + (for the gated fast path) 2 input NaN pre-scans. numpy does 1 pass (NaN is the native missing marker; no separate validity bitmap). LEVER (architectural, NOT a micro-tweak): give LazyAllValidFloat64 a THIRD validity state - 'validity not yet computed' - so a fully-valid f64 arithmetic result returns immediately after the single add pass, deferring the NaN->validity scan until isna()/dropna()/any-validity-consumer actually runs (memoized via OnceLock, like sort_order/duplicate_cache on Index). Most arithmetic results are consumed by more arithmetic or reductions that re-derive NaN anyway, so the scan is often never needed. ISOMORPHISM: validity must be IDENTICAL whenever observed; only its computation is deferred. Must preserve the Scalar::Null(NaN) vs Float64(NaN) materialization contract (input-NaN -> Null(NaN), generated inf-inf NaN -> Float64(NaN)) that golden_dump distinguishes - so the deferred path must reproduce BOTH variants exactly (the hard part). Golden: series_add/series_add_same 1000/100000 + the full conformance arithmetic suite byte-identical. Target: series-add ~0.10-0.12ms (Score>=2.0) and a broad uplift across mul/sub/div/elementwise. Alien-graveyard angle: lazy/deferred metadata + succinct validity. This is the deeper swing the no-ceiling addendum calls for after the d3mfh micro-lever rejection.
close_reason: Kept: fused single-pass NaN-tracked kernel for aligned_binary_f64_same_positions. apply_f64_slices_nan_tracked computes data + input_nan/output_nan in one sweep, collapsing the 4 passes (2 input pre-scans + add + from_f64_values rescan) to 1. Branch outcomes byte-identical to old fast path (input_nan==old two-scan disjunction; output_nan splits into no-rescan all-valid stamp vs identical rescan); input-NaN falls to unchanged general path (Null(NaN)/Pow NaN**0 preserved). Golden 7 ops n=100000 byte-identical (add ce1f2070..). Bench op-only min-of-5: add 0.159->0.050ms 3.2x, mul 3.1x, div 2.8x; hyperfine paired 2.50x/reversed 2.42x. Gates: fp-columnar lib 404/0 (same_positions==general NaN/inf lock green), fp-frame arith 59/0, clippy -D clean, fmt clean, no unsafe. Commit 37ecfcec. Witness tests/artifacts/perf/9houf_fused_nan_tracked_same_index_add_witness.md

title: [perf] join_left/outer ~1.15x slower than pandas at 1M (modest, fp-join)
description: PROFILE-BACKED (1M float64, release-perf, 2026-06-15, newly-added head-to-head joins bench 6edb6e6c): unique-key Int64 merge (left 0..n, right 0,2,..,2n). join_inner fp WINS (0.49x@1M, 0.11x@100k = 9x faster). join_left 1.15x@1M (1.38x@100k), join_outer 1.16x@1M (2.58x@100k — the 100k inflation is fixed per-op overhead per fa3um, real gap is the ~1.15x at scale). Likely root cause: the null-introducing side gather (left-unmatched/right-unmatched rows produce nulls) + output frame assembly. Candidate lever: ensure the join output row-gather uses the typed-nullable take_positions path (5e080b5b) rather than a Scalar gather for the matched/null sides. Modest (sub-2x) + fp-join is peer-active, so lower priority than a clean >=2x target. NOTE pandas merge how=outer/left default sort=False; output ORDER may differ from fp (timing comparison is fair regardless; a parity check is separate).
close_reason: Completed: ordered-unique outer Int64 shared key coalesces via typed Column::from_i64_values; outer 1M hyperfine improved 368.2 ms to 320.8 ms and reverse 382.4 ms to 322.1 ms; golden sha256 unchanged 3da878cd8a0b40903116f5bc9d7e8687cf47af3d31d749f384d224582f9d9bce.

title: [security-audit-for-saas] fp-io JSON/JSONL readers Vec::with_capacity on unbounded input length (DoS via hostile file)
description:
fp-io has multiple read paths that allocate Vec::with_capacity(N) where N comes from untrusted input length:

1. read_json_str records orient (fp-io/src/lib.rs:3493): `Vec::with_capacity(arr.len())` per column. A 1B-element JSON array on a 10-column frame allocates ~80GB.

2. read_jsonl_str (fp-io/src/lib.rs:4126): accumulates `all_rows.push(obj)` line-by-line with no size cap. Hostile JSONL file with millions of lines exhausts memory.

3. read_pickle_bytes (fp-io/src/lib.rs:~1187): serde_pickle deserialization has no recursion depth cap — deeply nested pickle structures DoS via stack exhaustion or O(n²) parsing.

Attack model: parity-library users typically provide their own files, but defense-in-depth dictates a bound. The same code path is reachable from web frontends or shared-tenant pipelines.

Surgical fix: define a module-level MAX_INPUT_ROWS = 100_000_000 (or configurable via env). At the top of each read path, check `arr.len() <= MAX_INPUT_ROWS` and return IoError::JsonFormat / IoError::Pickle with a descriptive message. Same cap applies to JSONL row accumulator.

Tests: feed a hand-crafted JSON `[...100M entries...]` (mocked) and verify Err. Existing well-formed files stay green.

Lower priority because parity-lib usage rarely exposes untrusted input, but the hardening is cheap.
close_reason: Added READ_JSONL_MAX_ROWS=100M cap with typed JsonFormat error to read_jsonl_str. Commit f1cc613d.

[perf] Kendall rank-domain inversion kernel
description: Profile-backed after br-frankenpandas-2x7id rejection: reprofile shows df_kendall 512x5 at 72.5 ms +/- 0.9, with the benchmark harness explicitly exercising Kendall's O(M^2) per-pair path. CSV remains larger but repeated CSV micro-levers rejected Score<2; this is a different algorithmic primitive. One lever only: precompute no-tie finite column row-to-rank maps and count Kendall discordances over rank-domain usize sequences instead of rebuilding/comparing f64 y_order per pair. Preserve the existing finite/no-tie gate, fallback for ties/missing/non-finite values, output symmetry, ordering, floating-point formula, RNG/tie behavior, and golden SHA. Baseline/golden required before edit; keep only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped Kendall rank-domain inversion kernel. Baseline df_kendall 512x5 71.8 ms +/- 3.9; paired confirmation 70.0 ms +/- 4.3 before vs 62.2 ms +/- 3.6 after, 1.12x +/- 0.10. Golden SHA unchanged dcce8a4e1f13d887361650ec24fcfee13c6c4c3954936fd124a1feb53cf125ce. Isomorphism: same finite/no-tie gate, same sorted row order, same discordant count formula, same fallback for ties/missing/non-finite, no RNG/tie changes. Score=3.0 (Impact 2.0 x Confidence 3.0 / Effort 2.0).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
pandas read_csv infers ONE dtype PER COLUMN: a column [1,2,abc] -> object ['1','2','abc'] (the 1,2 become STRINGS). FP's parse_scalar is PER-CELL -> [Int64(1),Int64(2),Utf8('abc')], then Column::from_values (fp-columnar, cod-reserved) coerces. NEED TO VERIFY FP's actual output: if Column::from_values coerces Int64+Utf8 -> Utf8 ['1','2','abc'] it MATCHES pandas; if it errors/mixes/forces-numeric it diverges. Couldn't run FP this session. 1
2
abc') column values + dtype. If diverges, fix is per-column inference in fp-io read_csv (collect column dtype before building, like pandas) — fp-io editable even though Column::from_values isn't.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: VERIFIED. FP read_csv column-level dtype inference for the [1,2,abc] case is CORRECT: infer_dtype (fp-types) returns Utf8 when string+non-string mix, and the rendered round-trip is ['1','2','abc'] — matches live pandas 2.2.3 object column exactly. title: [bug?] Series.drop_duplicates keys nulls by NullKind (Null vs NaN distinct) but unique() collapses all missing — verify vs live pandas
description: MR-DD1 (br-frankenpandas-1bgc1) found: scalar_key_allow_missing (fp-frame:642) keys Scalar::Null(kind) by its NullKind, so a column holding both Null(Null) and Null(NaN) yields drop_duplicates len 2 while Series::unique() (fp-frame:5514, single missing_seen flag) collapses to 1. Per epic hazard #1, an Int64/Float64 column CAN hold a preserved Null(NaN) alongside canonical Null(Null). pandas always collapses all missing to one for both unique() and drop_duplicates(). NEEDS live-pandas verification (legacy_pandas absent) before any fix; may be a fixture-only state. Same NullKind-distinct keying also affects DataFrame.duplicated/drop_duplicates and DataFrame-level value_counts paths that route through scalar_key_allow_missing.
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Reachability analysis (no live pandas): a Float64 column CANNOT hold mixed null-kinds via Column::new — normalize_missing_for_dtype maps Null(Null) to the dtype canonical missing which IS Null(NaN) for Float64, and preserves Null(NaN); both collapse to NaN. Only an Int64 column can hold both Null(Null) [canonical] and a preserved Null(NaN). But pandas nullable Int64 uses pd.NA only — you cannot place NaN in an Int64 column in real pandas, so the divergent input is FIXTURE-ONLY unless some FP op (reindex/where/join) inserts Null(NaN) into an Int64 column instead of upcasting Int64 to Float64 like pandas. ACTION before any fix to scalar_key_allow_missing (45 call sites incl groupby/merge/pivot/value_counts): verify vs live pandas whether reindex/where on Int64 upcasts to float64. The collapse fix is a no-op for all single-null-kind columns.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: RESOLVED vs live pandas 2.2.3 (python3 importable; /dp legacy path absent but system pandas works). Finding REVERSES the original framing: pandas OBJECT columns keep np.nan / None / pd.NA as DISTINCT values for BOTH unique() and drop_duplicates() — e.g. pd.Series([np.nan,None,1.0,np.nan,pd.NA],dtype=object).unique() -> [nan, None, 1.0, <NA>] and .drop_duplicates() -> same (nunique(dropna=False)=4). Numeric float64 collapses all missing to one NaN (only one kind exists). So the REAL bug is in Series::unique() (fp-frame:~5514), which collapses ALL missing to a single entry via its  bool — it should preserve distinct NullKinds like drop_duplicates already does (scalar_key_allow_missing keys by NullKind, which is pandas-CORRECT for object columns). Fix direction: make unique() use the same NullKind-aware keying as drop_duplicates so the two agree and both match pandas. NOTE: fix lands in fp-frame which is currently RESERVED by RubyGoose — coordinate. MR-DD1 in proptest_properties already gates the cross-check to no-null series to stay green until this is fixed.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
VERIFIED vs live pandas 2.2.3 (system python) — REAL BUG, not a fixture defect.
Ground truth: drop_duplicates and unique are ALWAYS CONSISTENT in pandas; None-vs-NaN distinctness is a function of DTYPE:
- object [None,nan,None,nan]: drop_duplicates->[None,nan]; unique->[None,nan]  (both keep None & NaN distinct)
- object [1,None,nan,1,None]: both -> [1,None,nan]
- float  [None,nan,None]: both -> [nan]  (None coerced to NaN, all collapse)
- Int64  [1,NA,1,NA]: both -> [1,<NA>]
FrankenPandas's drop_duplicates (keeps NullKind distinct) and unique (collapses all missing) DISAGREE — pandas never does.
FIX SPEC (fp-frame, reserved by RubyGoose): make Series.unique() share the SAME Scalar->Key null-keying as drop_duplicates so they agree (object/NullKind-preserving -> keep Null vs NaN distinct in unique() too; float already collapses in both). Verified by cc-pandas; fix blocked on fp-frame reservation.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: FIXED + CLOSED in 97c437ab. Made Series.unique() and nunique_with_dropna(false) share drop_duplicates' scalar_key_allow_missing NullKind keying so all three agree and match live pandas 2.2.3 (object keeps None/NaN distinct, float/Int64 collapse single kind). Updated the test that codified the buggy len-1 collapse. fp-frame lib 2768/0, conformance 419/0, clippy code-clean.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::add_prefix/add_suffix lose index name (pandas preserves it)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:5175 (add_prefix) and :5193 (add_suffix) — both build Index::new(new_labels) without applying rename_index(self.index.name()).

Pandas:
  >>> pd.Series([1,2], index=pd.Index(['a','b'], name='myidx')).add_prefix('p_').index.name
  'myidx'

Our code returns an unnamed index.

Surgical fix: replace `Index::new(new_labels)` with `Index::new(new_labels).rename_index(self.index.name())` at both call sites.

Tests: Series with named index → add_prefix → result.index().name() preserved. Same for add_suffix.
close_reason: Applied rename_index to both add_prefix and add_suffix. Commit 56bb41e5. 1918 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [perf] Contiguous UTF-8 factorize without scalar materialization

title: Add RangeIndex isna/notna/hasnans/dropna/fillna/format/factorize closed-form methods
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): IndexVariants RangeIndex is missing the standard missing-data accessors and format/factorize entry points. RangeIndex is int64-typed and uniqueness-by-construction, so each method is a closed-form expression with no per-position scanning.

Add seven inherent methods on RangeIndex:
- isna() -> Vec<bool> — vec![false; len]
- isnull() -> Vec<bool> — alias for isna
- notna() -> Vec<bool> — vec![true; len]
- notnull() -> Vec<bool> — alias for notna
- hasnans() -> bool — false
- dropna() -> Self — self.clone()
- fillna(_value: i64) -> Self — self.clone()
- format() -> Vec<String> — values stringified
- factorize() -> (Vec<usize>, Self) — identity codes [0..len], uniques == self

Mirrors the existing closed-form pattern from br-frankenpandas-mrchb (argmax/argmin/argsort/duplicated/drop_duplicates). Add regression tests covering empty range, ascending range, and descending range. Single PR.
close_reason: Shipped in 005432f: RangeIndex now exposes pandas-parity isna/isnull/notna/notnull/hasnans/dropna/fillna/format/factorize as closed-form constants and identity factorization. 3 regression tests pass; 255 fp-index tests total.

title: [perf] Index::nunique materializes labels for affine Int64 (= len, O(1))
description: Index::nunique -> nunique_with_dropna calls self.unique().labels.iter().filter(!missing).count() (fp-index/src/lib.rs ~2560/2568). For an affine Int64 backing (int64_affine_range: RangeIndex/unit/affine), unique() is an O(1) clone but the final iter().count() Derefs to as_slice() and materializes Vec<IndexLabel>. Affine is strictly monotonic => all len values are distinct, and Int64 backings carry no missing labels => nunique == affine.len regardless of dropna. FIX: return affine.len early in nunique_with_dropna when int64_affine_range().is_some(). O(1), bit-identical. Same vein as k9tlb/ikbh9/gzi1i. Add test asserting nunique == len + materialized.get().is_none() for affine, and equivalence vs object path.
close_reason: Added affine-Int64 fast path to Index::nunique_with_dropna (avoids unique().labels.iter().count() materializing Vec<IndexLabel>). Affine Int64 (RangeIndex/unit/affine) is strictly monotonic => all len distinct, no missing => nunique==affine.len for either dropna. O(1), bit-identical. Test int64_nunique_avoid_label_materialization_a55d8 covers asc/desc affine + object-path equivalence + materialized.get().is_none(). Verified: targeted test pass, full fp-index 414 passed/0 failed, ubs 0 critical. Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/a55d8_index_nunique_lazy_int64.txt

title: [porting-to-rust] Series::quantile missing interpolation parameter (lower/higher/nearest/midpoint)
description:
Skill: /porting-to-rust.

pandas:
  pd.Series([1,2,3,4]).quantile(0.5, interpolation=...)
  - linear   -> 2.5  (default; weighted mean of neighbors)
  - lower    -> 2    (lower neighbor)
  - higher   -> 3    (upper neighbor)
  - nearest  -> 2    (closest neighbor; ties go to lower)
  - midpoint -> 2.5  (midpoint of neighbors)

fp-frame Series::quantile(q) (lib.rs:4274) only implements linear via
percentile_linear_series helper — no interpolation parameter at all.
Same pandas API gap exists for the broader DataFrame::quantile and
GroupBy::quantile but the Series surface is the foundational one.

Port: add Series::quantile_with_interpolation(q, interpolation) returning
Result<Scalar, FrameError>. Validate interpolation up front. Reuse the
existing sort + neighbor-position logic; switch the interpolation kernel.

title: [reality-check] Series::last_offset uses .unwrap() on index.labels().last() when self.is_empty() is false (panic if mismatched len/labels)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:8375 — `let last_label = self.index.labels().last().unwrap();`

The docstring claims this only panics on "internal index corruption." But the guard above (`if self.is_empty() { return Ok(self.clone()) }`) checks self.len() == 0 (column-length), not self.index.labels().is_empty(). If a Series has len() > 0 but its index has zero labels (which can happen during construction from misaligned values, or via an internal bug), this unwrap panics.

Fix: replace `.last().unwrap()` with `.last().ok_or_else(|| FrameError::CompatibilityRejected("Series reports non-empty but index has no last label".to_owned()))?`.

Same pattern likely exists for DataFrame::last_offset at line 33365.

Tests: construct a Series with mismatched len/labels and verify last_offset returns a CompatibilityRejected error instead of panicking. Existing well-formed test stays green.
close_reason: Replaced .unwrap() with ok_or_else(CompatibilityRejected) on both Series and DataFrame last_offset. Updated panic-contract conformance test. Commit b4053ff8. 1901 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [bug] CategoricalIndex.sort_values/argsort sorted lexicographically instead of by category code order
description: CategoricalIndex::argsort delegated to to_index().argsort() which sorts the utf8 labels LEXICOGRAPHICALLY, ignoring the category order. pandas sorts a Categorical by its category CODES (position within categories) for both ordered and unordered categoricals (Categorical._values_for_argsort returns self.codes). So categories=[b,a,c] with labels=[a,c,b,a] must sort to [b,a,a,c] (codes 1,2,0,1 -> stable argsort [2,0,3,1]), not lexicographic [a,a,b,c]. The existing test 482qd used lexicographic categories so it could not catch the divergence (test codified-masked the bug). Fix: argsort sorts positions by category code via category_index_map() (stable, ties keep original order). Lexicographic category orders produce identical output; only non-lexicographic orders corrected. Added regression test with non-lex categories (ordered + unordered). fp-index 375 / fp-frame 2818 green.
close_reason: done

title: SeriesGroupByResample::get_group loses source axis name

title: Series arithmetic ops drop index name when both operands share it

title: [perf] CSV cold parser profile isolation and typed row-blocked primitive
description:
Profile-backed follow-up after br-frankenpandas-i0w4c. The retained ryu fixture-emission lever improved cold CSV-panel timings, but the post-keep one-iteration perf_profile still includes setup: ryu::pretty::format64 30.53% and fp_io::parse_f64_csv_number 13.17% for csv_read 1000000x1. The normal criterion io/csv_read benchmark builds the CSV outside the timed loop, while high-iteration perf_profile stresses cache-hit verification.

First pass must establish a fixture-free/cold-parser target via RCH: either add a profiling-only scenario that builds the CSV before timing and bypasses cache exactly, or use an existing command shape that proves parser work is sampled without setup/cache dominance. Do not optimize parser code until the profile top frames are parser-owned.

Candidate alien primitive if profile confirms it: typed row-blocked CSV parser/transposition that parses row blocks into per-column typed buffers with exact fast_float2/std fallback semantics, preserving row order, column order/names, dtype promotion, null/NaN behavior, bad-line errors, float bits, RNG/tie/hash behavior. One lever per commit, golden sha256 before/after, paired hyperfine/criterion via rch, keep only Score>=2.0. Avoid RubyGoose-owned typed-columnar files unless coordinated.

After: csv_read 1.015s +/-0.113, csv_read_options 1.041s +/-0.070, csv_read_no_na_filter 1.163s +/-0.123. Paired 1000000x10: csv_read 1.987s -> 1.385s, options 1.996s -> 1.503s, no_na 1.883s -> 1.442s. Golden sha256 unchanged at 17f95925cec162c66933d5e935566b6f6fec5f8ef56872b98b00fb97ebf59fff; row/column ordering, float parsing, null/NaN, dtype/error behavior, tie/hash/RNG unaffected. title: [testing-conformance-harnesses] live-oracle skip detection is fragile string-match against LIVE_ORACLE_SKIP_MARKER; silent skips count as passes
description:
fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:5270-5473 — LIVE_ORACLE_SKIP_MARKER detection:
```
let skipped = output.matches(LIVE_ORACLE_SKIP_MARKER).count().min(passed);
let ran = total_tests.saturating_sub(skipped.saturating_add(failed));
```

If the pandas import or oracle setup fails silently — output doesn't contain the marker — but the test was actually skipped, our `skipped` counter is 0 and the test counts as `passed`. The LiveOracleReport then reports parity coverage that doesn't exist.

Fix: parse pytest's `=== N passed, M skipped ===` summary line directly (or pytest -v explicit SKIPPED markers) rather than substring-matching a custom marker. Fail loudly when `require_live_oracle = true` and the marker count exceeds zero (or when actual pytest skipped > 0).

Tests: feed a synthetic pytest output with `2 skipped` and no marker; assert the report flags it as skipped, not passed.
close_reason: Harvested cargo's ignored count alongside LIVE_ORACLE_SKIP_MARKER substring matches. is_green still requires skipped==0 when require_live_oracle. Commit 86c62cc1. 2 new parse_live_oracle tests pass.

description: Profile-backed post-uza04.11 target: tests/artifacts/perf/fp_reprofile_after_uza04_11_jadebadger.json ranks csv_read 100000x20 as the top row at 535.6 ms +/- 44.1, with retained CSV GDB evidence in tests/artifacts/perf/gdb_csv_read_azu2u_current.txt sampling try_read_csv_str_simple_typed_numeric, is_pandas_default_na_bytes, push_csv_default_numeric_field, and parse_f64_csv_number. Current ptrace sampling is blocked (tests/artifacts/perf/gdb_csv_read_after_uza04_11_jadebadger.txt), but the retained stacks still match the current parser. Prior CSV decimal parser, worker-pool, capacity, header, and SIMD delimiter levers were rejected or too small, so this is a different parser-state primitive: a numeric-byte certificate. One lever only: attempt an admitted numeric-only simple CSV parser that rejects any byte outside digits/sign/dot/exponent/delimiters before field commit; for admitted fields, bypass pandas default-NA/bool/trim checks because those tokens cannot occur under the certificate, and fall back to the existing parser for whitespace, NA-like, bool-like, quoted, CR, ragged, nonnumeric, NaN, or object data. Proof obligations: RCH-built baseline before edit, golden sha256 unchanged for csv_read/csv_read_options/csv_read_no_na_filter, preserve row/column order, dtype promotion, floating-point parse bits, null/NaN/default-NA/bool fallback, errors, RNG/tie/hash behavior, focused fp-io tests/check/clippy/fmt/UBS as feasible; keep only Score>=2.0 or remove source.

title: [test] property test: Scalar::semantic_cmp total-order axioms + NaN/NaT degeneracy
description: Scalar::semantic_cmp (fp-types) underpins ALL ordering in the library — sort, min/max, is_monotonic, searchsorted, groupby key order — and is the reference my other differential harnesses rely on (semantic_le/ge delegate to it). It has no property test of its order axioms. Add a deterministic (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks) property test asserting, over random SAME-dtype finite/non-NaT scalars (Int64, finite Float64, Utf8, Bool): reflexivity cmp(a,a)=Equal; antisymmetry cmp(a,b)=cmp(b,a).reverse(); transitivity (a<=b && b<=c => a<=c); and le/ge/eq consistency (semantic_le(a,b)==cmp!=Greater, semantic_ge==cmp!=Less, both==Equal). Separately PIN the intentional degenerate behavior: Float64 NaN / Timedelta NAT / Timestamp NAT / Period sentinel compare Equal to everything same-dtype (partial_cmp().unwrap_or(Equal)) — documents why ordering ops must treat NaN/NaT as missing. Test-only; fp-types.
close_reason: Added property test semantic_cmp_total_order_axioms_ay8o9 (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks): 6000 iters over finite same-dtype scalars (Int64/finite Float64/Utf8/Bool) asserting Scalar::semantic_cmp total-order axioms (reflexive/antisymmetric/transitive + le/ge/eq consistency), plus pinning the intentional NaN degeneracy (NaN cmp finite/self = Equal via partial_cmp().unwrap_or(Equal)). Validates the comparison foundation underpinning all library ordering (sort/min/max/is_monotonic/searchsorted/groupby) and the reference my differential harnesses rely on. Result: PASS. Verified: targeted 1 passed, full fp-types 237 passed/0 failed, my test adds 0 criticals (3 pre-existing panic FPs). Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/ay8o9_scalar_semantic_cmp_axioms.txt

title: perf(fp-frame): expanding std/var O(n^2) -> O(n) via rolling_var_online (window=len)
description: Expanding::std()/var() (fp-frame ~17935) route through apply_expanding, which re-folds the ENTIRE growing prefix every step: per output i it does a two-pass mean=sum(prefix)/n then sum((x-mean)^2)/(n-1) over nums[0..=i]. O(n^2) (n=100k => 1e10 ops). pandas implements Expanding as Rolling(window=len(obj)) over the O(n) compensated online roll_var (Welford add-only). FP already HAS that exact machinery: rolling_var_online + RollingVarianceState (landed under slt1p for rolling std/var). Route Expanding var/std through a synthetic full-window Rolling{window=len.max(min_periods).max(1), center:false} so window_bounds(i)==(0,i+1) (pure add-only, no removes) — bit-faithful to pandas roll_var, O(n). Conformance is tolerance-based; expanding std/var goldens are display-rounded so basic-data goldens should survive (regen if display shifts, sanctioned like slt1p). PROOF: isomorphism cross-check online vs naive two-pass within 1e-9 over random+adversarial (NaN/signed-zero/large-mag/constant) inputs; fp-frame + conformance green; rch hyperfine before/after Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped @5ccc7a5a (main+master). Expanding std/var routed through O(n) online rolling_var_online via full-window trailing Rolling (window=len.max(min_periods).max(1), pure add-only sweep = pandas Expanding-as-Rolling). Bench n=100k: 7661ms -> 3.24ms = 2365x. Isomorphism vs naive two-pass max rel diff 4.6e-15. Goldens bit-stable (no regen): fp-frame 2887/0, conformance expanding 11/0. Added isomorphism cross-check test.

title: [profiling] mode_values O(n²) Vec::iter_mut().find — build ModeKey to enable HashMap bucketing across cross-dtype numeric equivalence
description:
Skill: /profiling-software-performance.

mode_values (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:711) is the cross-dtype mode helper used
by DataFrame::mode_with_options axis=1 (line 27539). Each row may contain
heterogeneous Scalar variants. The bucket key uses pandas-style numeric
equivalence (grouped_scalar_eq, line 517):
  Bool(true) == Int64(1) == Float64(1.0)
  Bool(false) == Int64(0) == Float64(0.0)
  Float64(f) == Int64(n)  iff f == n as f64

The current impl walks counts: Vec<(Scalar, usize)> via iter_mut().find()
on every input — O(n × m) where m = distinct count. For wide DataFrames
with many rows, this becomes the rate-limiting step in axis=1 mode.

Fix: define a ModeKey enum that normalizes the equivalence classes:
  Int(i64): Bool, Int64, Float64-whole-finite-in-i64-range
  NonIntFloat(u64): Float64 otherwise (via to_bits)
  Utf8(&str), Timedelta(i64), Null, NaN
Use HashMap<ModeKey, usize> for index lookup. O(n) amortized.

The Series-level mode_with_dropna (line 5143) already uses ScalarKey-based
HashMap (br-cec8f) since Series has homogeneous columns. This fix brings
the same speedup to the cross-dtype helper.

title: Add Datetime/Timedelta/Period/Range Index take/repeat/isin forwarders
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): the typed Index variants still miss take/repeat/isin. Mirrors the forwarder pattern from br-z9guv/vq4pf/i1q1c/zke9k, restricted to operations that don't require monotonic ordering.

Add three methods on each of DatetimeIndex / TimedeltaIndex / PeriodIndex / RangeIndex:
- take(positions) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — clones the underlying values at each given position; out-of-bounds positions return IndexError::OutOfBounds.
- repeat(repeats) -> Self — repeats each value 'repeats' times.
- isin(values) -> Vec<bool> — per-position membership mask against a slice of the variant's value type (i64 nanos / i64 nanos / Period / i64 ints).

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: a 3-element index where take swaps positions, repeat doubles each value, isin matches a subset, plus out-of-bounds rejection.
close_reason: Shipped in e3a04f0: Datetime/Timedelta/Period/Range Index now expose pandas-parity take/repeat/isin with bound-checking and OOB rejection. 4 regression tests pass; 281 fp-index tests total.

title: [test] property test: common_dtype promotion lattice (commutativity/idempotence/associativity)
description: common_dtype (fp-types) is the dtype-promotion lattice underpinning ALL binary ops, alignment, and concat — the crown-jewel correctness area (AGENTS.md: dtype coercion rules are sacred). It has explicit per-pair match arms (e.g. (Bool,Int64)|(Int64,Bool)=>Int64) which are easy to make asymmetric. No property test guards the lattice axioms. Add an EXHAUSTIVE deterministic test (all 13 DType variants x 13 = 169 pairs, no rand, no mocks) asserting: commutativity common_dtype(a,b)==common_dtype(b,a) (same Ok value AND same ok-ness — catches any asymmetric arm => df1+df2 != df2+df1 dtype bug); idempotence common_dtype(a,a)==Ok(a); Null identity common_dtype(Null,a)==common_dtype(a,Null)==Ok(a); and guarded associativity common_dtype(common_dtype(a,b),c)==common_dtype(a,common_dtype(b,c)) over the Ok-closed subset. Test-only; fp-types.
close_reason: Added exhaustive deterministic property test common_dtype_lattice_axioms_be314 (no rand, no mocks): all 13 DType variants, asserting common_dtype lattice axioms — idempotence common_dtype(a,a)=Ok(a); Null identity; commutativity (169 pairs, same Ok value + ok-ness, catches asymmetric promotion arms => df1+df2 dtype != df2+df1); guarded associativity (2197 triples, Ok-closed). common_dtype underpins all binary ops/alignment/concat (crown-jewel dtype-coercion correctness). Result: PASS — promotion is commutative/idempotent/Null-identity/associative, order-independent. Verified: targeted 1 passed, full fp-types suite green, my test adds 0 criticals (3 pre-existing panic FPs). Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/be314_common_dtype_lattice.txt
