title: Add DatetimeIndex day-of-X and quarter/leap accessors
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): IndexVariants is missing the broader DatetimeIndex date accessors. Mirrors the existing year/month/day pattern (chrono::Datelike via map_datetime_labels) and the new hour/minute/second time-of-day pattern (br-znejf).

Add eight pandas-named methods:
- dayofyear / day_of_year -> Vec<Option<u32>> via chrono::Datelike::ordinal (1..=366).
- dayofweek / day_of_week / weekday -> Vec<Option<u32>> via Weekday::num_days_from_monday (0..=6, Monday=0).
- quarter -> Vec<Option<u32>> via (month - 1) / 3 + 1 (1..=4).
- is_leap_year -> Vec<Option<bool>> via chrono::Datelike::leap_year().
- days_in_month / daysinmonth -> Vec<Option<u32>> via num_days_in_month(year, month) helper (28/29/30/31).

Single PR. Add regression tests covering ordinal day, weekday alignment to pandas Mon=0..Sun=6, quarter at month boundaries, is_leap_year for 2000 (true) / 2024 (true) / 2100 (false) / 2023 (false), days_in_month for Feb 2024 (29) / Feb 2023 (28) / Apr (30) / Dec (31), plus NAT propagation.
close_reason: Shipped in 6b44c46: DatetimeIndex now exposes pandas-parity dayofyear/day_of_year/dayofweek/day_of_week/weekday/quarter/is_leap_year/days_in_month/daysinmonth via map_datetime_labels + chrono::Datelike + days_in_calendar_month helper. 2 regression tests pass; 259 fp-index tests total.

title: [perf] Index::is_monotonic_increasing/decreasing materialize Int64 labels (affine = O(1))
description: Index::is_monotonic_increasing/is_monotonic_decreasing (fp-index/src/lib.rs ~2086/2105) iterate self.labels.windows(2), which Derefs to as_slice() and materializes the full Vec<IndexLabel> via materialized.get_or_init — the lazy-Int64 materialization tax (same vein as gzi1i/bqfj0/6ubrp dtype/memory_usage/argsort). For an affine Int64 backing (int64_affine_range: RangeIndex/unit-range/affine — the DEFAULT index, very common) monotonicity is O(1) from the step sign (increasing iff step>=0, decreasing iff step<=0; len<=1 => both true). For typed non-affine Int64, scan the int64_view() &[i64] slice instead of the wider IndexLabel window. Bit-identical: all-Int64 backings have no missing labels and IndexLabel::Int64 Ord matches i64 Ord. FIX: add affine O(1) + int64_view fast paths before the IndexLabel windows fallback; add a test asserting materialized.get().is_none() after the predicates (vein invariant).
close_reason: Added lazy-Int64 fast paths to Index::is_monotonic_increasing/decreasing (avoids Vec<IndexLabel> materialization via windows()->as_slice). Affine backing (RangeIndex/unit/affine): O(1) via step sign (inc<=>step>=0, dec<=>step<=0). Typed non-affine Int64: scan int64_view() &[i64] (no label vec). Bit-identical (all-Int64 = no missing, IndexLabel::Int64 Ord == i64 Ord). New test int64_monotonic_predicates_avoid_label_materialization_k9tlb asserts correctness + materialized.get().is_none() invariant. Verified: targeted test passes, full fp-index suite exit 0, ubs 0 critical. Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/k9tlb_index_is_monotonic_lazy_int64.txt

title: [test] value oracle: fp-join left/outer join null-fill semantics
description: 5foyp validated INNER join value-level row matching. Extend to LEFT and OUTER joins, whose null-FILL semantics for unmatched rows are the subtlest join behavior (and where bugs hide). Add a deterministic (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks) value oracle: random many-to-many Int64-key left/right DataFrames with distinct per-row payloads; for LEFT join assert the multiset of output (key,left_payload,right_payload-or-NULL) triples == { (k,lp,rp) for matched } UNION { (k,lp,NULL) for left rows whose key has no right match }; for OUTER additionally include { (NULL-or-k, NULL, rp) for right rows whose key has no left match }. Represent NULL as a sentinel distinct from any payload. Guards left dense fast path (524e3566) + outer (9dd94d0b) null-fill at the value level. Test-only; fp-join.
close_reason: Added value oracle left_outer_join_nullfill_value_oracle_kaj3t (seeded LCG, no rand, no mocks): 1500 iters of many-to-many Int64-key joins, asserting LEFT/OUTER output (key,lv,rv) triple multisets == independent null-fill oracle (LEFT: matched UNION (k,lp,NULL) for unmatched-left; OUTER: + (k,NULL,rp) for unmatched-right, coalesced key). Confirmed+modeled the pandas-parity Int64->Float64 upcast on null-filled payload columns (initial draft surfaced it as a failure; fp is correct, extractor now accepts finite Float64). Guards left (524e3566) + outer (9dd94d0b) null-fill, key coalescing, and upcast at the value level. Result: PASS. Verified: targeted 1 passed, full fp-join suite exit 0, ubs my-lines clean (3 pre-existing FPs). Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/kaj3t_join_leftouter_nullfill_oracle.txt

title: Add Datetime/Timedelta Index min/max/sort_values
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): DatetimeIndex and TimedeltaIndex still miss the basic ordering reductions and sort surface. Mirrors br-bbgg3/834v9 pattern.

Add three methods on each:
- min() -> Option<i64> — minimum non-NAT label (pandas returns NaT/None for all-NAT or empty).
- max() -> Option<i64> — maximum non-NAT label.
- sort_values() -> Self — ascending sort. NAT sorts first because i64::MIN/Timedelta::NAT < every other value (matches pandas with na_position='first', the default for datetime indexes).

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: min/max on a 4-element index with NAT, all-NAT returns None, and sort_values on duplicated input.
close_reason: Shipped in 46f279b: Datetime/Timedelta Index now expose pandas-parity min/max/sort_values with NAT skip and na_position='first' default. 2 regression tests pass; 287 fp-index tests total.

description: to_period (fp-frame ~29576: 'to_period frequency is not supported yet') and to_timestamp (~29781: 'frequency not supported yet') reject several Period frequencies. Period machinery is niche but part of total parity.
notes: PROGRESS 2026-06-14 (BlackThrush, shipped 8cb3fb89): anchored WEEKLY freqs W-MON..W-SAT now supported in to_period/to_timestamp (fp-frame-local; week ends on anchor weekday, START/END label; to_timestamp split is anchor-independent). That's the architectural enum-change slice; weekly was the freq-local slice and is DONE.

title: [parity-bug] str.wrap lacks break_long_words / break_on_hyphens (Python textwrap)
description:
Oracle pandas 2.2.3: 'abcdefghijklmnop'.str.wrap(5) -> 'abcde
fghij
klmno
p' (break_long_words=True); 'foo-bar-baz qux'.str.wrap(7) -> 'foo-
bar-baz
qux' (break_on_hyphens=True). FP wrap_text only packs whitespace-split words, never breaking long words or hyphens, so it emits over-width lines. Full Python textwrap parity (wordsep regex incl hyphen rule + break_long_words) needed; partial fixes create new divergences.
notes:
break_long_words DONE in 4d5de1cf (oracle-verified: abcdefghijklmnop/5, aa bbbbbbbb/5, supercalifragilistic/7, etc. all match; non-long-word wrapping byte-identical incl golden). REMAINING: break_on_hyphens — hyphenated over-long words wrap width-compliantly but not pandas-identically (foo-bar-baz/7 -> FP 'foo-bar
-baz
qux' vs pandas 'foo-
bar-baz
qux'). Needs Python textwrap wordsep hyphen rule (split after a hyphen only between letters).
close_reason: break_on_hyphens implemented: faithful CPython textwrap port (_munge_whitespace/_split wordsep/_wrap_chunks/_handle_long_word); 141-case live-oracle battery green incl em-dashes, digit no-break, long-word hyphen breaks, drop_whitespace=False; 183 fp-frame str_ tests pass, clippy clean

Series.and/or + DataFrame.combine built union in self-first-then-new order; pandas sorts the differing-index union (and combine sorts columns): [2,1]&[2,1,0] was [2,1,0] now [0,1,2]; combine [0,2]+[1] was [0,2,1] now [0,1,2]; combine cols [a]+[b] now sorted [a,b]. title: to_json serializes datetime/timedelta divergently from pandas (ISO/format string + raw-nanos index vs pandas epoch-millis int)
description: pandas 2.2.3 DataFrame/Series.to_json() default (date_format='epoch', date_unit='ms') serializes datetime64 AND timedelta64 as EPOCH-MILLISECOND INTEGERS for BOTH values and index. Verified: datetime ['2020-01-01','2020-01-02'].to_json()={'d':{'0':1577836800000,'1':1577923200000}}; timedelta [1s,2s]->{'t':{'0':1000,'1':2000}}. FP (fp-io) diverges THREE ways: (1) scalar_to_json Datetime64 -> ISO string via format_datetime_ns ('2020-01-01 00:00:00'); (2) scalar_to_json Timedelta64 -> Timedelta::format string ('0 days 00:00:01'); (3) index_label_to_json (lib 4240-4241) Datetime64/Timedelta64 -> RAW NANOSECONDS int (json!(*ns)) — matching NEITHER pandas (millis) NOR FP's own value format (ISO). The index-as-raw-nanos is an unambiguous internal inconsistency. ALSO: format_datetime_ns is second-granular so sub-second is silently dropped even in the string form (sister to lr7kb/cm5fy). SCOPE/RISK: changing write to epoch-millis makes read_json round-trip LOSSY (an int column reads back as Int64, losing datetime type) — but that EXACTLY MATCHES pandas (read_json default does not restore datetime without the convert_dates column-name heuristic). So full parity = (a) to_json/to_jsonl emit epoch-millis ints for datetime/timedelta values+index, (b) optionally a date_format option ('epoch'|'iso') to preserve the current nicer ISO round-trip, (c) read_json convert_dates heuristic for type restoration. This is a DESIGN DECISION (parity-exact lossy vs usable ISO round-trip) — needs owner input before flipping; FP may have intentionally chosen ISO. Period to_json is NOT a target (pandas itself raises OverflowError on Period columns). Found via Phase-B differential probe; fp-io editable+testable. Sister: 5ioai (pct_change default), 8ic7c (interpolate).
close_reason: Fixed in c8298760: to_json now serializes datetime64/timedelta64 as epoch-millisecond ints (ns/1e6) for values (scalar_to_json), index-as-value (index_label_to_json, split orient), and index-as-object-key (new index_label_json_key, columns/index orients) — matching pandas 2.2.3 (date_unit='ms'). read_json maps the ints back to Int64 like pandas read_json default (datetime not auto-restored). Verified across columns/index/split orients. fp-io 563/0, clippy clean. Period to_json out of scope (pandas itself raises OverflowError). Sub-second/date_format-option enhancements remain possible future work but the default now matches pandas.

title: [perf] stable LSD radix lexsort for sort_values_multi (O(n) vs O(n log n); target >=2x)
description: Follow-up to 1tuf5 (abaad0a4). Typed comparison got 1.57x but the O(n log n) comparison-sort permutation is the floor. Lever: stable LSD radix lexsort — sort the index permutation by the LAST by-column first, then each preceding column, each pass a STABLE radix over that column's typed key, starting from order 0..n. Reuse the single-column radix machinery (memory: sort_values is radix for i64+f64). Result == lexicographic order with original-position tie-break (stable radix preserves prior order). Bit-identicality RISK (the hard part): must encode per-column ascending/descending + na_position + Float64 total order (NaN handling, -0.0) into the radix key EXACTLY as compare_scalars_with_na_position. Single-column f64 radix already solved the f64 bit-order; extend with the missing-bucket (na_first/na_last) + per-column asc flip. Gate to all-typed (Int64/Float64) by-columns; fall back to the typed-comparison path (abaad0a4) for Utf8/Bool/mixed. Golden sort_multi a56322a7 must hold. Bench n=500k 2-key: target <=190ms (>=2x over 380).
close_reason: Shipped 85ba32f1: stable LSD radix lexsort for sort_values_multi (O(n*k) vs O(n log n)). Column::typed_radix_keys (order-preserving u64 per col, asc/desc baked in, no-NaN-f64 gate) + fp_columnar::radix_argsort_multi_u64 (8-pass stable counting sort per col, last sort key first). All-typed all-valid columns only; Utf8/Bool/nullable/NaN fall back to 1tuf5 typed-comparison. Bit-identical (golden sort_multi a56322a7 before==after; 378 fp-columnar + 82 fp-frame sort tests green). n=500k 2-key (Int64+Float64), quiet box: 127->44 ms = 2.9x over the typed comparator. CLEARS 2.0 — the algorithm-REPLACEMENT (radix vs comparison sort) the sub-2.0 streak was pointing to.

title: [reality-check] fp-frame coerce_scalar silently truncates INFINITY/out-of-range f64 to i64::MAX during astype(Int64)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:1544 — coerce_scalar for DType::Int64:
```rust
DType::Int64 => match val {
    Scalar::Int64(_) => val.clone(),
    Scalar::Float64(f) => Scalar::Int64(*f as i64),  // silent truncation
    Scalar::Utf8(s) => match s.parse::<i64>() { ... }
    ...
}
```

The unchecked `*f as i64` cast clamps INFINITY → i64::MAX, NEG_INFINITY → i64::MIN, NaN → 0, and out-of-range finite values get truncated, all silently. Pandas raises OverflowError/IntCastingNaNError on astype('int64') of NaN or non-finite values.

Reached from df.astype(Int64) and similar coercions — user-input pathway.

Surgical fix: add `f.is_finite() && in-range` check before cast; return Scalar::Null(NaN) (or eventually Result-propagating error) otherwise. cast_scalar in fp-types already does proper range checking via LossyFloatToInt — fp-frame coerce_scalar should consistent.

Tests: coerce f64::INFINITY/NaN/9e18 to Int64 returns Null (not silent truncation); normal in-range values preserved.
close_reason: Added range check (is_finite + i64-range) before f64→i64 cast in coerce_scalar. INFINITY/NaN/out-of-range now return Null. Commit ef87da6f. 1903 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [perf] left_join dense builder lane fill: per-row Scalar pushes in build_single_key_dense_left_merge_output (left_join ~2.6x worse per row than inner; untouched by 1bvcl)
description: Measured on NEW perf_profile left_join (100k card512 50% overlap, 446443f0): ~820-980 ms/iter for 9.8M output rows vs inner's 40ms for 19.5M — ~2.6x worse per row, and 1bvcl's typed reindex did NOT move it (left joins route through build_single_key_dense_left_merge_output ~2359 and siblings, which fill output lanes with per-row pushes — see the repeat_count push loop at ~2238-2241 region for the outer sibling). Levers proven on the inner builder, in order: (1) muis1 right bucket value tapes, (2) 6bsw3 row-chunked disjoint-write parallel fill from a matched plan, (3) 3ad4n lazy repeat-run left lanes (avg fanout>=2 gate, parallel expansion). Apply the same sequence to the left (2359+), right_all_matched (1869+... wait that one is inner-shaped), and outer (2069+) dense builders. The unmatched-left rows make left lanes NOT pure repeat-runs (null right values) — left lanes of matched rows are runs, unmatched rows are singleton runs, so RLE still applies; right lanes are nullable -> need the LazyNullableFloat64 chunked-fill variant or stay eager. Profile FIRST (perf record left_join) to rank which of the three levers dominates. Gates: left_join golden 745e5a46 + outer_join d774e320 unchanged, fp-join+fp-columnar suites, conformance join proptests, paired hyperfine at load<40, Score>=2.0.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::dropna loses index name (pandas preserves it even when result is empty)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:4167-4179 — Series::dropna calls Self::from_values with new_labels but no index name carry-over:
```rust
Self::from_values(self.name.clone(), new_labels, new_values)
```

from_values internally does `Index::new(index_labels)` which sets name=None. Result: dropna on a Series with a named index loses the index name.

Pandas preserves the index name through dropna (even when result is empty):
> s = pd.Series([np.nan], index=pd.Index(['a'], name='idx'))
> s.dropna().index.name == 'idx'

Surgical fix: build the index manually with rename_index(self.index.name()), then construct via Self::new directly:
```rust
let index = Index::new(new_labels).rename_index(self.index.name());
let column = Column::from_values(new_values)?;
Self::new(self.name.clone(), index, column)
```

Tests: Series with named index → dropna → assert resulting index.name() == Some("idx"). Existing dropna tests stay green.

May also affect Series::drop and DataFrame::dropna paths — out of scope for this bead.
close_reason: Switched Series::dropna from Self::from_values to Self::new with rename_index, preserving index name. Commit 109afc96. 1908 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [perf][no-gaps] DataFrame.corr()/cov() pairwise matrix: own safe-Rust Gram kernel (symmetry + lean inner loop), bit-identical
close_reason: Shipped 5d4b2e4c: DataFrame.corr()/cov() now run the O(M) moment accumulation once per unordered pair (i<=j) and finalize both (i,j) and (j,i) cells, with marginals swapped for (j,i) to replay the exact non-associative rounding. BIT-IDENTICAL: golden sha256 of df.corr/df.cov (64-col) unchanged (corr=6f577ab8.., cov=beb4e3f5..); 76 corr/cov unit tests pass. Bench 20000x64x20: corr 1.433->0.836s (1.71x), cov 1.417->0.843s (1.68x). Own safe-Rust Gram kernel, no C BLAS. Ceiling ~1.7x here because the shared Scalar->f64 column extraction (untouchable without breaking parity / part of the columnar epic) dilutes the halved pairwise work; SIMD on the inner reduction would need reassociation -> breaks bit parity.

title: [perf] Fused single-pass groupby multi-agg (sum+mean+std share count/sum/sumsq accumulators)
description: PROFILE-BACKED (1M float64, release-perf, 2026-06-15): groupby_agg_multi is the ONLY op where fp is slower than pandas at scale — 1.36x@1M (fp=31053us vs pd=22829us), 1.27x@100k (ratio ~constant => real, not fixed-overhead). Root cause: DataFrameGroupBy::agg(&["sum","mean","std"]) (fp-frame ~17621 and the groupby variants) LOOPS over funcs and calls self.sum()? self.mean()? self.std()? as THREE separate full groupby passes (each re-iterates all rows + re-buckets by group). pandas df.groupby(key).agg({col:[sum,mean,std]}) computes them together. LEVER: a fused single-pass accumulator — one pass computing (count, sum, sumsq) per group yields sum (=sum), mean (=sum/count), std (=sqrt((sumsq - sum^2/count)/(count-1))) simultaneously. var/sem/skew/kurt extend the same moment set. Detect a moment-compatible func subset in agg() and route to the fused accumulator; fall back to per-func for non-moment funcs (median/first/last/prod). TARGET: groupby_agg_multi 1.36x -> <=0.7x (>=2x speedup, 3 passes -> 1). Discipline: isomorphism proof (bit-identical agg outputs vs the 3-call path, same NaN/empty-group/ddof semantics), golden sha256, Score>=2.0, rch crate-scoped. NOTE bench fp-bench groupby_agg_multi calls gb.sum()/mean()/std() manually — switch it to gb.agg(&[...]) so it measures the fused path once shipped.
notes: SLICE 2 SHIPPED e04cd540: extended typed-reducer routing to agg_dict_list + agg_multi (same generic-Scalar 6x slow path), consolidated via shared agg_typed_pairs(specs) helper + is_typed_agg_func predicate. agg_list/agg_dict_list/agg_multi all delegate; validation/error semantics preserved; 52 agg + 192 fp-frame groupby + 108 conformance groupby green, bit-identical. DEEP FUSION (remaining, target 1.67x->~1x): agg_typed_pairs still calls aggregate_named_func once per DISTINCT func = N x build_groups + N x dense precompute. CLEANEST FACTORING: split aggregate_named_func (~53796) into (a) PREPARE = build_groups() + labels + the dense gid precompute (go_gid, gid_per_row, ngroups) at ~53864-53995, returning a struct; (b) aggregate_named_func_with_prepared(func, &prepared) = the per-column accumulation loop ~54001-54300 + format_output. Then aggregate_named_func = str_dense-early-return-check + prepare + with_prepared; and agg_typed_pairs computes prepare ONCE and calls with_prepared per func (saves N-1 build_groups, the dominant cost). str_dense single-key case (~53806 early return) bypasses build_groups so keep calling aggregate_named_func per func there (it's already fast). Validate via the same 52 agg + 192 groupby + 108 conformance suites; bit-identity is automatic (same accumulation code, just shared grouping).
close_reason: Accepted: fused groupby multi-agg moments shipped in 07112d63/e04cd540/e37bbd6b; supplemental hyperfine candidate 2.29x forward and 2.20x reversed; golden SHA 4b7c746e9413cb9f16da963e2ba248db8d538bd5a8b56631ac040aeb368d3b8a matched baseline/candidate; Score 10.0.

title: [perf] series_add_align segmented/succinct alignment plan primitive
description:
Post-8zgjh rejected dense unit-range branch: retained pass-27 binary still shows series_add_align as the profile-backed top target (post-n921e 765.6 ms +/- 14.0; fresh 8zgjh baseline 773.2 ms +/- 28.1). The attempted dense unit-stride Int64 range path preserved golden SHA but regressed (A/B old 777.8 ms +/- 22.8 vs candidate 804.2 ms +/- 17.4), so do NOT repeat that lever.

Alien primitive: apply graveyard §7.1 Succinct Data Structures / Elias-Fano sorted sequences and segmented-array representation, not micro-tuning. Investigate representing sorted unique outer alignment plans as compressed run/segment descriptors or lazy position accessors that feed Float64 aligned arithmetic without materializing full Vec<Option<usize>> maps. Fallback must be the existing AlignmentPlan materialization for duplicates, non-sorted/non-unique, sparse/disjoint, non-Float64, or any semantic uncertainty.

Required loop: baseline and golden via RCH-built retained binary first; profile/sampling must confirm position-vector materialization or witness/fingerprint cost remains top-5; implement exactly one structural lever only if Score>=2.0; prove ordering/tie-breaking/null-NaN/floating-point/RNG behavior unchanged and golden sha256 identical; rebench via RCH-built candidate; retain only if paired A/B wins with Score>=2.0; then reprofile.
close_reason: Rejected: sorted-unique streamed Float64 alignment preserved golden SHA but failed keep gate (old 767.0 ms +/- 16.6 vs candidate 789.0 ms +/- 13.8; code removed, evidence retained).

title: [mock-code-finder] fp-columnar Column::pct_change silently returns NaN for Timedelta64 input
description:
fp-columnar/src/lib.rs:2167-2176 — pct_change uses `match (cur.to_f64(), prev.to_f64())` with a catch-all `_ => NaN` arm. Timedelta64.to_f64() returns TypeError::NonNumericValue, so pct_change on a Timedelta64 column silently produces all-NaN output.

Pandas: pct_change on a Timedelta64 series returns dimensionless f64 ratios (ns deltas divide as scalars). NaT propagates.

Fix: add a Timedelta64 fast path before to_f64 match — computing `(cur_ns - prev_ns) as f64 / prev_ns as f64` with NaT propagation and zero-divisor → NaN, mirroring the fp-frame Series::pct_change fix from br-naeja.

Tests: pct_change on Timedelta64 Column returns Float64 ratios. NaT propagation. Existing Int64/Float64 tests remain green.
close_reason: Implemented Timedelta64 fast path in Column::pct_change. Commit b8a81e1b. Tests: 2 new + 7 existing pct_change tests pass.

title: rolling/ewm return Null where pandas returns a value under min_periods<window with nulls (gauntlet CONF-RC3)
description: Live pandas 2.2.3: [1,nan,3,4,5].rolling(3,min_periods=1).sum() = [1,1,4,7,12]. FrankenPandas returns Null at idx0 (and similar) for rolling sum/count/max/var and ewm.mean when a window has >=min_periods valid values but is not full. Failing packets: FP-P2D-335/338/339/340/341/345 (series_rolling_sum/max/var/count, series_ewm_mean, dataframe_rolling_mean), all *_null_hardened. CONFIRMED subject bug (live-verified). Fix: honor min_periods (count non-null in window; emit value when valid_count>=min_periods). Not landed in gauntlet session due to rolling-semantics breadth; well-characterized for implementation.
acceptance_criteria: VERIFY-FIRST vs live pandas 2.2.3: [1,nan,3,4,5].rolling(3,min_periods=1).sum()==[1,1,4,7,12]. Then rolling+ewm honor min_periods (emit value when valid_count>=min_periods, skip nulls) for sum/count/max/min/mean/std/var. UNIT TESTS: happy (full window), edge (leading partial window, all-null window, single valid, min_periods=1 vs window, NaN interior), error. CONFORMANCE: FP-P2D-335/338/339/340/341/345 green via T9_DELTA, ZERO red-flips. Cover DataFrame + GroupBy rolling. clippy+ubs clean.
notes: RESOLVED + GATE-VERIFIED 2026-05-26 by the gauntlet executor. ROOT CAUSE was a FIXTURE/HARNESS defect, NOT a subject bug — the rolling subject code was correct throughout (the swarm's repeated 'fix the rolling code' closes were chasing a phantom). Three-part fix: (1) added serde aliases so the harness reads the packet's window (alias rolling_window->window_size) and min_periods (alias rolling_min_periods->min_periods) — previously both were silently ignored (window defaulted to 3, min_periods to window); (2) recorded the oracle's actual min_periods in the packets: FP-P2D-340 count + FP-P2D-339 var = min_periods 1, FP-P2D-345 dataframe_mean = min_periods 2; (3) corrected FP-P2D-345 expected_frame column B idx1 from Null to 15.0 — it was internally inconsistent (column A implied mp=2 but B implied a non-pandas rule); the real df.rolling(3,min_periods=2).mean() gives B=[None,15,15,30,45], which FP produces. VERIFIED via the conformance gate: series_rolling_count, series_rolling_var, dataframe_rolling_mean all GREEN; gate red count 8->7; zero regressions. ewm_mean was already green from cd78282d.
close_reason: Fixed: rolling count checks non-null count (not window size) against min_periods. EWM mean carries forward last value for nulls after first observation.

title: Add RangeIndex argmax/argmin/argsort/duplicated/drop_duplicates
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): IndexVariants is missing 134 pandas methods without open child beads. RangeIndex's argmax/argmin/argsort/duplicated/drop_duplicates are five of the simplest gaps because RangeIndex is uniqueness-by-construction and monotonic — every result is a closed-form expression of (start, stop, step, len).

Add five inherent methods on RangeIndex:
- argmax() -> Result<usize, IndexError> — len-1 when step>0; 0 when step<0; error on empty.
- argmin() -> Result<usize, IndexError> — mirror of argmax.
- argsort() -> Vec<usize> — [0..len) when step>0; [len-1..=0] when step<0.
- duplicated(keep: DuplicateKeep) -> Vec<bool> — always vec![false; len] (RangeIndex enforces uniqueness).
- drop_duplicates() -> Self — clones self because RangeIndex never has duplicates.

Add regression tests covering ascending step, descending step, empty index, and large step values. Single PR. Mirrors existing pattern on base Index (lines 1779/1849 etc. for round/diff).
close_reason: Shipped in e61c4fd: RangeIndex now exposes pandas-parity argmax/argmin/argsort/duplicated/drop_duplicates with O(1) closed-form implementations and pandas-style empty-sequence rejection. 4 regression tests pass; 252 fp-index tests total.

title: [perf] Optimize dense i64 inner output repeat-fill memory-write lane after p2tfu
description: Post-br-frankenpandas-p2tfu reprofile shows the shifted dense inner join hotspot inside build_dense_i64_inner_output_column after parallel per-column fill: perf_profile inner_join 100000 3 improved to 196.500 ms/iter, and the remaining samples sit in the left-side repeat-fill/memory-write path (Vec resize/reserve style work) plus right gather writes. Next pass must baseline via RCH from current main, keep one lever only, and prove the exact p2tfu isomorphism: left probe order, right bucket insertion order, output column names/order/dtypes, null/mixed/sorted/indicator/validate fallbacks, no FP/RNG. Candidate alien primitives: pre-sized Vec with slice fill for left repeat runs, range-partitioned disjoint writes, or a run-plan/prefix-sum writer that avoids repeated capacity growth while preserving row-major output. Golden gates: p2tfu inner_join/join_1to1 sha256 be7d1711/18988b22 unchanged; fp-join + focused fp-conformance prop_inner_join; paired hyperfine; keep only Score>=2.0.

Paired hyperfine inner_join 100000x3: 592.5 ms +/- 40.7 -> 446.8 ms +/- 25.7 (1.33x +/- 0.12); join_1to1 100000x20 control: 36.1 ms +/- 2.9 -> 33.7 ms +/- 2.0 (1.07x +/- 0.11). Golden sha256 unchanged and byte-identical: inner_join 494106fca6e3310a318f1685c74041a2788089a4d2409107d4eef4a00c7a0764; join_1to1 102690aa39952cc2d13bcc41547aacdeac1946113e43d62472fdb93440bc56a7. Isomorphism: positioned_values[i] == spec.values[positions[i]], so right bucket insertion order is identical; left probe order, output column names/order/dtypes, null/mixed/sorted/indicator/validate fallbacks, floating-point behavior, and RNG behavior unchanged. Validation: cargo fmt -p fp-join --check; RCH cargo test -p fp-join (101/0); RCH cargo clippy -p fp-join --all-targets -- -D warnings; RCH cargo test -p fp-conformance prop_inner_join --test proptest_properties (4/0); UBS ran with only pre-existing file-wide heuristic inventory. Score Impact 3 x Confidence 5 / Effort 1 = 15 KEEP. Reprofile after: inner_join 100000x3 138.690 ms/iter; next shifted target filed as br-frankenpandas-6bsw3 for left repeat-fill/output memory lifecycle.

CRITICAL: gate on index.is_unique() — for duplicate labels the general Left-align maps each self row to cond's FIRST occurrence, which positional access would not reproduce (proptest prop_dataframe_where_is_translation_covariant caught this). where 58->3.7=15.8x, mask 69->4.1=16.9x bit-identical (golden 171d2f9d). DONE.
close_reason: Shipped 81198827: typed where_cond/mask via shared where_mask_typed_f64 (identical+UNIQUE index + finite Float64 fill + all-Float64 -> select from f64+validity slices, no cond reindex/no self Scalar/typed output). where 58->3.7=15.8x, mask 69->4.1=16.9x bit-identical (golden 171d2f9d). KEY: is_unique() gate — proptest caught dup-label Left-align-to-first-occurrence divergence. fp-frame 2830 + conformance 0 failures.

Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): the typed Index variants miss the format() stringifier that pandas exposes for diagnostic printing.

Add format() -> Vec<String> on each of:
- DatetimeIndex — chrono::DateTime::to_string for non-NAT, 'NaT' for NAT.
- TimedeltaIndex — fp_types::Timedelta-style formatting via the existing Timedelta helpers; 'NaT' for NAT.
- PeriodIndex — Period::to_string via Display.
- CategoricalIndex — labels are already strings; clone them.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: each variant produces strings of the expected shape and NAT-aware empty cells where applicable.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::pct_change silently returns NaN for Timedelta64 input
description:
Skill: /mock-code-finder — silent stub via to_f64-else-NaN.

fp-frame Series::pct_change (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:7437) uses to_f64() on each element. Scalar::Timedelta64 returns TypeError::NonNumericValue from to_f64, so the match arm '_ => out.push(Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN))' silently produces all-NaN for Timedelta64 Series.

Pandas computes (current - previous) / previous on Timedelta64 in nanoseconds, returning Float64 ratios:
  >>> pd.Series([pd.Timedelta('1h'), pd.Timedelta('2h'), pd.Timedelta('4h')]).pct_change()
  0    NaN
  1    1.0
  2    1.0
  dtype: float64

fp-frame currently returns all-NaN.

Fix: add a Timedelta64 fast path before the to_f64 fallback that computes (cur_nanos - prev_nanos) / prev_nanos in f64. Treat NaT current or previous as NaN. Treat prev=0 as inf/NaN per IEEE.

Tests:
- pct_change on Timedelta64 Series of [1h, 2h, 4h] returns [NaN, 1.0, 1.0].
- pct_change with NaT propagates as NaN.
- pct_change with zero previous Timedelta returns NaN/inf consistent with f64 division.
- existing Int64/Float64 tests remain green.

Also affects Series::pct_change_with_fill (line 7472) which shares the same code path.
close_reason: Shipped in 494fef01: Series::pct_change now exposes pandas-parity Timedelta64 handling — (cur - prev)/prev computed as f64 ratio with NaT propagation. pct_change_with_fill picks up the fix via delegation. 2 regression tests pass; 13 pct_change tests total green.

title: [perf] csv_read parse Float64 columns directly with fast_float
description: Post-56b5d44c RCH reprofile ranks csv_read 100000x20 as the top unowned profile-backed target: 675.8 ms +/- 15.9. Retained GDB CSV profile sampled parse_f64_csv_number / parse_i64_ascii / has_float_marker / push_csv_default_numeric_field, while fresh perf/gdb attach is blocked by kernel ptrace/perf settings. Current typed numeric CSV parser keeps scanning every Float64-column field for '.'/'e' and tries std i64 parse for integral-looking fields before fast_float. Alien primitive: parser-state specialization -- once a column has promoted to Float64, use fast_float directly for all fields after NA/bool/trim checks, avoiding marker scans and std integer parsing in the float-column state. Scope fp-io only. Proof obligations: default NA/bool rejection unchanged, Int64-column promotion unchanged, integer-looking fields in Float64 columns parse to the same f64, overflow and exponent behavior still delegated to fast_float/std fallback, row/column/index order and FP bits unchanged, golden csv_read sha256 unchanged. Baseline with RCH hyperfine + golden first; keep only if Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Completed: Float64-state CSV numeric parser now uses fast_float directly after existing NA/bool/trim checks. RCH hyperfine csv_read 100000x20 improved 680.5 ms +/- 20.8 ms -> 619.5 ms +/- 21.1 ms (1.10x). Golden sha256 unchanged: 17f95925cec162c66933d5e935566b6f6fec5f8ef56872b98b00fb97ebf59fff. Isomorphism: Int64-state promotion unchanged; Float64-state integer-looking fields parse to the same f64 through fast_float; NaN/invalid/NA/bool fallback behavior unchanged.

title: [perf][no-gaps] typed contiguous-Utf8 Index backing — kill n String-alloc + n from_utf8 in string-index output (str_groupby/sort_values/setops)
description:
GAP: ops emitting a STRING INDEX build Vec<IndexLabel::Utf8(String)> — n heap String allocs + n std::str::from_utf8 re-validations (25% of str_groupby_var/std/median/sum on ~unique keys; aggregate_str_dense + the generic build_groups). The source key bytes are already a valid contiguous Utf8 buffer (LazyContiguousUtf8), so both the alloc and the validation are redundant. fp-index IndexLabels already has lazy typed backings (int64_unit_range, int64_typed/dxqpm) but NO Utf8 backing.

LEVER (different memory layout): add a contiguous-Utf8 IndexLabels backing { bytes: Arc<[u8]>, offsets: Arc<[usize]> } (mirror of fp-columnar's LazyContiguousUtf8 / the cmxjz LazyNullableUtf8 work) + constructor Index::from_utf8_contiguous. as_slice() materializes IndexLabel::Utf8 lazily once; add Utf8 fast paths to the hot IndexLabels consumers (position_map, get_indexer, gather/take, Debug/Display) so the common flows never materialize. Then aggregate_str_dense / str sort_values / str set-ops build the output index by GATHERING key byte-spans into one buffer (no per-label String, no from_utf8) instead of Vec<IndexLabel::Utf8>.

ISOMORPHISM: materialized IndexLabel::Utf8(span) == the String-built label; first-seen/sorted order preserved. Bit-identical.

TARGET: ~1.4-1.8x on str_groupby unique-key ops (removes the 25% from_utf8 + the n-String-alloc ~15%); broader wins on string sort_values output + Index set-ops. 

BLAST RADIUS: new IndexLabels variant -> ~enumerate exhaustive matches in fp-index (compiler-guided, like the ScalarValues LazyNullableUtf8 which needed only ~3 arms) + Utf8 fast-path opt-ins in fp-frame consumers. Multi-step; needs LOW machine load for reliable A/B. Add str_groupby + str_sort_values index-output goldens.

NOTE: filed under high load (17-22); the per-op ceiling of str_groupby WITHOUT this backing is ~1.1x (from_utf8 byte-radix dedup alone) — not worth shipping standalone. This backing is the structural unlock.
close_reason: Core primitive landed b13f24cd main+master: IndexLabels lazy utf8_contiguous backing + Index::from_utf8_contiguous, used by str-groupby output. 1.13-1.60x bit-identical (goldens unchanged), 385+2830 tests green. Follow-ups (byte-radix order keys, consumer Utf8 fast-paths, str sort_values/setop output reuse) remain as smaller items.

title: [perf] CSV simple-numeric integer fast path after cache residual
description: Profile-backed follow-up after br-frankenpandas-66n6a. The cache-hit memcmp residual cannot be skipped exactly for public read_csv_str(&str) because callers can reuse a String allocation with same length but changed content, so pointer-only equality is rejected. Route to the uncached simple numeric CSV parser residual: perf_report_csv_read_current_cod_pandas shows fp_io::parse_f64_csv_number 30.52%, push_csv_default_numeric_field 19.59%, parse_i64_ascii 2.96%, and fast_float2::compute_float 5.82% on csv_read. Candidate one-lever primitive: replace parse_i64_ascii's UTF-8 + str::parse path with a single-pass ASCII checked integer parser and use that exact integer result for Float64 columns with no decimal/exponent marker; overflow/non-integer inputs fall through to the existing fast_float2/std parser, preserving behavior. Require RCH baseline on csv_read/csv_read_options/csv_read_no_na_filter with iters=1 for uncached parse, golden sha256 unchanged, focused fp-io tests, UBS, rebench Score>=2.0, then close and reprofile.
close_reason: Rejected: single-pass ASCII integer parser plus Float64 integer-field fast path failed Score gate. Golden sha256 unchanged at 17f95925cec162c66933d5e935566b6f6fec5f8ef56872b98b00fb97ebf59fff, but paired large uncached hyperfine regressed/failed to improve: csv_read 500000x3 before 648.7 ms +/- 40.9 vs after 674.2 ms +/- 34.7; csv_read_options before 646.6 ms +/- 37.2 vs after 662.3 ms +/- 42.7. Code reverted. Next primitive: deeper simple-numeric CSV parser replacement using a two-phase column type classifier / row-blocked parser to reduce per-field enum dispatch and keep exact fast_float fallback semantics.

title: [perf] contiguous-Utf8 gather fast path in Column::take_positions (filter/sort/dedup/take on string columns)
description: Column::take_positions (fp-columnar, the cross-cutting gather behind filter/iloc/sort/drop_duplicates/reindex/head/tail/groupby-row-select) has typed fast paths for Float64/Int64/nullable-f64 (uza04) but NOT for Utf8. An all-valid contiguous-Utf8 (LazyContiguousUtf8) source falls through to positions.iter().map(|p| self.values[p].clone()) — materializing a heap String per gathered row AND re-materializing the lazy Scalar on each index. Lever: add an as_utf8_contiguous() fast path inside the validity.all() block — gather k spans into one fresh bytes Vec + offsets Vec, emit lazy_contiguous_utf8 output (zero per-row String allocs). Same memory-layout win as the sort/groupby contiguous work. Bit-identical: same bytes, same order, all_valid(n) mask; output materializes Scalar::Utf8 of identical spans. Golden sha256 on a string-column filter; Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Shipped 580f8fdd: contiguous-Utf8 gather in Column::take_positions (filter/sort/dedup/take on string columns). Bit-identical golden (4-col iloc_bool, before==after 2dd0a491). n=1M 4 Utf8 cols: 85.8->26.7 ms/iter = 3.21x. take_positions contract tests + 145 fp-frame utf8/iloc/filter/take/dedup tests green.

title: [reality-check] Series::ewm.sum panics on non-numeric (Utf8/etc) inputs via unwrap on to_f64()
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:10697 — EWM sum's missing-value guard uses `val.to_f64().is_ok_and(f64::is_nan)`, which is true ONLY when to_f64() returns Ok(NaN). For non-numeric inputs (Utf8, Timedelta64 mixed cases), to_f64 returns Err and the guard misses → line 10701's `.unwrap()` panics.

Sister method `ewm_mean` at line 10916 correctly uses `val.to_f64().map_or(true, |v| v.is_nan())`, which treats conversion errors as NaN candidates and emits Null instead of panicking.

Surgical fix: change line 10697 from `is_ok_and(f64::is_nan)` to `map_or(true, |v| v.is_nan())`, matching the ewm_mean pattern.

Tests: Series with Utf8 elements → ewm.sum() returns Null per Utf8 row (no panic). Existing numeric EWM tests stay green.
close_reason: Switched ewm.sum guard from is_ok_and to map_or to match ewm_mean/ewm_cov. Commit 8afc13be. 1927 fp-frame tests pass.

title: [perf] fp-index Int64 astype_int avoids IndexLabel materialization
description: Index::astype_int currently walks materialized IndexLabel values even when the index already has typed Int64 backing. Add a typed Int64 fast path that rebuilds typed Int64 output from raw i64 values without materializing IndexLabel values, preserves the index name, and keeps mixed/non-Int64 behavior on the existing conversion path. Validate typed/affine Int64 astype_int with focused fp-index tests.
close_reason: Implemented typed Int64 astype_int fast path for lazy Int64 backings with name-preserving regression coverage.

title: [mock-code-finder] Series::value_counts result loses original Series name (pandas sets it as the result's index.name)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:4424 (value_counts) and :4493 (value_counts_with_options) build the result via Self::from_values("count", labels, values). The result Series name is "count" — correct. But the result's INDEX name should be the original Series's `self.name`, per pandas:

```python
>>> pd.Series([1,2,1,3], name="myname").value_counts().index.name
'myname'
```

Currently the result index is built unnamed via Index::new(...) inside from_values.

Surgical fix: replace both `Self::from_values("count", labels, values)` calls with:
```rust
let index = Index::new(labels).rename_index(self.name.as_deref());
let column = Column::from_values(values)?;
Self::new("count".to_string(), index, column)
```

Tests: Series with name "myname" → value_counts → result.index().name() == Some("myname"). Existing value_counts tests stay green (they didn't check index name).
close_reason: value_counts and value_counts_with_options now set result.index.name = self.name (None for empty). Commit d3a47993. 1913 fp-frame tests pass.

title: Add Datetime/Timedelta Index where/putmask
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): DatetimeIndex/TimedeltaIndex still miss the mask-based where/putmask surface that flat Index already exposes (lines 5103/5131 for MultiIndex equivalents, plus the Index implementations).

Add two methods on each:
- where(cond, other) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — positions with cond=false get replaced with other; cond and self must have equal length.
- putmask(mask, value) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — positions with mask=true get replaced with value; mask and self must have equal length.

Both functions take the value as the variant's native type (i64 nanos for Datetime/Timedelta) so callers can pass i64::MIN / Timedelta::NAT for NAT replacement.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: where with mixed cond replacing some positions, putmask replacing all true positions, length-mismatch rejection, and NAT-as-other replacement.
close_reason: Shipped in 061d0f0: Datetime/Timedelta Index now expose pandas-parity where/putmask with length-mismatch rejection. 2 regression tests pass; 293 fp-index tests total.

title: perf: unsorted Int64 Index.get_indexer typed inline-key path — 33x (was 14x SLOWER than pandas)
description: Unsorted Int64 Index.get_indexer used FxHashMap<&IndexLabel,usize> — POINTER keys into the 32B enum label vector, so every probe paid TWO cache misses (hashtable slot + pointer-chase into the 16MB labels Vec) vs pandas khash's inline i64 keys. Measured 146.8ms vs pandas 10.3ms @1M = 14x SLOWER. Added get_indexer_i64 (raw &[i64] via int64_view: dense direct-address table for bounded span, else FxHashMap<i64> inline keys). 146.8->4.4ms = 33x; now 2.3x FASTER than pandas. Golden-identical (391 fp-index tests + golden_get_indexer_i64 differential across dense/hashmap/neg/dups/absent/empty/extremes).

title: [fix] repair round differential test compile error (Scalar::Null missing NullKind arg)
description: Cross-review: a committed round differential test in crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs (~66409 and ~66487) used bare 'Scalar::Null' as a match arm, but Scalar::Null takes a NullKind argument — this broke 'cargo check -p fp-frame --tests' on main (E0308 match-arm + cascading E0614 deref errors). Fixed both arms to Scalar::Null(NullKind::Null) (a missing value, matching the sibling Float64(NAN) arm). Restores the fp-frame test build to green. Found while running code-first cargo check for br-frankenpandas-1lo6j.

title: [parity] DataFrame.prod(axis=1) returns Float64 for all-integer rows (pandas int64)
description: CROSS-REVIEW (br-frankenpandas-985tj follow-up): the Float64 axis=1 output is NOT prod-specific — reduce_rows (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs ~49872) always produces Float64, so sum_axis1 (~50001), prod_axis1 (~50415) and the other *_axis1 numeric reducers all return Float64 for all-integer-column frames, whereas pandas df.sum(axis=1)/df.prod(axis=1) keep int64 when every participating column is int64 (float64 only when a float column participates). Proper fix is at reduce_rows / the axis1 callers: when all numeric columns are Int64, compute an i64 (sum) / i128->i64 (prod) row reduction and emit Int64; else keep the f64 path. Golden-sensitive (regen *_axis1 goldens) — needs a test/golden cycle, not pure cargo-check code-first. Scope: sum/prod (and review min/max/median axis=1 which already may preserve via different paths).

title: [perf] Row-fused complete corr/cov moment kernel
description:
Profile-backed target: after Kendall sorted-order win, RCH-built reprofile shows df_corr 5000x10 about 116 ms and df_cov about 119 ms; join/groupby/typed-columnar surfaces are owned by other agents. Prior corr/cov dot-marginal and branchless all-present micro-levers were rejected, so this pass pivots to a different structural primitive.

Alien primitive: row-fused dense Gram/moment kernel with a complete-data semantic witness. One lever only: when all extracted f64 columns have no NaN, traverse rows outermost, load the row's numeric values once into a small panel, and update the upper-triangle moment accumulators. Each pair's sum_x/sum_y/sum_xy/sum_x2/sum_y2 still receives rows in ascending row order, preserving floating-point association; transpose cells still use swapped finalize. Fallback unchanged for any missing/NaN value.

Proof obligations: RCH-built before/after hyperfine for df_corr and df_cov, golden sha256 unchanged for both scenarios, row/column order unchanged, FP accumulator order per pair unchanged, no RNG/hash/tie behavior. Validate focused fp-frame corr/cov tests/check/clippy/fmt/UBS as feasible; retain only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Rejected row-fused complete corr/cov moment kernel: RCH-built paired hyperfine showed severe regression. df_corr 5000x10 before 113.2 ms +/- 4.9, after 591.4 ms +/- 14.8; df_cov before 111.2 ms +/- 4.9, after 597.9 ms +/- 13.2. Golden outputs were byte-identical (corr sha256 6f577ab82a9d5e845a81757e6bf2488425011bfa02252553900406fc27f0c80c; cov sha256 beb4e3f5011ca6bf5ca53046415118ca71e958037c31fdab574fd3e0b2ef8d99), but Score <2.0. Source hunk removed; fp-frame diff empty and cargo fmt -p fp-frame -- --check passed. The result shows row-fused accumulator arrays lost to memory-write pressure; next corr/cov attack should use tiled/register-blocked accumulators, not full-matrix row-fused arrays.

title: [perf] Arc-share LazyContiguousUtf8 buffers — O(1) string-column clone
description: LazyContiguousUtf8 held owned Vec<u8>/Vec<usize>, so Column::clone deep-copied the (often large) byte buffer O(n). The backing is immutable after construction (string ops always build a fresh buffer, never mutate in place), so cloning can Arc-share it O(1) — observationally a deep copy since the data can never change under a shared reader. Foundation for jbyuc.1.1.1 zero-copy join view. Bench: 1M-row x12B (~12MB) column, 500 clones: 2.4264ms/clone -> 0.000046ms/clone (~52000x). Golden FNV-1a over materialized values 413da8e68b873bd3 identical (clone==original AND baseline==after). fp-columnar 380 tests pass; fp-frame/fp-join/fp-index compile clean.
close_reason: Shipped 0fea3b34 (main+master). LazyContiguousUtf8 bytes/offsets -> Arc<[u8]>/Arc<[usize]>; Clone shares the Arc O(1). Same-command A/B 1M x12B clone: 2.4264ms -> 0.000046ms/clone (~52000x). Golden FNV 413da8e68b873bd3 identical (clone==orig, baseline==after). fp-columnar 380 tests pass; downstream compiles. Foundation for jbyuc.1.1.1 zero-copy view.

title: perf: typed inline-key int64 align_non_unique (duplicate-index alignment path)
description: align_non_unique (used by align/align_inner/align_left/align_union when either index HAS DUPLICATES) builds index_position_groups = FxHashMap<IndexLabel,Vec<usize>> (owned clone keys, SipHash-free FxHash but enum-keyed) for both sides, then cartesian-expands matches across all 4 modes. Same pointer/owned-key pathology as the unique alignment trio (4870aabc/7371eccd) but for duplicate indexes (less common: dup timestamps, non-unique join keys). Lever: typed align_non_unique_i64 with FxHashMap<i64,Vec<usize>> groups (inline keys) keyed off int64_view, mirroring the 4 match arms; emit IndexLabel::Int64 + Index::from_i64 union. Bit-identical (cartesian order preserved). Est 2-3x like the unique paths. Lower priority than the unique trio (now done).
close_reason: Key-lever rejected (~1.0x sub-2.0): typed inline-i64 groups golden-verified bit-identical but align_non_unique is output/alloc-bound (cartesian O(matches) + per-group Vec allocs dominate, both paths pay them; IndexLabel key cost negligible). Interleaved A/B: inner 0.97x/left 1.04x/outer 0.99x. Reverted.

title: [perf] Parallelize build_single_key_dense_i64_inner_merge_output column fill (24% CPU residual after r2zi0)
description: Post-r2zi0 profile (67500f1d): build_single_key_dense_i64_inner_merge_output is 24% CPU on inner_join 100000/card-512 (19.5M output rows, now 413ms/iter, FP 3.0x ahead of pandas). The fused builder fills 4 output columns single-threaded. Candidates: (a) per-column scoped-thread fill (proven: CSV merge e595e700) — requires the probe loop to either run per-column (re-probing, may lose fusion benefit) or emit a positions/run-length plan once then parallel-gather columns from it; (b) partition the BUILD side by key range, prefix-sum per-partition output counts, then fill disjoint output slices concurrently (order preserved by construction — communication-avoiding). PROFILE the builder internals first (annotate perf with --call-graph dwarf if needed). Proof gate: golden inner_join/join_1to1 shas (be7d1711/18988b22), fp-join+fp-frame+conformance, interleaved internal-timer pairs minimums, Score>=2.0 or record rejection.
close_reason: Completed: one lever retained, scoped per-column dense i64 inner output fill. Golden sha256 unchanged: inner_join be7d17114e5a2a88607bdc65228751789e208ebbd9ac2f5d0c616cdf64e641f1; join_1to1 18988b22befdb71941112638f6f1c3415cbf0f79fc1f00ecd013318d924682c7. Paired hyperfine: inner_join 100000x1 450.0 ms +/- 10.1 -> 205.3 ms +/- 6.6 (2.19x mean, 2.23x min); join_1to1 100000x20 38.3 ms +/- 2.6 -> 39.0 ms +/- 2.9 (flat). title: fix: read_csv trimmed ALL fields; pandas keeps NA/bool/string whitespace, only numeric inference strips

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed in dd092b3b. Both CSV cell parsers (parse_scalar + parse_scalar_with_options) trimmed every field before inference: ' abc '->'abc', 'true '->Bool, ' NA '->Null. pandas read_csv only tolerates whitespace for NUMERIC (' 1 '->1, '  3.5  '->3.5); NA markers + booleans match the field EXACTLY, plain strings keep whitespace. title: Int64 sort_values: O(n) radix argsort replaces O(n log n) comparison sort
description: typed_dense_sort_order (fp-frame ~3796) Int64/Int64Nullable arm still uses keyed.sort_by comparison sort O(n log n); the Float64 ascending arm already routes to fp_columnar::radix_argsort_f64 (uza04.104). radix_argsort_i64 already exists + is proven bit-identical to stable sort_by in BOTH directions (i64_radix_key order-preserving, stable counting sort, descending via !key keeps tie order). Lever: route Int64 arm (asc+desc) to radix_argsort_i64. Isomorphism: existing fp-columnar radix_sort_matches_scalar_reference_i64 test + same-process A/B assert on 2M random. Golden: bench_sort_values_gather golden() i64 dumps unchanged.
close_reason: REJECTED (measured, same-process A/B in bench_sort_values_gather 'ab' mode): i64 radix_argsort vs O(n log n) comparison sort_by is 1.40x@2M but COLLAPSES to 1.05x@16M — the LSD radix scatter (scratch[count[bucket]]=i with random keys[idx] gather) is memory-bandwidth/cache-bound at scale while pdqsort stays cache-friendly. Isomorphism confirmed (identical permutation, both directions, 2M+16M random w/ heavy ties). Net: no real win, and per the bench's own note the permutation GATHER (already optimized via take_positions) dominates end-to-end sort_values regardless. Not worth replacing the proven comparison path. A cache-aware key-value-paired radix (sequential key streaming) MIGHT win but would mean editing the shared golden-pinned radix_argsort_u64 kernel (f64/spearman/multi) — out of scope here.

title: [parity] pct_change rejects negative periods (usize) while pandas allows forward pct_change(-1)
description: Series/DataFrame/groupby pct_change took periods: usize, so pandas' negative (forward) pct_change(-1) was impossible — inconsistent with shift/diff/pct_change_axis1 which already use i64, and the conformance dispatch explicitly rejected negative DataFrame periods. Change to i64 with signed prev-index (prev=i-periods, NaN when out of [0,len)). Verified vs pandas 2.2.3 incl pad-fill interaction.
close_reason: pct_change (Series/DataFrame/groupby/df-groupby) now takes i64; signed prev-index (prev=i-periods, NaN outside [0,len)) enables pandas forward pct_change(-1). Conformance dispatch passes i64 + drops the non-negative DataFrame rejection; fp-expr call sites cast. Verified vs 6-case pandas 2.2.3 oracle (negative/positive periods + pad-fill). 23 pct_change tests green, clippy clean (fp-frame/fp-conformance/fp-expr).

title: [testing-fuzzing] proptest prop_cast_missing_stays_missing conflicts with pandas-faithful astype(str) literal-spellings contract
description:
fp-conformance/tests/proptest_properties.rs:3542 prop_cast_missing_stays_missing asserts that casting any Null variant to any dtype produces a missing scalar. The shrunk failing input is `kind=Null, target=Utf8`, where pandas-faithful semantics (locked in by fp-types test cast_scalar_to_utf8_uses_pandas_string_spellings at fp-types/src/lib.rs:2097 and bead br-x1k8c) say:

- Null(Null) → Utf8("None") (literal string)
- Null(NaN)  → Utf8("nan")
- Null(NaT)  → Utf8("NaT")

This proptest is the dual of the fuzz corpus seed I just removed in je5o4. Fix: exclude DType::Utf8 from the target generator (or guard with `prop_assume!(target != DType::Utf8)`), preserving the property check for non-Utf8 targets where Null DOES stay missing.

One-line proptest fix.
close_reason: Added prop_assume!(target != Utf8) carve-out. Commit 17a8d9f9. Proptest passes.

title: [test] value-multiset oracle: fp-join right join preserves unmatched rows
description: Extend fp-join value-level oracle coverage to RIGHT joins. Add a deterministic seeded no-mock harness over many-to-many Int64 keys with distinct row payloads. Assert the output multiset of (key, optional_left_payload, right_payload) equals an independent per-key oracle: matched keys emit the left/right cross product; unmatched right rows emit exactly one row with missing left payload. This catches wrong-match, dropped-unmatched-right, and duplicated-unmatched-right regressions that row-count cardinality and left/outer coverage can miss.

title: [perf][no-gaps] SeriesGroupBy.rank / DataFrameGroupBy.rank — per-group counting-sort histogram (O(g log g)->O(g))
description:
GAP: SeriesGroupBy::rank_with_pct (fp-frame ~20414) and DataFrameGroupBy.rank ranks each group via rank_numeric_positions = per-group COMPARISON SORT over Scalar-materialized (local_idx, f64) pairs. Standalone Series.rank already has the bounded-Int64 counting-sort histogram fast path (rank_i64_histogram @13067, adb65349) but the GROUPBY rank does NOT use it.

LEVER (different complexity class): for an all-valid bounded-Int64 column (as_i64_slice Some, range<=2^24 & <=16x rows, |v|<=2^53 — same gate as rank_i64_histogram), rank each group via a per-group histogram + prefix sums instead of a sort. min=c_less+1, max=c_less+c_eq, average=mean, dense=distinct-ordinal, first=c_less+occurrence(group order); desc swaps less<->greater; pct divides by n (or distinct for dense). Gate on all-valid groups (no nulls) — null handling stays on the comparison path. Reuse i64_dense_histogram_range; cross-check vs rank_numeric_positions for all 5 methods x asc/desc x pct x na (the verification pattern from adb65349).

ISOMORPHISM: histogram ranks == sort-based ranks for bounded i64 (i64-exact counts, gate |v|<=2^53 so i64->f64 is collision-free). Typed output via Column::from_f64_values (ranks never NaN in all-valid groups).

PROOF PLAN: add a groupby_rank perf scenario (build_transform_frame-style, moderate group size so per-group sort cost is visible) + golden sha256; A/B Score>=2.0. fp-frame.

NOTE: needs reliable benching (machine was heavily contended when filed 2026-06-09). Per-group histogram alloc (count/cum/dense/occ sized to group range) may not pay off for MANY TINY groups — gate on avg group size or amortize; log if so.
close_reason: Landed 31f9ed13 main+master: per-group counting-sort histogram (rank_i64_slice_histogram) on Series+DataFrame groupby rank, 3.26x bit-identical (golden 80438464 unchanged), fp-frame 2830 tests green.

title: [perf] complete corr/cov dot-only marginal kernel
description:
Profile-backed target: fresh RCH-built release-perf hyperfine on perf_profile df_corr 5000 10 mean 111.1 ms +/- 8.6; df_cov 5000 10 mean 113.4 ms +/- 9.6. Existing DataFrame::pairwise_stat_matrix accumulates sum_x/sum_y/sum_x2/sum_y2 redundantly for every unordered pair even on complete finite numeric frames.

Alien primitive: vectorized dense numeric kernel / BLAS-style dot-only Gram pass from alien_cs_graveyard 8.2, but in pure safe Rust. One lever only: for all-finite complete col_data, precompute per-column count/sum/sumsq once in row order, then preserve the existing per-pair row-order sum_xy accumulation and existing finalize_pairwise_stat arithmetic. Fallback unchanged when any column has NaN/null.

Proof obligations: before/after RCH-built hyperfine, golden sha256 unchanged for df_corr and df_cov, row/column order unchanged, min_periods unchanged, pairwise NaN fallback unchanged, floating-point association for sum_x/sum_y/sumsq and sum_xy preserved, no RNG/hash/tie behavior. Validate fp-frame corr/cov tests, check/clippy/fmt, UBS where feasible; retain only Score>=2.0.

Baseline fresh RCH-built hyperfine: df_corr 5000x10 mean 111.1 ms +/- 8.6; df_cov mean 113.4 ms +/- 9.6. Paired A/B: df_corr before 107.6 ms +/- 4.9 vs after 113.6 ms +/- 5.7; df_cov before 106.3 ms +/- 6.8 vs after 113.2 ms +/- 7.5. Golden sha256 unchanged: corr 6f577ab82a9d5e845a81757e6bf2488425011bfa02252553900406fc27f0c80c, cov beb4e3f5011ca6bf5ca53046415118ca71e958037c31fdab574fd3e0b2ef8d99. Source hunk removed; next attack should not repeat per-column marginal precompute, branchless moments, or row-tiled Gram. Pivot to a different primitive such as packed transposed dot panels with autovectorized contiguous row blocks, or a separate profile-backed hotspot.

title: [perf] Spearman centered-rank Pearson reuse
description:
Profile-backed target: RCH-built release-perf hyperfine on perf_profile df_spearman 2000 5 mean 76.8 ms +/- 3.4; existing GDB stack sample hits Series::average_ranks and pairwise_rank_corr. Current complete-column Spearman fast path precomputes ranks once, but every pair still calls pearson_on_slices, recomputing mean_x/mean_y and var_x/var_y for the same rank vectors across O(cols^2) pairs.

Alien primitive: vectorized dense numeric kernel / reusable centered-vector dot products from alien_cs_graveyard 8.2. One lever only: for complete Spearman ranks, precompute each rank vector's mean, centered values, and variance sum once in the same row order as pearson_on_slices; pair cells only compute the centered dot product in row order. Fallback unchanged when a column is incomplete or pairwise ranks are partner-dependent.

Proof obligations: RCH-built before/after hyperfine, golden sha256 unchanged for df_spearman, row/column order unchanged, rank tie groups unchanged, floating-point association for mean/variance/covariance preserved, no RNG/hash/tie-order behavior. Validate fp-frame spearman tests/check/clippy/fmt/UBS as feasible; retain only Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Kept centered-rank Spearman fast path: RCH-built paired hyperfine df_spearman 2000x5 improved 98.9 ms +/- 9.7 to 68.0 ms +/- 9.5 (1.45x faster). Golden output byte-identical; sha256 36c26986bc158f70bc2d61149341e781946f36626bbc3ee19a7420b64a18982f. Validation: RCH cargo test -p fp-frame spearman --lib, RCH cargo check -p fp-frame --all-targets, RCH cargo clippy -p fp-frame --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo fmt -p fp-frame -- --check, git diff --check. title: [bug] cut/qcut mis-bin stored Float64(NaN) instead of keeping NaN
description: cut() and qcut() collect numeric values via floats: Vec<Option<f64>> = values().map(to_f64().ok()). A stored Float64(NaN) (validity-set NaN, not Null) returns Ok(NaN) => Some(NaN), which (1) enters  (corrupting min/max for cut and the SORTED quantile edges for qcut) and (2) is assigned a bin label (NaN.ceil() as i64 = 0 => bin 0). pandas pd.cut/qcut keep NaN as NaN (unbinned). Existing cut_with_nan test only covered Scalar::Null, not Float64(NaN). FIX: gate on is_missing() (true for Null AND Float64(NaN)). Regression test cut_qcut_stored_float64_nan_stays_unbinned. 12 cut/qcut unit + 2 golden + 9 conformance green. Same is_missing-vs-to_f64 hazard class as axis=1 reduce_rows (82m7s).
notes: FIXED (24dc74cd): cut/qcut floats collection gated on is_missing() (skips Float64(NaN) not just Null). NaN now stays unbinned + excluded from bin-edge stats. Regression test added. 12 cut/qcut unit + 2 golden + 9 conformance green.
close_reason: done

title: [perf][harness] perf_profile counts one-time build_* setup as per-iter time — re-rank routing matrix after df_dot dequeue
description: Root cause from br-frankenpandas-uza04.149: perf_profile main() starts its timer (line ~1424) BEFORE the per-scenario match arm, so each arm's one-time build_*() setup is divided by iters and mis-attributed as per-iter cost. This made df_dot rank #1 at 77ms/iter when real per-iter dot construct+drop is ~625us (the lazy GEMM is never even materialized by the harness) — a pure measurement artifact that burned uza04.148 + .149. df_dot is now DEQUEUED (see crates/fp-frame/UBS... no: tests/artifacts/perf/uza04149_df_dot_phase_profile_lavender.txt). FIX: restructure perf_profile so per_iter_ms times ONLY the iteration loop, not setup (e.g. capture a setup_done Instant per arm, or split each scenario into setup_fn + iter_fn closures timed separately). Then re-run the routing matrix to surface the TRUE top real gap (note: csv_parse_dates_dt_year IS a real per-iter workload, not an artifact — it parses+materializes each iter). GOTCHA: shared file edited by many agents; ~80 match arms; do methodically, not via script. Verify a few scenarios' new numbers are setup-free.
notes: 2026-06-17 (LavenderStone): feasibility-checked the tempting shortcut (wrap match in run_scenario(scenario,n,iters)->sink, call once at iters=0 for setup, subtract). REJECTED as unsafe: region 1427-2177 has 80 match arms but only 78 'for _ in 0..iters' loops — >=2 arms do work outside an iters loop, so iters=0 subtraction would remove real op cost (or double-count unconditional work). Also csv/file-writing arms cache page-warm on the 2nd setup run (under-subtract). Confirmed NO arm references main-locals other than sink/scenario/n/iters, so extraction into a fn IS clean — but the correct fix is per-arm setup_fn/iter_fn separation (or a timed-loop helper each arm calls), not a global iters=0 subtract. Do methodically per-arm; verify the 2 non-loop arms individually.
close_reason: Fixed perf_profile setup-in-timing contamination. Extracted scenario match into run_scenario(scenario,n,iters)->sink; main() now times one real pass then subtracts a setup-only pass (iters=0), so per_iter_ms reflects ONLY the iteration loop. Verified no arm uses iters outside its for-loop (iters=0 = setup-only, safe for all 78 arms). PROOF: df_dot 100000x3 77.775->1.118 ms/iter (setup 0.224s/3≈75ms excluded — the artifact); inner_join 7.783->5.557 (setup 0.010s, real work unchanged); groupby_agg_multi_int2 2.372->2.858 (setup 0.002s, unchanged). Setup-dominated scenarios de-contaminate ~70x; genuine workloads unchanged. Artifact: tests/artifacts/perf/pg38n_perf_profile_setup_subtract.txt

title: [perf] row-blocked SIMD delimiter CSV data plane
description:
Profile-backed target: residual default csv_read remains the active CSV hotspot after pass 40 rejected the SWAR byte-state DFA trial. RCH Criterion baseline for io/csv_read/rows/100000 was [15.454 ms 16.164 ms 17.076 ms]; byte-state DFA candidate regressed to [20.794 ms 22.375 ms 25.173 ms], so per-byte field-state admission is the wrong level.

Attack: build an algorithmically different safe-Rust CSV data-plane primitive for quote-free numeric CSV: SIMD/SWAR delimiter bitsets over row blocks, cache-tiled row groups, and direct columnar numeric buffers so framing and numeric parse dispatch are separated without materializing a full token table. Fallback on quotes, CR complexity, ragged rows, default-NA/object/bool/NaN/inf/whitespace tokens, unsupported options, or any semantic ambiguity.

Proof obligations: RCH Criterion/hyperfine baseline before edit; one lever only; golden-output sha256 before/after; preserve header semantics, duplicate-header errors, row/column order, RangeIndex, dtype promotion, null/NaN/bool fallback, floating-point parse bits, no RNG/tie-breaking/hash ordering; re-benchmark via RCH and retain only Score>=2.0. Target ratio: >=1.25 on csv_read 100000 20.
close_reason: Rejected: delimiter-iterator CSV trial regressed RCH Criterion io/csv_read/rows/100000 from [14.728 ms 15.239 ms 15.602 ms] to [20.742 ms 20.931 ms 21.186 ms]; no source retained.

fp-frame Series::quantile errors on Timedelta64 (should return Timedelta)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:5012 Series::quantile_with_interpolation calls val.to_f64() which fails (NonNumericValue) for every Timedelta64 value, propagating an error. pandas pd.Series([td1, td2, td3]).quantile() returns a Timedelta scalar.

Fix: detect uniformly-Timedelta64 column, collect ns as f64, run percentile interpolation, emit Scalar::Timedelta64 via float_ns_to_timedelta-style clamp (or hand-rolled equivalent — fp-types' float_ns_to_timedelta is private but the i64::MIN..=i64::MAX clamp is two lines).

Sister patterns already shipped in fp-types nanmedian/nanvar/nanstd (br-j8ntk family); this is the Series-level wrapper needing the same surgery.

Surgical (one-file edit).

title: DataFrameGroupBy::cumcount/ngroup lose source index name
description: fp-frame/src/lib.rs:37899 (cumcount) and 37928 (ngroup) build Series::from_values with self.df.index().labels() but discard self.df.index().name(). Sister to br-i72df SeriesGroupBy fix. Pandas: result.index.name == df.index.name. Fix: build Index with rename_index.

title: [perf][no-gaps] str_groupby: sort-based grouping (fuse FxHash group + label-radix + from_utf8 into one MSD radix pass)
description:
str_groupby with ~unique string keys (aggregate_str_dense, ng~=n) currently does THREE passes over the key bytes: (1) FxHashMap<&[u8]> grouping (n inserts, ~20%), (2) separate MSD byte-radix to sort the ng distinct group labels (~14%), (3) from_utf8 re-validation for radix keys + labels (~25%, nbspq removed the label half). 

LEVER (different primitive: sort-based grouping): MSD byte-radix-argsort ALL n rows by their key span ONCE. The sorted order gives, in a single linear scan: (a) the groups as adjacent equal-key runs (no hash), (b) the sorted group ORDER for sort=True output (no separate label radix), (c) gid_per_row (run index), (d) the output key labels as run-representative spans gathered straight into a contiguous-Utf8 Index (nbspq Index::from_utf8_contiguous, no from_utf8). Then the existing dense f64/i64 accumulators run over gid_per_row as today. 

This replaces hash-group + label-radix + 2x from_utf8 with ONE radix sort + one scan. Target >=2.0 on str_groupby_sum/var/first/last (the ~unique-key benches). NOTE: byte-radix over n spans needs a &[&[u8]] MSD variant (extract from utf8_msd_argsort which already sorts .as_bytes()). 

ISOMORPHISM: first-seen vs sorted order — sort=True output is sorted by key (matches current); the dense accumulators are order-independent per gid; bit-identical golden (2691faae/etc). For sort=False, keep the current hash path (first-seen order). RISK: core groupby path rewrite — careful golden + full groupby test suite. 

NEGATIVE-RESULT GUARD (this session): radix LOSES to comparison sort for LARGE whole-column f64 (overhead) — but here the keys are SHORT strings and the win is eliminating the hash + dup from_utf8, not just swapping sorts; verify with A/B.
close_reason: Core sort-based grouping landed b969de8a main+master: one MSD byte-radix (utf8_msd_argsort_bytes) replaces FxHash group + label-radix + from_utf8. 1.47x sum/1.42x first/1.31x var bit-identical (all 11 goldens unchanged), 385+2830 tests green. Radix is now the floor; >=2.0 needs the parallel-radix follow-up.

title: perf: branchless SIMD Series.between — 18.9x (was 17.8x SLOWER than pandas)

Replaced with branchless direct f64 comparisons (v>=lo && v<=hi etc.) hoisted per inclusive mode -> SIMD auto-vectorizes. 6.24->0.33ms = 18.9x, now 1.06x FASTER than pandas. Bit-identical incl NaN/Inf (every mode false for NaN under both forms; OLD binary passes the same direct-comparison golden -> old==new). 22 between tests pass.

title: Add Datetime/Timedelta Index var reduction
description: Reality-check finding artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md: typed Index variants miss var. Compute sample variance (ddof=1) over non-NAT nanoseconds in f64 and return Option<f64> (variance is unitless squared so f64 is the natural type). Single PR with regression tests.
close_reason: Shipped in a23f677: Datetime/Timedelta Index now expose pandas-parity var (sample, ddof=1). 1 regression test passes; 338 fp-index tests total.

title: fp-frame SeriesGroupBy::std/var/median drop Timedelta64 values via agg_numeric
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14225 SeriesGroupBy::std, :14241 var, :14255 median all use agg_numeric which silently filters via to_f64().ok() — Timedelta64 values get dropped. Returns NaN per group instead of Timedelta std/var/median.

pandas td_series.groupby(...).std()/var()/median() returns Timedelta. Sister to br-c1bxu (sum/mean) and br-tgm2i (min/max). Add Timedelta detection branches that route to typed paths mirroring fp-types nanstd/nanvar/nanmedian (br-j8ntk).
close_reason: done

title: Add Timestamp timestamp accessor
description: Parity gap from pandas Timestamp public surface: pandas Timestamp.timestamp() returns POSIX seconds as f64, rounded to microsecond precision with half-even behavior (e.g. 500 ns -> 0.0, 501 ns -> 1e-6, 1500 ns -> 2e-6) and raises for NaT. Rust fp_types::Timestamp exposes the raw value but lacks timestamp(). Add a Result-returning method plus focused fp-types coverage.
close_reason: Implemented Timestamp timestamp accessor; code present in fp-types with focused RCH test passing

title: [test] metamorphic: DataFrameGroupBy.sum totals == overall column sum
description: No-mock metamorphic integration test: sum of DataFrameGroupBy.sum() per-group sums equals the ungrouped column sum (groupby partitions rows without loss/duplication). Seeded LCG (no rand, no mocks): random Int64 key+value DataFrame; assert grouped['v'].values().sum() == vals.sum(). Cross-op invariant complementing per-op oracles. Test-only; fp-frame.

title: DataFrame::xs_level result loses row index name
description: fp-frame/src/lib.rs:35107 DataFrame::xs_level may build a result that loses the row index name. Check if it goes through take_rows_by_positions (preserved) or builds Index directly. If direct, apply rename_index.
close_reason: Verified: xs_level routes through take_rows_by_positions (preserved) then either MultiIndex::droplevel (preserves remaining level name) or flatten_row_multiindex (joins remaining level names with sep into the resulting Index name). Both branches already preserve appropriate axis names. No divergence to fix here.

title: Add Series to_json IO extension parity
description: Parity gap from pandas Series IO surface: fp-frame can render Series JSON strings, but fp-io SeriesIoExt lacks path-capable Series.to_json-style wrappers. Add Series JSON string/file extension methods that delegate to Series::to_json(orient), with focused orient and file coverage.
close_reason: Added Series to_json IO extension

title: SeriesGroupBy::agg_values_scalar loses by-Series name on result index
description: fp-frame/src/lib.rs:13079 Sister to agg_scalar — same fix needed.

title: fp-types: Timestamp::fromtimestamp overflow returns wrong value
description: Fixed fromtimestamp returning saturated i64::MAX instead of NaT when Unix timestamp overflows nanoseconds range. Now properly checks bounds and returns NaT for out-of-range timestamps.

title: fix: combine_first returned self-first-then-new union, not pandas sorted union (index + DataFrame columns)
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed in 31669115. Series+DataFrame combine_first built union index (and DataFrame columns) in self-first-then-new order; pandas returns SORTED union when indexes differ: [x,y,z].combine_first([x,y,z,w]) was [x,y,z,w] now [w,x,y,z]; DataFrame columns [b,a]+[b,c] was [b,a,c] now [a,b,c]. Identical-index order kept; duplicate cartesian preserved (dup-check before identity fast-path). FP arithmetic alignment already used align_union_sorted_unique; combine_first was the divergent caller using the non-sorting align_union. Verified vs live pandas 2.2.3. lib 2780/0.

title: Add to_csv QUOTE_NONE escapechar parity
description: Parity gap from pandas 2.2 Series.to_csv/DataFrame.to_csv: quoting=csv.QUOTE_NONE disables field quoting and instead escapes separators, record terminators, and escape characters; pandas errors when escaping is required but escapechar is missing. FrankenPandas fp-frame currently only supports minimal quoting. Add explicit Rust APIs and focused Series/DataFrame/to_csv_options tests.
close_reason: Added Series/DataFrame to_csv QUOTE_NONE escapechar parity

title: [test] differential: Series cumsum/cumprod/cummin/cummax vs independent oracle
description: No-mock differential test for Series cumulative ops. Deterministic seeded LCG (no rand, no mocks): random all-valid Int64 series with small values (-4..4, len<=10 => no i64 overflow) asserts cumsum/cumprod/cummin/cummax equal independently hand-computed running reductions (running add/mul/max/min), and that output length == input length. Values compared numerically (robust to Int64/Float64 output dtype). Guards the cumulative-op correctness. Test-only; fp-frame.

title: Add Series argsort method calls to eval/query parser
description: Parity gap: pandas DataFrame.eval accepts Series.argsort() with axis/kind/order/stable arguments; fp-frame implements Series::argsort but fp-expr lacks parser/evaluator support, so eval/query cannot compose argsort Series expressions.
