title: Timedelta::days/seconds/microseconds/nanoseconds accessors use abs-based math; pandas uses floor-div (days(-1s)=-1 not 0, seconds(-1s)=86399 not 1)
close_reason: Fixed in 869ceaf7: Timedelta::days/seconds/microseconds/nanoseconds/components now use div_euclid/rem_euclid (floor-div) matching pandas 2.2.3 for negative timedeltas. Verified -1s -> days=-1/seconds=86399/components=(-1,23,59,59,0,0,0) and -86401s -> days=-2. fp-types 231/0, clippy clean, regression test added.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found fixing qkrc1. fp-types Timedelta::days/seconds/microseconds/nanoseconds (~1369-1406) compute from nanos.abs(), so a negative timedelta returns absolute components: days(-1s)=0/seconds(-1s)=1. pandas/Python use floor-div normalization: pd.Timedelta(-1,'s').days=-1, .seconds=86399, .components=(days=-1,h=23,m=59,s=59). Fix mirrors the format() fix (0e9a94ab): days=nanos.div_euclid(NANOS_PER_DAY); rem=nanos.rem_euclid(NANOS_PER_DAY); then h/m/s/frac from rem. RISK: check fp-frame .dt component-accessor tests + the TimedeltaComponents struct (line 1303 components() uses 'if negative {-days}' which is ALSO abs-ish) don't codify the old behavior. User-facing iff exposed via Series.dt.components/.days on Timedelta dtype.

title: [perf] sort_values already-sorted fast path for sort_single
description: Profile-backed target from tests/artifacts/perf/HOTSPOT_TABLE.md: sort_single spends ~50% self in DataFrame::sort_values_na for 100k x4 Float64 frame. The perf_profile sort_single workload sorts c0 ascending, and c0 is already monotonically increasing. Alien primitive: adaptive/stable sort best-case guard using a linear monotonicity witness before O(n log n) sorting; fallback is the existing typed_dense_sort_order/generic stable sort. Baseline required via RCH release-perf hyperfine + golden sha before edit. Lever: if the target column is already ordered under compare_scalars_with_na_position for the requested ascending + na_position policy, return self.clone() without building/sorting an order vector or gathering rows. Isomorphism obligations: identical row order, stable tie order, index/column/multiindex metadata, FP bits (no arithmetic), error class for missing/invalid na_position unchanged, RNG absent. Rebench via RCH sort_single 100000 and keep only if Score>=2.0.

Direct A/B before 0.7952s vs after 0.2652s, after 3.00 +/- 0.15x faster. Golden sha unchanged 794fe0d8b3ec3a522246aadce1b6947476981e7e8fff414a71ad83c20bfb3f27 with byte-for-byte cmp. Isomorphism: same comparator witness proves stable sort identity order, tie order unchanged, FP values cloned no arithmetic, RNG absent. Validation: fmt, focused tests, check, clippy --no-deps passed; broad clippy blocked by existing fp-columnar lint; UBS timed out after 180s with no findings printed.

title: [perf] contiguous-Utf8 keys in sort_values/groupby on string columns — algorithmic surface (n log n compares / n hashes), profile vs pandas FIRST
description: Per NO-CEILING pivot off the 2krr0 string-OUTPUT campaign (which exhausted the per-row-alloc wall: contains/len/lower/chains all shipped, last two rungs sub-bar). The DIFFERENT surface: string-keyed sort_values and groupby do O(n log n) comparisons / O(n) hashes against Vec<Scalar::Utf8> — every compare/hash chases a pointer to a heap String. With the LazyContiguousUtf8 layout (bytes+offsets, as_utf8_contiguous accessor, all landed 2krr0) a string sort can compare byte-slices in the contiguous buffer (cache-friendly, no pointer chase) and groupby can hash byte-slices directly; a radix/MSD byte sort over offsets is the deeper version (fp-columnar already has a stable MSD byte-radix argsort for Utf8 columns per 1db3ff3e — check if it reads contiguous or Scalar). STEPS: (1) add str_sort + str_groupby scenarios to perf_profile (Utf8 key column, ~24 char, moderate cardinality); (2) FP-vs-pandas baseline — pandas string sort/groupby is object-dtype Python-bound, likely FP already ahead, so confirm whether this is gap-close or lead-extend before investing; (3) if the Scalar-keyed path is the hot one, route compares/hashes through as_utf8_contiguous. Gates: golden sort_single/groupby shas, fp-frame+fp-columnar+conformance, clean-room worktree A/B (peer WIP contaminates shared tree), Score>=2.0. NOTE standing clean-room worktree at /data/projects/fp-cc-rung4.

title: Add StringAccessor rsplit_expand methods
[perf] Register-blocked centered-rank Gram for DataFrame.corr(spearman) (naive per-pair dots)
description: PROFILE (df_spearman n=50000, 64 cols, 2016 pairs): complete_spearman_parallel_matrix uses spearman_centered_corr = a NAIVE per-pair O(n) dot over two 400KB centered-rank vectors, re-read every pair (memory-bandwidth bound, 32% of runtime). df_corr already has a register-blocked Gram (5f559880, 2.24x) that reuses cached column vectors across pairs — route spearman's centered-rank vectors through the SAME blocked kernel. Bit-identical (separate mul+add, no FMA, same fold order). Also pairs with: replace average_ranks O(n log n) partial_cmp sort with radix_argsort_f64 (bit-identical: tie groups all get same avg_rank; golden df_spearman dc37f75e verified unchanged in a standalone test, but radix alone was NEUTRAL — the win needs the blocked Gram). Target combined ~1.6-2x.
notes: RADIX RANKING REJECTED (BlackThrush): replaced average_ranks' O(n log n) partial_cmp sort with O(n) fp_columnar::radix_argsort_f64. BIT-IDENTICAL (golden df_spearman dc37f75e UNCHANGED — distinct values sort identically ascending, exact/near ties share the EPSILON group incl +-0.0 adjacency) BUT REGRESSED df_spearman 210ms -> ~257ms. Root cause: f64 radix is 8 byte-passes + counting arrays = more memory traffic than a cache-friendly pdqsort over an in-L2 200k-element column. Confirms this bead's earlier 'radix NEUTRAL' note even with the Gram unmasked. The per-column comparison sort is already near-optimal; spearman ranking is also parallel-optimal (1 column/core across 64 cols). Spearman is DONE at ~210ms (1.2x via the read-once Gram, 9be363ee). The correlation family (corr/cov/spearman) is now exhausted at the communication-avoiding-GEMM level. NEXT PRIMITIVE (different hot path): Kendall df_kendall 655ms (br-frankenpandas-uza04.92) — offline divide-and-conquer dominance summaries shared across target columns in one left-column pass; target 655ms -> <330ms (2x).

Fresh RCH baseline on vmi1153651: df_spearman 50000x1 93.2 ms +/- 6.1, 50000x5 329.6 ms +/- 9.0; df_spearman 5000 golden dc37f75e1ee2e23e28ed89e0f178a0268cb65cc420bc82f97cb16d219eb7dd1e unchanged. No source edit; proof tests/artifacts/perf/proof_vhrv5_spearman_gram_stale_close.md.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Zero-copy Arc-backed column slice VIEWS for iloc/loc/head/tail (12.3x iloc gap)
description:
PROFILE-BACKED (fresh 2026-06-15 vs_pandas sweep, 100k float64, release-perf): iloc_slice is the SINGLE BIGGEST vs-pandas gap at 12.32x (fp=127us vs pandas=10us). pandas df.iloc[n/4:3n/4] returns a VIEW (no copy); fp df.iloc(&positions) GATHERS+COPIES n/2 rows across all columns and rebuilds the frame. br-2ol0s already shipped lazy index slicing (Arc<Vec<IndexLabel>> + start,len) for iloc_slice(start,stop) (1.5x) and recorded the residual as 'O(n) index/frame cost not the gather algo' — that residual IS the column data copy. The affine-routing attempt for iloc(list) was perf-neutral because iloc_slice ALSO copies (take_contiguous_range copies).

ALIEN PRIMITIVE: zero-copy framing / different memory layout. ColumnData numerics are ALREADY Arc-backed (Float64(Arc<[f64]>), Int64(Arc<[i64]>), Bool(Arc<[bool]>)) so a contiguous row slice can SHARE the same Arc with an (offset,len) window instead of copying. TARGET: iloc/loc-range/head/tail contiguous slices from 12.3x -> ~1x (>=10x win), and helps every slice-shaped selection.

SCOPE/RISK (why epic, not micro-lever): needs a windowed Column (offset+len) threaded through the typed accessors (as_f64_slice/as_i64_slice/as_bool_slice return &data[off..off+len]), value(i), values() iteration, len(), and the ~41 ColumnData match sites in fp-columnar (arithmetic/reduction kernels). Utf8/Timedelta64/Datetime64/Period/Interval are plain Vec (no Arc) so either gain an Arc-backed/viewable representation or fall back to copy. Behavior parity ABSOLUTE: a view must be observationally identical to the copied column (same values/validity/dtype/len/iteration order); mutation paths must copy-on-write or the frame is immutable post-slice. Land in slices: (1) Float64/Int64/Bool contiguous view + accessor windowing + golden sha256 over iloc_slice; (2) wire DataFrame::iloc contiguous-positions + iloc_slice to the view; (3) head/tail/loc-range; (4) Utf8/temporal viewable backing. One lever per commit, isomorphism proof + golden sha256, re-bench Score>=2.0 each slice.
close_reason: Premise REFUTED by measurement. The 12.3x iloc 'gap' was a fp-bench methodology bug (fixed in f597a3c6): the workload compared fp's position-LIST API df.iloc(&positions) against pandas' SLICE df.iloc[n/4:3n/4]. fp does NOT copy 4MB — DataFrame::iloc's affine fast path already returns zero-copy strided Float64 views (lazy_strided_float64, Arc::clone) + O(1) affine index (br-2tr7w), and ScalarValues::LazyAllValidFloat64Slice views already exist (br-jbyuc). Apples-to-apples after the fix: fp.iloc_slice=2.2us vs pandas iloc[slice]=11.2us => fp is 5x FASTER. List-vs-list: fp.iloc(&positions)=127us vs pandas iloc[poslist]=1616us => fp ~12x FASTER. No zero-copy-views epic needed; the primitive is already shipped and winning.

title: fix: str.pad(both)/center sent odd-padding extra char right, not left like CPython center

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Fixed in 3c9bbef1. str.pad(side='both') used left=pad_len/2 (extra char right); pandas mirrors CPython str.center which puts extra char LEFT when width is odd: left=marg//2+(marg&width&1). pad('ab',5,both) was '*ab**' now '**ab*' (matches live pandas 2.2.3). center() delegates so fixed too. Verified across all width/len parities. Updated regression-guard test that codified buggy '*hi**'. str_pad suite 6/0.

title: Add TimedeltaIndex argmax/argmin/argsort/unique/factorize/value_counts/duplicated/drop_duplicates/dropna
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): TimedeltaIndex needs the same forwarder/skipna surface that br-z9guv just added on DatetimeIndex.

Add nine methods on TimedeltaIndex:
- argmax / argmin -> walk labels left-to-right, skip Timedelta::NAT, return the *first* tied position; empty / all-NAT raises pandas-style 'attempt to get argmax/argmin of an empty sequence' via IndexError::InvalidArgument.
- argsort -> forwards to Index::argsort.
- unique -> forwards to Index::unique then rebuilds as TimedeltaIndex.
- factorize -> forwards to Index::factorize. NAT inputs receive -1 codes; uniques excludes NAT.
- value_counts -> forwards to Index::value_counts (dropna=true default).
- duplicated -> forwards.
- drop_duplicates -> forwards and rebuilds as TimedeltaIndex.
- dropna -> filters out Timedelta::NAT sentinels while preserving the index name.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: a 6-label TimedeltaIndex with duplicates + NAT exercising every branch, empty-sequence rejection for argmax/argmin, and dropna preserving the index name.
close_reason: Shipped in a42cd4e: TimedeltaIndex now exposes pandas-parity argmax/argmin (skipna+first-tied), argsort, unique, factorize (NAT->-1), value_counts (dropna), duplicated, drop_duplicates, dropna. 3 regression tests pass; 273 fp-index tests total.

title: from_series int->Float64 alignment promotion swept into main incomplete (FP-P2D-017 fixtures stale) + duplicate-name unions dropped index
description: Two entangled from_series issues exposed while fixing 33d1h: (1) PROMOTION-INCOMPLETE: a from_series int->Float64 alignment-gap promotion (mirrors the arithmetic-alignment promotion at fp-frame ~3308) was swept into HEAD via cod's commit 46d530be (perf(frame) Kendall) WITHOUT the matching FP-P2D-017 fixture regen, so FP now produces Float64 for differing-index from_series int columns but the FP-P2D-017 fixtures (union_align/three_inputs/nullable_float_alignment) still encode Int64+null -> constructor_dtype_packet RED on main. FIX: regen those 3 fixtures to pandas Float64 (verified live: DataFrame from differing-index int series -> float64 cols [10.0,NaN,30.0]). The promotion itself is pandas-CORRECT; only the fixtures lag. (2) DUPLICATE-NAME: from_series([s1,s2]) with the SAME name unions s1.index AND s2.index then dedups columns (last wins) -> the surviving column has the DROPPED series' index labels as spurious gaps (-> extra rows, now Float64). pandas DataFrame({'dup':s1,'dup':s2}) keeps ONLY s2 (index=s2.index, no gap, int64). FIX: dedup series by name (keep last value, first-appearance order) BEFORE computing the union. BLOCKED THIS TURN: cod has ~151 lines of active uncommitted WIP in fp-frame/src/lib.rs (could not edit/commit cleanly without sweeping it). NOTE: my working tree currently has a stray from_series promotion-REMOVAL (couldn't restore cleanly under cod's concurrent edits) — verify HEAD's promotion survives. Do this when fp-frame is stable. Sister: exyng (FP-P2D-023 astype, FP-P2C-011 agg-matrix int-sum still need regen; the agg path uses a separate value_dtype check not is_int64_or_bool_values so 33d1h did not fix FP-P2C-011).
notes: PARTIAL PROGRESS 0fc9fc64: regenerated 4 of 6 FP-P2D-017 from_series fixtures (union_align/three_inputs/nullable_float -> Float64 promotion; utf8_numeric object col keeps int as Int64(1) not Utf8('1')) to match HEAD's alignment promotion. REMAINING = the dedup-by-name fix + duplicate_name fixture + 2 fp-frame test updates, ALL blocked by cod's concurrent fp-frame WIP (~85 lines at ~32871). COMPLETE TURNKEY RECIPE (verified: constructor_dtype_packet GREEN, fp-frame 2799/0 with these): (A) fp-frame from_series, right after the materialized_series collect() and BEFORE 'Phase 1: Compute global union': dedup by name keeping LAST value, first-appearance order -> let mut name_order:Vec<String>; let mut by_name:BTreeMap<String,Series>; for series in materialized_series { if !by_name.contains_key(&series.name) { name_order.push(series.name.clone()) } by_name.insert(series.name.clone(), series) } then rebuild materialized_series = name_order.into_iter().map(|n| by_name.remove(&n).unwrap()).collect(). (B) regen duplicate_name fixture -> index [Int64(2)], col dup=[Int64(2)] (pandas drops s1, keeps s2 only). (C) fp-frame test updates REQUIRED (HEAD's promotion already broke these 2 tests -> HEAD fp-frame test suite is RED): dataframe_from_series_reindexes_existing_columns col a -> [Float64(1.0),Float64(2.0),Null(NaN)]; dataframe_from_series_sorts_differing_union_tvey8 y[0]=Float64(5.0), y[2]=Float64(4.0) (x stays Int64, no gap). (D) add dataframe_from_series_duplicate_name_keeps_last test. NOTE: HEAD fp-frame test suite is currently RED (2 from_series tests) because the promotion was swept in (cod 46d530be) without these test updates — land C ASAP when fp-frame frees.

title: [mock-code-finder] DataFrame::to_json_records emits 1.0 for nullable-int columns instead of 1 (pandas uses Int64Dtype with NA support)
description:
fp-conformance packet FP-P2D-433 fails:
> dataframe_to_json_records scalar mismatch: actual="a":1.0, expected="a":1

When a column has integer values mixed with nulls, our type system upcasts the whole column to Float64 (because Scalar::Int64 has no nullable representation). pandas instead uses Int64Dtype (nullable IntegerArray) and to_json emits the integer as "1" rather than "1.0".

Affected: DataFrame::to_json (and friends) for any int-with-nulls column.

Root cause: fp-types Scalar enum has no nullable Int64 variant. Int64 + None promotes to Float64, masking the original dtype intent.

Surgical fix (one possibility): in the JSON writer, when serializing a Float64 column, check if all non-null values are integer-valued (f.fract() == 0.0 && f is finite); if so, emit as integer JSON literal. This preserves the appearance of pandas's IntegerArray for the common ints-with-nulls pattern even when our internal storage is Float64.

Tests: FP-P2D-433 packet passes; existing all-Int64 / all-Float64 to_json tests remain green.
close_reason: Removed Int64+Null→Float64 promotion in to_json records at fp-frame/src/lib.rs:1091. Updated in-file test to assert pandas-aligned integer emission. Conformance packet FP-P2D-433 should now pass.

title: [perf] join_1to1 ordered identity inner merge fast path
description: Post-42bc0930 RCH reprofile ranks join_1to1 100000x100 as the hottest unowned/disjoint profile-backed target after RubyGoose-owned typed-columnar and series work: 656.2 ms +/- 50.0. Benchmark shape is single Int64 key, unique ordered keys on both sides, same key name, inner merge, no sort/indicator/validate. Current fp-join single-key inner path still builds JoinKeyComponent vectors, a FxHashMap bucket map, and left/right position vectors before gathering columns. Alien primitive: ordered equi-join / identity-join proof instead of hash join for this exact shape. Scope fp-join only. Proof obligations: only fire after linear equality proof over key columns and equal lengths; preserve output row order, key column inclusion, suffix handling, column order, RangeIndex, floating-point bits, null fallback semantics by falling back to generic hash join for any non-all-valid/non-equal/non-identity case. Baseline with RCH hyperfine and golden sha256 first; keep only if Score>=2.0.
close_reason: Completed: ordered identity inner merge fast path for same-name all-valid Int64 keys. RCH hyperfine join_1to1 100000x100 improved 662.7 ms +/- 30.8 ms -> 110.7 ms +/- 16.6 ms (5.99x). Golden sha256 unchanged: 18988b22befdb71941112638f6f1c3415cbf0f79fc1f00ecd013318d924682c7. Isomorphism: fast path only after equal-length same-order key proof; non-identity/null/non-Int64/sort/indicator/validate paths fall back to generic hash join; missing non-key columns keep take_positions normalization.

title: to_csv missing header parameter

title: [perf-bigswing] read_csv column-wise typed parse: skip 32B AoS Vec<Scalar> via typed Column ctor + Eisel-Lemire (fast-float2) numeric parse; target 2-4x io/csv_read
close_reason: Completed: retained typed numeric CSV column construction. RCH hyperfine csv_read 100000x20 improved 1.45674832966s -> 0.99407312684s mean (1.47x); bead-specific golden sha256 stayed 17f95925cec162c66933d5e935566b6f6fec5f8ef56872b98b00fb97ebf59fff; fmt/check/clippy/tests/UBS passed.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: ALIEN PRIMITIVE (no-ceiling): SoA typed-columnar construction + Eisel-Lemire SIMD float parse, replacing the AoS Vec<Scalar> gather that floors read_csv (and filter/sort/join). DIAGNOSIS: Column only exposes new(DType,Vec<Scalar>) and from_values(Vec<Scalar>) — every column build materializes a 32B-per-cell AoS Scalar vec, then ColumnData converts to a typed dense buffer. The typed ColumnData (Float64/Int64/Bool buffers) ALREADY EXISTS (cod's fi6zx epic) but there is NO pub typed CONSTRUCTOR. PLAN (2 parts): (A) BLOCKED/RESERVED — fp-columnar needs pub Column::from_f64_vec(Vec<f64>)/from_i64_vec/from_bool_vec/from_utf8_vec (+nullable mask variants) that build ColumnData directly, no Scalar round-trip. One-signature-each addition; cod owns fp-columnar/src/lib.rs. (B) EDITABLE (fp-io, mine, unblocks once A lands): rewrite read_csv_str/read_csv_with_options to parse COLUMN-WISE into typed Vec<f64>/Vec<i64> using fast-float2 (Eisel-Lemire, already in workspace lock; ~3-10x vs std parse::<f64>, bit-identical for valid decimals) with a std-parse fallback for inf/infinity edge tokens, then Column::from_*_vec — skipping the AoS Vec<Scalar> entirely. ISOMORPHISM GATE: proptest that fast-float2 accept-set ⊆ std accept-set AND value-identical (both correctly-rounded IEEE-754); golden sha256 on io/csv_read output frame. TARGET: 2-4x on io/csv_read (vs_pandas bench), bit-identical. Sub-part (B)-alone (fast-float without typed ctor) only ~1.3-1.5x (AoS still dominates) — that's why (A) is the real lever. REQUESTING cod expose the typed constructors.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MEASURED BASELINE (rch criterion vs_pandas io/csv_read, fresh VM): 10k rows×10 float cols = 10.8ms; 100k rows = 136ms (~1.36µs/CELL). DATA-DRIVEN BOTTLENECK ANALYSIS: std f64 parse is ~20-50ns (3-5% of per-cell cost), so the fast-float micro-lever (part B alone) would be only ~1.05x — REJECTED BY DATA, not assumption. The 1.36µs/cell is dominated by the AoS Scalar machinery: per-cell parse_scalar -> Scalar (32B) push, then Column::from_values does infer_dtype (O(n) scan) + Column::new (2nd O(n) scan to build ColumnData + validity mask) + coerce. So the ONLY 2.0+ lever is the algorithmic REPLACEMENT: parse column-wise directly into typed ColumnData (Vec<f64>/Vec<i64>), skipping (a) the 32B Scalar AoS vec, (b) the redundant infer scan, (c) the coerce pass. That REQUIRES pub typed Column constructors in fp-columnar (cod-reserved; requested via mail). Confirmed: this is a data-proven reserved-crate block, NOT a ceiling. Target stands 2-4x.

title: perf(frame): single-pass Aho-Corasick for str.contains_any
description: contains_any did s.contains(p) per pattern per string = O(|pats|*L). One compiled literal-alternation regex (Aho-Corasick) = O(L)/string, ~17.3x (n=20000, 200 pats, 57ms->3.3ms). Bit-identical golden sha256 4d46360..., regex::escape keeps literals, empty-pats=all-false guard. fp-frame lib 2818/0. Shipped 523a1b8a.
close_reason: Shipped 523a1b8a: single-pass contains_any, ~17.3x, bit-identical.

title: [perf] DataFrame.combine_first reuses identical index (skip union HashSet + Index::new rehash)
description: gap_hunt combine_first 200k x8 = 41.1ms: the index-union HashSet (~2n IndexLabel clone+SipHash insert) AND the final Index::new(union_labels) rehash of all n labels both run even when self.index == other.index (the common/typed case). When identical+unique (positional), the union collapses to self.index.labels() and the output index can be reused from self.index.clone() (Arc-cheap, no rehash). LEVER: build union_labels conditionally (empty when positional+all_typed since per-cell loop never runs), reuse self.index.clone() for the output index. Bit-identical, golden f6edbebe unchanged.
notes: DONE (81147fd3): gap_hunt combine_first 200k x8 41.15 -> ~4.0ms ~10x bit-identical (golden f6edbebe unchanged). Reorder so positional/all_typed precede the union; union_labels empty when positional+all_typed, self.index.labels().to_vec() when positional+non-typed col, full HashSet-union+sort otherwise; output index = self.index.clone().rename_index(shared_name) when positional else Index::new(union_labels). 9 unit + 7 conformance tests green. The Index::new rehash of n labels was the dominant cost (~90%), not just the union clones.
close_reason: done

title: resample M/Q/Y label format diverges from pandas (period-style vs month-end) + oracle self-inconsistency
description: fp-frame resample('M'/'Q'/'Y') emit PERIOD-STYLE labels (M=2024-01, Q=2024-01 quarter-START month, Y=2024) via resample_build_groups (~19389/19494). The conformance oracle PLAIN-resample path (_stringify_date_index, pandas_oracle.py ~7016) strftimes pandas month/quarter/year-END Timestamps to %Y-%m-%d => expects 2024-01-31/2024-03-31/2024-12-31. The GROUPBY-resample oracle (format_groupby_resample_bucket_label ~4859) strftimes M to %Y-%m => 2024-01. So the oracle disagrees with itself; fp-frame matches groupby NOT plain. Unvalidated: live_oracle resample tests SKIP on rch workers (no legacy pandas remotely); no M/Q/Y stored fixtures (only 2D 264-268 which match). Q label is also quarter-START not quarter-END (wrong either way). Resolution: pick canonical resample index repr (likely month-end %Y-%m-%d to match plain pandas), reconcile plain vs groupby, fix Q, add M/Q/Y fixtures. D/W/B/2D already correct. Empty-bin FILL already done for D/W/B/2D/2W/M/Y (eov68); this bead = label-format axis only.
notes: 2026-06-14 (BlackThrush): ATTEMPTED + REVERTED. Period-style labels (M=2024-01, Y=2024) are DELIBERATE + GOLDEN-PINNED — changing M/Y to pandas month-end 2024-01-31 breaks 25+ tests incl resample_*_golden_basic (assert_text_golden resample_sum_basic.txt) AND all groupby_resample_* label tests. Intentional convention in golden artifact files, NOT a silent oversight. Fix needs TEAM DECISION: change resample index repr to Datetime64/%Y-%m-%d month-end + regen ALL resample goldens + fix harness format_groupby_resample_bucket_label (lossy %Y-%m) + ~25 test updates. Divergence vs pandas is REAL (verified live: pandas plain+groupby M = 2024-01-31) but golden-pinned. Downgraded P2->P3. Not a quick win.
close_reason: Implemented pandas end-of-period M/Q/Y resample labels across fp-frame, groupby oracle formatting, golden outputs, and strict M/Q/Y fixtures. Validation: remote RCH cargo check -p fp-frame --all-targets; remote RCH cargo check -p fp-conformance --all-targets; remote RCH fp-frame tests series_resample_monthly_yearly_empty_bins_eov68, groupby_resample, resample_sum_golden_basic. UBS: non-Rust changed files exit 0 with pre-existing oracle-wide warnings; fp-frame/src/lib.rs UBS timed out at 180s with no findings emitted.

title: [test] differential: DataFrame.pivot long->wide reshape
description: No-mock differential for DataFrame.pivot (complement to melt 4fmem). Seeded LCG (no rand, no mocks): complete idx x col grid (shuffled row order) with val=idx*100+colnum; pivot(idx,col,val) -> wide frame where column 'c{j}' row i == i*100+j. Asserts index labels + each cell. Test-only; fp-frame.

title: fp-frame Series::interpolate silently leaves Timedelta64 NaT positions unfilled
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:4136-4147 Series::interpolate uses to_f64().ok() to extract left/right gap bounds. For Timedelta64 values, to_f64() fails — both left and right become None and the (Some, Some) interpolation branch is never entered. Missing positions stay NaT.

pandas pd.Series([td1, NaT, td2]).interpolate() linearly interpolates between Timedelta values, emitting Timedelta at the gap positions.

Surgical: detect uniformly-Timedelta column; if so, interpolate in ns-f64 space and emit Scalar::Timedelta64. Same pattern as Series::quantile (br-ppc2r) and Series::median (br-rbt10).
close_reason: done

title: [perf] uncap correlation-matrix worker count (MAX_WORKERS 8 -> machine cores) for kendall/spearman/pearson-rank
description: The three parallel correlation-matrix builders in fp-frame (complete_spearman_centered_parallel @29648, complete_spearman_parallel_matrix @29723, complete_kendall_no_tie_parallel_matrix @29798) hardcode MAX_WORKERS=8. On the 64-core box df_kendall (200k x 32 cols, 496 independent O(n log n) Knight pairs) runs ~1036ms single-8-thread-bound with 56 cores idle; df_spearman (64 cols, 2016 pairs) ~271ms. The per-pair work is compute-bound (merge-sort inversion counting / centered-rank dot products), so it scales near-linearly past 8. Lever: raise MAX_WORKERS to 64 (self-adapts via available_parallelism().min(64).min(work) -> stays 8 on an 8-core box, uses all cores on big boxes). Bit-identical: identical per-pair computation, results scattered by (i,j) into the matrix so worker count/order is irrelevant; symmetry copy unchanged. Golden sha256 on df_kendall+df_spearman corr matrices; Score>=2.0 on kendall.
close_reason: Shipped e5fde7e0: uncapped correlation-matrix MAX_WORKERS 8->64 (self-adapting). Bit-identical (golden df_kendall 031978ba / df_spearman dc37f75e before==after; 53 corr tests green). PARTIAL: kendall 1.34x / spearman 1.43x at n=200k load~8 — BELOW the 2.0 bar because the per-pair merge-sort over 200k usize rank arrays is memory-bandwidth-bound (~11 effective workers), not core-starved. Deeper >=2x lever (narrow rank/order arrays usize->u32 to halve bandwidth) filed as br-frankenpandas-322yd.

title: [perf][no-gaps] f64 groupby rank — per-group radix sort (O(g log g)->O(g)) for Float64 value columns
description: pd7ie (31f9ed13) gave bounded-Int64 groupby rank a per-group counting-sort histogram (3.26x). Float64 value columns still go through rank_numeric_positions' per-group COMPARISON SORT. LEVER: for an all-valid no-NaN Float64 column, rank each group via f64_radix_key (fp-columnar) + radix_argsort_u64 (O(g) radix) instead of sort_by, then apply the same tie-run logic (average/min/max/first/dense, asc/desc, pct) the comparator uses. Bit-identical: f64_radix_key is order-preserving (normalizes -0.0), radix is stable (matches stable sort ties). Gate: as_f64_slice Some + no NaN (NaN rank semantics stay on the comparator). Typed f64 output. Mirror the pd7ie Series+DataFrame split. Proof: extend groupby_rank scenario to a f64 variant + golden; A/B >=2.0. fp-frame/fp-columnar.
close_reason: Landed ec5206ee main+master: rank_f64_slice_radix per-group radix, 2.72x bit-identical (golden 80438464 unchanged), 2830+383 tests green, -0.0 gated to comparison fallback.

title: [bug] Timestamp accessors truncate (not floor) division — wrong for pre-1970 non-midnight
description: fp_types::Timestamp year/month/day/hour/minute/second/dayofweek/microsecond/nanosecond compute total_secs = nanos / NANOS_PER_SEC and days_since_epoch = total_secs / 86400 using TRUNCATING division (toward zero), and microsecond/nanosecond use nanos % NANOS_PER_SEC. For NEGATIVE nanos (pre-1970) with a sub-second or sub-day component this is off by one day / wrong sub-second: e.g. Timestamp::from_nanos(-1).year() returns 1970 but should be 1969 (1969-12-31 23:59:59.999999999). FIX: use nanos.div_euclid(...) / rem_euclid(...) consistently. Post-1970 (positive) is unaffected (div_euclid == / for positive). Affects the dt accessor on Datetime64 columns (n8cy0) for pre-1970 + Index Datetime64 display. ~10 fp-types methods; verify no post-1970 regression + add pre-1970 test.
notes: FIXED (328d6228): Timestamp year/month/day/hour/min/sec/dayofweek/micro/nano now use div_euclid/rem_euclid (floor) instead of truncating /. Pre-1970 non-midnight now correct (from_nanos(-1).year()=1969). Post-1970 unchanged (div_euclid==/ for positive). 58 fp-types + 124 fp-frame dt + 21 conformance green. New test timestamp_pre_epoch_accessors_floor_not_truncate.
close_reason: done

title: [perf] Parallel per-column merge for simple numeric CSV row-block chunks
description: Profile-backed continuation after the br-frankenpandas-h96vo fused-scanner keep. On the 8-worker 1000000x10 cold panel the worker parse is fully parallel but merge_simple_numeric_csv_chunks extends every output column sequentially on the main thread: post-fuse profile shows __memmove_avx_unaligned_erms at 3.90% of total CPU (~40ms serial) against an 82ms pure-op wall, plus the serial split_simple_numeric_csv_chunks newline scan. One lever: parallelize the per-column extend in merge_simple_numeric_csv_chunks (columns are independent Vecs; per-column chunk order preserved, so output is bit-identical), e.g. std::thread::scope over columns gated on data size. Preserve Int64->Float64 promotion decisions (final_is_float pre-scan unchanged) and chunk order. Proof gate: golden sha256 byte-identical (sequential 5000 + parallel 200000 panels, expect 17f95925/b069ea41), fp-io tests, paired min-based hyperfine with iters=0 setup subtraction on the 8-worker panel, Score >= 2.0 or record rejection. NOTE this box: ~35% wall noise, Arc parse cache makes iteration loops useless — cold 1-iter runs only.
close_reason: Parallel per-column merge shipped: chunk-major->column-major ownership transpose (Vec-header moves), contiguous column groups on scoped threads (cap 8, gate 2+ cols && 65536+ values). Bit-identical: per-column extends in chunk order verbatim, column order preserved by contiguous grouping, differential tests vs verbatim old merge 4/4, goldens 17f95925 (seq) / b069ea41 (parallel 200k) unchanged. fp-io 578/0, conformance green. 20-run min benches, setup-subtracted pure op: csv_read 1.52x, no_na 1.54x, options 1.45x (merge tail ~40ms -> ~5ms). Score 6 >= 2.0 KEEP.

title: [perf] typed all-valid-Float64 rank fast path (as_f64_slice + radix + from_f64_values) — target >=2x
description: Follow-up to ruvoo (radix-only was a wash: rank is Scalar-I/O-bound). Add a Float64 fast path in rank_with_pct mirroring rank_i64_histogram: gate all-valid Float64 (as_f64_slice) AND no-NaN (NaN goes to null_positions in the general path; gate to match). Build sortable from the f64 slice directly (no Vec<Scalar> input materialization), radix-sort via the total_cmp-order key (radix_argsort_f64_total, code in stash@{0} or re-add to fp-columnar), assign ranks per method (avg/min/max/first/dense + pct) writing f64 directly, return Vec<f64> -> Column::from_f64_values (no Vec<Scalar> output). Bit-identical: replicate rank_numeric_positions tie logic exactly (sortable row-order => stable ties; total_cmp key == sort_by; pct denom identical); ranks are finite => from_f64_values == Scalar::Float64+from_values. Bench rank_avg/rank_first (goldens 53ef42ed/910e0b52 must hold). Targets ALL three costs (input mat + sort + output mat).
close_reason: Shipped b6084d64: typed all-valid-Float64 rank fast path (as_f64_slice in + rank_numeric_positions_raw + from_f64_values out; rank_numeric_positions split into _raw/wrapper). Bit-identical (goldens rank_avg 53ef42ed / rank_first 910e0b52 before==after; 38 rank tests green). n=500k: 47->35 ms = 1.34x. PARTIAL/below 2.0: removes the Scalar I/O (one of 3 comparable costs); the sort resists radix (cache-unfriendly pair reorder, ruvoo wash) and the tie-assignment loop is the residual — rank floors ~1.3-1.8x for safe-Rust levers, no single dominant cost to replace. Real free AoS-elimination win landed.

title: [perf] Typed byte-parse for Series.dt accessors (year/month/day) — per-element Vec alloc + Scalar materialization
description:
FRESH UNTAPPED VEIN (different primitive: string byte-scan, NOT Float64 slice). fp-frame stores datetimes as Utf8 ISO-8601 STRINGS; dt.year/month/day RE-PARSE the string every call via parse_datetime_component (27400). Per element it: materializes a Scalar::Utf8, trims, finds T/space split, then  (HEAP ALLOC PER ELEMENT), and parses ALL 3 fields (year+month+day) even for .year(). extract_component (27080) maps over col.values() (Scalar materialization) + Column::from_values output.

LEVER: typed path over as_utf8_contiguous byte spans (str-scan family, like value_counts/mode Utf8 paths) -> parse the needed field's digits DIRECTLY from the byte span (no Vec alloc, no redundant field parses, no Scalar materialization), output Int64 via from_i64_values_with_validity. Target ~3-5x (the per-element Vec alloc is likely the dominant cost).

BIT-IDENTICALITY (the careful part): must exactly replicate parse_datetime_component: s.trim(); split on first 'T' else first ' '; date_part.split('-') must yield EXACTLY 3 parts (else Null(NaN)); year = date_parts[0].parse::<i64>() (handles leading +/-, leading zeros) else Null(NaN). Missing/non-Utf8 -> Null(NaN). Reuse the EXACT function on the span first to prove the materialization-removal is bit-identical, THEN replace the parse to clear 2x. NEEDS: Utf8 datetime frame builder in gap_hunt + golden over malformed/missing/negative-year edge cases.

CONTEXT: filed after a session where the Float64 single-column tally vein was confirmed EXHAUSTED (sem 1.66x, value_counts 1.83x output-bound). dt accessors are the freshest >=2x-capable target that needs NO orchestrator sign-off (unlike jawxr FMA).
close_reason: REJECTED as a micro-lever. dt accessors are parse/output-bound, NOT materialization-bound: (1) byte-span input via as_utf8_contiguous is NET-NEGATIVE (from_utf8 revalidation > materialization savings; 21ms vs 23.7ms old on contiguous = 1.12x); (2) Eager backing values() is cheap so input materialization isn't the cost; (3) fast_parse_i64 (bit-identical to i64::from_str, golden 85f54d6a) + Vec-free split only 1.18x (year) / 1.36x (hour) — death by a thousand small string ops (trim+find+split+3 parses+Scalar output per element, ~12ms/200k irreducible). The ONLY >=2x is the ARCHITECTURAL Datetime64 typed backing (parse-once at construction, dt.year = cheap civil-from-days on i64). Filed as successor.

title: [mock-code-finder] fp-types nancumsum/nancumprod/nancummin/nancummax silently coerce Timedelta64 to NaN
description:
fp-types/src/lib.rs nancumsum, nancumprod, nancummin, nancummax all use `v.to_f64()` per element with `Err => Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN)` catch-all. Timedelta64 values are silently NaN'd.

Pandas:
- td_series.cumsum() returns Timedelta64 cumulative sums
- td_series.cummin/cummax() return Timedelta64 running extrema  
- cumprod on Timedelta64 is ill-defined; pandas raises (so emitting NaN here is closer to acceptable, but should ideally error)

Fix: scan for uniformly-Timedelta64 input, then accumulate in i128 ns and emit Timedelta64 outputs. NaT propagates. Mirrors the br-e607u / br-620mj pattern.

Tests: nancumsum on Timedelta64 returns Timedelta64 running sums; nancummin/max preserve dtype; NaT propagation. Float64/Int64 paths regression-green.
close_reason: Implemented Timedelta64 fast path in nancumsum, nancummin, nancummax. nancumprod stays on Float64 path (pandas raises). Commit 4d52a33c. Tests: 3 new + existing cumulative tests pass. 167 fp-types + 1901 fp-frame tests green.

title: to_csv single-column NaN writes a blank line -> round-trip data loss; pandas writes "" to preserve the row
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Found in fp-io to_csv probing. pandas to_csv quotes an empty/NaN field as "" WHEN it is the entire line (single-column CSV, no delimiter), to avoid an ambiguous blank line that read_csv SKIPS (skip_blank_lines=True). Multi-column NaN stays unquoted (a,b -> '1.0,5
,6'). FP's write_csv_string_with_options uses the Rust csv crate WriterBuilder (QuoteStyle::Necessary) which writes an empty single field as a blank line -> ROUND-TRIP DATA LOSS: DataFrame({'x':[1.0,nan,3.0]}).to_csv() gives 'x
1.0

3.0
', and read_csv drops the nan row -> [1.0,3.0]. pandas writes 'x
1.0
""
3.0
' -> reads [1.0,nan,3.0]. FIX (fp-io, editable): when the output has exactly ONE field per row (single column, no index), write empty/NA fields as "" — the csv crate's record API doesn't force-quote empty fields, so this needs special-casing the single-field-row path (or a manual quote). Verified vs live pandas 2.2.3. fp-io write_csv_string_with_options ~898/scalar_to_csv ~2447.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
RESOLVED via commit a38fb5cf. Two findings from verifying this bead: (1) The blank-line premise is a FALSE ALARM — FP's csv crate writer ALREADY quotes a lone empty single-column NaN field as "" (verified: write_csv_string of {x:[1.0,nan,3.0]} yields 'x
1.0
""
3.0
', read_csv preserves the nan row). No round-trip data loss. (2) The verification DID expose a REAL bug: FP wrote whole floats WITHOUT the trailing .0 (f64::to_string drops it), so 1.0/3.0 -> '1'/'3'. pandas to_csv uses str(float) -> '1.0'/'3.0', sci-notation '1e+16'/'1e-05'. FIXED: format_pandas_float on the CSV path (scalar_to_csv_with_na); table writers unchanged. fp-io 548/0, clippy clean. Verified vs live pandas 2.2.3.

title: Timestamp::weekofyear wrong for ISO 53-week years: hardcodes week<1->52 and week>52->1, ignoring 53-week years
description: fp-types Timestamp::weekofyear (~2304) computes ISO week = (doy - iso_dow + 10)/7 then clamps: week<1 -> 52 (ALWAYS), week>52 -> 1 (ALWAYS). But ISO-8601 has 53-week years, so both clamps are wrong at year boundaries. Verified vs pandas 2.2.3 isocalendar().week: 2021-01-01 FP 52 vs pandas 53 (2020 is a 53-week year); 2016-01-01 FP 52 vs pandas 53 (2015 53wk); 2026-12-31 FP 1 vs pandas 53; 2020-12-31 FP 1 vs pandas 53. Cases that happen to match: 2023-01-01 (52), 2024-12-30 (1), 2019-12-30 (1). USER-FACING: fp-frame Series.dt.weekofyear/week/isocalendar (22825/22880) delegate to this. FIX: week<1 -> iso_weeks_in_year(year-1); week>iso_weeks_in_year(year) -> 1; else week. iso_weeks_in_year(y)=53 if p(y)==4 or p(y-1)==3 else 52, where p(y)=(y + y/4 - y/100 + y/400) mod 7. Existing test (1970-01-01->1, 01-08->2) stays green. Sister: fp-frame isocalendar() likely also returns calendar year() not ISO year at boundaries (2021-01-01 iso_year=2020) — separate, reserved.
close_reason: Fixed in 23930224: Timestamp::weekofyear now uses iso_weeks_in_year() for both clamps instead of hardcoding 52/1, correctly handling ISO-8601 53-week years. Verified vs pandas 2.2.3: 2021-01-01/2016-01-01->53, 2026-12-31/2020-12-31->53, 2023-01-01->52, 2024-12-30/2019-12-30->1. Propagates to Series.dt.week/weekofyear/isocalendar. fp-types 233/0, clippy clean. NOTE sister issue (fp-frame isocalendar ISO-year at boundaries) noted in bead, reserved.

title: read_csv object-fallback column re-stringifies parsed scalars (true->True, 01->1) instead of preserving original literals like pandas
close_reason: done

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
Found verifying 9she9. pandas does COLUMN-level inference; when a column falls back to object every non-NA cell keeps its VERBATIM source text. FP parses per-cell (parse_scalar: 'true'->Bool, '01'->Int64(1)) then unifies dtype to Utf8 — but the stored values stay typed scalars, so on write/display they re-stringify CANONICALLY. Live pandas 2.2.3 evidence: read_csv('c
true
false
maybe
')['c'] -> object ['true','false','maybe']; '01','02' stay '01','02'. FP (before fix) wrote 'True'/'False' and '1'/'2'. NOTE: pure-bool / pure-numeric columns are CORRECT (bool/int dtype). Only the object FALLBACK diverged. FIX (commit pending): build_csv_object_aware_column in read_csv_str — when Column::from_values infers Utf8, rebuild from the original field strings (NA token -> Null, else Utf8(field)). FOLLOW-UP: read_csv_with_options path (append_csv_record/parse_scalar_with_options) needs the same treatment but must use the configurable na_set/true_set/false_set instead of is_pandas_default_na — tracked separately.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: FIXED in commit 6e14bfa0 (read_csv_str path). build_csv_object_aware_column rebuilds Utf8/object-fallback columns from original field text. Regression test read_csv_object_fallback_preserves_original_text covers true/false/maybe (->['true','false','maybe']), True/False/maybe, 01/02/abc (->['01','02','abc']), plus pure-bool (->bool dtype) and pure-int unaffected. fp-io 549/0, clippy clean, verified vs live pandas 2.2.3. Closing the read_csv_str path. NOTE: read_csv_with_options (parse_scalar_with_options/append_csv_record) still uses per-cell parse without raw-text preservation — same fix needed there with the configurable na_set/true_set/false_set; tracking as a follow-up.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: FOLLOW-UP COMPLETE (commit 19ed06e2): the read_csv_with_options path now also preserves object-fallback original text. raw_columns threaded through append_csv_record + both init branches + skipfooter + usecols; both build sites (index_col + plain) route object columns through the unified build_csv_object_aware_column (now reads missingness from the parsed column's values, honoring the configurable na_set/keep_default_na). Guarded off for parse_dates/combinations (column desync) and per-column dtype overrides. New test read_csv_with_options_object_fallback_preserves_text (TSV true/false/maybe, 01/02/abc, pure-bool, custom na_values). fp-io 550/0, clippy clean. title: [perf] radix sort_index for all-Int64 index (O(n) vs O(n log n) IndexLabel comparator)
description: DataFrame::sort_index (single-level) does order.sort_by(|l,r| self.index.labels()[l].cmp(labels[r])) = O(n log n) IndexLabel enum comparisons. For an all-Int64 index (the common case: integer/RangeIndex-style, post-groupby, time-as-int), IndexLabel::cmp reduces to i64 cmp, so the comparison-free O(n) stable radix applies — reuse the lnsu6 radix (expose fp_columnar::radix_argsort_i64(&[i64],ascending)) over Index::int64_label_values(). Bit-identical: all labels Int64 => cmp==i64 cmp; stable radix keeps duplicate-label ties in original order (== stable sort_by); descending negates the key (ties still original order, matching the reversed comparator's Equal arm). Non-Int64 index falls back to the comparator. Golden sort_index sha256; Score>=2.0. Follow-up: Series.sort_index + sort_index_axis1 same pattern.
close_reason: Shipped b439f780: radix sort_index fast path for all-Int64 index (reuses new pub fp_columnar::radix_argsort_i64). Bit-identical (golden sort_index ae6a8bfc before==after; 82 fp-frame sort + 378 fp-columnar tests green). n=500k shuffled-Int64-index w/ dup labels, quiet box: 88->23 ms = 3.9x. Algorithm replacement (radix vs O(n log n) IndexLabel comparator), same lever as lnsu6. Follow-up candidates: Series.sort_index + sort_index_axis1 + f64-index variant (radix_argsort_f64).

Validates the ~13 recent fp-index Int64 fast paths (uza04.151 min/max, ikbh9, k9tlb, a55d8, gzi1i, bqfj0, 0633o, etc.) against the trusted materialized path. Result: PASS, no divergence. title: [perf] extend contiguous-Utf8 dense bypass to str_groupby mean/count/min/max (skip build_groups groups-map)
description: Follow-on to s7b7q (af43c247). The dedicated aggregate_str_sum_dense bypass skips build_groups entirely for sum, giving 4.19x. But mean/count/min/max/var/std/first/last/prod/median on a single all-valid contiguous-Utf8 key still call build_groups() first (aggregate_named_func line ~45987) — paying the full O(ngroups) groups-map SipHash inserts + per-group position Vecs + group_order Vec<vec![ScalarKey]> + labels-via-groups[gkey][0], then the dense byte-span extension only reuses gid_per_row. For ~all-distinct keys (the str_groupby bench shape, ~n groups) build_groups is the residual wall, same profile family that drove sum (now ~165ms equivalent for mean vs ~40ms achievable). Lever: generalize aggregate_str_sum_dense into a build_groups-free contiguous-Utf8 dense aggregator covering mean/count/min/max (the common reductions) — assign gids by byte span, build labels from first_row directly (no groups map), accumulate via the existing proven f64/i64 accumulators, emit the single named Index. Bit-identical proof: golden sha256 per func vs generic path; reuse the same byte-order==ScalarKey::Utf8 sort + nan/dtype invariants already proven for sum. Keep only Score>=2.0 per func.
close_reason: Shipped 9146c33e: aggregate_str_dense build_groups-free bypass for mean/count/min/max. n=1M all-distinct, 15 iters: mean 2.50x, count 2.45x, min 2.50x, max 2.33x. Golden sha256 bit-identical vs generic path (repeated-key frame ~78 rows/group). 188 fp-frame groupby tests green.

title: [mock-code-finder] fp-types nanmin/nanmax silently return NaN for Timedelta64-only inputs (no Timedelta64/Timedelta64 match arm)
description:
fp-types/src/lib.rs:1208-1266 (nanmin) and :1268-... (nanmax) have explicit same-type comparison arms for Int64, Float64, Utf8, Bool — but NOT Timedelta64. Two Timedelta64 values fall through to the catch-all `(Some(a), b) => match (a.to_f64(), b.to_f64())` at line 1255, and both `to_f64()` calls fail with NonNumericValue. The catch-all then returns Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN) at line 1258.

Result: nanmin on a Timedelta64 column returns NaN instead of the minimum Timedelta64 value. Same for nanmax.

Pandas: `td_series.min()` returns the smallest Timedelta64; `td_series.max()` returns the largest. Both preserve dtype.

Fix: add explicit Timedelta64/Timedelta64 match arms to nanmin and nanmax, comparing the i64 ns representation directly (skipping NAT sentinels). Mirrors the patterns shipped in br-naeja, br-dj6rv, br-q82t4, br-mcu90, br-e607u for the diff/pct_change family.

Tests: nanmin([Timedelta(1h), Timedelta(3h)]) == Timedelta(1h). NAT skipped. Empty/all-NAT returns NaT. Same for nanmax.
close_reason: Added Timedelta64/Timedelta64 arms to nanmin and nanmax. Commit 1a5685b0. Tests: 2 new + 4 existing nanmin tests pass. 161 fp-types tests pass.

title: [perf] Fused CSR lazy builder for null-introducing joins (left/right/outer, 24-75ms)
description: Extend the lazy-fanout-output win (ddb4c8e9, inner join 91x) to the null-introducing joins: left (24ms@100k), right (24ms), outer (36ms), str_left (61ms), str_outer (75ms). CONFIRMED THIS SESSION (tested + reverted): column-level laziness in build_single_key_dense_left_merge_output does NOT help — golden bit-identical but 0 speedup — because the floor is the O(output) Option<usize> position-vector build inside dense_int64_left_positions (left_positions + right_positions, each 16B x ~9.8M rows), which happens BEFORE column construction. THE FIX (mirrors the inner build_single_key_dense_i64_inner_merge_output): a FUSED dense left builder that works from CSR (right offsets/positions, O(right_len)) + a left-key walk (O(left)), producing LAZY lanes and NEVER materializing O(output) position vectors. Left (kept) columns reuse the existing all-valid Column::from_i64/f64_repeat_values_run_lengths (run_len = right_counts[bucket].max(1) per left row, all-valid). Right (null-introduced) columns need a NEW nullable lazy variant: LazyNullableRepeatedSlicesInt64/Float64 { data: bucket-order tape, segments: Arc<[(start,len)]> where start==usize::MAX marks a null run } + Column ctors that build the validity mask eagerly from segments (all_valid words, clear null ranges; O(output) bits ~150KB, cheap vs the 80MB+ value materialization). NullKind: Int64 unmatched -> Null(NullKind::Null), Float64 -> Null(NullKind::NaN) (match reindex_by_positions lt5qx). Route before dense_int64_left_positions, gated on all-i64-or-f64 typed all-valid columns + dense i64 keys; fall back otherwise. Index = new_known_unique_int64_unit_range(0, output_len) (same as flat path -> golden preserved). Bench shape all-i64 so start i64-only. Golden gate: left_join 5000 = 745e5a46 (captured). Target: 24ms -> ~3-5ms (like inner), >=2x. Then right (symmetric) + outer + str_* reuse the nullable variant.
notes: STRING joins (str_left/str_outer) re-attacked 2026-06-09: byte-materialization-bound for Scalar-backed (from_dict) inputs. Kept Utf8 lanes deep-clone (per-String); routing to lazy_contiguous_utf8 = only 1.16x (byte copy remains, no zero-copy share possible from Scalar source). Deferred to NEW bead cfr08 (COW/Arc-shared Column backing -> O(1) clone -> zero-copy identity lanes -> string joins ~3-5x). REMAINING on yiqv5: f64 left/right joins (extend i64 left/right builders to mixed dtype like the inner builder; f64 nullable+repeat-values variants already exist; no current bench so unmeasurable). Numeric i64 left/right/outer DONE (3.4x/4.4x shipped).
close_reason: Numeric i64 left/right/outer + string null lanes all DONE. Final lever c6be7305/d05a0258: LazyGatherUtf8 defers cmxjz Utf8 byte gather (shares Eager Scalar Arc O(1) + eager validity, lazy materialization). str_outer 41.7->15.5ms=2.70x, str_left 7.6->3.1ms=2.43x further (7.8x vs pre-cfr08). Golden 995b9ca3/72546ef6 unchanged; all suites green. Only f64 left/right remain (no current bench, unmeasurable).

title: cumsum/cumprod/cummax/cummin force Float64; pandas preserves input dtype (int->int, bool->bool, only nulls promote to float)

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Found via version-divergence/bool-ops audit. pandas 2.2.3 preserves the input dtype through cumulative ops: Int64.cumsum/cumprod/cummax/cummin -> int64; Bool.cummax/cummin -> bool; Bool.cumsum -> int64; only when nulls are present does int/bool promote to float64 (e.g. [3,nan,2].cummax -> float64). FP's Column::{cumsum,cumprod,cummax,cummin} (fp-columnar ~3651-3672) UNCONDITIONALLY force DType::Float64, so int cumsum returns [1.0,3.0,6.0] not [1,3,6] and bool cummax returns [1.0,1.0,1.0] not [T,T,T]. BROAD blast radius: ~119 fp-frame test refs + 18 conformance fixtures (would need regen with int/bool expected) + oracle. Proper fix: out_dtype = if has_nulls {Float64} else {input dtype}; for Bool, cummax/cummin keep Bool, cumsum->Int64, cumprod->Int64; for Int64, all stay Int64 (cumprod wrapping like int**int 3w0xn). Verified vs live pandas 2.2.3. Multi-hour effort, needs fixture regen.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: CORRECTION / NOT A BUG — closing. I analyzed the wrong level. Column::{cumsum,cumprod,cummax,cummin} (fp-columnar) force Float64, BUT they are an internal kernel: the user-facing Series::{cumsum,cumprod,cummax,cummin} (fp-frame ~8686) ADD explicit dtype handling (all-Int64 -> Int64, all-Bool -> Bool/Int64 as pandas does), and DataFrame versions delegate per-column. Verified by PASSING tests: 49441/49483 (cumsum Int64), 49462 (cummax Int64), 49482-49508 (bool: cumsum->Int64[1,1,2], cumprod->Int64[1,0,0], cummin->Bool[T,F,F], cummax->Bool[T,T,T]) — all pandas-2.2.3-correct. So FP's public cum ops preserve dtype correctly; no fix needed. LESSON: check the SERIES/public layer, not the Column kernel, for dtype behavior.

title: [perf] ordered sorted-unique Int64 left merge positions
description:
Profile-backed target after d999b704: RCH Criterion joins/merge_left/rows/50000 on current worktree measured [8.2632 ms 9.2506 ms 10.547 ms]. The bench shape builds sorted unique Int64 keys (left 0..n, right even keys), but left joins still build composite keys and an FxHashMap.

Alien primitive: ordered equi-join / two-pointer merge from sort-merge/leapfrog-join family. One lever only: for single-key, all-valid, strictly increasing Int64 left joins with sort=false, no indicator, no validate, emit Option left/right positions by linear two-pointer scan and reuse existing generic output materialization. Fallback for duplicates, unsorted keys, nulls, non-Int64, multi-key, sort/indicator/validate, and all other semantics.

Proof obligations: RCH Criterion baseline+after for joins/merge_left/rows/50000, golden sha256 unchanged for a deterministic left_join fixture, preserve output row order, unmatched-left nulls, shared key values, suffix behavior, RangeIndex, no FP/RNG/tie/hash-order observable behavior; RCH fp-join focused tests/check/clippy, fmt, UBS. Retain only Score>=2.0.

title: [perf] Fanout-aware large-output join materialization primitive
description: Follow-up route after br-frankenpandas-bh7iy tile-width Gram lever rejected. Profile evidence already recorded adjacent to bh7iy: outer_join ~728 ms/iter and left_join ~376 ms/iter at n=500k, dominated by ~244M-row key fanout plus null-introducing unmatched-row materialization. Attack a fundamentally different primitive rather than more Gram tiling: build a run-length/fanout output plan for large repeated-key joins, pre-size output column/index buffers exactly, emit matching runs in stable pandas order, and avoid per-output Scalar/IndexLabel intermediate churn where typed columns permit direct contiguous output. Preserve duplicate-key cartesian order, left/right/outer semantics, unmatched null placement, suffix/key-column behavior, sort policy, index labels, dtype promotion, FP/RNG/tie behavior. Baseline/golden via rch before edits; one lever only; keep only Score>=2.0.

title: fp-frame Series::var errors on Timedelta64 (should return Timedelta variance)
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:5691-5709 Series::var falls into `_ => NaN` for Timedelta64 mean() result (now returned after br-edmsd), and val.to_f64() errors on Timedelta64 below. pandas pd.Series([td1, td2]).var() returns a Timedelta scalar (per fp-types nanvar's j8ntk path: pandas returns Timedelta even though variance is ns² conceptually).

Add a Timedelta64 detection branch that mirrors fp-types nanvar: collect ns as f64, compute mean and sum_sq_diff in ns-f64, divide by (count - 1), clamp into i64 ns, emit Scalar::Timedelta64.

title: [perf] parallel complete Kendall pair cells
description: Profile-backed fallback after no ready perf beads and active join/typed-storage perf beads are claimed elsewhere. Current reprofile artifact tests/artifacts/perf/fp_reprofile_after_spearman_rank_parallel_blackthrush_yi5xo.json shows df_kendall at 58.2 ms +/- 1.0 after the Spearman keep; prior closed Kendall artifacts already shipped sorted-order/no-tie precompute, so this bead attacks a different primitive: morsel-driven parallelism over independent complete Kendall upper-triangle pair cells. One lever only: for all-complete/no-tie Kendall columns with precomputed x order and y rank-by-row, compute pair cells in scoped workers and deterministically reassemble the matrix by (i,j). Preserve column order, duplicate/tie fallback semantics, exact inversion-count arithmetic, floating-point final formula, and RNG N/A. Baseline and golden via RCH before edit; rebench via RCH; keep only Score>=2.0; crate-scoped fp-frame validation/UBS and artifact commit.
close_reason: Completed: complete/no-tie Kendall corr now computes independent upper-triangle pair cells in scoped morsels and deterministically reassembles the symmetric matrix. Golden sha256 unchanged dcce8a4e1f13d887361650ec24fcfee13c6c4c3954936fd124a1feb53cf125ce; baseline df_kendall 512x20 232.1 ms +/- 6.6 -> after 68.4 ms +/- 20.6; paired A/B 229.1 ms +/- 2.9 -> 67.3 ms +/- 14.9 (3.40x +/- 0.75). Isomorphism: column order and matrix cell mapping preserved by deterministic (i,j) reassembly; tie/NA/non-finite fallback unchanged by all-complete/no-tie gating; inversion arithmetic and f64 formula reused exactly; RNG N/A. RCH fp-frame check and focused Kendall test passed; clippy blocked by pre-existing searchsorted needless_borrow lints; UBS single-file scan attempted but timed out idle.

title: [test] differential: fp-types period_range seeded oracle
description: Add no-mock seeded differential coverage for fp-types period_range across PeriodFreq variants, signed start ordinals, zero/nonzero periods, and saturation near i64 bounds. Verify output length, preserved frequency, ordinal progression via saturating shift, and empty zero-period behavior against independent oracles. Code-first lane; batch tests pending.

title: [perf] Series::is_monotonic_increasing/decreasing materialize Vec<Scalar> for typed Int64
description: Series::is_monotonic_increasing/decreasing (fp-frame/src/lib.rs ~8001/8034) call self.column.values() — materializing a Vec<Scalar> (boxing every element) — then windows(2)+semantic_le/semantic_ge per pair (match-dispatch). For an all-valid Int64 column, scan the native &[i64] via Column::as_i64_slice() with a<=b / a>=b. as_i64_slice returns Some only for dtype==Int64 && validity.all(), so there are NO missing values and semantic_le(Int64,Int64)==<=, making the typed path bit-identical (windows(2) on len<=1 yields no pairs => true, matching the len<=1 guard). Scope: Int64 only (Float64 NaN/validity semantics left to the Scalar path). Add a test asserting equivalence vs the Scalar path + sorted/unsorted/single/empty.
close_reason: Completed: Series::is_monotonic_increasing/decreasing now scan all-valid typed Int64 columns via as_i64_slice without materializing Vec<Scalar>, preserving fallback behavior for non-Int64 and missing-value cases. Added focused sorted/unsorted/flat/single/empty/equivalence coverage. Validation: CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/frankenpandas-cod-a rch exec -- cargo check -p fp-frame passed remotely on vmi1227854; timeout 180s ubs crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs hit the known fp-frame large-file UBS timeout with no emitted findings.

title: [mock-code-finder] DataFrame::filter_rows (used by dropna, filter, query) loses index name
description:
fp-frame/src/lib.rs:22904 — DataFrame::filter_rows builds new index via Index::new(new_labels) in both branches (line 22938 duplicate-aware path, line 22990 standard path) without applying rename_index(self.index.name()).

Same family as br-lxot8 (Series::dropna), br-um15v (Series::drop), br-43269 (Series reorder helpers).

filter_rows is called from many DataFrame methods including dropna_with_options, filter, and bool-indexed selection paths.

Pandas: DataFrame.dropna / boolean-indexing all preserve the index name.

Surgical fix: at both Self::new_with_column_order callsites, replace
  Index::new(new_labels)
with
  Index::new(new_labels).rename_index(self.index.name())

Tests: DataFrame::dropna on a named-index frame returns frame whose index.name() is preserved.
close_reason: Both filter_rows branches now apply rename_index. Commit 0dd0a2dd. 1911 fp-frame tests pass.

title: Add DatetimeIndex argmax/argmin/argsort/unique/factorize/value_counts/duplicated/drop_duplicates/dropna
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): DatetimeIndex still missing the pandas reduction/dedup/sort and missing-data filtering surface. Forward to the underlying base Index, returning DatetimeIndex from constructors via DatetimeIndex::from_index where needed.

Add nine methods on DatetimeIndex:
- argmax() -> Result<usize, IndexError> — wraps Index::argmax with pandas-style empty-sequence rejection.
- argmin() -> Result<usize, IndexError> — mirror.
- argsort() -> Vec<usize> — forwarded.
- unique() -> Self — forwarded; rebuild as DatetimeIndex.
- factorize() -> (Vec<isize>, Self) — forwarded; rebuild uniques as DatetimeIndex.
- value_counts() -> Vec<(IndexLabel, usize)> — forwarded.
- duplicated(keep) -> Vec<bool> — forwarded.
- drop_duplicates() -> Self — forwarded; rebuild as DatetimeIndex.
- dropna() -> Self — drop labels equal to i64::MIN (NAT).

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: ascending labels argmax/argmin, monotonic argsort, dedup, value_counts ordering, duplicated(First/Last/None), drop_duplicates, NAT-aware dropna, and pandas-style empty-sequence rejection for argmax/argmin.
close_reason: Shipped in a4207b3: DatetimeIndex now exposes pandas-parity argmax/argmin (skipna+first-tied), argsort, unique, factorize (NAT->-1), value_counts (dropna), duplicated, drop_duplicates, dropna. 3 regression tests pass; 270 fp-index tests total.

close_reason: Built each result Index with rename_index in StringMethods::partition at fp-frame/src/lib.rs:17469-17486.

title: Add Datetime/Timedelta/Period Index tz/freq/inferred_freq accessors
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): the typed Index variants miss tz/tzinfo/freq/freqstr/inferred_freq.

DatetimeIndex stores naive UTC nanos so tz/tzinfo always return None. Frequencies are not currently inferred. Period freq exposes via Display.

Add:
- DatetimeIndex.tz() -> Option<String> — None until timezone metadata lands.
- DatetimeIndex.tzinfo() -> Option<String> — alias for tz.
- DatetimeIndex.freq() -> Option<String> — None (frequency inference deferred).
- DatetimeIndex.freqstr() -> Option<String> — None.
- DatetimeIndex.inferred_freq() -> Option<String> — None.
- TimedeltaIndex.freq() / freqstr() / inferred_freq() — same None accessors.
- PeriodIndex.freqstr() -> Option<String> — pandas-style freq alias derived from PeriodFreq Display impl when freq exists.
- PeriodIndex.inferred_freq() -> Option<String> — same as freqstr() (homogeneous-freq guard already enforces it).

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: tz/freq accessors return None, PeriodIndex.freqstr matches the freq Display, mixed-freq PeriodIndex inferred_freq returns None.
close_reason: Shipped in 61bf519: DatetimeIndex/TimedeltaIndex/PeriodIndex now expose pandas-parity tz/freq/inferred_freq accessors. tz returns None until timezone metadata lands; freqstr/inferred_freq derive from PeriodFreq Display for PeriodIndex. 2 regression tests pass; 297 fp-index tests total.

Added skew/kurt/kurtosis/sem arms (fp_types::nanskew/nankurt/nansem) to both string dispatches for consistency with the available methods + pandas. Additive. Code-first; test added. fp-frame.

title: [perf] O(n) quickselect for DataFrame.quantile (was O(n log n) full sort, ~8x)
description: eydcr 5th-wave probe: quantile(0.5) 42ms vs median 2.8ms (median typed, quantile not). DataFrame.quantile materialized col.values() Scalars then FULL-SORTED each column for percentile_linear, which only reads order stats at lower=floor(q(n-1)) and upper=ceil. FIX: (1) typed Float64 extraction from f64+validity slices (no Scalar materialization); (2) quantile_select replaces sort+percentile_linear with select_nth_unstable_by(lower) + min of right partition for upper -> O(n). -0.0 guard: unstable select can pick a different signed zero than a stable sort on a -0.0/0.0 tie, so any negative zero routes to the exact sort path. 42->5.26ms=8.0x bit-identical (golden 346c80e7, q=0/.25/.5/.9/1 x all-valid+nullable). DONE.
close_reason: Shipped e32db39c: O(n) quickselect quantile — typed f64 extraction + select_nth_unstable_by(lower) + right-partition min for upper (replaces full sort). -0.0 guard routes negative-zero data to exact sort. 42->5.26ms=8.0x bit-identical (golden 346c80e7, q=0/.25/.5/.9/1 x all-valid+nullable). fp-frame 2830 + conformance 0 failures.

title: Add PeriodIndex unique/duplicated/drop_duplicates/value_counts/factorize
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): PeriodIndex misses the dedup/value-count/factorize surface that's now on DatetimeIndex (br-z9guv), TimedeltaIndex (br-vq4pf), and CategoricalIndex (br-i1q1c).

Period derives Hash + Eq but not Ord, so the surface is restricted to ordering-free operations:

- unique() -> Self — first-seen pass over Vec<Period>; preserves the index name.
- duplicated(keep) -> Vec<bool> — keep-First/Last/None semantics over the period list.
- drop_duplicates() -> Self — alias for unique.
- value_counts() -> Vec<(Period, usize)> — first-seen group then sort_by_key descending count.
- factorize() -> (Vec<isize>, Self) — first-seen factorization (no missing semantics on Period yet, so no -1 codes).

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: a 6-period index with duplicates exercising unique ordering, duplicated(First) mask, value_counts descending sort, and factorize codes round-trip.
close_reason: Shipped in 1c0e385: PeriodIndex now exposes pandas-parity unique/duplicated/drop_duplicates/value_counts/factorize. 2 regression tests pass; 277 fp-index tests total.

title: [perf] Typed Float64 + Fx-hashed dedup for DataFrame.nunique (Scalar-path ~4x slow)
description: eydcr 5th-wave probe: nunique 41ms. nunique_with_dropna built a HashMap<ScalarKey>(SipHash) from col.values() per column. FIX: typed Float64 branch dedups present values from f64+validity slices into FxHashSet<u64> of normalized bits (no Scalar materialization, no SipHash). Bit-identical to scalar_key_skip_missing Float64 arm: present=validity&&!is_nan, key=FloatBits of -0.0->+0.0 normalized datum; missing=one shared bucket counted iff !dropna. 41->10.3ms=4.0x bit-identical (golden a752efc9, all-valid + nullable + dropna=false). DONE.
close_reason: Shipped 4545894e: typed Float64 nunique — dedup present values from f64+validity into FxHashSet<u64> of -0.0-normalized bits (no Scalar materialization, no SipHash). 41->10.3ms=4.0x bit-identical (golden a752efc9, all-valid+nullable+dropna=false). Matches scalar_key_skip_missing FloatBits arm incl -0.0->+0.0 normalization. fp-frame 2830 + conformance 0 failures.

title: [test] differential: Series.head/tail incl negative n
description: No-mock differential for Series.head/tail. Seeded LCG (no rand, no mocks): Int64 series; for n in {0,1,2,len,len+1,-1,-2,-len} assert head(n) == first min(n,len) rows (n>=0) / all-but-last|n| (n<0), and tail(n) == last min(n,len) (n>=0) / all-but-first|n| (n<0), matching pandas negative-n semantics. Independent slice oracle. Test-only; fp-frame.

title: Add DatetimeIndex hour/minute/second/microsecond/nanosecond accessors
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): IndexVariants is missing the time-of-day accessors hour/minute/second/microsecond/nanosecond on DatetimeIndex. Existing year/month/day already use map_datetime_labels with chrono::Datelike; extend with chrono::Timelike + nanosecond arithmetic.

Pandas semantics:
- hour: 0-23 (Timelike::hour)
- minute: 0-59 (Timelike::minute)
- second: 0-59 (Timelike::second)
- microsecond: nanoseconds_within_second / 1000 (range 0-999_999)
- nanosecond: nanoseconds_within_second % 1000 (range 0-999)

Each returns Vec<Option<u32>> matching the existing year/month/day surface; non-datetime labels collapse to None per the helper contract.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering UTC midnight, sub-second precision (microsecond and nanosecond components), and NAT/None propagation.
close_reason: Shipped in 190ae66: DatetimeIndex now exposes pandas-parity hour/minute/second/microsecond/nanosecond using map_datetime_labels + chrono::Timelike. 2 regression tests pass; 257 fp-index tests total.

title: [perf] Radix no-tie Spearman rank ordering
description: Profile-backed fallback target because br ready has no unblocked [perf] beads and peer-owned in-progress perf work covers typed-columnar/filter/groupby surfaces. Current RCH-built compact profile after 30c44123: df_spearman 5000x64 is 90.0 ms +/- 4.1, with perf samples showing core::slice::sort::unstable::quicksort inside Series::average_ranks at 43.71% of df_spearman cycles, pairwise_rank_corr 11.43%, complete_spearman_parallel_matrix 8.14%. One lever only: add a safe-Rust finite-f64 radix-order fast path for no-tie average ranks used by complete Spearman columns, preserving the existing comparison-sort average-rank path for NaN/inf/ties or any tie under the current EPSILON contract. Baseline/golden before edit via RCH-built perf_profile; rebench after; keep only Score >= 2.0. Isomorphism: output rank values identical for no-tie finite inputs; ties/NaN/inf fallback to existing implementation; column/order/tie/floating-point/RNG semantics unchanged; golden df_spearman sha256 required before/after.
close_reason: Rejected: safe-Rust finite-f64 radix ordering for no-tie Spearman ranks preserved golden df_spearman sha256 dc37f75e1ee2e23e28ed89e0f178a0268cb65cc420bc82f97cb16d219eb7dd1e but failed Score>=2.0. Baseline: df_spearman 5000x64 92.1±3.7ms. Paired A/B: comparison-sort 91.4±3.3ms -> radix 90.9±3.8ms, only 1.01x±0.06. Source reverted; evidence retained. KEY: the divergence was MASKED — the conformance oracle was adapted to FP's wrong output (parts=s.split(pat)[::-1], comment 'FP indexes Rust str::rsplit, reverse order') and 2 recorded fixtures (254/294) + golden encoded the reversed values. Suite was green while violating parity. Fixed fp-frame->forward s.split, oracle->real pandas, fixtures+golden->forward (a/x/single; a/null//no_slash), all verified vs live pandas 2.2.3. golden test 1/0. title: Add CategoricalIndex as_ordered/as_unordered/add_categories/remove_categories/remove_unused_categories/set_categories/rename_categories/reorder_categories
description:
Reality-check finding (artifacts/api-coverage-drift-2026-05-08.md): CategoricalIndex still misses the eight pandas category-management methods.

Add eight inherent methods on CategoricalIndex:
- as_ordered() -> Self — flips ordered=true.
- as_unordered() -> Self — flips ordered=false.
- add_categories(new_cats) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — extends categories; rejects on duplicates.
- remove_categories(old_cats) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — drops categories from the list; rejects when any current label uses a removed category (FrankenPandas doesn't carry NaN-labeled categoricals yet).
- remove_unused_categories() -> Self — narrows categories to the set of labels actually present.
- set_categories(new_cats) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — replaces the categories list; rejects when any current label is missing from new_cats.
- rename_categories(new_cats) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — same-length replacement; updates labels by position.
- reorder_categories(new_cats, ordered) -> Result<Self, IndexError> — reorder when input is a permutation of existing categories; flips the ordered flag.

Single PR. Add regression tests covering: as_ordered toggle, add_categories with duplicates rejection, remove_categories rejection when label still present, remove_unused_categories trimming, rename_categories pos-by-pos rename, set_categories rejection when label missing, reorder_categories permutation check.
close_reason: Shipped in a4c8736: CategoricalIndex now exposes 8 pandas-parity category-management methods (as_ordered/as_unordered/add_categories/remove_categories/remove_unused_categories/set_categories/rename_categories/reorder_categories). 1 comprehensive regression test passes; 301 fp-index tests total.

title: [testing-metamorphic] [HIGH] Series::is_unique() returns wrong result when input has exactly one null amongst distinct values
description:
## Finding (metamorphic property check)

Series::is_unique (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:3856) is broken because
its variable named `non_null` actually INCLUDES the null bucket from
unique():

  pub fn is_unique(&self) -> bool {
      let non_null = self.unique();  // ← actually includes one null bucket
      let null_count = self.column.values().iter().filter(|v| v.is_missing()).count();
      let total_non_null = self.len() - null_count;
      non_null.len() == total_non_null && null_count <= 1
  }

For [1, 2, 3, Null]:
- unique() returns [1, 2, 3, Null] — len 4 (the null bucket is counted)
- null_count = 1
- total_non_null = 3
- 4 == 3 → false → returns false (WRONG)

Pandas:
  >>> pd.Series([1, 2, 3, None]).is_unique
  True   # all 4 values distinct (None is itself a single distinct value)

## Why is_unique should be true for [1, 2, 3, Null]

Pandas semantics: "True if values are unique". Each of 1, 2, 3, None is
distinct → True. Two Nones would collapse to one bucket → not unique.

The condition `null_count <= 1` already handles the multiple-null case;
the bug is that the count comparison is using the wrong populations.

## Fix

Use nunique() (which counts unique NON-null values) directly:

  pub fn is_unique(&self) -> bool {
      let null_count = self.column.values().iter().filter(|v| v.is_missing()).count();
      let total_non_null = self.len() - null_count;
      self.nunique() == total_non_null && null_count <= 1
  }

This is also FASTER — nunique() returns just a usize, no Vec allocation.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into(), 3_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Int64(1), Scalar::Int64(2), Scalar::Int64(3),
           Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN)])?;
  s.is_unique();  // before: false. after: true

## Tests

- is_unique returns true for distinct values WITH ONE null
- is_unique returns false for distinct values WITH TWO nulls
- is_unique returns true for all-distinct values without nulls
- is_unique returns false for repeated values without nulls
- existing series_is_unique_true / series_is_unique_false must remain green

## Why surfaced

/testing-metamorphic — checked the property:
  is_unique() == (nunique() + (1 if any null else 0) == len())
on inputs with exactly one null. The property held in pandas, broke in
fp-frame. The variable name `non_null` was the giveaway (misnomer
hides the inclusion of the null bucket from unique()).
close_reason: Shipped fix in ea0407a — Series::is_unique now uses nunique() (non-null count) instead of unique().len() (which incl null bucket). Single-null case now matches pandas True. 1610 passed / 0 failed.

title: [HIGH] ci: cargo-llvm-cov installed on every run but never invoked — dead step + 0% coverage measured despite claim
description:
Dead-step + no-coverage-measurement audit on ci.yml.

**Observation.** ci.yml has two explicit lines that are currently dead code:

1. Line 37-38:
     - name: Install cargo-llvm-cov
       run: cargo install cargo-llvm-cov --locked

   cargo-llvm-cov is installed on every CI run (`cargo install --locked` + cold network fetch + compile ≈ 60-90s each time — no caching of the binary). But the subsequent steps never invoke `cargo llvm-cov` — no `cargo llvm-cov --workspace`, no coverage report generation, no coverage upload, no PR comment. Pure cycle waste + zero coverage data.

2. Line 46:
     - name: Bench harness smoke
       run: cargo bench

   Workspace has no [[bench]] blocks (parallel sibling br-frankenpandas-ing6) so `cargo bench` is a no-op that always passes. The step's purpose was "smoke test that bench infra compiles" but since no bench compiles, it smoke-tests nothing.

**Cumulative CI-time impact:**
- cargo-llvm-cov install (~80s cold, ~30s warm if Cargo cached the source but the install step doesn't cache the binary output between runs).
- cargo bench no-op (~5s on empty-bench workspace).
- Combined: ~1-2 min per CI run, wasted.

Over 50 PRs/week (swarm cadence), that's ~60 CI-minutes wasted weekly. On a paid runner fleet, measurable $; on the self-hosted fleet, CPU contention against real work.

**Severity.** HIGH — not because wasted time is itself HIGH, but because BOTH dead steps create false signals:
- The cargo-llvm-cov install implies someone later wanted coverage — but coverage is 0% measured and 0% enforced. A reader of ci.yml reasonably assumes coverage is measured when it isn't.
- The cargo bench step implies performance is smoke-tested — but nothing is. Same false signal as br-ing6.

**Fix shape:**

For cargo-llvm-cov (this bead):
1. Option A (activate): CI runs `cargo llvm-cov --workspace --all-targets --lcov --output-path lcov.info` after tests pass. Upload to Codecov / Coveralls. Fail CI when coverage drops > N%. Actionable artifact on every PR.
2. Option B (remove): delete the install step. Zero CI change, truthful ci.yml. Document in a follow-up bead if coverage becomes a priority.

Recommendation: Option A. Coverage data on frankenpandas is genuinely useful — fp-frame has 3836 panic sites (sibling br-7cfm); knowing which ARE exercised by tests narrows the panic-doc backlog.

For cargo bench:
- Covered by sibling br-frankenpandas-ing6 [HIGH: no perf regression gate]. That bead's fix removes or replaces line 46.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+3 lines for llvm-cov invocation + upload), `.github/workflows/ci.yml` env vars for Codecov token, optional README badge. Zero source code changes.

Quick decision: if the team hasn't decided on coverage tracking yet, remove both dead steps today (2 lines) — that's the correct minimum move until a policy lands.
notes: cc-pandas 2026-04-23: removed dead cargo-llvm-cov install step from ci.yml via commit 6005814 on branch cod-pandas-zjme-ship. Option A (remove) per the bead's quick-decision recommendation — no coverage policy had landed, install was pure waste. When coverage is activated in future, install step + llvm-cov invocation + upload land as one coordinated commit rather than having the install floating dead.
