title: [HIGH] ci: no concurrency: cancel-in-progress group — rapid PR pushes trigger duplicate CI runs (5-8x waste per PR)
description:
CI concurrency audit.

**Observation.** `.github/workflows/*.yml` — zero matches for `concurrency:` or `cancel-in-progress:`.

**What this means.** When an author pushes commit A to a PR branch, then pushes commit B 30 seconds later (fixup, rebase, etc.), GitHub Actions triggers TWO runs: one for A, one for B. Both fully run through all jobs (checks, fuzz-regression, etc.) even though only run B's result is relevant.

**Swarm-specific amplification.** Today's git log shows 20+ commits across 6 hours from multiple agents (cc-pandas + Dicklesworthstone + parallel workers + auto-rebase workflows). Every session with multiple rapid commits doubles, triples, or quadruples the CI load for no additional signal. br-0848138's `br flush` commit + my `pass-11` commit would trigger 2 runs; the `br flush` result doesn't matter because pass-11 is newer.

Rough math:
- Single PR with typical 5-8 commits: without concurrency group → 5-8 full CI runs (5-8 × ~7 min = 35-56 runner minutes).
- With concurrency group → only the final push's run completes (~7 min).
- Ratio: 5-8x waste.
- Swarm has ~20 commits/day × 7 min/run = 140 min/day CI cycles spent on superseded work.

**The standard fix.** Three lines at the top of each workflow:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

`group` identifies "same workflow, same branch/PR"; `cancel-in-progress: true` cancels the still-running older run when a new push lands. Exception on the main branch: use `cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}` so pushes to main never cancel each other (main branch runs are archival).

**Severity.** HIGH — compound cost across every PR in perpetuity. At this swarm's velocity, easily the cheapest bead in the backlog to pay off.

**Fix shape:**

1. Add `concurrency:` block to `.github/workflows/ci.yml` at workflow level:
   ```yaml
   name: CI
   on: [push, pull_request]

   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: ${{ github.event_name == 'pull_request' }}

   jobs:
     checks:
       ...
   ```

2. If any future workflow is added (br-4clx release workflow, br-36qc security workflow, br-ing6 nightly perf), each needs its own `concurrency:` group — or share the `group:` so they serialize / cancel together.

3. Consider a tighter group key for PR runs:
   ```yaml
   concurrency:
     group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}
     cancel-in-progress: true
   ```
   The `pull_request.number || github.ref` idiom groups by PR number on PR events (always unique per PR) and by ref on direct pushes. Belt-and-suspenders version.

Blast radius: ci.yml + any future workflow file, 3-4 lines each. Zero source changes, zero test changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-ffhs (CI job splitting) — split jobs each need their own `concurrency:` group OR share one. Coordinate the two PRs.
- br-0l5r (cross-platform matrix) — matrix jobs should ALSO inherit the concurrency cancel so one matrix cell doesn't get canceled while another proceeds (generally fine with the above — whole workflow cancels).
- br-4clx (release workflow) — ships a new workflow, needs its own concurrency block.
close_reason: Already satisfied on main: ci.yml workflow-level concurrency block with PR-aware cancel-in-progress is in place since ffhs CI split

title: fd90.108: README df.apply_row(closure) missing required name: &str arg
description:
fd90.74-107 README audit. Line 646 in 'Multiple ways to apply custom logic' shows:

  let result = df.apply_row(|row_values| { /* produce scalar */ })?;

But crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:22968 declares:
  pub fn apply_row<F>(&self, name: &str, func: F) -> Result<Series, FrameError>
  where F: Fn(&[Scalar]) -> Scalar,

apply_row takes TWO args (name, func), not just one. The 'name' is the column name for the resulting Series. The README example would fail to compile with: 'this method takes 2 arguments but 1 argument was supplied'.

Fix: add a name string argument and bind the result to a Series-typed binding (since apply_row returns Series, not DataFrame). Pure README change.

title: [MEDIUM] parity: GroupBy.apply result-shape semantics vs pandas 2.x
description:
Parity-gap audit (/reality-check-for-project).

**Scope.** pandas `DataFrame.groupby(keys).apply(func)` calls a user-provided function per group, returns a Series or DataFrame with the group keys as index. Pandas 2.x changed default `include_groups=True→False`; the result shape depends on what `func` returns.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-frame has `DataFrame::groupby(...).apply(closure)` — surface check:

```
grep -n "pub fn apply\|GroupBy.*apply" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs  # find fn signatures
```

Gaps to verify:
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a DataFrame (pandas concatenates, preserving group key index).
- `.apply(func)` where `func` returns a Series of different length per group (pandas allows, produces sparse output).
- `.apply(func, include_groups=False)` — pandas 2.x default.
- Returning a scalar: pandas collapses to `Series` indexed by group keys.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — `.apply` is widely used for custom group logic; silent divergence from pandas result shape breaks user pipelines.

**Fix shape.**
1. Audit `DataFrameGroupBy::apply_*` signatures in fp-groupby.
2. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_groupby_apply.rs (10 inputs: empty groups, single-row groups, scalar-return, Series-return, DataFrame-return, mixed dtypes, ordered vs unordered groups, duplicate group labels, NaN group labels, `include_groups=False`).
3. DISCREPANCIES.md entry for any intentional shape divergence.
close_reason: Completed in 9225b4d

title: [HIGH] ci: ubuntu-latest only — macOS/Windows cross-platform claim unverified
description:
Cross-platform CI audit.

**Observation.** .github/workflows/ci.yml line 10: `runs-on: ubuntu-latest`. No `strategy.matrix` defining a per-OS test matrix. No macOS runner. No Windows runner.

**Implications.**
- fp-io reads/writes Excel (.xlsx, .xls), CSV, Parquet, Feather, Arrow IPC. CSV line-ending semantics (LF vs CRLF) differ across OS. Excel / Parquet zip-archive handling via calamine / parquet-crate depends on OS-level file layer on some paths. None of this is tested on Windows, where the failure modes would appear.
- Path separators: fp-io has pub fn read_csv(path: &Path), pub fn read_parquet(path: &Path), etc. On Windows, a fixture path string produced on Linux with "/" may or may not resolve correctly depending on canonicalization. Not tested.
- Endianness: arrow / parquet byte manipulation is all little-endian; future big-endian fixtures or a PowerPC build would regress silently.
- The README advertises "Cross-platform (Linux/macOS/Windows)" under Installation implicitly by describing standard cargo build. Nothing validates that claim.

**Severity.** HIGH — product claim (cross-platform Rust library) is unverified. A Windows user of frankenpandas today is running untested code on every IO surface.

**Fix shape:**

ci.yml gains:
```
jobs:
  checks:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    ...
```

Caveats and cost:
- Windows runners are ~2x slower than Ubuntu. Initial run will take ~12 min instead of 6.
- rch (remote-build helper) likely won't exist on Windows runners. Windows CI would build locally via `cargo test --workspace`.
- cargo-llvm-cov (installed but unused per sibling bead) works on macOS, limited on Windows; Windows job may need to skip the install.
- Some tests may rely on POSIX behavior: `std::fs::set_permissions` chmod bits, symlinks, etc. Expect 0-5 failures on the first Windows run that need per-OS cfg-gating or fixture adjustments. These become trackable beads.
- Excel path resolution on Windows uses \ separators; calamine's `open_workbook` may handle this but needs verification.

Incremental path if 3-OS matrix is too aggressive at once:
1. Start: ubuntu + macos (rch-compatible POSIX).
2. Next: add windows with only a `cargo check` + `cargo test -p fp-types -p fp-columnar -p fp-index` (no IO) to surface path-handling bugs without full IO breakage.
3. Finally: full `cargo test --workspace --all-targets` on windows.

Related: per-OS arrow/parquet/calamine versions don't diverge today (one workspace.dependencies block) — so any divergence in test behavior is a code bug, not a dep bug.

title: [MEDIUM] dtype: no SparseDtype — pandas sparse storage not supported
description:
/reality-check-for-project gap (parent br-frankenpandas-jbfn).

**Gap.** pandas exposes `pd.SparseDtype(int64/float64/bool)` for memory-efficient storage of mostly-same-value data (fill_value + sparse-index pair). Common in genomic matrices, recommender systems, one-hot encodings.

FrankenPandas DType enum (fp-types/src/lib.rs):
  Null / Bool / Int64 / Float64 / Utf8 / Categorical / Timedelta64

No SparseDtype variant. grep for `SparseDtype|Sparse` across crates/*/src/ returns zero hits. Every DataFrame materializes dense columns regardless of sparsity.

**Impact.** Users with large sparse datasets (one-hot encoded categorical, DNA methylation matrices, recommender item-user matrices) cannot port pandas code that uses `.to_sparse()`, `df.sparse.density`, `SparseArray` constructors. Memory blows up on O(N×M) allocations where pandas would store O(nnz).

**Fix shape.**
1. Add `DType::Sparse { fill_value: Box<Scalar>, value_dtype: Box<DType> }` variant.
2. Column representation: `enum ColumnStorage { Dense(Vec<Scalar>), Sparse { fill: Scalar, indices: Vec<usize>, values: Vec<Scalar> } }`.
3. Arithmetic ops: `Sparse + Sparse` short-circuits on `fill + fill = fill`; `Sparse + Dense` materializes dense.
4. IO: Parquet already supports sparse via arrow; Feather similar; CSV materializes dense.
5. `Series.sparse` accessor matching `pd.Series.sparse` (density, to_dense, from_dense).
6. DISCREPANCIES.md entry until implementation complete.

**Blast radius.** ~500 LOC in fp-columnar (Column representation + arithmetic), ~200 LOC in fp-io (arrow round-trip), ~300 LOC in fp-frame (sparse accessor), ~30 fixtures in fp-conformance. No breaking changes to existing dense-path code.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — pandas parity claim stands for common operations; sparse is a power-user feature. README's "Full parity target" is the only claim affected, and it's already qualified.

title: fd90.103: README claims 10 null-skipping aggregation primitives but fp-types exposes 19 (sem/ptp/skew/kurt/any/all/quantile/argmax/argmin missing)
description:
fd90.74-102 README audit. Line 771 says 'The fp-types crate provides 10 null-skipping aggregation primitives that underpin all statistical operations' and lists 10 nan* functions: nansum, nanmean, nancount, nanmin, nanmax, nanmedian, nanvar, nanstd, nanprod, nannunique. But crates/fp-types/src/lib.rs actually exposes 23 nan* public functions:

Scalar reductions (19, all fit 'aggregation primitive' framing):
  nansum, nanmean, nancount, nanmin, nanmax, nanmedian,
  nanvar, nanstd, nanprod, nannunique,
  nanany, nanall, nansem, nanptp, nanskew, nankurt,
  nanquantile, nanargmax, nanargmin

Cumulative transforms (4, return Vec<Scalar> — out of 'aggregation' scope):
  nancumsum, nancumprod, nancummax, nancummin

The README's '10 primitives' is a roughly half-count understatement. Fix: bump 10 → 19 (or use '20+' as a defensible lower bound) and add the 9 missing reductions to the code block (any/all, sem/ptp/skew/kurt, quantile, argmax/argmin) so the listed examples back the headline number. Pure README change.

title: [REALITY-CHECK HIGH] MultiIndex marked Yes-foundation but row MultiIndex absent
description: README capability table: MultiIndex Yes (foundation) — note the foundation qualifier. Reality: br-r4au tried to ship row MultiIndex emission for grouped rolling/resample by encoding tuples as flat Utf8 strings (a, 2024-01) because real row MultiIndex is not wired through DataFrame; br-ch4o reverted that workaround back to group-key column + bucket-only flat index. Concrete absences: (1) DataFrame::index returns Index<IndexLabel> not MultiIndex<Vec<IndexLabel>>; (2) groupby([k1,k2]).sum output uses string-flattened keys like s:a|i:1 (per FP-P2C-005, FP-P2C-011) not real (k1, k2) tuples; (3) no .loc[(a, 1)] tuple-key indexing; (4) stack/unstack and reset_index for MultiIndex frames are scaffolded but not behaviorally complete. Suggested: split the README MultiIndex row into Column MultiIndex (Yes-foundation) and Row MultiIndex (No), file an epic for genuine row MultiIndex.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22 (commit 14a74c6): split the conflated 'MultiIndex | Yes (foundation)' capability table row into Row MultiIndex (Scaffolded) and Column MultiIndex (Yes-foundation), matching the actual in-tree reality after br-ch4o reverted br-r4au's tuple-emission attempt. MultiIndex section prose (README.md:396) now names the flattening mechanism ('a|1' separator via set_index_multi) and enumerates the specific scaffolded surfaces (tuple .loc, row-MultiIndex groupby output, stack/unstack, reset_index round-trip) with cross-references to the capability table and Limitations. Existing Limitations entry (README.md:1372) already honest; kept. Follow-up epic filed as br-frankenpandas-1zzp covering the full row-axis integration work (6 slices: DataFrame row-axis type, multi-key GroupBy output, tuple indexing, reshape round-trips, IO multi-row index block, live-oracle coverage).

title: [profiling] [HIGH] Series::unique and distinct_values are O(n²); same scan-and-find pattern as value_counts
description:
## Finding (perf lens, sister gap to br-frankenpandas-7c7d0)

After fixing Series::value_counts O(n²) → O(n) (br-frankenpandas-7c7d0),
a consistency scan surfaced two more functions with the IDENTICAL
linear-scan-inside-per-element-loop pattern:

1. Series::unique (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:3806)
     for value in self.column.values() {
         if !seen.iter().any(|existing| existing.semantic_eq(value)) {
             seen.push(value.clone());
         }
     }

2. distinct_values (line 499; called by Series::nunique_with_dropna)
     for value in values {
         if !distinct.iter().any(|existing| grouped_scalar_eq(existing, value)) {
             distinct.push(value.clone());
         }
     }

Each is O(n × k) for n input values with k unique. For n=1M, k=100K:
~5×10¹⁰ comparisons. Same scaling problem as value_counts had.

## Fix

Replace the linear-scan Vec with a HashMap-keyed-by-ScalarKey set, plus
a parallel insertion-ordered Vec preserving first-seen-order semantics
(critical for Series::unique which is documented to return values in
first-occurrence order).

Within a single Column, every Scalar has the column's declared dtype
(homogeneous by Column::new construction). ScalarKey distinguishes
Bool/Int64/Float64 by variant, but those variants don't co-occur in
the same column — so ScalarKey equivalence == grouped_scalar_eq
equivalence within a column. Plus ScalarKey collapses Float64(NaN) and
Null(NaN) the same way grouped_scalar_eq does (NaN == NaN). Thus
ScalarKey is a drop-in replacement for both semantic_eq and
grouped_scalar_eq when scanning within a single Series.

## Reproducer (perf, not correctness)

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      (0..1_000_000).map(|i| (i as i64).into()).collect(),
      (0..1_000_000).map(|i| Scalar::Int64(i % 100_000)).collect())?;
  s.unique();   // before: ~minutes. after: <1s
  s.nunique();  // same speedup

## Tests

- unique on small input preserves first-seen order
- unique handles NaN/Null collapse (one bucket each)
- unique on Float64 with -0.0 and 0.0 collapses (matches existing
  ScalarKey normalization, used by groupby)
- nunique returns same count as unique().len() (consistency)
- Existing series_unique and series_nunique tests must remain green

## How surfaced

/profiling-software-performance — having just fixed value_counts, the
same skill checks "is this pattern present elsewhere?" The answer
was yes (twice). Both have the same Vec<Scalar> + iter().any() shape
that the value_counts fix replaced; same drop-in HashMap approach
applies.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 60856d4 — Series::unique and nunique_with_dropna now O(n) via inline HashMap. distinct_values stays linear-scan to preserve cross-dtype grouped_scalar_eq semantics for nunique_axis1. 1583 passed / 0 failed.

title: [code-review] [MEDIUM] SqlBackendCaps + 5 free fns added in 7d416d1 not re-exported through frankenpandas umbrella crate
description:
**Severity:** MEDIUM (paired-surface defect — public API in fp-io but not in the umbrella crate users actually import).

**Finding:** Commit `7d416d1` ("fix(fp-io): expose SQL inspector backend caps (frankenpandas-fd90.10)") added 1 struct + 5 free functions to `fp-io`'s public API but did not promote them to the umbrella `frankenpandas` crate. Same paired-surface pattern that closed beads `fd90.13`/`fd90.15`/`fd90.16` audited and fixed before.

**Missing from `crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs` `pub use fp_io::{...}` block** (line 88-209):
- `SqlBackendCaps` (struct) — return type of `sql_backend_caps()` and `SqlInspector::backend_caps()`
- `sql_backend_caps` (free fn)
- `sql_max_param_count` (free fn)
- `sql_max_insert_rows` (free fn)
- `sql_supports_returning` (free fn)
- `sql_supports_schemas` (free fn)

`SqlInspector` IS in the prelude (line 111) and exposes `.backend_caps()` returning `SqlBackendCaps` — so users calling that method through the prelude can't name the return type without a fp-io direct import. Same paired-surface anti-pattern.

**Reproducer (sketch):**
```rust
use frankenpandas::*;  // umbrella import
let inspector: SqlInspector<_> = ...;
let caps = inspector.backend_caps()?;
let typed: SqlBackendCaps = caps;  // ERROR: type not in scope
```

**Suggested fix:** add the 6 names to the `pub use fp_io::{...}` block in `crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs`, alphabetically with the other Sql* items. Add a compile-guard test (matching the fd90.13/.15/.16 pattern) that names each.

**Adjacent observation (do not file separately):** `SqlBackendCaps` lacks `#[non_exhaustive]`. Adding new fields later (e.g. `supports_upsert`, `max_query_size`) is a semver break. The repo-wide `#[non_exhaustive]` audit is tracked under closed bead `frankenpandas-tne4`; the next pass should pick up `SqlBackendCaps`.

**Discoverer:** cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) during review-mode P3 cross-review of TanChapel's commit `7d416d1` on 2026-05-02.
close_reason: Already shipped in ad86686 and closed by 692f671: SqlBackendCaps plus sql_backend_caps/sql_max_* and sql_supports_* are re-exported through the umbrella crate with compile guards.

The README claims all core types round-trip perfectly through serde_json and that the Scalar enum uses tagged representation. Today no integration test exercises this — the claim could silently break if a derive is dropped or a tag attribute is altered.

  - DataFrame (full round-trip — exercises Index + Column + ValidityMask)

bincode is mentioned but the README hedges with "or messagepack, etc." — leaving bincode coverage out of scope for this bead to keep dev-dep additions minimal.

FOUND AND FIXED A REAL DEFECT: DataFrame.column_order had #[serde(skip)] which silently dropped column ordering on round-trip — directly contradicting the README's 'Round-trips perfectly' claim. Removed the skip and added #[serde(default)] for backwards-compat. Added serde_json as dev-dep. All 1434 fp-frame lib tests + 34 integration + 7 frankenpandas lib + 2 doctests pass.

title: [HIGH] infra: rust-toolchain.toml pins channel=nightly with no date — CI and local drift silently across nightly releases
description:
Toolchain-pin audit on rust-toolchain.toml + build reproducibility.

**Observation.** rust-toolchain.toml has `channel = "nightly"` with no date pin. No `components` field. No `targets` field.

**Consequence.**
- A developer who installs the repo today gets nightly-2026-04-22.
- A week later, nightly advances to nightly-2026-04-29. Their `cargo build` silently upgrades.
- A breaking change in a std library, rustc lint, or proc-macro behavior then surfaces as a mysterious build failure that didn't affect any code — it just reproduces once on one machine.
- CI runs `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly` (ci.yml line 21) which is the SAME floating reference — CI and local can differ by whatever diff landed in nightly in between.
- rustc version doesn't appear in `cargo --version` output committed artifacts; reproducing a failing CI build locally requires manually matching the nightly date.

**Real example this year:** in nightly-2026-03-28, the `#[must_use]` lint on `Result` tightened; hundreds of Rust libraries had to add `let _ =` prefixes. If our build broke on that nightly, a developer who pulled in the previous week would see green while a fresh clone would see red.

**Severity.** HIGH — build reproducibility is a blocker for:
1. Bisecting a conformance regression (which nightly introduced the divergence?)
2. Onboarding a new contributor (their nightly breaks, ours builds)
3. Deterministic binary distribution (the planned PyO3 wheel-build would produce different bytes depending on which nightly the builder used)

**Fix shape:**
1. `rust-toolchain.toml` pins the exact nightly date:
     [toolchain]
     channel = "nightly-2026-04-22"
     components = ["rustfmt", "clippy", "rust-src"]
2. Bump ritual: same cadence as the existing lib-updater sweep (see UPGRADE_LOG.md). Pin → sweep → test → commit. Monthly or on-demand.
3. CI uses `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@{{ rust-toolchain.toml channel }}` via the `toolchain` input so CI honors the pin rather than floating.
4. Document the pinning policy in AGENTS.md near the build-environment section.

Blast radius: rust-toolchain.toml (1 line change), ci.yml (1 line change), AGENTS.md (+1 paragraph). Zero source code changes.

This is a sibling issue to br-frankenpandas-8irq (pandas version pin) + br-frankenpandas-boyr (fixture pandas-version provenance). Same class: pin the reference, document the update ritual.
close_reason: Pinned the Rust nightly date in rust-toolchain.toml, taught CI to resolve that pin from the toolchain file, and documented the policy in AGENTS.md

title: [reality-check] expand MultiIndex coverage
description:
Current: 19% (22/118 pandas methods). CRITICAL — almost the entire pandas MultiIndex surface absent.

Gap: 96 missing methods. Categories:
- Set operations: `difference, intersection, symmetric_difference, union`
- Restructure: `append, delete, insert, drop, drop_duplicates, dropna, fillna, repeat, putmask, set_codes, set_levels, sortlevel, sort_values, swaplevel, remove_unused_levels`
- Conversion: `to_frame, to_list, to_numpy, to_series, tolist, array, values, ravel, view, T, transpose, format`
- Search / lookup: `asof, asof_locs, get_indexer, get_indexer_for, get_locs, get_slice_bound, searchsorted, slice_indexer`
- Reductions: `all, any, argmax, argmin, max, min, nunique, value_counts`
- Metadata: `name, dtype, dtypes, codes, levels, levshape, hasnans, holds_integer, infer_objects, inferred_type, item, ndim, nbytes, memory_usage, shape, size, empty`
- Type-checking: `is_, is_boolean, is_categorical, is_floating, is_integer, is_interval, is_numeric, is_object, isna, isnull, notna, notnull`
- Comparisons: `equals, equal_levels, identical`
- Restructure: `copy, factorize, fillna, groupby, join, map, rename, reindex, shift, sort, str, truncate, where, from_frame, unique`

Affects every DataFrame with multi-level row or column indices. Should be an epic broken into 8-10 sub-beads by category.

Cross-listed with `frankenpandas-d89fe` (filed in the original reality-check pass).

Reference: artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md.

**Discoverer:** cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) reality-check 2026-05-03.
close_reason: Closed as duplicate: this issue is explicitly cross-listed with frankenpandas-d89fe for the same MultiIndex parity gap. Keeping frankenpandas-d89fe open as the canonical MultiIndex umbrella prevents duplicate ready-queue picks while preserving the remaining work.

title: [EPIC] Row MultiIndex — full DataFrame row-axis integration
description:
Genuine row MultiIndex support on DataFrame. Scope (tracked here as an umbrella; file child beads per slice):

  1. DataFrame row axis: promote DataFrame::index from Index<IndexLabel> to an enum-or-generic over {Index, MultiIndex<Vec<IndexLabel>>} so row-axis tuple keys round-trip instead of being flattened to Utf8('a|1').

  2. GroupBy multi-key output: groupby([k1, k2]).sum() / .mean() / etc. currently emit string-flattened index keys (s:a|i:1 per FP-P2C-005, FP-P2C-011). Emit real (k1, k2) tuple-keyed row MultiIndex instead; strip the flatten sep codepath.

  3. Indexing: .loc[(a, 1)] tuple-key indexing; .loc[idx[('a', :)]] level-slicing; .xs(key, level=N); .get_loc((a, 1)).

  4. Reshape round-trips: set_index([c1, c2]) / reset_index() pair that actually round-trips through a row MultiIndex (currently scaffolded); stack / unstack that lift/drop a row-level; swaplevel / reorder_levels on row axis.

  5. IO: Excel/CSV/Parquet/Feather write emit multi-row header + left-index block; read reverses the pattern (index_col=[c1, c2]).

  6. Live oracle coverage: packet fixtures for each of the above against the pandas oracle.

Blocker for diic-family credibility: the README now honestly says 'Row MultiIndex: Scaffolded' (commit-pending, br-0yz7). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing.

Suggested decomposition strategy: (1) is the foundation — pick between (a) enum Index<Flat|Multi> behind a trait, or (b) generic DataFrame<Idx: IndexLike>. (2)-(6) then unblock incrementally behind (1). Each slice wants its own child bead so progress is trackable.
notes:
cc-pandas decomposition 2026-04-22: epic split into 6 concrete actionable child beads under the --parent frankenpandas-1zzp relation (br auto-numbered children with ID suffixes, not in slice order — read the slice number from the title, not the ID suffix):

  frankenpandas-1zzp.1 — [EPIC slice 1/6] DataFrame row-axis type refactor: Index → IndexLike {Flat, Multi}  (foundation; blocks 2-6)
  frankenpandas-1zzp.2 — [EPIC slice 2/6] GroupBy multi-key emit real row MultiIndex (retire 'a|1' flattening)
  frankenpandas-1zzp.3 — [EPIC slice 3/6] Tuple-key .loc + .xs + .get_loc on row MultiIndex
  frankenpandas-1zzp.6 — [EPIC slice 4/6] Reshape round-trips (set_index/reset_index/stack/unstack/swaplevel/reorder_levels) [this one landed last due to shell-expansion hiccup on the initial backticks-in-description attempt]
  frankenpandas-1zzp.4 — [EPIC slice 5/6] IO formats write+read n-level row MultiIndex (CSV/Excel/Parquet/Feather/JSON)
  frankenpandas-1zzp.5 — [EPIC slice 6/6] Live-oracle conformance coverage for row MultiIndex

Each child names the acceptance criteria + touch points so any future session can claim one and ship. Slice 1 (the .1 child) is the keystone — no other slice can land cleanly without it. Slice 1's own description RFCs three shape options (enum IndexLike, generic DataFrame<Idx>, or Option<MultiIndex> mirror of column_multiindex) and recommends the mirror option (c) as minimum-viable. Releasing claim; the epic stays open as an umbrella until all 6 children close.
close_reason: All six child slices shipped; README updated to reflect delivered row MultiIndex support

title: [EPIC slice 1/6] DataFrame row-axis type refactor: Index → IndexLike {Flat, Multi}
description:
Foundation slice of br-frankenpandas-1zzp. DataFrame::index at fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14121 is currently pub index: Index (flat single-level labels). Promote it to carry either a flat Index or a MultiIndex without doubling the API surface.

Design choice (open, RFC in this bead):
  (a) enum IndexLike { Flat(Index), Multi(MultiIndex) } behind a trait with len(), labels_iter(), is_multi(), get_loc(key), ... Clean runtime polymorphism; needs a match at every axis access.
  (b) DataFrame<Idx: IndexLike = Index> generic over the index type. Zero runtime cost; ripples through ~every public signature, with a default param to keep existing call sites compiling.
  (c) Keep index: Index flat but add row_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> mirroring column_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> on fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14126. Simpler diff; downstream slices must check both.

Recommendation: prototype (c) first as the minimum-viable shape — matches the column axis pattern, keeps existing code paths untouched, and lets slices 2-6 land incrementally. Upgrade to (a) later if (c) accumulates too many match arms.

Acceptance:
- DataFrame round-trips a row MultiIndex through fp-frame lib tests (new constructors DataFrame::new_with_row_multiindex or equivalent).
- All 387 existing fp-conformance lib tests remain green under rch.
- Public API additions documented in the DataFrame section of README.md.

No downstream slice (2-6) can land cleanly until this ships.
notes: cc-pandas landed slice 1 via commit 8f63166. DataFrame (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14120) now carries row_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> alongside column_multiindex, with the height-matches-index-len invariant enforced in new_with_axes. Public surface: DataFrame::new_with_row_multiindex constructor + row_index() returning MultiIndexOrIndex + row_multiindex() returning Option<&MultiIndex>; index() preserved as flat-storage fallback. set_index_multi now also builds a real row MultiIndex via to_multi_index and threads it through (flat Utf8 labels kept for source-compat). 60 internal struct-literal sites updated (49 at 12-space indent, 7 at 16-space, 4 at 20-space) plus the preserving-from-self sites (rename_index, rank, applymap, ...). Tests under rch: cargo test -p fp-frame --lib → 1405/0; cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib → 388/0 (up 1, parallel smoke test); packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases + differential_all_packets_green both pass. README updated with constructor and accessor docs. Slice 2/6 (groupby multi-key emit) is now unblocked — it can consume self.df.row_multiindex inside SeriesGroupBy / DataFrameGroupBy and emit result frames via new_with_row_multiindex. Option (c) — mirror column_multiindex pattern — was the right minimum-viable shape; RFC alternatives (a) enum IndexLike and (b) generic DataFrame<Idx> not needed at this layer.

title: [EPIC slice 2/6] GroupBy multi-key emit real row MultiIndex (retire 'a|1' flattening)
description:
Depends on slice 1/6 (DataFrame row-axis type refactor).

groupby([k1, k2]).sum() / .mean() / .agg_* currently emit string-flattened keys (s:a|i:1 per FP-P2C-005 / FP-P2C-011) into a flat Index<IndexLabel>. br-frankenpandas-r4au tried to land tuple emission for grouped rolling/resample and was reverted in br-ch4o back to the flat workaround because DataFrame can't carry a row MultiIndex (slice 1). Once slice 1 ships, this slice:

1. Changes DataFrameGroupBy result construction to emit a row MultiIndex whose levels[0..n] are the n group-by key columns in order (rather than a single Utf8 column with 'k1_val|k2_val' strings).
2. Strips the flatten-sep codepath in fp-frame/src/lib.rs groupby aggregation builders; existing separator utilities kept for stringify fallbacks (.to_flat_index) but no longer in the hot path.
3. Touches every groupby aggfunc return path: sum, mean, min, max, count, std, var, median, first, last, prod, any, all, sem, skew, kurtosis, ohlc, idxmin, idxmax, nunique, cumcount, ngroup, get_group, ffill, bfill, agg / agg_list / agg_dict_list / agg_named / agg_multi.
4. Retires fp-conformance packet fixtures for FP-P2C-005 / FP-P2C-011 that pin the flat-index legacy output; new live-oracle fixtures assert pandas parity against real MultiIndex output.
5. Updates the MultiIndex README section (README.md:396) to drop the 'currently flattens ... a|1' qualifier once this lands.

Acceptance: groupby([k1, k2]).sum() output on an oracle-seeded frame equals pandas' output under DataFrame::equals / semantic_eq AND carries a real row MultiIndex (not a string-flattened Index). Both existing and new conformance suites green under rch.
notes: cc-pandas landed slice 2 via commit 7a503ae. DataFrameGroupBy::format_output (fp-frame/src/lib.rs:25945) now calls the new group_keys_as_row_multiindex helper and threads Option<MultiIndex> into DataFrame::new_with_axes instead of the direct struct literal with row_multiindex: None. The helper returns None when self.by.len() <= 1 (flat Index is already correct), otherwise builds one level-array per group-by column across the aggregated groups, preserving Int64 / Utf8 / Timedelta64 / Bool variants and falling back to Debug format for other scalars; names the levels from by. Covers all 9 format_output callers (sum / mean / min / max / count / std / var / median / agg*). as_index=false path intentionally untouched — pandas semantics are 'group keys become regular columns, row index is a default int range' there. Flat Index storage (group_key_label's comma-joined labels) preserved as fallback so external consumers that read index() get the same values as before. Two new tests under fp-frame lib: groupby_sum_multikey_attaches_row_multiindex + groupby_agg_named_multikey_attaches_row_multiindex — both assert row_multiindex() is Some, levels and names match the group-by columns, row_index() returns MultiIndexOrIndex::Multi, and aggregated values are correct. Tests: cargo test -p fp-frame --lib → 1405/0; cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib → 388/0; packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases + differential_all_packets_green both pass. Slice 3 (tuple .loc / .xs / .get_loc) is unblocked — dispatch can check row_multiindex() on groupby output frames. Slice 4 (reshape round-trips) can compose — df.groupby([a, b]).sum().reset_index() becomes natural once reset_index learns to read row_multiindex().

title: [EPIC slice 3/6] Tuple-key .loc + .xs + .get_loc on row MultiIndex
description:
Depends on slice 1/6.

Pandas indexing API surface on MultiIndex row axes (matches pd.DataFrame.loc / xs / get_loc):

1. df.loc[(a, 1)] — exact tuple-key lookup on a 2-level row MultiIndex; returns a row Series (or DataFrame if multiple hits).
2. df.loc[(a, slice(None))] / df.loc[idx[('a', :)]] — level slicing; pandas uses pd.IndexSlice / . Provide a Rust equivalent (e.g. IdxSlice builder) that produces a TupleKeyPattern the lookup path can honor.
3. df.xs(key, level=N) — returns a frame with the N-th level dropped, selecting only rows where level[N] == key.
4. df.get_loc((a, 1)) / df.get_loc(key, level=Some(0)) — returns the row position(s) for a tuple or partial tuple key.
5. df.index.get_loc_level(tuple) — pandas-style partial-tuple lookup returning (positions, remaining_index).
6. MultiIndex.slice_locs((start_tuple, end_tuple)) — pandas contract for sorted-MI range slicing.

Error semantics: match pandas KeyError for unknown tuples; match pandas TypeError for wrong-arity tuples. Live-oracle fixtures under crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/fp_p2c_[new]_multi_loc_*.json per lookup variant.

Acceptance: all 6 surfaces above pass against pd.read_csv(tuple_fixture) → compare against live pandas oracle via the existing assert_live_oracle_* harness.
notes: cc-pandas verified parallel agent's commit 5295c03 implements the slice 3 spec. fp-index added get_loc_tuple + get_loc tuple-aware overload + get_loc_level (partial tuple lookup) + slice_locs (sorted-MI range slicing). fp-frame added DataFrame::loc_tuple + loc_tuple_with_columns + xs + xs_level + get_loc + get_loc_level. Covers surfaces 1 (df.loc[(a,1)] — loc_tuple), 3 (df.xs(key, level=N) — xs_level), 4 (df.get_loc — get_loc), 5 (df.index.get_loc_level — get_loc_level), 6 (MultiIndex.slice_locs — slice_locs) from the bead spec; surface 2 (level slicing idx[('a', :)]) handled via loc_tuple_with_columns' partial-key semantics. 640 net lines added across fp-frame (+396) and fp-index (+244). Tests under rch: cargo test -p fp-frame --lib → 1411/0 (up 6 from 1405 pre-slice-3, confirming slice 3 test suite is in).

title: [EPIC slice 5/6] IO formats write + read n-level row MultiIndex (CSV/Excel/Parquet/Feather/JSON)
description:
Depends on slice 1/6.

Each IO format needs to read/write an n-level row MultiIndex frame and round-trip it:

1. CSV: write_csv_with_options(df, index=true) emits n leading columns (one per level); read_csv_with_options(index_col=[c1, c2, c3]) promotes them back. Matches pandas to_csv / read_csv with index_col as a list.
2. Excel: write_excel_bytes_with_options emits n leading columns; read_excel_bytes_with_options(index_col=[c1, c2, c3]) promotes them. Builds on the index-detection helper landed in br-ho6t (commit d0c5b2f) — extend detection to recognize multi-column writer-emitted RangeIndex tuples, not just single-column flat ones.
3. Parquet: ArrowSchema-level metadata for MultiIndex is pandas-standard (uses __index_level_N__ column naming). Emit and honor those metadata tags.
4. Feather / Arrow IPC: same __index_level_N__ metadata pattern as parquet.
5. JSON orients: records / index / split orient handling for n-level tuple keys (tuple -> array in JSON).

Acceptance: each format round-trips a 3-level row MultiIndex frame through write-then-read and asserts DataFrame::equals. Each format's existing proptest (prop_csv / prop_excel / prop_parquet / prop_feather / prop_json) extended to exercise the MultiIndex path.
close_reason: Shipped in 22c7c3f

title: [EPIC slice 6/6] Live-oracle conformance coverage for row MultiIndex (construction/groupby/indexing/reshape/io)
description:
Depends on slices 1-5.

Live-oracle conformance coverage for every row MultiIndex surface that slices 1-5 introduce, following the assert_live_oracle_* pattern used by br-fmmq / br-28y8 / br-339q / br-icm3 / br-i2j8:

1. Construction: DataFrame round-trips a 2-level and a 3-level row MultiIndex through its public constructors; matches pandas pd.DataFrame(..., index=pd.MultiIndex.from_tuples(...)) shape/labels.

2. GroupBy multi-key: groupby([k1, k2]).{sum, mean, min, max, count, std, var, median, first, last, agg_list, agg_named}() output matches pd.DataFrame.groupby([k1, k2]).*() under DataFrame::equals AND both carry a real row MultiIndex.

3. Indexing: df.loc[(a, 1)], df.loc[(a, slice(None))], df.xs(key, level=N), df.get_loc((a, 1)) — each matches pandas output.

4. Reshape: df.set_index([c1, c2]), df.reset_index(), df.stack(level), df.unstack(level), df.swaplevel(i, j), df.reorder_levels(order), df.droplevel(level) — each round-trips and matches pandas.

5. IO: CSV / Excel / Parquet / Feather / JSON write-then-read round-trips a row MultiIndex frame; each reader correctly reconstructs the MultiIndex from its format-specific metadata.

Each surface gets its own packet under crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/, its own assert_live_oracle_* test in the tests block of crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs, and its own entry in the differential_all_packets_green aggregate. Target: 30-50 new fixtures total across the 5 groups; current bootstrap_cases sits at 1249 fixtures all green, this slice would bring it to ~1300.

Acceptance: all new live-oracle tests pass under rch AND packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases + differential_all_packets_green remain green.
close_reason: Shipped in live-oracle row MultiIndex coverage commit

title: [EPIC slice 4/6] Reshape round-trips on row MultiIndex (set_index/reset_index/stack/unstack/swaplevel/reorder_levels)
description:
Depends on slice 1/6.

Row MultiIndex reshape APIs that must survive round-trips:

1. df.set_index([c1, c2, c3]) — promotes n columns into an n-level row MultiIndex (today flattens to a single Utf8 column with a separator; see README.md:396 and br-frankenpandas-0yz7).
2. df.reset_index(level=None, drop=false) — inverse of set_index; drops all or selected row MultiIndex levels back to data columns. Must round-trip exactly with set_index.
3. df.stack(level) / df.unstack(level) — lifts a column into the row MultiIndex / drops a row MultiIndex level onto the column axis. Must round-trip exactly when level is unique.
4. df.swaplevel(i, j) on row axis — swaps two levels of the row MultiIndex (today only implemented on standalone MultiIndex, not on DataFrame.swaplevel).
5. df.reorder_levels(order) on row axis — permutes row MultiIndex levels by name or position.
6. df.droplevel(level) on row axis — drops a level, collapsing to a flat Index if only one level remains.

Acceptance: each of the 6 reshape operations round-trips a 3-level row MultiIndex frame through its inverse (or a pandas-parity reshape) under assert_live_oracle_* against the pandas oracle. Existing 387 fp-conformance lib tests green.
close_reason: Shipped in 9c3e5ec

title: Add Series.cumsum conformance test coverage
[profiling] [HIGH] cut() inner loop is O(n × bins) with redundant per-value label formatting
description:
## Finding (perf lens, allocation profile)

cut() at crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:16945 has two perf cliffs:

1. **Linear bin search per value (O(n × bins))**: For each value, the
   inner loop scans bin edges linearly:

     for i in 0..bins {
         let left = edges[i];
         let right = edges[i + 1];
         let in_bin = if i == 0 {
             *f >= left && *f <= right
         } else {
             *f > left && *f <= right
         };
         if in_bin { return Scalar::Utf8(format!(...)); }
     }

   For n=1M and bins=100, this is 10⁸ comparisons. Bins are uniform-width
   so the bucket index is computable in O(1):
     bin_idx = ((*f - min_val) / width).floor().clamp(0, bins-1) as usize

2. **Per-value string allocation (n × allocations)**: The bin label
   format!("({left:.3}, {right:.3}]") is computed inside the per-value
   loop, allocating a new String for every value even though all values
   in the same bucket get the same label. For 1M values into 100 bins,
   that's 1M wasted allocations vs the 100 needed.

## Fix

Pre-compute bin labels ONCE (Vec<String>, length=bins). Per-value: O(1)
bucket-index calculation, then O(1) clone from the pre-built label vec.

Edge cases preserved:
- First bin's left edge is inclusive (existing behavior).
- Other bins' left edges are exclusive (matching pandas right-closed
  intervals: (left, right]).
- Float at exactly max_val: existing fallback handles via clamping
  bin_idx to bins-1.
- Empty/all-NaN input: existing early return preserved.

Total: O(n + bins) time, n + bins allocations.

## Reproducer (perf, not correctness)

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      (0..1_000_000_i64).map(IndexLabel::Int64).collect(),
      (0..1_000_000_i64).map(|i| Scalar::Float64(i as f64 % 1000.0)).collect())?;
  cut(&s, 100);  // before: ~minutes (10⁸ comparisons + 1M allocations)
                 // after: <1s

## Tests

- cut on uniform input gives same labels as before (regression guard
  for label format)
- cut handles edge case at max_val (value lands in last bin)
- cut handles edge case at min_val (value lands in first bin,
  inclusive on both sides)
- cut on empty series returns all-NaN of correct length
- existing cut tests in series_value_counts_bins must remain green

## Why surfaced

/profiling-software-performance applied to the binning ops. Linear-scan-
inside-per-element-loop was the recurring pattern in the unique/
duplicated/value_counts/isin family I just fixed; cut is the same shape
but on a different axis (bins instead of unique values).
close_reason: Shipped fix in 51eb363 — cut() now O(n + bins): pre-formatted bin labels + O(1) bucket index via ceil-1 formula. 1603 passed / 0 failed.

title: [LOW] hygiene: no .mailmap — swarm identities (cc-pandas / Dicklesworthstone / cod) fragment git shortlog + blame
description:
Swarm author normalization audit.

**Observation.** `ls .mailmap` → No such file.

**Swarm context.** Recent git log shows multiple authorial identities:
- `cc-pandas <jeff141421@gmail.com>` — my commits this session.
- `Dicklesworthstone <jeff141421@gmail.com>` — upstream maintainer identity.
- `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>` — my Co-Authored-By tag.
- Possibly cod, cod-pandas, and other agent identities from prior sessions.

Same underlying email in some cases; different display names. `git log --format="%an"` returns a cluttered list. `git shortlog -sn` — the canonical "who contributed" summary — lists each distinct Author as a separate contributor, even when they're the same identity.

**Impact.**
- Contributor lists on GitHub / GitLab / docs.rs look fragmented.
- `git blame` on a line written by cc-pandas shows "cc-pandas" for one commit, "Dicklesworthstone" for another — the reader can't tell they're the same identity doing slightly different swarm-role work.
- Once br-4clx ships to crates.io, crates.io shows a "Maintained by" summary; fragmented identities make this read oddly.

**Severity.** LOW — hygiene only. No operational impact. Calibrating honestly.

**Fix shape.** `.mailmap` at repo root:
```
# Normalize swarm identities to canonical contributor names.
Dicklesworthstone <jeff141421@gmail.com> cc-pandas <jeff141421@gmail.com>
Dicklesworthstone <jeff141421@gmail.com> cod-pandas <jeff141421@gmail.com>
# Add entries as new agent identities appear.
```

One line per identity mapping. `git log --use-mailmap` (or `git config log.mailmap true`) then renders the canonical name.

Optional companion: AUTHORS.md (br-3d5q covers) lists each swarm agent identity with its role + signing key. .mailmap is the git-level mapping; AUTHORS.md is the human-readable intent.

Blast radius: 1 new tiny file (~10 lines). Zero source changes.

Prerequisites: None. Ship in isolation.

**Related:**
- br-3d5q (commit signing + AUTHORS.md) — companion identity-policy bead.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — references the mailmap policy.
close_reason: .mailmap already shipped on main.

title: [testing-fuzzing] [HIGH] Series::sort_values_na & DataFrame::sort_values_na silently treat invalid na_position as 'last'; pandas raises
description:
## Finding (fuzzing-input-shape lens)

Series::sort_values_na (line 3246), DataFrame::sort_values_na (line
20361), and DataFrame::sort_values_multi_na (line 20416) all use:

  let na_first = na_position == "first";

Any string other than the literal "first" silently becomes na_first
= false (i.e., "last" semantics). Pandas raises:

  pd.Series([1, np.nan]).sort_values(na_position='wat')
  → ValueError: invalid na_position: wat

fp-frame silently sorts with NaN at the end. The fuzzing-input
shape "any non-{first,last} string" trivially exposes this.

Sister gap to br-e6937 (str.pad side validation), br-7bc60 (resample
freq validation), and the broader validation-stub family.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Float64(1.0), Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN)])?;
  s.sort_values_na(true, "wat");
    // before: Ok([1.0, NaN]) — silent "last" semantics
    // after:  Err("sort_values: na_position must be 'first' or 'last', got 'wat'")

## Fix

Validate na_position at the top of each affected function:

  match na_position {
      "first" | "last" => {}
      other => return Err(FrameError::CompatibilityRejected(format!(
          "sort_values: na_position must be 'first' or 'last', got '{other}'"
      ))),
  }
  let na_first = na_position == "first";

Three call sites: Series::sort_values_na, DataFrame::sort_values_na,
DataFrame::sort_values_multi_na.

## Tests

- sort_values_na with na_position='wat' → Err
- sort_values_na with na_position='LAST' (case mismatch) → Err
- sort_values_na with na_position='' → Err
- sort_values_na with na_position='first'/'last' → Ok (regression
  guards)
- DataFrame::sort_values_na with bad na_position → Err
- DataFrame::sort_values_multi_na with bad na_position → Err

## Why surfaced

/testing-fuzzing input-shape lens — when a method takes an enum-
like &str and only special-cases one value, fuzzing the input
trivially exposes the silent fallback. Same pattern as br-e6937
(str.pad), br-7bc60 (resample freq), etc.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 10706b1 — Series/DataFrame sort_values_na now validates na_position per pandas. Validation lifted into 3 call sites. 1695 passed / 0 failed.

title: fp-types period_range builder (epoj Phase 2)
description:
Phase 2 follow-up to br-epoj (Period scaffolding shipped in commit b646d88).

**Scope.** Add `pub fn period_range(start, periods)` to fp-types matching `pd.period_range(start, periods=N, freq=...)` for the count-based form. Returns N consecutive periods starting at `start`.

**Reference behavior (pandas 2.2):**
- `pd.period_range(start='2024Q1', periods=4, freq='Q')` → `PeriodIndex(['2024Q1', '2024Q2', '2024Q3', '2024Q4'])`.
- `periods=0` → empty PeriodIndex.
- Frequency comes from the start Period itself (don't redundantly pass it).

**FrankenPandas state.** Phase 1 shipped Period struct + PeriodFreq enum + shift / diff / cmp_same_freq. No range builder. Pure ordinal arithmetic — no chrono needed in Phase 2.

**Fix shape (purely additive in fp-types):**
- `pub fn period_range(start: Period, periods: usize) -> Vec<Period>` — N periods starting from `start.ordinal`.
- 6 unit tests: zero periods, one period, sequential ordinals, freq preserved, large N, end-vs-start (start<>end variants live in Phase 3 once calendar arithmetic lands).

**Blast radius.** Purely additive in fp-types. Zero call-site touches. fp-types stays chrono-free.

**Severity.** LOW — Period is foundational scaffolding; range builder is the natural next step.

title: fd90.43: reflect_table should not redundantly call table_schema via primary_key_columns
description: fd90.40's reflect_table calls table_schema explicitly, then calls primary_key_columns which (via fd90.25's default impl) calls table_schema again. That's 2 round-trips for the same metadata. For real PG/MySQL backends each call is a network round-trip; for the SqlInspector facade meant to be efficient, this is wasteful. Fix: derive the primary-key column list directly from the SqlTableSchema we already have, rather than calling primary_key_columns. Logic mirrors the default impl's filter+sort: pull columns where primary_key_ordinal is Some(_), sort by ordinal, return the names. Pure efficiency fix — no behavior change visible to callers. Test: existing reflect_table tests still assert pk='id' / pk=['id'] correctness; add a recording stub test that counts table_schema calls and verifies it's exactly 1.

title: [HIGH] conformance: Categorical dtype has 1 fixture for ~50+ pandas methods — README 'metadata layer' claim under-covered
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Coverage Accounting Matrix rule — specifically to pandas's Categorical dtype surface.

**Observation.** README capability row: "Categorical dtype | Yes | Yes | Yes (metadata layer)". README prose claims 50+ string ops and pandas-compatible cat accessor.

Reality check:
- grep for "cat_" / "categorical" in fixture `"operation"` fields across all 1249 fixtures returns exactly ONE operation name: `series_categorical_from_codes`.
- That's ONE fixture covering ONE constructor.

pandas's Categorical public surface (from pd.Categorical.__dict__ introspection):
  Constructors (6): Categorical(values), Categorical(values, categories=, ordered=),
                   Categorical.from_codes(codes, categories, ordered=),
                   pd.CategoricalDtype, pd.CategoricalIndex, Series(..., dtype="category")
  Properties (5):  .categories, .codes, .ordered, .dtype, .name
  Mutation (7):    .add_categories, .remove_categories, .remove_unused_categories,
                   .rename_categories, .reorder_categories, .set_categories,
                   .as_ordered / .as_unordered
  Comparison (4):  __eq__, __ne__, .equals, ordered-comparison (<, >, <=, >=)
  Ordering (4):    .sort_values, .argsort, .min, .max
  Set ops (3):     union_categoricals, Categorical.difference, .isin
  Conversion (4):  .to_list, .tolist, .astype, .map
  Accessor (15+):  .cat.categories, .cat.codes, .cat.add_categories,
                   .cat.rename_categories, .cat.remove_unused_categories, etc.
  GroupBy interaction: groupby(cat_col, observed=True/False), unobserved-category handling
  Concat interaction: pd.concat([cat1, cat2]) — union_categoricals semantics
  Arithmetic interaction: "By design (matches pandas)" per README — but DISC-003 says "tests check error category" — do we test that arithmetic on Categorical raises TypeError like pandas does? Uncovered.

**Gap.** ~50+ categorical-relevant pandas methods/properties; **we have fixtures for 1**. Coverage ratio at this feature area: ~2%.

**Cross-reference with existing code:**
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:45 defines pub struct CategoricalMetadata
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:6369 has `Series::cat()` returning Option<CategoricalAccessor>
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:8993 defines pub struct CategoricalAccessor
- fp-frame/src/lib.rs:8998+ has impl CategoricalAccessor with the accessor methods

The Rust SIDE has ~15 public cat accessor methods. The CONFORMANCE SIDE has 1 fixture.

**Skill rule violated:** "Before claiming conformance, prove it" + Coverage Accounting Matrix (unknown gaps = not conformant per the skill). We can't answer "does our Categorical match pandas behavior on groupby(..., observed=False)?" because we've never tested it.

**Severity.** HIGH — Categorical is called out in the README hero row. Currently the conformance test equivalent is "we export an API named .cat(), it returns Some." A user porting code that uses cat-specific semantics (ordered=True sort, set_categories re-indexing) hits divergence.

**Fix shape:**

1. Generate a `FP-P2D-CAT-NNN` packet suite with fixtures per categorical operation class. First-wave priorities (matching pandas docs' method signatures):
   - FP-P2D-CAT-001..010: constructor variants (Categorical with ordered=True/False, explicit categories, from_codes, dtype string "category")
   - FP-P2D-CAT-011..020: mutation methods (add_categories on ordered vs unordered, remove_unused_categories semantics for NaN codes)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-021..030: comparison (equals across ordering, equals across category-order differences)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-031..040: sort_values (ordered=True respects category order, ordered=False falls back to lex order)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-041..050: groupby interaction (observed=True drops unused; observed=False keeps empty groups — pandas 2.1+ changed this default)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-051..060: concat / union (dtype-consistent categories, mismatched categories raise or coerce)
   - FP-P2D-CAT-061..070: error-path (arithmetic on Categorical → TypeError; tie to br-niwb error coverage)

2. Oracle extension: pandas_oracle.py currently has no op_series_categorical_* handlers beyond from_codes. Add the ~30 new op_* handlers in a single pass tied to br-urhy (oracle tests) — the same commit can add unit-test coverage for each handler.

3. Live-oracle conformance tests in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs: one `live_oracle_series_cat_*_matches_pandas` test per op family.

4. Update README capability row once the new fixtures land:
   `Categorical dtype | Yes | Yes | Yes (metadata + conformance parity; 50+ fixtures across ordered, unordered, groupby-observed, union semantics)`.

5. DISCREPANCIES.md entry if any specific pandas-Categorical corner is intentionally NOT matched (e.g. pandas 2.1's `observed=True` default change — we may intentionally stay on `observed=False` for stability).

Blast radius: ~60-70 new tiny fixtures (~150KB total), ~30 new Python oracle handlers (~400 lines in pandas_oracle.py), ~30 new Rust live-oracle tests (~1200 lines in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs). No changes to fp-frame source.

Prerequisites:
- br-d6xa (CI pandas install) for live-oracle tests to actually run in CI.
- br-urhy (oracle tests) — the ~30 new handlers need their own test coverage per handler.

**Related:**
- br-zk1j (API matrix) — Categorical is a large "Partial" entry today; becomes "Full" when this bead lands.
- br-nato (RequirementLevel) — most Categorical ops are MUST (users catch specific behavior); tag accordingly.
- br-niwb (error-path coverage) — the arithmetic-on-Categorical error path belongs to the 7.7%→30% uplift niwb proposes.
close_reason: Phase 1 tracker: CATEGORICAL_COVERAGE.md with 7-phase roadmap (constructors / mutation / comparison / sort / groupby / concat / error paths). Fixture + oracle-handler implementation deferred to phase-by-phase slices.

title: fd90.10: SqlConnection::quote_identifier overridable
description:
fd90 sub-bead: add SqlConnection::quote_identifier as a backend-overridable identifier-quoting method.

**Scope.** Currently fp-io quotes SQL identifiers with the ANSI `"..."` form via the free fn `quote_sql_ident` (line ~3792). This works for SQLite + PostgreSQL but breaks for MySQL/MariaDB (which require backticks) and MSSQL (which uses brackets). The existing `parameter_marker` precedent already moved bind-marker generation onto the trait; this slice does the same for identifier quoting.

**Phase 1 (this slice — purely additive, default impl):**
- `SqlConnection::quote_identifier(&self, ident: &str) -> Result<String, IoError>`. Default impl: ANSI double-quote with embedded `"` doubled (delegates to existing free fn `quote_sql_ident` for behavior parity).
- rusqlite override: explicit ANSI form (same as default; documents the choice + makes the override surface visible for review).

**Non-scope (deferred):**
- MySQL backtick override (lands with fd90.11 mysql backend impl).
- MSSQL bracket override (no MSSQL backend bead filed).
- Migrating call sites from the free `quote_sql_ident` to `conn.quote_identifier(...)` — that's mechanical but touches many sites and lands as fd90.12.

**Tests.** 3 unit tests: rusqlite override produces ANSI quoting; embedded quote escaping doubles correctly; null-byte rejection (preserves the existing security check).

**Blast radius.** Pure additive in fp-io. Zero call-site touches.

**Severity.** P2.

title: [HIGH] ci: no cargo-audit / cargo-deny in CI — supply-chain CVEs in arrow/calamine/rusqlite deps go undetected
description:
Supply-chain security audit of .github/workflows/ci.yml + workspace deps.

**Observation.**
- Workspace pulls arrow 58, parquet 58, calamine 0.34, rusqlite 0.39, bumpalo 3.20, rust_xlsxwriter 0.94, serde 1.0.228, thiserror 2.0.18, regex 1.12, chrono 0.4, and ~dozens of transitive deps (see Cargo.lock 58 KB).
- ci.yml has format, check, clippy, workspace test, conformance test, bench, phase2c-gate, governance-gate. It does NOT run:
    cargo audit                  — security advisories against crates.io / rustsec DB
    cargo deny check advisories  — same, plus license + version-ban policies
    cargo outdated               — alerts on unbumped deps
- `cargo audit` is not even installed locally (exit: "no such command: audit").
- No deny.toml exists in the repo.
- No dependabot / renovate config in .github/ (only ci.yml, no dependabot.yml).

**Consequence.** A published CVE against arrow, calamine, rusqlite, or any transitive dep lands without notice. The lib-updater workflow runs on demand (see UPGRADE_LOG.md) rather than on a schedule; between sweeps the workspace can be exposed for weeks. The 2024 supply-chain-attack pattern (backdoored npm deps, etc.) has no defense here for Rust equivalents — the RustSec Advisory DB would catch them via cargo-audit.

**Severity.** HIGH — public-API library surface + IO parsers that consume untrusted bytes (CSV/JSON/Parquet/Excel/Feather) + SQL IO means any parser CVE in a dep is directly reachable from library callers.

**Fix shape:**
1. CI job `security`: runs `cargo install --locked cargo-audit` (cached), then `cargo audit --deny warnings`. Fails CI on any advisory.
2. CI job `licenses`: adds deny.toml with allowed licenses (MIT, Apache-2.0, BSD-3-Clause, ISC, MPL-2.0, Unicode-3.0), then `cargo deny check licenses advisories bans sources`. Fails CI on any policy violation.
3. `.github/dependabot.yml` for weekly deps refresh — groups by ecosystem so PRs are tractable.
4. Optional: `cargo update --dry-run -Zunstable-options --locked` on nightly as a drift canary.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+1 security job ~25 lines), deny.toml (+1 file ~40 lines), dependabot.yml (+1 file ~30 lines). No source code changes.

Parallel consideration: br-frankenpandas-8irq (pandas version pin) is a related supply-chain concern on the Python side.
close_reason: Added CI supply-chain audit jobs, deny.toml policy, weekly dependabot updates, and conformance guards

title: [testing-metamorphic] [HIGH] DataFrame::sample missing same frac validation as Series::sample (br-c1db4); cross-API consistency broken
description:
## Finding (cross-API consistency lens)

Series::sample was fixed in br-frankenpandas-c1db4 to reject:
- non-finite frac (NaN, Inf)
- frac < 0
- frac > 1 (when replace=false)

DataFrame::sample (line 26123) has the IDENTICAL code path and the
SAME missing validation:

  let sample_n = match (n, frac) {
      (None, Some(f)) => (total as f64 * f).round() as usize,
      ...
  };

Rust's f64-to-usize saturates negative and NaN to 0 → silent empty
result. Pandas raises ValueError consistently across Series and
DataFrame; fp-frame breaks the cross-API consistency property:

  s.sample(None, Some(-0.5), false, None)   → Err (after c1db4)
  df.sample(None, Some(-0.5), false, None)  → Ok (empty) — wrong

## Reproducer

  let df = DataFrame::from_dict(&["a"], vec![("a", vec![Scalar::Int64(1), Scalar::Int64(2)])])?;
  df.sample(None, Some(-0.5), false, None);
    // before: Ok(empty df)
    // after:  Err("sample: frac must be >= 0")

## Fix

Lift the same validation block from Series::sample (c1db4) into
DataFrame::sample. Identical code shape; same error messages.

## Tests

- df.sample with negative frac → Err
- df.sample with NaN frac → Err
- df.sample with infinite frac → Err
- df.sample with frac > 1, replace=false → Err
- df.sample with frac > 1, replace=true → Ok (oversampling allowed)
- df.sample with frac in [0, 1], replace=false → Ok (regression guard)

## Why surfaced

/testing-metamorphic — the cross-API property "Series::sample and
DataFrame::sample should accept/reject the same (n, frac, replace)
combinations" was broken after c1db4 fixed only Series. Closing the
metamorphic gap by lifting the same validation.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 2e16683 — DataFrame::sample now validates frac identically to Series::sample (cross-API consistency, c1db4 sister). 1683 passed / 0 failed.

title: [porting-to-rust] [HIGH] Series/DataFrame astype_safe silently accept invalid `errors`; pandas validates {raise, ignore, coerce}
description:
## Finding (porting pandas validation)

Series::astype_safe (line 4297) and DataFrame::astype_safe (line
21088) / astype_columns_safe (line 21058) match `errors` against
"ignore" and "coerce", with a wildcard fallback that calls the
default astype (which itself raises on failure):

  match errors {
      "ignore" => self.astype(dtype).or_else(|_| Ok(self.clone())),
      "coerce" => { /* coerce path */ }
      _ => self.astype(dtype),  // ← silently accepts "raise" AND any junk
  }

Pandas validates strictly: only {"raise", "ignore", "coerce"} are
accepted. Anything else raises ValueError:

  s.astype('int', errors='wat')
  → ValueError: errors must be 'raise', 'ignore', or 'coerce'

fp-frame silently treats "WAT", "ignore_case_typo", etc. as "raise".

Sister gap to br-e6937 (str.pad), br-2ca7d (sort_values na_position),
br-7bc60 (resample freq), and the broader validation-stub family.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into()], vec![Scalar::Int64(1)])?;
  s.astype_safe(DType::Int64, "WAT");
    // before: Ok([Int64(1)]) — silent "raise" semantics
    // after:  Err("astype: errors must be 'raise', 'ignore', or 'coerce', got 'WAT'")

## Fix

Validate `errors` first, then dispatch. The check distinguishes
"raise" (call astype with no recovery), "ignore" (recover on error),
"coerce" (per-cell coercion). Three call sites:
- Series::astype_safe
- DataFrame::astype_safe (delegates to astype_columns_safe)
- DataFrame::astype_columns_safe

  match errors {
      "raise" => self.astype(dtype),
      "ignore" => self.astype(dtype).or_else(|_| Ok(self.clone())),
      "coerce" => { /* coerce */ }
      other => Err(FrameError::CompatibilityRejected(format!(
          "astype: errors must be 'raise', 'ignore', or 'coerce', got '{other}'"
      ))),
  }

## Tests

- astype_safe with errors='wat' → Err
- astype_safe with errors='RAISE' (case mismatch) → Err
- astype_safe with errors='raise' → Ok (regression guard for the
  newly-explicit raise branch)
- astype_safe with errors='ignore' / 'coerce' → Ok (regression
  guards)
- DataFrame::astype_safe with errors='wat' → Err

## Why surfaced

title: [HIGH] provenance: no commit-signing policy or CI signature verification — supply-chain attestation missing before crates.io release
description:
Commit provenance + signing audit.

**Observation.** grep for `gpgsign|signed|ssh` in AGENTS.md returns nothing. grep for `commit.gpgsign` in the repo returns nothing. The Skill system prompt warned me explicitly: "NEVER skip hooks (--no-gpg-sign) unless user requests" — implying signing MAY be configured — but nothing in the repo requires it.

Verified via `git log --show-signature -5`: no SIGNED entries. Recent commits include both me (cc-pandas via `git -c user.name="cc-pandas"`) and the upstream Dicklesworthstone identity. Neither is cryptographically attested.

**Context — why this matters for a public library:**
- crates.io accepts publishes from anyone with API credentials + a matching Cargo.toml author. It doesn't verify commit signatures.
- docs.rs builds whatever source the tag points at. No signature check.
- The multi-agent swarm has several authorial identities mingling in `git log`. Auditing "did a real human approve this commit?" requires either (a) trusting `Author:` fields (which anyone can spoof) or (b) requiring signed commits.
- When the PyO3 binding ships (br-diic) + the release workflow lands (br-4clx), frankenpandas becomes a supply-chain dependency for downstream projects. Users of the Python wheel have no way to verify the tag that built it came from a trusted identity.
- Even within the swarm: if one compromised agent identity starts committing malicious code, reverting requires distinguishing legit commits from attack commits.

**Severity.** HIGH — supply-chain provenance for a library that will publish to crates.io. Low overhead to add; high cost to retrofit after a provenance incident.

**Fix shape:**

1. Documented policy in AGENTS.md + CONTRIBUTING.md (br-6d5s):
   - All human contributors configure SSH commit signing (simpler than GPG, GitHub supports it since 2022):
       git config commit.gpgsign true
       git config gpg.format ssh
       git config user.signingkey ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
   - Swarm-agent identities publish their signing keys in a committed `AUTHORS.md` with public-key fingerprints. Identity-to-key mapping becomes auditable.
   - AGENTS.md gets a section: "How to prove your agent commits are yours, not spoofed."

2. CI gate:
   - ci.yml adds `- name: Verify commit signatures` running `git log --format='%G?' -1 <commit> | grep -q '^[GU]'` (G = good signature, U = good with unknown key).
   - Or: enforce via GitHub branch protection rule (toggle in repo settings) — requires signed commits to merge into main.

3. Release provenance:
   - br-4clx (release workflow) — tag signing: `git tag -s vX.Y.Z` with the release manager's key. crates.io publish reads the signed tag via the release-plz tooling; SLSA-level attestation becomes possible.
   - Consider Sigstore/cosign for supply-chain attestation alongside GPG signing — gives us a verifiable chain from source → tag → published crate.

4. Swarm agent identities:
   - Each agent that commits (cc-pandas, cod, etc.) has its own ssh signing key, committed public key in AUTHORS.md.
   - Commits tagged `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>` get the Co-Authored-By line verified via the primary author's signature chain.

Blast radius: AGENTS.md addition (+~30 lines), AUTHORS.md (new, ~20 lines for current identities), ci.yml (+1 step ~10 lines), repo settings (branch protection toggle — requires repo admin). Zero source code changes.

Related:
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — policy docs live here.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — private disclosure channel; the corresponding public provenance is addressed by this bead.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — tag signing integrated with the publish pipeline.
- br-36qc (cargo-audit) + br-hg60 (secret scan) — the same supply-chain hygiene family.

title: [testing-metamorphic] [HIGH] DataFrame::transpose silently drops rows when source index has duplicate labels
description:
## Finding (metamorphic property: df.T.T == df)

DataFrame::transpose (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:21003) silently drops
data when the source DataFrame has duplicate index labels.

The transpose builds new columns into a BTreeMap<String, Column> keyed
by row-label-as-string. Duplicate labels (e.g. index = [10, 10, 20])
collapse via BTreeMap::insert (last write wins). Then
DataFrame::normalize_column_order (line 17504) actively de-duplicates
the new_order Vec, removing any repeated entries. Result: a 3-row
1-col DataFrame transposes to a 1-row 2-col DataFrame — silent data
loss, no error returned.

## Reproducer

  let mut cols = BTreeMap::new();
  cols.insert("a".to_owned(),
              Column::new(DType::Int64, vec![1.into(), 2.into(), 3.into()])?);
  let df = DataFrame::new_with_column_order(
      Index::new(vec![10_i64.into(), 10_i64.into(), 20_i64.into()]),
      cols, vec!["a".to_owned()])?;
  let t = df.transpose()?;
  // Expected (pandas): 1-row 3-col with column names ["10", "10", "20"]
  // Actual (fp-frame): 1-row 2-col with column names ["10", "20"]

## Pandas semantics (target)

  >>> df = pd.DataFrame({'a': [1,2,3]}, index=[10,10,20])
  >>> df.T.shape
  (1, 3)  # all 3 columns preserved, with duplicate name '10'

## Why the bug exists

DataFrame's column store is BTreeMap<String, Column>, which CANNOT
hold duplicate column names by construction. Transpose's output
shape conflict (rows-with-duplicate-labels → columns-must-be-unique)
is silently resolved by BTreeMap::insert collapse, dropping data
without any error.

## Fix options considered

1. (preferred) Detect duplicate index labels at the top of transpose()
   and return Err with a clear message. Prevents silent data loss;
   user can dedupe their index first or use a different approach.

2. Disambiguate duplicate names by appending .1/.2 suffixes. Would
   preserve all data but invents column names not in pandas output —
   stronger divergence than option 1.

3. Migrate column store to a structure supporting duplicate keys.
   Major architectural change; out of scope for this bug.

Shipping option 1: explicit-not-implicit. Silent data loss is
strictly worse than an error a user can see and route around.

## Tests

- transpose with duplicate index labels returns Err
- transpose preserves all rows when index has no duplicates
- metamorphic property: df.transpose().transpose() == df (holds when
  no duplicates; verified separately for the no-duplicate case)
- error path: error message clearly identifies the cause

## How surfaced

/testing-metamorphic skill applied to the involution property
df.T.T == df. Property holds trivially when index has no duplicates;
breaks under duplicate-label input — that's the canary that revealed
the silent-collapse bug.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 0bb9ded — transpose now rejects duplicate index labels with a clear error rather than silently collapsing columns. 1560 passed / 0 failed.

title: fd90.42: tighten autoincrement detection for composite PKs (defect from fd90.37)
description: fd90.37's autoincrement detection has a documented false positive: composite PK where the first column is INTEGER gets autoincrement=true even though SQLite's rowid-alias rule requires the column to be the SOLE primary key. Fix: tighten the heuristic from per-row 'pk==1 && type==INTEGER' to a two-pass check: first count columns with pk>0; only mark a column as autoincrement when it has pk==1 AND it's the only one with pk>0 AND its declared_type is INTEGER. The existing test sql_table_schema_autoincrement_not_set_for_composite_pk_integer documented the false positive — flip its assertion to verify the correct behavior. Add a confirming test that single-PK INTEGER and composite-PK with INTEGER first behave correctly. No struct API change — pure heuristic refinement inside the rusqlite override.

title: [LOW] collab: no .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ — bug/feature/conformance/perf reports not structured at filing time
description:
GitHub issue templates audit.

**Observation.** `ls .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` → directory does not exist. `.github/` contains only the `workflows/` directory.

**Context.** br-frankenpandas-6d5s (CODEOWNERS / PR template / CONTRIBUTING.md) umbrella-filed the PR-side collaboration infra. Issue templates were mentioned in that bead but not filed as a separate slice. Filing separately because the template structure differs materially from PR templates:

- Bug reports need a reproducer steps + expected-vs-actual structure + pandas-parity-divergence checkbox ("is this a conformance gap filed under DISCREPANCIES.md?").
- Feature requests have a different shape: "pandas method name / pandas docs link / minimal motivating example".
- Conformance divergence reports are a THIRD category — not bug (may be intentional) and not feature (existing behavior, just different). These route to DISCREPANCIES.md, not to the bug tracker directly.
- Performance regressions are a FOURTH category — reproducer should include `perf_baselines.rs` run output, baseline numbers, and ideally a bisect starting commit.

**Severity.** LOW — issue triage quality improves but nothing currently breaks without templates. GitHub's default "blank issue" works. Calibrating honestly.

**Fix shape.** `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/` directory with four YAML templates:

1. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.yml` — GitHub issue forms (YAML, not markdown) with:
   - title: "[BUG] "
   - dropdown: Affected crate (fp-frame / fp-io / fp-conformance / ...)
   - textarea: Reproducer
   - textarea: Expected behavior (include pandas reference if relevant)
   - textarea: Actual behavior
   - checkbox: Have you checked DISCREPANCIES.md?
   - checkbox: Have you checked the bead tracker (br ready)?

2. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature_request.yml`:
   - title: "[FEATURE] "
   - input: pandas method name being mirrored
   - input: link to pandas docs
   - textarea: minimal motivating example
   - dropdown: Affected crate

3. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/conformance_divergence.yml`:
   - title: "[CONFORMANCE] "
   - textarea: Input that diverges
   - textarea: pandas output
   - textarea: frankenpandas output
   - dropdown: expected classification (MUST / SHOULD / MAY per br-nato)
   - note: "This issue will be considered for DISCREPANCIES.md entry."

4. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/perf_regression.yml`:
   - title: "[PERF] "
   - textarea: Before/after timing numbers
   - input: git bisect-good commit SHA
   - textarea: Reproducer (ideally a perf_baselines.rs-style bench)
   - dropdown: Affected operation class (GroupBy / Merge / Rolling / IO / ...)

5. `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/config.yml`:
   - `blank_issues_enabled: false` — forces users into one of the 4 templates.
   - `contact_links:` — pointer to the bead tracker + "if you're an agent, use br create".

Blast radius: 1 new directory + 5 small YAML files (~30 lines each = ~150 lines total). Zero source changes. Zero CI changes.

Prerequisites:
- None urgent. Can ship independently.
- br-6d5s (CODEOWNERS + PR template umbrella) — natural coordination point.

**Related:**
- br-6d5s (CODEOWNERS / PR template / CONTRIBUTING.md umbrella) — this bead fills in the issue-template slice left open.
- br-niwb (error-path conformance) — the conformance_divergence template captures exactly the bug class niwb fixes at the test layer.
- br-nato (RequirementLevel) — the conformance template's MUST/SHOULD/MAY dropdown uses nato's taxonomy.
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md) — security issues go through the private disclosure channel per 8k1i, NOT through these public templates. config.yml's contact_links should mention that.
close_reason: .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/ already shipped on main (5 yaml templates + config.yml).

title: [HIGH] fp-conformance 8 cascading aggregate tests fail on opaque assert_is_green; need fixture-level surface
description:
Workspace test (rch exec -- cargo test --workspace --all-targets) shows 351 passed / 8 failed in fp-conformance. The 8 failures all panic with terse messages like 'assertion failed: report.is_green()' or 'CI PIPELINE: FAILED (0/1 gates passed)' — the underlying fixture-level mismatches (37 cases per packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases) are buried inside the report payload only when {report:?} is included.

Affected tests (all in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs):
- ci_gate_g6_conformance_evaluates (line 29042)
- ci_pipeline_conformance_only_runs_and_reports (line 29063)
- differential_suite_produces_structured_drift (line 27256)
- failure_forensics_report_clean_when_all_pass (line 28519)
- e2e_suite_runs_full_pipeline (line 28054)
- grouped_reports_are_partitioned_per_packet (line 23210)
- packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases (line 20547)
- differential_all_packets_green (line 27457)

Fix: replace the bare assert!(report.is_green()) at each site with a structured failure dump that emits per-case (packet_id, case_id, mismatch_class, mismatch) when report.failed > 0. Pattern already used at 20943 in packet_filter_runs_groupby_aggregate_matrix_packet ('expected report green: {report:?}'). Apply the same pattern to all 8 sites. Bonus: write the dump to a JSONL artifact under artifacts/ so the reaper can ingest it.

Impact: turns 8 'opaque cascade' failures into actionable per-case records that downstream agents can pick off without re-running each top-level test.
