We already have dataframe_to_record_batch and record_batch_to_dataframe conversion utilities (used by Parquet I/O). Arrow IPC/Feather is the recommended columnar exchange format (faster than CSV/JSON, more portable than Parquet for simple cases). pandas recommends Feather over HDF5.
close_reason: Implemented Arrow IPC / Feather v2 read/write I/O. Added: write_feather_bytes/read_feather_bytes (in-memory), write_feather/read_feather (file-based), write_ipc_stream_bytes/read_ipc_stream_bytes (streaming format). DataFrameIoExt: to_feather_file/to_feather_bytes. Reuses existing dataframe_to_record_batch/record_batch_to_dataframe conversions. 7 new tests. All 65 fp-io tests pass, workspace clippy clean.

title: [mock-code-finder] [HIGH] Series::nlargest/nsmallest silently filter out Utf8 values via to_f64.ok().map(); pandas supports lex ordering
description:
## Finding (mock-code-finder lens, silent-filter stub)

Sister gap to br-frankenpandas-7db78 (idxmin/idxmax Utf8 fix), but
WORSE because the failure mode is silent rather than Err:

Series::nlargest (line 5646), nsmallest (line 5680), and
nlargest_smallest_impl (line 5728) extract f64 keys via:

  filter_map(|(i, val)| {
      if val.is_missing() { None }
      else { val.to_f64().ok().map(|v| (i, v)) }
  })

For Utf8 input, val.to_f64() returns Err → filter_map returns None →
EVERY Utf8 value is silently filtered out. Result: empty Series,
not an Err.

Pandas:
  pd.Series(['cat','apple','banana']).nsmallest(2) → ['apple','banana']
  pd.Series(['cat','apple','banana']).nlargest(2) → ['cat','banana']

fp-frame: returns empty Series — silent data loss.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Utf8("cat".into()), Scalar::Utf8("apple".into()),
           Scalar::Utf8("banana".into())])?;
  s.nsmallest(2).unwrap();
    // before: empty Series (silent filter)
    // after:  Series with ["apple", "banana"]

## Fix

Replace the f64-based comparator with Scalar::semantic_cmp, which
works on all comparable types (Int64, Float64, Bool, Utf8,
Timedelta64, with Int64↔Float64 cross-type ordering preserved).
This eliminates the silent-filter stub.

  let mut indexed: Vec<(usize, &Scalar)> = self
      .column.values().iter().enumerate()
      .filter(|(_, val)| !val.is_missing())
      .collect();
  indexed.sort_by(|a, b| a.1.semantic_cmp(b.1));  // or reversed for nlargest
  indexed.truncate(n);

Numeric performance is essentially the same (semantic_cmp is a
match arm, not a heavy abstraction). No allocation change for the
common case.

## Tests

- nlargest/nsmallest on Utf8 returns lex-largest/smallest values
- nlargest/nsmallest on Utf8 with mixed Null/Utf8 skips nulls
- existing Int64/Float64 tests must remain green
- nlargest_keep / nsmallest_keep with 'first'/'last'/'all' on Utf8
- metamorphic property: s.nlargest(s.len()) reverses sort_values(asc=true)
  values (modulo null position)

## Why surfaced

/mock-code-finder lens — the to_f64.ok().map() pattern is a textbook
silent-filter stub. Earlier sister gaps (idxmin/idxmax 7db78,
min/max 83c2a, cummin/cummax c4898) all errored on Utf8; this one
was even worse — silent empty-result failure, harder to detect.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 6eb918d — nlargest/nsmallest/nlargest_smallest_impl now use Scalar::semantic_cmp; Utf8 supported, no more silent-filter empty-result data loss. 1637 passed / 0 failed.

title: fd90.129: TypeError + GroupByError not re-exported from frankenpandas (other 6 of 8 documented errors are)
description:
fd90.74-128 README audit. Error Architecture table at lines 821-830 documents 8 error types as part of the 'frankenpandas error surface' that users will pattern-match. The example at line 835 imports them via 'use frankenpandas::{IoError, FrameError, ExprError};' but two of the 8 documented error types aren't re-exported:

- TypeError (fp-types) — defined at crates/fp-types/src/lib.rs:301, pub
- GroupByError (fp-groupby) — defined at crates/fp-groupby/src/lib.rs:91, pub

Verified at crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs: searches for 'TypeError' and 'GroupByError' return nothing in the re-export section. A user reading the error architecture and trying to pattern-match would hit:
  error[E0432]: unresolved import 'frankenpandas::TypeError'

The other 6 error types (ColumnError, IndexError, FrameError, ExprError, JoinError, IoError) ARE re-exported. fd90.121/125/126/127/128 set the precedent that the frankenpandas top-level surface should match the README's documented surface.

Fix: re-export TypeError from fp_types and GroupByError from fp_groupby at the top level of frankenpandas. Errors don't need to be in the prelude (matches existing pattern — IoError etc are top-level only), but they do need to be reachable via 'use frankenpandas::{TypeError, GroupByError}'. Pure re-export expansion.

title: fp-types Timestamp struct + Timedelta arithmetic (4r56 Phase 2)
description:
Phase 2 of 4r56 (Timedelta arithmetic shipped in commit 68e2087).

**Scope.** Add a `Timestamp` struct to fp-types providing:
- Nanosecond-precision i64 storage (Unix epoch anchored).
- NaT sentinel (i64::MIN) for missingness.
- Arithmetic: `Timestamp + Timedelta -> Timestamp`, `Timestamp - Timestamp -> Timedelta`, `Timestamp - Timedelta -> Timestamp`.
- Saturation on overflow; NaT propagation.
- Serde round-trip + semantic_eq.

**Non-scope (deferred to Phase 3).**
- TZ-aware Timestamp: a `tz: Option<String>` field is reserved now, but TZ-dependent arithmetic (DST transitions, tz conversion via chrono_tz) lands in a separate slice that pulls chrono_tz into fp-types's dep graph.
- ISO 8601 parsing / strftime display: chrono integration stays in downstream crates; Phase 2 ships constructor-from-nanos only.
- Display: Phase 2 uses a debug-ish format `"Timestamp[<nanos>, UTC]"`; pandas notation lands in Phase 3.

**Blast radius.** Purely additive in fp-types (no existing call-site touched). Zero dep-graph change (no chrono in fp-types yet).

**Tests (10):**
- Construction from nanos.
- Timestamp + Timedelta arithmetic.
- Timestamp - Timestamp = Timedelta.
- Timestamp - Timedelta arithmetic.
- NaT propagation (Timestamp or Timedelta is NaT).
- Saturation on overflow.
- semantic_eq NaT-aware.
- Ordering (non-NaT comparison respects chronological order).
- Serde round-trip.
- Compile-time Send+Sync assertion.

title: [HIGH] REVIEW: mixed naive/offset to_datetime utc parity drift
description:
Found while building frankenpandas-ow57 datetime conformance matrix.

Failing input: series_to_datetime, datetime_utc=true, values=["2024-01-15 10:30:00", "2024-01-15 10:30:00+05:30"].

Expected from live pandas oracle: ["2024-01-15 10:30:00+00:00", null].

Actual from FrankenPandas: ["2024-01-15 10:30:00+00:00", "2024-01-15 05:00:00+00:00"].

Impact: pandas inferred-format coercion rejects the offset-aware second value in this mixed naive/offset sequence, while FrankenPandas accepts and normalizes it.
close_reason: Fixed to_datetime mixed naive/offset UTC parity drift

title: fd90.221: Expose Bayesian Runtime Policy inspection types (DecisionRecord/etc.)
description:
fd90.221: Expose Bayesian Runtime Policy inspection surface (7 fp-runtime types).

The README's "Bayesian Runtime Policy" section (lines 378-403) documents the EvidenceLedger as user-inspectable: "Every decision is recorded in the EvidenceLedger with full trace: timestamp, mode, prior, posterior, Bayes factor, and the evidence terms that drove it."

EvidenceLedger.records() returns &[DecisionRecord]. DecisionRecord has public fields whose types are all hidden from frankenpandas:
- mode: RuntimeMode
- action: DecisionAction
- issue: CompatibilityIssue
- metrics: DecisionMetrics
- evidence: Vec<EvidenceTerm>

Plus IssueKind (variant enum on CompatibilityIssue) and GalaxyBrainCard (return type of decision_to_card).

User code:
    for record in ledger.records() {
        match record.action {
            DecisionAction::Allow => ...,    // unreachable
            DecisionAction::Reject => ...,
            ...
        }
    }

cannot compile from frankenpandas alone.

Scope:
- Add to top-level fp_runtime re-export: RuntimeMode, DecisionAction, IssueKind, CompatibilityIssue, EvidenceTerm, DecisionMetrics, DecisionRecord, GalaxyBrainCard, decision_to_card
- Add to prelude
- Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.

title: fd90.139: fd90.138's where_mask_df_other duplicates pre-existing where_cond_df — convert to delegator
description:
fd90.74-138 self-review found a regression in fd90.138: the new where_mask_df_other (line 18640) is functionally identical to the pre-existing where_cond_df (line 18545). Both:
  - take (cond: &Self, other: &Self)
  - return Result<Self, FrameError>
  - implement: where cond=true keep self, where cond=false take from other (with NaN for missing)
  - use the same alignment + per-column reindex pattern

I missed where_cond_df in the fd90.136 audit because I grep'd for 'where_mask' not 'where_cond'. The codebase has two parallel naming schemes (where_cond / where_mask) for similar operations.

Fix-forward (don't revert fd90.138 history; that already shipped to origin):
1. Convert where_mask_df_other into a thin delegator that calls where_cond_df. This keeps the API symmetry that fd90.136 wanted (mask_df_other ↔ where_mask_df_other) without duplicating code.
2. Keep the new test (it exercises the public surface — same input passes through delegation).
3. Update the README to mention both names exist (where_cond_df is the canonical implementation; where_mask_df_other is the symmetry alias for users matching mask_df_other).

Verify with cargo build -p fp-frame + cargo test -p fp-frame --lib where (all 4 should pass: df_where_mask_df, df_where_mask_df_ignores_extra_rows, df_where_mask_df_other_with_dataframe_replacement, plus existing where_cond_df tests). Pure refactor — no behavior change.

title: [porting-to-rust] [HIGH] Series::sum/prod always return Float64; pandas preserves Int64/Bool with numpy wrap-on-overflow semantics
description:
## Finding (porting-to-rust lens)

Sister gap to the just-shipped Series::min/max fix (br-frankenpandas-e7d61).

Series::sum (line 4273) and Series::prod (line 4573) use f64 accumulators
and return Scalar::Float64 unconditionally — silently casting Int64 and
Bool input to Float64 output, breaking dtype contract.

## Pandas semantics (target)

  >>> pd.Series([1, 2, 3]).sum()           # 6 (int64)
  >>> pd.Series([1, 2, 3]).prod()          # 6 (int64)
  >>> pd.Series([True, False, True]).sum() # 2 (int64) ← Bool→Int64
  >>> pd.Series([True, False, True]).prod()# 0 (int64)
  >>> pd.Series([], dtype='int64').sum()   # 0 (int64)
  >>> pd.Series([], dtype='int64').prod()  # 1 (int64)
  >>> pd.Series([2**62, 2**62]).sum()      # i64 wrap-on-overflow

## Why this requires more than a min/max-style branch

Unlike min/max (which just compare existing values and never overflow),
sum/prod have arithmetic accumulator semantics. Pandas matches numpy's
**wrap-on-overflow** for Int64:

  numpy.array([2**62, 2**62], dtype='int64').sum()
  # = -9223372036854775808 (i64 overflow wraps to MIN)

The faithful port uses i64::wrapping_add / wrapping_mul, NOT i64::checked_*
or saturating_*, to preserve numpy parity exactly.

## Fix

Branch on column.dtype() at top of sum/prod:

- DType::Int64: scan in i64, accumulate via wrapping_add (sum) or
  wrapping_mul (prod). Skip nulls. Empty series → 0 (sum) / 1 (prod).
  Return Scalar::Int64.

- DType::Bool: same as Int64, but cast each Bool(b) to i64::from(b).
  Pandas: bool sum is "count of true" (int64); bool prod is the
  logical-AND result (1 if all true else 0).

- Everything else: existing Float64 fallback (already correct for
  Float64 input).

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Int64(10), Scalar::Int64(20), Scalar::Int64(30)])?;
  s.sum()  // before: Scalar::Float64(60.0). after: Scalar::Int64(60)
  s.prod() // before: Scalar::Float64(6000.0). after: Scalar::Int64(6000)

  let b = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Bool(true), Scalar::Bool(false), Scalar::Bool(true)])?;
  b.sum()  // before: Scalar::Float64(2.0). after: Scalar::Int64(2)

## Tests

- sum on Int64 returns Int64
- prod on Int64 returns Int64
- sum on Bool returns Int64 (count of true)
- prod on Bool returns Int64 (1 if all true else 0)
- sum on empty Int64 series returns Int64(0)
- prod on empty Int64 series returns Int64(1)
- sum/prod on Float64 still returns Float64 (regression guard)
- Int64 wrap-on-overflow test: [i64::MAX, 1].sum() == i64::MIN
- Update existing series_sum_basic / series_sum_with_nulls tests that
  were pinning the buggy Float64 output

## Why surfaced now

/porting-to-rust skill — last tick (e7d61) I deferred sum/prod from
the min/max fix because they need wrap-on-overflow handling. This
tick ports the numpy semantics faithfully.
close_reason: Shipped fix in bf860c7 — Series::sum/prod preserve Int64/Bool dtype with numpy wrap-on-overflow. 1577 passed / 0 failed.

title: [mock-code-finder] [HIGH] Series::clip unconditionally outputs Float64 regardless of input dtype; pandas preserves Int64 when bounds fit
description:
## Finding (mock-code-finder lens, always-coerced-output stub)

Series::clip (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:4067) materializes its output
using `Scalar::Float64(clamped)` for every input value, regardless of
the input dtype. The function:

  let v = val.to_f64().map_err(ColumnError::from)?;
  let mut clamped = v;
  if let Some(lo) = lower && clamped < lo { clamped = lo; }
  if let Some(hi) = upper && clamped > hi { clamped = hi; }
  out.push(Scalar::Float64(clamped));

For Int64 input with integer bounds (or no bounds), this silently
casts to Float64 — a dtype divergence from pandas:

  >>> pd.Series([1,2,3,4]).clip(2, 3)
  0    2
  1    2
  2    3
  3    3
  dtype: int64    ← pandas preserves Int64 when bounds fit

  >>> pd.Series([1,2,3]).clip()
  0    1
  1    2
  2    3
  dtype: int64    ← no-bound case stays Int64

fp-frame returns Float64 in both cases, breaking dtype contract for
downstream callers that depend on the integer dtype (e.g. integer
indexing, hash join keys, integer-only arithmetic).

## Pandas behavior table

  Input dtype  | lower    | upper    | Pandas output dtype
  -------------+----------+----------+--------------------
  Int64        | None     | None     | Int64
  Int64        | Int64    | None     | Int64
  Int64        | None     | Int64    | Int64
  Int64        | Int64    | Int64    | Int64
  Int64        | Float64  | *        | Float64 (mixed-type promotion)
  Int64        | *        | Float64  | Float64
  Float64      | *        | *        | Float64

## Fix

In Series::clip, branch on input value type:
- If val is Scalar::Int64 AND both bounds are integer-valued (each
  is either None or an f64 that round-trips through i64 cleanly),
  produce Scalar::Int64 output.
- Otherwise, fall back to the existing Float64 path.

The integer-valued check: `lo.fract() == 0.0 && lo.is_finite() &&
lo >= i64::MIN as f64 && lo <= i64::MAX as f64`. Same for upper.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Int64(1), Scalar::Int64(5), Scalar::Int64(9)])?;
  let clipped = s.clip(Some(2.0), Some(7.0))?;
  // before: vec![Float64(2.0), Float64(5.0), Float64(7.0)]
  // after:  vec![Int64(2), Int64(5), Int64(7)]

## Tests

- clip on Int64 with integer bounds preserves Int64 dtype
- clip on Int64 with no bounds is a no-op (Int64 preserved)
- clip on Int64 with float (non-integral) bounds promotes to Float64
- clip on Float64 input stays Float64 (regression guard)
- clip on Int64 with None lower / Int upper preserves Int64

## Why this surfaced now

/mock-code-finder skill applied to non-test code; rg of self.clone()
fns and rg of `Ok(()) }` patterns turned up no stubs, but reading
through Series numerical helpers revealed clip always uses
Scalar::Float64 — a quiet "always-coerce" pattern that's a stub of
the dtype-preservation contract.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 85a7a45 — Series::clip now preserves Int64 dtype when bounds are integer-valued (or absent), matching pandas mixed-promotion rule. 1565 passed / 0 failed.

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: add fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch target (HashMap+Vec aggfunc routing)
description:
fuzz/fuzz_targets/ has fuzz_groupby_sum but no coverage for the variadic agg dispatch surface that just landed multiple beads (br-715r dict-of-list, br-no1t transform_fn callable, etc.). The match-arm dispatch in DataFrameGroupBy::agg/agg_list/agg_dict_list/agg_named is wide enough to harbor unhandled-variant panics that cargo test won't surface (e.g. unrecognized aggfunc names crash with FrameError but a panic in Series::skew/kurt with degenerate inputs would cascade).

Proposed target: fuzz_groupby_agg.rs with:
1. Synth mode: 1 group key col (Utf8) + 1 numeric col, 2-16 rows, group cardinality picked from input bytes.
2. Pick aggfunc set from a fixed enum: a single string ('sum'/'mean'/etc), a Vec<String> for agg_list, a HashMap<String, String> for agg, or a HashMap<String, Vec<String>> for agg_dict_list.
3. Run the corresponding agg method; if Ok, assert output rows == unique groups × outputs-per-input-col.
4. If Err, assert it is FrameError::CompatibilityRejected (no panic).

New fp-conformance pub fn fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes(input: &[u8]). Catches regressions like skew on len<3 groups where the underlying Series::skew can return NaN but the dispatch must propagate it cleanly.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: verified parallel agent's commit 3ee01b2 implements the bead spec. fp-conformance adds fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes (lib.rs:6895) which projects bytes into a groupby-ready frame (grp + val cols) plus a func list via fuzz_groupby_agg_funcs_from_bytes, then exercises all four agg-dispatch entry points via a mode_tag % 4 switch — agg_list(&Vec<&str>), agg(HashMap<col, func>), agg_dict_list(HashMap<col, Vec<func>>), and agg_named(Vec<(output, col, func)>). Goes beyond the bead's proposed HashMap + single agg routing. Invariant check is fuzz_validate_groupby_agg_result which asserts group count and column count match expectation. fuzz/Cargo.toml has the matching [[bin]] fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch block; fuzz_targets/fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch.rs wraps fp_conformance::fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes under libfuzzer_sys. Tests: 4/4 unit tests pass under rch (fuzz_groupby_agg_bytes_accepts_empty_input + ..._compat_rejected_seed + ..._supported_agg_list_seed + ..._named_aggregation_seed).

3. Test: run the script, verify FEATURE_PARITY.md gains a fresh table with 430 entries (or a compact aggregate by status).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Constraint check (cc-pandas 2026-06-07): the proposed hdf5-rust crate BINDS the C libhdf5 library (unsafe FFI + system C dependency) — in tension with the workspace's #![forbid(unsafe_code)] culture and the NO-GAPS no-C-linkage directive (its spirit: we don't take C library shortcuts). Precedent nuance: fp-io already depends on rusqlite (bundled C SQLite), so C-in-deps is not absolutely forbidden — but HDF5 is a much larger C surface. Options when this gets picked up: (a) accept the dep like rusqlite (document the exception), (b) a pure-Rust HDF5 reader subset (hdf5 format is complex; read-only basic datasets feasible), (c) defer in favor of Parquet/Arrow which are already supported pure-Rust. Recommend (c)+(a) discussion before any implementation.

title: [LOW] fp-conformance assert_excel_roundtrip helper diverges from public default to satisfy reader; document as test-only gap
description:
Reviewing 989b/mxho fixes (cc): both excel round-trip helpers were patched to write with index=false because the default reader doesn't promote the leading index column back. Public write_excel_bytes default remains pandas-aligned (index=true). This is a test-only divergence — the harness now exercises a non-default code path.

Risk: production users calling write_excel_bytes(default) and read_excel_bytes(default) will hit the same column-leak bug the harness sidesteps. The conformance suite no longer surfaces this regression because the harness avoids it.

Suggested follow-up:
1. Add a separate fixture (or augment FP-P2D-129/excel_round_trip_basic_strict) that writes with index=true and reads with the matching index_col so the default-pair round-trip is genuinely tested end-to-end.
2. Or: ship a read_excel_bytes default that auto-detects the leading index column emitted by write_excel_bytes (via a sentinel header or 'inferred index_col' option) so the defaults round-trip cleanly.

Low priority: doesn't block conformance-to-zero work, but the test harness is currently 'green for the wrong reason' on this surface.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: the inline comment at crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs:5020-5025 already documents the helper's divergence (index=false) from the public write_excel_bytes default (index=true), citing br-989b. Both suggested follow-ups (fixture with matching index_col, or reader-side index auto-detection) are product-level scope decisions rather than conformance-gate bugs and do not block conformance-to-zero. Deferring the follow-up as a documented known issue; reopen if the default write/read pair is reprioritized.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no memory/time bounds on fuzz harness — crafted input can OOM-kill or timeout-kill silently
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill's "No input size guards → OOM kills mask real bugs" anti-pattern + the related OUTPUT-size gap.

**Observation.** Every existing fuzz target has an INPUT size cap:
  fuzz_csv_parse.rs:        MAX_CSV_BYTES    = 64 KB
  fuzz_fixture_parse.rs:    MAX_FIXTURE_BYTES = 64 KB
  fuzz_feather_io.rs:       MAX_FEATHER_BYTES = 512 KB
  fuzz_excel_io.rs:         MAX_EXCEL_BYTES   = 512 KB
  fuzz_dataframe_eval.rs:   MAX_EVAL_FUZZ_BYTES = 256 KB
  etc.

Good — that's the skill's minimum defense (anti-pattern #2 fixed). But there is NO corresponding cap on:
- Peak allocation during parse (the parser can decompress / expand / materialize memory much larger than the input).
- Result DataFrame size (a parser that produces a 1B-row frame from a 64KB input is a logic bug but not a panic).
- Total wall-clock time per invocation (an O(n²) parser on a 64KB input at 10^9 ops = minutes).

**Concrete amplification vectors to worry about:**
- `zip`-compressed Excel/.xlsx payloads — a 512KB xlsx can legitimately decompress to 500 MB of sharedStrings XML. calamine's Excel reader mallocs during decompress.
- Deeply nested JSON in read_json_str — 256 KB of {"a":{"a":{"a":... reaches thousands of levels; serde_json can stack-overflow or allocate per-level frame. Our MAX_EVAL_FUZZ_BYTES cap helps with the INPUT dimension but not with the recursion-depth / heap-alloc dimension.
- Parquet rowgroup metadata that claims 2B rows in a single group — parquet reader attempts to preallocate a Vec<Int64> of that size → OOM.
- SQL queries (if fuzzed per sibling br-gpxk) constructing huge result sets via cross-join.

**Skill rules involved:**
- Anti-pattern #2: "No input size guards → OOM kills mask real bugs." Partially addressed (input guard exists) but the skill's intent is that we should also have a memory-limit / alloc-hook so OOM doesn't kill the fuzzer silently.
- Rule 1: 1000 exec/s minimum for parsers, 100 for stateful. Long-running crash inputs (not-quite-hang, not-quite-crash) violate this by slowing exec/s without emitting a crash. Time-limit the harness.

**Severity.** HIGH — we don't detect memory-DoS or time-DoS via crafted input. Real users running our library against untrusted data (a common pattern: an analytics pipeline accepting customer-uploaded CSVs) could be hit with a denial-of-service that our fuzz corpus can't surface. The README's fp-io positioning as a drop-in pandas reader means users will point it at adversarial inputs.

**Fix shape:**

1. New global wrapper macro / helper applied at the top of every fuzz_target! body:
   ```rust
   const MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN: usize = 16 * 1024 * 1024;   // 16 MB per invocation
   const MAX_WALL_CLOCK: Duration = Duration::from_millis(500);

   fuzz_target!(|data: &[u8]| {
       let _guard = cap_memory_and_time(MAX_ALLOC_PER_RUN, MAX_WALL_CLOCK);
       if data.len() > MAX_EXCEL_BYTES { return; }
       let _ = fp_conformance::fuzz_excel_io_bytes(data);
   });
   ```

2. `cap_memory_and_time` implementation options:
   - libFuzzer-level: `-rss_limit_mb=16 -timeout=1` flags. Simple, no code change beyond fuzz/Cargo.toml per-bin args.
   - Custom global allocator wrapping `std::alloc::System` with a runtime-accumulated byte counter; returns AllocError when over budget. Integrates with the invariant-checker pattern. More intrusive but catches logic bugs, not just DoS.
   - Both. libFuzzer flags for coarse RSS cap + tracking allocator for fine-grained tracking per test case.

3. For CSV/Excel/Parquet targets: add a per-format cap on the reader's internal `with_capacity` calls. Upstream crates (calamine, parquet) already accept bounds via config — route the bound through.

4. Regression test: once the cap is in place, add a "known-bomb" test case (handcrafted file that attempts to OOM the old path). Include as a permanent fuzz seed so the cap is always exercised.

Blast radius: fuzz/Cargo.toml (+~8 lines: rss_limit_mb + timeout per [[bin]]), new helper in fuzz/src/cap.rs (+~30 lines), 5-10 crafted regression-bomb seeds added to corpus. No changes to fp-* source.

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — the CI job needs to surface OOM / timeout as distinct failure categories (not silent kills).
- br-liai (differential vs pandas) — the differential oracle's python subprocess has its own memory policy; need to align to avoid Rust-succeeds / python-OOMs divergence.
- br-k05t (scale fixtures) — conformance at scale; sibling goal but separate mechanism (conformance tests, not fuzz).

title: [profiling] [HIGH] DataFrame binary ops (add/sub/mul/div/floordiv/mod/pow) waste 2×N×M Scalar clones on equal-shape inputs
description:
## Finding (static-analysis perf lens, allocation profile)

DataFrame::binary_df_op (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:27480) unconditionally calls
self.align_on_index(other, AlignMode::Outer) even when self.index == other.index
and self.column_order == other.column_order — the dominant case in user code
(`df + df`, `df1 + df2` from same pipeline).

The alignment round-trip costs:
1. position_map_first_ref() BTreeMap build (O(N log N))
2. Two Vec<Option<usize>> of size N
3. **2×N×M Scalar::clone() calls** materializing left/right aligned DataFrames
   that are byte-identical to self and other
4. Then the actual op loop does another N×M of work

The 2×N×M Scalar clones are pure waste — they are immediately consumed by the op
loop and discarded. For a 1M-row × 10-col Float64 frame this is 20M unnecessary
clones (~160MB of transient allocation) per `df.add(other)` call.

## Affected callers (7 hot ops)
- DataFrame::add_df / sub_df / mul_df / div_df
- DataFrame::floordiv_df / mod_df / pow_df

## Fix

Add a fast path at the top of binary_df_op: when self and other share index +
column_order + key set and have no duplicate labels, skip alignment entirely
and iterate self.columns / other.columns directly. Saves the alignment plan
build and the 2×N×M intermediate Scalar clones; result is identical.

Slow path (genuinely-misaligned frames) is unchanged.

## Repro
df = DataFrame with 1M rows, 10 Float64 columns, sorted RangeIndex
df.add(&df)  # before: 30M Scalar allocations; after: 10M

## Verification
- Existing tests: 4 dataframe arithmetic tests (test_dataframe_add_basic etc.)
  must remain green
- Add a test asserting the fast path produces results identical to the slow path
  on a hand-aligned input

## Why this surfaced now
Picked up via `/profiling-software-performance` reading binary_df_op +
align_on_index together; the aligned-rebuild-then-op-loop pattern is a
textbook double-pass that can be one-pass when shapes already match.
close_reason: Shipped fast path in 39b91b2 — equal-shape df+df now skips align_on_index, saving 2×N×M intermediate Scalar clones. 1551 passed / 0 failed.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] [HIGH] fp-io parse_scalar_with_options NA/true/false checks are O(|set|) per cell; sister gap to br-fcf5d (fp-frame)
description:
## Finding (consistency lens, sister gap to fp-frame fix)

After br-frankenpandas-fcf5d fixed fp-frame's CSV NA HashSet, the
cross-crate consistency property "fp-io and fp-frame CSV readers
should both use HashSet for NA detection" was broken. fp-io's
parse_scalar_with_options (crates/fp-io/src/lib.rs:761) has THREE
linear-scan checks per cell:

  let is_custom_na = na_values.iter().any(|na| na == trimmed);
  ...
  if true_values.iter().any(|value| value == trimmed) { ... }
  if false_values.iter().any(|value| value == trimmed) { ... }

Called per cell from append_csv_record in a hot per-row loop. For
N rows × M cols × (|na| + |true| + |false|): O(N × M × |sets|).
For N=1M, M=10, total set sizes ~30: ~3×10⁸ comparisons.

## Fix

Build HashSet<&str> for each set ONCE at the parent function's
top (before the per-row loop), then pass refs through
append_csv_record → parse_scalar_with_options.

Signatures change to:
  parse_scalar_with_options(... na_set: &HashSet<&str>,
                             true_set: &HashSet<&str>,
                             false_set: &HashSet<&str>, ...)

Inside, the three calls become O(1):
  let is_custom_na = na_set.contains(trimmed);
  if true_set.contains(trimmed) { ... }
  if false_set.contains(trimmed) { ... }

Total: O(|sets|) build + O(N × M) cell lookups.

## Tests

- Existing fp-io CSV reader tests must remain green
- (No new test added — fp-io is integration-tested via
  fp-conformance; the regression is about scaling behavior, and
  the existing read_csv tests confirm correctness)

## Why surfaced

/modes-of-reasoning consistency lens — fp-frame's CSV reader was
fixed in fcf5d using a HashSet; fp-io's parser remained on the
slow path. Cross-crate consistency property exposed the gap.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 824b969 — fp-io's parse_scalar_with_options now uses pre-built HashSets for NA/true/false detection. 476 / 0 fp-io, 1720 / 0 fp-frame.

title: fd90.112: README df.clip(f64, f64) wants Option<f64> + df.replace(&S, &S) wants &[(S, S)] slice
description:
fd90.74-111 README audit. Two arity/type mismatches in element-wise op examples:

1. Line 1049: 'let clipped = df.clip(0.0, 100.0)?;' but
   crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:3690 declares
   pub fn clip(&self, lower: Option<f64>, upper: Option<f64>) -> Result<Self, FrameError>
   The args are Option<f64>, not f64. A user copy-pasting hits:
   error[E0308]: expected enum 'Option', found floating-point number

2. Line 1060: 'let cleaned = df.replace(&Scalar::Int64(-999), &Scalar::Null(NullKind::NaN))?;'
   but crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:3571 declares
   pub fn replace(&self, replacements: &[(Scalar, Scalar)]) -> Result<Self, FrameError>
   replace takes a slice of (from, to) pairs, NOT two separate Scalar args. A user
   copy-pasting hits:
   error[E0061]: this method takes 1 argument but 2 arguments were supplied

Fix: Wrap the clip args in Some(...) and convert the replace example to a slice of pairs. Pure README change.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no concurrency / TSan campaign — DataFrame Send+Sync contracts + parallel iteration safety unverified
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill decision tree: "Concurrency code → TSan campaign".

**Observation.** The existing fuzz corpus runs single-threaded. No target has a `thread::spawn`, no `rayon::par_iter`, no cross-thread DataFrame reference. skill's Sanitizer Discipline table: "TSan if target has concurrency" — we have concurrency latent in the type system but no TSan campaign.

**What's concurrency-relevant in fp-frame despite single-threaded core logic:**

1. **Send + Sync contracts.** DataFrame, Series, Column, Index, MultiIndex all DERIVE Clone (some auto-derive Send/Sync via Vec<Scalar>). Users legitimately pass frames across thread boundaries (`thread::spawn(move || df.clone().sum())`). If any internal field has interior mutability that the compiler auto-disables Send/Sync for — good, compile error. If any `Arc<Mutex<...>>` internal state exists — we need TSan to verify it behaves across parallel iteration. Cargo tree suggests: bumpalo (single-threaded arena), arrow (Send+Sync-assuming), rusqlite (Connection: !Send by default).

2. **Parallel iteration via downstream consumers.** README advertises "Parallel execution (rayon) | Not started; architecture supports it (columns are independent)". But the PUBLIC API already exposes `&Column`, `&Series`, `.values()` — a user writing `rayon::par_iter` over a DataFrame's columns must trust our data types don't share interior-mutable state between columns. Unverified by our test suite.

3. **future: br-fd90 SQL backends.** tokio-postgres is async; `Connection: Send + !Sync` (typical async-client shape). The SqlConnection trait will need careful Send/Sync bounds.

4. **future: br-diic PyO3 bindings.** Python's GIL wraps, but once `py.allow_threads(|| df.sum())` pattern lands (the standard PyO3 escape hatch for compute-heavy calls), Rust-side thread safety becomes load-bearing.

**Skill rule invoked:** Pattern 7 (Concurrency archetype) + Sanitizer Discipline ("TSan campaign for concurrent code"). Current test suite's entire concurrency coverage is `#[test] fn send_sync_traits_auto_derive() {}` — implicit compile-time check, no runtime verification.

**Severity.** HIGH — concurrency bugs are (a) the hardest to reproduce, (b) often silent for months until a hot production path exposes them, (c) a common cause of "seemed fine in tests, corrupted data in prod" disasters. For a dataframe library targeting pandas parity + Rust performance, concurrency safety is part of the implicit contract.

**Fix shape:**

1. `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_parallel_dataframe.rs` — wraps `fp_conformance::fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes`.

2. `fp-conformance::fuzz_parallel_dataframe_bytes(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), String>`:
   - Project bytes into a frame via `fuzz_*_frame_from_bytes` (existing helper).
   - Clone the frame. Spawn 4-8 rayon par_iter jobs over clones, each running a different op:
       thread 1: sum_across_columns(&clone)
       thread 2: series.rolling(5).mean()
       thread 3: groupby + count
       thread 4: merge with a second frame
   - Join all threads. Assert each thread's result matches the same op run single-threaded on the original frame.
   - Invariant: arithmetic on &DataFrame must be pure (no shared-state mutation between threads).

3. CI setup for TSan:
   - `RUSTFLAGS="-Zsanitizer=thread" cargo +nightly fuzz run fuzz_parallel_dataframe` — separate build (TSan incompatible with ASan).
   - Optional: add a scheduled nightly CI job for TSan runs (skill's Rule 6: separate sanitizer campaigns).

4. Shadow-model concurrency assertion (Phase 2):
   - Use `loom` for exhaustive concurrency exploration of critical fast paths (e.g. groupby's HashMap-based accumulator if it ever goes parallel).
   - Today this is overkill — but file a follow-up bead once br-fd90 async SQL lands and we have genuine concurrency code to verify.

Seed corpus:
- empty frame + 1 thread
- 1-row frame + 2 threads (likely-safe smoke test)
- 100-row frame + 8 threads (stress the rayon work-stealing path)
- frame with a Categorical column + parallel groupby (categorical metadata must not race)

Expected cost: TSan is 5-15x slowdown per skill's sanitizer table. Fuzz_parallel_dataframe runs at maybe 50-100 exec/s (well below the 1000/s parser floor, but WITHIN the 100/s stateful floor which this effectively is).

**Related:**
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — sanitizer campaigns need their own CI job config (one per sanitizer, shared corpus per skill Rule 6).
- br-gztr (fuzz dictionaries + sanitizers) — this bead is the concurrency slice of gztr's "configure sanitizers" item.
- br-fd90 slice 1/7 (SqlConnection trait) — Send/Sync bounds on the trait need fuzz-verification.
- br-diic (PyO3 bindings) — `py.allow_threads(|| ...)` requires Rust-side thread safety; this bead lights up the path.

title: [HIGH] conformance: 1249 golden fixtures have no pandas-version provenance — silent drift against pandas releases
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Fixture Provenance rule + the "fixtures can become stale" concern.

**Observation.** crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ contains 1249 JSON fixture files. Each carries expected_series or expected_frame payloads computed by pandas_oracle.py at some generation point. None of the fixture files record:
- Which pandas version produced them
- When they were generated
- Which oracle script commit generated them

The fixtures/README.md describes the generation workflow but the Reference line says "pandas (system install, typically 2.x)" — no version pin. .github/workflows/ci.yml neither installs pandas nor regenerates fixtures; it compares Rust output against the frozen 2026 snapshot.

**Concrete risk.** Pandas 2.3.0, 3.0.0, etc. will land. When a pandas point release changes a semantic (they do — see https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/whatsnew/ changelog), our 1249 fixtures keep reflecting the OLD behavior. Rust passes against the old behavior and CI stays green, BUT real users calling frankenpandas and pandas side-by-side hit divergence. The longer the fixtures age without regeneration, the wider the drift.

**Severity.** HIGH — this is a time-bomb: conformance-against-past-pandas silently replaces conformance-against-current-pandas. It will only surface when a user reports a divergence that our own test suite declares impossible.

**Fix shape:**
1. Pin pandas (closes br-frankenpandas-8irq): add crates/fp-conformance/oracle/requirements.txt with pandas==X.Y.Z. CI installs exactly this version.
2. Record provenance in every generated fixture: add pandas_version, oracle_script_sha, generated_at to the fixture JSON (one new optional field; serde skip_if_none so old fixtures stay readable).
3. Stale-fixture detector: new scripts/check_fixture_freshness.sh compares fixture pandas_version field to oracle/requirements.txt pin. Fails CI on mismatch.
4. Quarterly regeneration ritual: document in AGENTS.md a "fixture regen" playbook — bump pandas pin → regenerate all fixtures → `git diff fixtures/` → review every diff line (skill's rule) → file DISCREPANCIES.md entries for any intentional divergences → commit atomically. First regen after this bead lands.
5. Decouple live-oracle tests from the fixture corpus: live_oracle_* tests (closed by br-d6xa sibling CRITICAL bead on CI-pandas-install) catch drift as it happens; fixture-expected tests catch architecture regressions. Both layers needed.

Blast radius: scripts/ (+1 new file, ~40 lines), crates/fp-conformance/oracle/ (+1 requirements.txt), AGENTS.md (+1 playbook section), fixture schema (+3 optional fields, backward-compat).

Depends on br-frankenpandas-d6xa landing first (CI needs pandas to install to regenerate fixtures).

title: [modes-of-reasoning] [HIGH] Series::sample silently accepts negative/NaN/oversized frac; pandas raises ValueError
description:
## Finding (consistency lens, validation-stub family sister gap)

Sister gap to br-d895b (ewm), br-7c962 (rolling), br-7bc60 (resample):

Series::sample (line 6100) does not validate `frac`:

  let sample_n = match (n, frac) {
      (None, Some(f)) => (total as f64 * f).round() as usize,
      ...
  };

In Rust, `f64 as usize` saturates negative values and NaN to 0:
  (-1.5 as usize) == 0
  (f64::NAN as usize) == 0

So:
  s.sample(None, Some(-0.5), false, None) → silently returns empty
  s.sample(None, Some(f64::NAN), false, None) → silently returns empty

Pandas raises ValueError on negative frac AND on `frac > 1` with
replace=False (the upper-bound check is currently caught by the
`sample_n > total` check, but only AFTER the f64-to-usize cast,
which works only because n=u32-ish ranges fit; for very large
frac, `(total as f64 * f).round()` overflows to f64::MAX which
casts to usize::MAX, and the > total check catches it. So the
upper bound IS caught, just by accident.)

The negative/NaN cases produce 0 — slip through the > total guard.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into(), 2_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Int64(1), Scalar::Int64(2), Scalar::Int64(3)])?;
  s.sample(None, Some(-0.5), false, None);
    // before: Ok(empty series)
    // after:  Err("sample: frac must be in [0, 1] (or > 0 with replace=True)")

## Fix

Add validation for `frac` BEFORE the f64-to-usize cast:
- frac must be finite (no NaN, no Inf)
- frac >= 0
- if replace=false: frac <= 1 (since you can't sample more than
  the population without replacement); pandas allows >1 only with
  replace=True

The existing `sample_n > total` check on the result remains as a
secondary defense for the rare edge where frac == 1.0 + epsilon
rounds up.

## Tests

- sample(None, Some(-0.5)) → Err mentioning "frac must be"
- sample(None, Some(f64::NAN)) → Err
- sample(None, Some(2.0), replace=false) → Err (or the existing
  sample_n > total path)
- sample(None, Some(2.0), replace=true) → Ok (oversampling allowed)
- sample(None, Some(0.5)) → Ok with sample_n = total/2
- sample(None, Some(0.0)) → Ok with empty result
- existing valid-input tests must remain green

## Why surfaced

/modes-of-reasoning consistency lens — same validation-stub family
as ewm/rolling/resample. After fixing those, the next obvious
sample-method-with-numeric-input was Series::sample. The Rust
`f64 as usize` saturating-to-zero behavior turns negative input
into a silent 0 — exact same shape as the silent-fallback bugs
in d895b/7c962/7bc60.
close_reason: Shipped fix in f5a6c62 — Series::sample now validates frac (finite, >=0, <=1 without replace) before f64-to-usize cast. 1678 passed / 0 failed.

title: fd90.105: README selection example uses df.loc(str) + df.loc_rows() — both wrong arg type / non-existent fn
description:

  let row = df.loc("row_label")?;           // Single row by label
  let subset = df.loc_rows(&["a", "b"])?;   // Multiple rows

Both lines wouldn't compile:
1. df.loc takes &[IndexLabel] (a slice of labels), NOT a single &str. Verified at crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:17168 — pub fn loc(&self, labels: &[IndexLabel]).
2. df.loc_rows does not exist. grep -rnE 'fn loc_rows' crates/ returns nothing. Only loc, loc_with_columns, iloc_rows (private).

The README's separation between 'single row' and 'multiple rows' methods is also fictional — there's only one loc() that takes a slice. Both single-row and multi-row calls go through the same function.

title: [modes-of-reasoning] [HIGH] mode_values is O(n²); single-Series caller can use HashMap fast path like nunique_with_dropna
description:
## Finding (consistency lens)

mode_values (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:670) has the IDENTICAL
linear-scan-and-find pattern I previously fixed across
value_counts/unique/duplicated/isin/map:

  for value in values {
      if let Some((_, count)) = counts
          .iter_mut()
          .find(|(existing, _)| grouped_scalar_eq(existing, value))
      {
          *count += 1;
      } else {
          counts.push((value.clone(), 1));
      }
  }

O(n × k) for n values × k unique. For n=1M, k=100K: ~5×10¹⁰ comparisons.

## Caller-aware constraint (same as distinct_values)

mode_values is called from TWO sites:
1. Series::mode_with_dropna (line 4955) — single-Series, homogeneous
   dtype (Column::new validation), ScalarKey-safe.
2. DataFrame::mode_with_options axis=1 (line 26840) — row-wise across
   multiple columns of potentially different dtypes; relies on
   grouped_scalar_eq's cross-dtype pandas-style equivalence
   (Bool(true) ≡ Int64(1) ≡ Float64(1.0)). ScalarKey distinguishes
   variants; can't be used here.

Same caller-aware split as br-15f51 (distinct_values vs
nunique_with_dropna): leave mode_values linear-scan for the
cross-dtype caller; inline an O(n) HashMap variant in the
single-Series Series::mode_with_dropna.

## Fix

In Series::mode_with_dropna, replace the call to mode_values with
inline O(n) logic:
- HashMap<ScalarKey, (Scalar, usize)> tracking first-seen value
  alongside counter
- Iterate once for counting; iterate the values once more to extract
  modes (entries where count == max_count), sort by scalar_key_cmp
  (preserves the existing total order from the linear-scan version)

mode_values stays as-is (with a comment documenting why) for
DataFrame::mode_with_options axis=1's cross-dtype use case.

## Reproducer (perf, not correctness)

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      (0..1_000_000_i64).map(IndexLabel::Int64).collect(),
      (0..1_000_000_i64).map(|i| Scalar::Int64(i % 10_000)).collect())?;
  s.mode();  // before: minutes. after: <1s

## Tests

- mode preserves first-occurrence ordering for tied modes
- mode handles all-null series correctly
- mode handles unique values (everyone is "mode")
- existing series_mode_* tests must remain green

## Why surfaced

/modes-of-reasoning consistency lens — having fixed the same
linear-scan pattern across 5+ functions, scanned for remaining
instances. mode_values is the structural twin of distinct_values
(both shared cross-dtype use); same caller-aware split applies.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 9ec24cf — Series::mode_with_dropna now O(n) via inline HashMap; mode_values helper stays for cross-dtype DataFrame.mode axis=1. 1620 passed / 0 failed.

title: [HIGH] fp-frame grouped-window flat-string label is not real MultiIndex (r4au)
description:
Reviewing 2f80719 (br-frankenpandas-r4au, cod): GroupByRolling::apply_grouped_rolling and GroupByResample paths now emit a flattened string label like 'IndexLabel::Utf8("(a, 2024-01)")' as a workaround for missing row MultiIndex support (crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:25735 grouped_window_label).

Findings:
- pandas-observable parity is broken: pandas returns a real MultiIndex(levels=[group, bucket], codes=[..]) which downstream code can index via .loc[("a", "2024-01")] or stack/unstack. A formatted Utf8 label cannot be re-decomposed and breaks group-key extraction in joins/aggregates.
- Tests under r4au were rewritten to assert against the new string format (assert_utf8_index_labels("(a, 2024-01)")), so the fp-frame unit tests are 'green' against the workaround instead of pandas truth.
- New emit-only-value-column path drops the group-key column from the output, so consumers expecting both grouping cols and value cols (the as_index=False contract) now see different shapes depending on as_index gate.

Suggested follow-up:
1. Land genuine row MultiIndex via fp-index::MultiIndex (or document why it's deferred).
2. Until then, gate the flat-string workaround behind a non-default GroupByOptions feature so default groupby().rolling/resample preserves the previous shape (group_col + bucket-only index) rather than silently changing the output schema.
3. Update the r4au tests to mark the flat-string label as a workaround assertion (constant in test scope), not a parity claim.

title: fd90.204: Extend prelude compile guard to cover fd90.182-203 additions
description:
fd90.204: Extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard to cover all recent prelude additions.

The compile guard test at frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:408 is supposed to ensure every prelude item stays reachable. Its docstring says "If anyone removes one of these from the prelude, this test refuses to compile." But the test was last updated for fd90.155 and doesn't reference items added since then:

- fd90.182: From<bool/i64/f64/&str/String> for Scalar (.into() ergonomics)
- fd90.192: DataFrameColumnInput
- fd90.201: MultiIndexOrIndex
- fd90.202: ColumnError, ExprError, FrameError, IndexError, IoError (5 of 7 — TypeError + GroupByError were referenced via crate::, not prelude)
- fd90.203: ValidityMask

Also the test imports `GroupByError` and `TypeError` via `use crate::{GroupByError, TypeError};` (line 409) — that's stale comment behaviour; both are now in the prelude per fd90.202 so the import should be removed.

Scope:
- Remove the stale `use crate::{GroupByError, TypeError};` import
- Add explicit name-checks for: DataFrameColumnInput, MultiIndexOrIndex, ColumnError, ExprError, FrameError, IndexError, IoError, ValidityMask
- Add `.into()` round-trip lines for each Scalar From impl

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs (single test, ~25 lines added).
Time estimate: 15-25min.
close_reason: Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard to cover the 5 prelude additions made since fd90.155: From<bool/i64/f64/&str/String> for Scalar (fd90.182, 5 .into() let-bindings), DataFrameColumnInput (fd90.192), MultiIndexOrIndex (fd90.201), ColumnError + ExprError + FrameError + IndexError + IoError (fd90.202, 5 fn-pointer type checks), ValidityMask (fd90.203). Removed the stale 'use crate::{GroupByError, TypeError}' import — both were already in the prelude. All 35 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: fd90.44: list_foreign_keys must resolve implicit-PK references via parent's primary_key_columns
description: fd90.29's list_foreign_keys silently drops FKs that use implicit-PK syntax. SQLite's PRAGMA foreign_key_list returns NULL for the 'to' column when the user declared FOREIGN KEY (col) REFERENCES parent (no column list — implicit reference to parent's PK). My filter 'columns.len() != referenced_columns.len()' then skips the whole FK, leaving callers blind to a real foreign key. SQLAlchemy.Inspector.get_foreign_keys resolves this by looking up the parent's PK columns. Fix: when all to_cols in a group are None, look up the parent table's primary_key_columns via the existing trait method (uses fd90.25 default impl which calls table_schema). Single round-trip per implicit-FK. If the parent has no PK or doesn't exist, fall back to the previous skip behavior with a comment about that being unrecoverable. Add tests: implicit-PK single-col FK resolves to parent.pk_col, implicit-PK composite FK resolves to parent's composite PK in order, explicit-col FK still works (no behavior change), parent without PK still falls through to skip.

Useful for to_sql validation when emitting auto-generated index/column names that may exceed backend limits. Free-fn sql_max_identifier_length wrapper. Tests cover SQLite-None default, multi-backend stub overrides for PG/MySQL/MSSQL.

title: [mock-code-finder] [HIGH] Series::skew/kurtosis return Err on too-few non-null values; pandas returns NaN
description:
## Finding (Err-as-stub-of-NaN-semantics)

Series::skew (line 6175) and Series::kurtosis (line 6196) bail out
with FrameError when the non-null count is too small (< 3 for skew,
< 4 for kurt):

  if count < 3 {
      return Err(FrameError::CompatibilityRejected(
          "skew requires at least 3 non-null values".to_owned(),
      ));
  }

But pandas returns NaN in these cases — never raises:

  >>> pd.Series([1, 2]).skew()       # nan, not error
  >>> pd.Series([1, 2, 3]).kurt()    # nan, not error
  >>> pd.Series([], dtype='float64').skew()  # nan
  >>> pd.Series([1]).skew()          # nan

The Rolling-window variants (line 8868+) ALREADY return NaN
correctly when window has < 3 / < 4 valid values — meaning the
correct semantic is well-known internally. The non-rolling
Series-level versions are an Err stub of the pandas NaN contract.

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Float64(1.0), Scalar::Float64(2.0)])?;
  s.skew();
    // before: Err("skew requires at least 3 non-null values")
    // after:  Ok(f64::NAN)

## Fix

In Series::skew, replace the early Err return with `Ok(f64::NAN)`.
Same in Series::kurtosis. The existing s2 == 0.0 fallback at line
6186/6207 already returns Ok — those paths show the function
shape supports NaN-return semantically.

## Tests

- skew on 1-element series returns NaN (was Err)
- skew on 2-element series returns NaN
- skew on empty series returns NaN
- kurt on 1-3 element series returns NaN
- kurt on empty series returns NaN
- existing skew/kurt tests with >= 3/4 values must remain green
- metamorphic: Rolling skew over a window of size < 3 returns NaN
  (already correct — sanity guard)

## Why surfaced

/mock-code-finder lens — applied across the recent Utf8 fix wave,
turned to Err-paths that should be NaN-returns. The Rolling variant
having the correct NaN behavior right next door makes this a
classic "one branch implements the contract, sister branch
implements a stub" pattern (see br-526a2 is_monotonic_increasing
for the same shape).
close_reason: Shipped fix in 3798012 — Series::skew/kurtosis now return NaN (matching pandas) for empty/too-few-values cases instead of erroring. 1653 passed / 0 failed.

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: 89 live_oracle_* tests silently skip in CI — core pandas-parity claim is not enforced
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Pattern 1 (Differential Testing) + the "Before claiming conformance, prove it" rule.

**Observation.** 89 live_oracle_*_matches_pandas tests exist in crates/fp-conformance/src/lib.rs. Each one dispatches through capture_live_oracle_expected → subprocess pandas_oracle.py. The default HarnessConfig sets:

  oracle_root: repo_root.join("legacy_pandas_code/pandas")
  allow_system_pandas_fallback: false

When oracle_root.exists() is false, capture_live_oracle_expected returns HarnessError::OracleUnavailable. The 89 tests match on that variant and early-return with an eprintln!("live pandas unavailable; skipping ..."). No test fails. No test is marked ignored. No summary counter is emitted.

**The repo intentionally gitignores legacy_pandas_code/** (confirmed: .gitignore line "legacy_pandas_code/"). .github/workflows/ci.yml does NOT install pandas, does NOT set FP_PYTHON_BIN, does NOT checkout a pandas oracle source tree, does NOT set allow_system_pandas_fallback=true.

**Consequence.** In CI as shipped today, all 89 live_oracle_* tests silently pass by skipping. The entire differential conformance layer — the exact thing the README markets as "differential conformance tests verify against the actual pandas oracle" — is a no-op in CI. The only thing CI actually verifies is the 1249 pre-generated packet fixtures (FixtureExpected mode, golden-file comparison), which themselves were generated by some earlier pandas version that is not pinned anywhere (br-frankenpandas-8irq covers the version pin separately).

**Severity.** CRITICAL — the core product claim (pandas parity, verified by oracle) is not actually enforced by CI. Any regression that only the live oracle would catch (e.g. a new pandas point release changing semantics we match; a Rust-side bug that golden fixtures predate) lands on main silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Count-and-fail: HarnessConfig gains a require_live_oracle: bool, default false. When true, OracleUnavailable converts to test failure instead of early-return. ci.yml sets the env var that flips this on.
2. CI installs pandas: ci.yml adds a Python setup step + pip install pandas==PINNED_VERSION (coordinates with br-frankenpandas-8irq). Then clones / mirrors legacy_pandas_code or points oracle_root at the pip-installed pandas via allow_system_pandas_fallback=true.
3. Skip counter: LiveOracleReport aggregates per-test skipped/run/failed counts, printed at end of lib-test run. CI asserts skipped_count == 0 or an explicit allowlist.
4. README update: add "When live pandas is unavailable, these 89 tests skip silently — not a gap in the oracle, a gap in the env" disclosure until (1)-(3) land.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+ ~12 lines), fp-conformance HarnessConfig + tests (+ ~60 lines for the counter + flag). No code-logic change.

Parallel bead: br-frankenpandas-8irq (pin pandas version) becomes a prerequisite for this bead — landing live-oracle-in-CI without a pinned pandas causes CI flakiness on any pandas update.
close_reason: Completed

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: DataFrame constructor + merge pub fns lack fuzz targets
description:
/testing-fuzzing coverage audit: DataFrame constructor + merge fuzz targets missing.

**Gap analysis.** Per /testing-fuzzing Rule 3 (every pub fn accepting untrusted input must have a fuzz target). Current coverage:

- fuzz_dataframe_eval — DataFrame.eval (br existing)
- fuzz_pivot_table — pivot_table (br existing)
- fuzz_groupby_sum, fuzz_groupby_agg_dispatch — groupby partials (br existing)
- fuzz_join_series — series-level joins (br existing)
- fuzz_dataframe_op_chain — stateful op sequences (br-wr9n)
- fuzz_parallel_dataframe — concurrency (br-bahw)

**Uncovered pub fns (all take untrusted Vec<Scalar> or &DataFrame):**

1. DataFrame::from_dict(column_order, data)                — 14843
2. DataFrame::from_dict_mixed(...)                         — 15121
3. DataFrame::from_dict_with_index(...)                    — 15425
4. DataFrame::from_dict_with_index_mixed(...)              — 15451
5. DataFrame::from_records(records, column_order, labels)  — 15037
6. DataFrame::from_tuples(records, columns)                — 15055
7. DataFrame::from_series(series_list)                     — 14786
8. merge_dataframes(left, right, on, join_type)            — fp-join 1102
9. merge_dataframes_on(left, right, on, join_type)         — fp-join 1092
10. merge_dataframes_on_with(left, right, left_on, right_on, join_type) — fp-join 817

Each is a canonical fuzz Rule 3 target: user-supplied data (potentially crafted) flows in, frame constructed / joined, results must preserve shape invariants.

**Skill rule invoked:** Rule 3 (fuzz every untrusted-input pub fn).

**Severity.** MEDIUM — same severity as the jkhg (expr) and gpxk (SQL) slices already shipped this session. No known bug, but a structural hole in fuzz coverage of the most-used public API.

**Fix shape (four new targets this slice):**

1. fuzz_dataframe_from_dict_bytes — bytes projected into a tiny (name→values) dict; call DataFrame::from_dict; assert ok(df) has row count == inferred n.
2. fuzz_dataframe_from_records_bytes — bytes projected into a rectangular Vec<Vec<Scalar>>; call DataFrame::from_records; assert rows == records.len.
3. fuzz_dataframe_from_series_bytes — bytes projected into Vec<Series> with shared index; call DataFrame::from_series; assert cols == series.len.
4. fuzz_dataframe_merge_bytes — two small frames with a shared 'k' column; call merge_dataframes(..., JoinType::{Inner,Left,Right,Outer}); assert per-join-type row-count bound (inner <= min; left >= left.rows for unique keys; outer >= max).

Each:
- pub fn fuzz_*_bytes in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs with size guard (≤ 64 KB).
- fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_*.rs wrapper.
- [[bin]] entry in fuzz/Cargo.toml.
- fuzz/corpus/<target>/ with 4 seeds.
- fuzz/artifacts/<target>/README.md.
close_reason: Already shipped on main: commits d7df1ba (constructor + merge fuzz targets) + e1e2894 (tightened merge invariants). Cleaning stale in_progress accounting.

title: [mock-code-finder] [HIGH] Series::ewm silently uses default alpha=2/11 when neither span nor alpha is given; pandas raises ValueError
description:
## Finding (mock-code-finder, silent-fallback-as-stub-of-validation)

Series::ewm (line 7701) silently falls back to alpha = 2.0 / 11.0
(equivalent to span=10) when neither span nor alpha is provided:

  let a = if let Some(s) = span {
      2.0 / (s + 1.0)
  } else {
      alpha.unwrap_or(2.0 / 11.0)  // ← silent default
  };

Pandas validates that exactly one of {com, span, halflife, alpha}
is given:
  pd.Series([1,2,3]).ewm()
  → ValueError: Must pass one of comass, span, halflife, or alpha

Additionally, pandas validates BOUNDS:
  ewm(span=0)      → ValueError: span must satisfy: span >= 1
  ewm(alpha=2.0)   → ValueError: alpha must satisfy: 0 < alpha <= 1
  ewm(alpha=-0.5)  → same

fp-frame silently accepts all of these and produces corrupted output
(e.g. ewm(span=0) → alpha = 2.0/(0+1) = 2.0, methods then run with
that nonsense alpha, producing arbitrary values).

## Reproducer

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      vec![0_i64.into(), 1_i64.into()],
      vec![Scalar::Float64(1.0), Scalar::Float64(2.0)])?;
  s.ewm(None, None).mean();
    // before: silently uses alpha=2/11 → arbitrary results
    // after:  Err("ewm: must specify one of span or alpha")

  s.ewm(Some(0.0), None).mean();
    // before: silently uses alpha=Inf → arbitrary results
    // after:  Err("ewm: span must satisfy: span >= 1")

## Fix

Validate inputs in Series::ewm and store the result inside the Ewm
struct as Result<f64, FrameError>. Each Ewm method (mean/sum/var/
std/cov/corr/agg) propagates the error via `?`. Validation rules:
- exactly one of {span, alpha} must be Some (pandas accepts all four
  of {com, span, halflife, alpha} but fp-frame's surface only has
  span/alpha for now)
- span >= 1 (pandas constraint)
- 0 < alpha <= 1 (pandas constraint)

Existing tests use ewm(Some(span), None) or ewm(None, Some(alpha)) —
all valid; tests remain green.

## Tests

- ewm(None, None).mean() → Err mentioning "span or alpha"
- ewm(Some(0.0), None).mean() → Err mentioning "span >= 1"
- ewm(None, Some(2.0)).mean() → Err mentioning "alpha"
- ewm(None, Some(-0.5)).mean() → Err mentioning "alpha"
- existing valid-input tests remain green
- ewm with both span and alpha both Some → Err

## Why surfaced

/mock-code-finder lens — the `unwrap_or(2.0 / 11.0)` is the canonical
silent-fallback stub: it papers over user error by inventing a
default that has no contractual basis in the API. Pandas treats this
as a configuration error and raises; the silent fallback hides it.
close_reason: Shipped fix in 2bf5da2 — Series::ewm now validates inputs eagerly per pandas; methods propagate validation errors. 1665 passed / 0 failed.

title: [reality-check] complete pandas.MultiIndex parity — 96/118 methods missing (CRITICAL)
description:
**Reality-check finding** (artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md). Severity: CRITICAL.

MultiIndex coverage 22/118 = 19%. Almost the entire pandas MultiIndex surface is absent. Affects every DataFrame with multi-level row or column indices.

**Missing categories** (96 total):
- Set operations: `difference, intersection, symmetric_difference, union`
- Restructure: `append, delete, insert, drop, drop_duplicates, dropna, fillna, repeat, putmask, set_codes, set_levels, sortlevel, sort_values, swaplevel, remove_unused_levels`
- Conversion: `to_frame, to_list, to_numpy, to_series, tolist, array, values, ravel, view, T, transpose, format`
- Search / lookup: `asof, asof_locs, get_indexer, get_indexer_for, get_locs, get_slice_bound, searchsorted, slice_indexer`
- Reductions: `all, any, argmax, argmin, max, min, nunique, value_counts`
- Metadata: `name, dtype, dtypes, codes, levels, levshape, hasnans, holds_integer, infer_objects, inferred_type, item, ndim, nbytes, memory_usage, shape, size, empty`
- Type-checking: `is_, is_boolean, is_categorical, is_floating, is_integer, is_interval, is_numeric, is_object, isna, isnull, notna, notnull`
- Comparisons: `equals, equal_levels, identical`
- Restructure: `copy, factorize, fillna, groupby, join, map, rename, reindex, shift, sort, str, truncate, where, from_frame, unique`

This is an epic — should be broken into sub-beads by category (8-10 sub-beads). Many are 1:1 ports from generic Index plus level-aware logic.

**Discoverer**: cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) reality-check 2026-05-03. Tied with SeriesGroupBy as the largest missing surface.
close_reason: Completed parent scope: all child beads are closed and a scripted audit of the method names listed in this reality-check bead reports required_count=94, absent_count=0 for impl MultiIndex. Broader semantic parity remains subject to future differential findings, but the tracked missing-method surface is now covered.

title: MultiIndex get_indexer/get_indexer_for parity
description: Narrow child of frankenpandas-d89fe. Add MultiIndex::get_indexer for unique source indexes with pandas-style -1 for missing target tuples and fail-closed duplicate-source rejection; add MultiIndex::get_indexer_for that delegates to get_indexer for unique sources and expands duplicates via existing get_indexer_non_unique for duplicate sources. title: [testing-metamorphic] [HIGH] Series::duplicated/duplicated_keep/drop_duplicates_keep all O(n²); same scan-and-find pattern
description:
## Finding (metamorphic + perf scan)

Sister gap to br-frankenpandas-15f51 (Series::unique O(n²) → O(n) fix).
A scan via metamorphic property `s.drop_duplicates() == s.unique()`
checks both implementations; both used the same Vec<&Scalar> +
iter().any(semantic_eq) linear-scan pattern.

Three more functions have the SAME O(n²) shape:

1. Series::duplicated (line 6761)
     for val in self.column.values() {
         let is_dup = seen.iter().any(|existing| existing.semantic_eq(val));
         ...
     }

2. Series::duplicated_keep (line 6780) — same in First/Last branches.
   The DuplicateKeep::None path is even worse:
     for (i, val) in vals.iter().enumerate() {
         let count = vals.iter().filter(|v| v.semantic_eq(val)).count();
         if count > 1 { flags[i] = true; }
     }
   Explicitly O(n²) — full scan per element, no short-circuit.

3. Series::drop_duplicates_keep (line 6833) — same shape, same None
   path with full filter().count() per element.

For n=1M, k=100K: ~5×10¹⁰ comparisons. Same scaling cliff as
value_counts/unique had.

## Fix

Use HashMap<ScalarKey, ...> for O(1) lookup:

- duplicated: HashMap<ScalarKey, ()> for seen tracking; first occurrence
  inserts and emits Bool(false), subsequent emit Bool(true). O(n).

- duplicated_keep::First / drop_duplicates_keep::First: same
- duplicated_keep::Last / drop_duplicates_keep::Last: reverse iteration
- duplicated_keep::None / drop_duplicates_keep::None: first pass builds
  HashMap<ScalarKey, count>; second pass marks/keeps based on count > 1
  (for duplicated_keep::None) or count == 1 (for drop_duplicates_keep::
  None). Two O(n) passes vs the existing single O(n²) pass.

Within a single Column, values are homogeneous (Column::new validation),
so ScalarKey equivalence matches semantic_eq for in-column pairs and
NaN/Null collapse identically.

## Reproducer (perf, not correctness)

  let s = Series::from_values("x",
      (0..1_000_000).map(|i| (i as i64).into()).collect(),
      (0..1_000_000).map(|i| Scalar::Int64(i % 100_000)).collect())?;
  s.duplicated();                               // before: minutes
  s.drop_duplicates_keep(DuplicateKeep::None);  // before: minutes
  // after: <1s each

## Tests

- duplicated: first occurrence false, rest true (metamorphic vs unique)
- duplicated_keep::First: same as duplicated()
- duplicated_keep::Last: last occurrence false, rest true
- duplicated_keep::None: ALL occurrences of duplicates marked true
- drop_duplicates_keep all variants preserve order
- metamorphic: drop_duplicates(First) values == unique() values

## Why surfaced

/testing-metamorphic checks `unique == drop_duplicates(First)` —
both should produce the same value sequence. While verifying that,
noticed both went through the same O(n²) shape; unique was already
fixed by 15f51, drop_duplicates and duplicated still O(n²).
close_reason: Shipped fix in e58fefe — duplicated/duplicated_keep/drop_duplicates_keep now O(n) via HashMap<ScalarKey>. DuplicateKeep::None now uses two O(n) passes vs prior O(n²). 1587 passed / 0 failed.

title: [HIGH] docs: doctest never run in CI — 8 existing doctests silently unverified + docs.rs will fail at release
description:
Doctest coverage audit.

**Observation.**
- grep for `/// \`\`\`rust` / `/// \`\`\`` / `/// \`\`\`no_run` / `/// \`\`\`ignore` across crates/*/src/lib.rs finds only **8 doctests total** — 2 in frankenpandas, 4 in fp-index, 2 in fp-columnar. The 10 other crates have zero doctests.
- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` runs `cargo test --workspace` at line 32 but does NOT run `cargo test --doc --workspace`. By default `cargo test --workspace` runs `#[test]` fns and integration tests but NOT doctests (those only run with `--doc` or when the default test target is configured to include them).
- Independent confirmation: I grepped for "doc" in ci.yml — returns only the cargo-llvm-cov-related word "Documentation" as false positive, no `--doc` invocation.

**Consequence.**
- The 8 existing doctests never execute in CI. A code example in a public README-level function that compiles today can be silently broken by an API change.
- More importantly, doctests are how Rust libraries document their API contracts with runnable examples. The public-API-contract bead (br-7cfm) called for documenting `# Panics:` on 1530 pub fns; good doctest coverage is the complementary half — runnable examples that VERIFY the documented behavior.
- docs.rs builds doctests when hosting crate docs. Once the release workflow lands (br-4clx) and docs.rs starts serving frankenpandas, any broken doctest fails the docs build → docs.rs shows "docs build failed" instead of the actual API reference → users land on a broken docs page from crates.io.

**Severity.** HIGH — compounds with br-7cfm (panic contract) and br-4clx (release). Without doctest coverage, the public API's "how do I use this?" examples rot silently until a docs.rs build fails publicly at release time.

**Fix shape:**

1. ci.yml adds `cargo test --doc --workspace` as a distinct step after the main `cargo test --workspace`. Catches doctest failures on every PR.
2. Add doctests to the existing pub fn docs systematically:
   - Priority 1: DataFrame constructors (from_dict, new, from_series, from_records) — most-used entry points.
   - Priority 2: Series arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) — high visibility in README examples.
   - Priority 3: GroupBy core (sum, mean, agg_named, rolling) — conformance-heavy surfaces where doctest doubles as conformance sample.
   - Priority 4-N: Remaining public fns by frequency of use (informed by br-zk1j API matrix once that lands).

   Target: lift from 8 doctests to ~150 over several passes. Each passing doctest also serves as a user-facing example, reducing the pure `# Panics:` prose burden from br-7cfm.

3. `cargo test --doc --doc-args="..."`: for slow doctests, use `rust ignore` or `rust no_run` to bail out of execution while keeping the example visible on docs.rs.
4. Doctest regression: add a `scripts/doctest_count.sh` that fails CI if the doctest count shrinks between commits. Prevents silent removal of examples.

Blast radius: ci.yml (+1 step), ~150 lines of doctest additions (parallelizable across agents, one pub fn at a time), 1 small script. No source logic changes.

Related:
- br-7cfm (panic contract) — doctests verify the panic contract in addition to documenting it.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — doctests build on docs.rs at release; adding them now means no surprise at release time.
- br-zk1j (API matrix) — the matrix can mark "has doctest: yes/no" as an extra coverage column.

title: fd90.123: README df.pct_change() missing required periods: usize arg
description:
fd90.74-122 README audit. Line 1050 in Cumulative and Sequential section shows:

  let pct   = df.pct_change()?;  // Percentage change

But crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:25118 declares:
  pub fn pct_change(&self, periods: usize) -> Result<Self, FrameError>

DataFrame::pct_change requires periods: usize (the n-period lag for the change). A user copy-pasting hits:
  error[E0061]: this method takes 1 argument but 0 arguments were supplied

The Series variant at 5435 also takes periods: usize, so no overload rescues this.

Fix: pass periods = 1 (the pandas default) so the example actually compiles. Pure README change.

title: fd90.138: add where_mask_df_other to mirror mask_df_other (closes API asymmetry noted in fd90.136)
description:
fd90.74-137 README audit. fd90.136 documented an API asymmetry — there's mask_df_other (DataFrame-other variant for mask) at crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:18591, but no where_mask_df_other. Both where_mask_df and mask_df only accept &Scalar.

This is a code-level gap. pandas' where()/mask() both support either a scalar or a DataFrame as the 'other' argument. FrankenPandas covers the scalar case for both and the DataFrame case for mask only — leaving where_mask_df_other as a missing convenience.

Fix: add 'pub fn where_mask_df_other(&self, cond: &Self, other: &Self) -> Result<Self, FrameError>' to fp-frame, mirroring mask_df_other (line 18591). The semantics:
  - where cond is True  → keep self's value
  - where cond is False → use other's value (aligned)
  - missing cond → NaN

Verify with cargo build -p fp-frame + cargo test -p fp-frame --lib (subset, since the workspace is large). Add a unit test that exercises the new function with a DataFrame replacement source.

Pure additive API expansion. No behavior change to existing functions.

Both merge_asof and AsofDirection are pub-used at the top level (crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:167-168) but NOT in the prelude. Verified with grep — neither name appears in the 'pub mod prelude' block. Plus merge_dataframes_on, join_series, MergedDataFrame are similarly top-level only.

A user copy-pasting the merge_asof example from prelude::* hits:

Continues the prelude-completeness pattern (fd90.121/125/126/127/128/130/141/142). Fix: add the merge family to the prelude:
  AsofDirection
  JoinError (for users matching join errors)
  MergedDataFrame (return type of merge_asof / merge_with_options)
  join_series
  merge_asof
  merge_dataframes_on

Verify with cargo build + cargo test -p frankenpandas --lib.

- Skip prelude (advanced API; df.eval/df.query are the common path)

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 5-10min.
close_reason: Exposed 9 fp-expr items at top-level: Expr (AST enum), EvalContext (evaluator state), SeriesRef (column-reference wrapper), Delta (incremental update), MaterializedView (caching primitive), evaluate, evaluate_on_dataframe, evaluate_on_dataframe_with_locals, eval_str, eval_str_with_locals. The DataFrameExprExt trait + ExprError were already in prelude (df.eval/df.query is the common path). Skipped prelude — these are the lower-level evaluator API. title: [REALITY-CHECK HIGH] No fp-python crate; drop-in pandas claim unbacked
description: ls crates shows 12 crates with no fp-python or fp-pyo3. README FAQ admits PyO3 bindings planned future step. Drop-in replacement claim unbacked. Disposition needed: ship fp-python or strip drop-in positioning until bindings ship.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22 (commit 2736f13): qualified the two Python-implying claims in README.md. Hero tagline now reads 'Drop-in pandas API in safe Rust — today. Python bindings (import frankenpandas) planned via PyO3; see the Roadmap.' and the Polars FAQ answer now explicitly scopes 'Python drop-in requires the planned PyO3 bindings; today FrankenPandas is a Rust library.' The defensible claim (pandas-API-parity-in-Rust, verified by the conformance oracle) stays; the Python-level drop-in implication is removed until the fp-python / PyO3 crate ships. Strip-not-ship path taken per the bead's own disposition choices.

title: [LOW] license: custom MIT+Rider is non-standard for Rust ecosystem — dual MIT/Apache-2.0 is convention (policy decision)
description:
License structure audit — policy question flagged.

**Observation.** `/LICENSE` is "MIT License (with OpenAI/Anthropic Rider)" — a customized MIT that adds a rider clause. No `LICENSE-MIT` / `LICENSE-APACHE` dual-license setup (the Rust-ecosystem convention used by serde, tokio, rand, clap, anyhow, thiserror, and dozens more).

**Context.** Rust crates dual-license as MIT OR Apache-2.0 because:
- **Apache-2.0 provides an explicit patent grant** (something MIT lacks). Corporate users with legal review processes often need this explicit grant.
- Dual-licensing lets downstream projects pick whichever of the two licenses fits their legal requirements.
- The de-facto convention reads in Cargo.toml as `license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"` and two LICENSE-* files at repo root.

Today:
- `license-file = "LICENSE"` per workspace Cargo.toml.
- The LICENSE file says MIT + a custom rider (restricting use by OpenAI / Anthropic per the filename hint).
- Corporate users whose legal review mandates "use only crates with explicit patent grant" (Apache-2.0 / AGPL-3.0 / etc.) will flag frankenpandas as non-compliant at legal review.
- The rider itself may run afoul of crates.io's SPDX-expression validation if we ever switch from `license-file` to `license = "..."`; crates.io accepts SPDX identifiers, not custom riders.

**Severity.** LOW — it's a policy question, not a technical gap. Severity depends on the adoption audience:
- Pure-OSS / academic audience: LOW, status quo is fine.
- Corporate / Fortune-500 audience: arguably MEDIUM, patent grant matters for legal review.
- Calibrating honestly as LOW because (a) the question needs a human policy decision, and (b) most users of a 0.1.0 library aren't corporate yet.

This is a POLICY-QUESTION bead, not a "add this file" bead — the maintainer needs to decide before any code change lands.

**Fix shape — three options for the maintainer to choose:**

1. **Keep as-is.** Custom MIT + Rider. Document in README why (if the rider is intentional policy against certain AI providers' training-data use, keep it and accept the corporate-review friction).

2. **Dual-license MIT OR Apache-2.0.** Drop the rider (or keep it on MIT only). Add:
   - `LICENSE-MIT` — plain MIT, no rider.
   - `LICENSE-APACHE` — plain Apache-2.0.
   - Cargo.toml `license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"` (SPDX).
   - `license-file` removed.
   - README "License" section: "frankenpandas is dual-licensed under MIT OR Apache-2.0. You may pick either license for your use."

3. **Triple-license.** Keep the custom MIT+Rider AS ONE OPTION + add Apache-2.0 + plain MIT:
   - SPDX doesn't support "OR" with non-standard licenses, so this forces `license-file` + a README explainer listing all three options.
   - Most complex; only worth it if the rider is genuinely important AND we want corporate adoption.

**Severity.** LOW (policy question, maintainer decides) — but flagging because it's a release-time decision that will affect every user who ever consumes the library. Deferring past 0.1.0 publish means changing terms in 0.2.0, which is noisy for downstream users who pinned 0.1.x.

Blast radius of option 2: +2 LICENSE files, -1 current LICENSE file if the rider gets dropped entirely (or retained only in the MIT variant). README "License" section update. Cargo.toml workspace.package license switch. Zero source changes.

Prerequisites:
- **HUMAN POLICY DECISION.** An agent cannot and should not pick which license option ships.
- br-4clx (release workflow) — license policy is fixed at first publish; decide before 0.1.0.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release) — license metadata goes to crates.io on first publish.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — contributors must accept whatever license applies; CONTRIBUTING documents the rule.
- README hero section — currently says "MIT + OpenAI/Anthropic rider" per the Shields badge.
close_reason: Maintainer-decision bead. The three license options (MIT+Rider / dual MIT-OR-Apache-2.0 / triple) are fully documented in the bead body. No file changes an agent can safely make here; reopening / re-filing is the right path if the maintainer wants to record a choice in repo rather than here.
