title: [reality-check] implement missing pandas IO formats — HDF5/ORC/Pickle/Stata/HTML/XML/LaTeX/Markdown
description:
**Reality-check finding** (artifacts/reality-check-2026-05-03.md).

fp-io covers CSV, Excel, Feather, IPC, JSON, JSONL, Parquet, SQL. Missing pandas-supported formats:
- HDF5 (`read_hdf, to_hdf`) — needs `hdf5` Rust crate
- ORC (`read_orc, to_orc`) — Apache ORC; arrow-rs has support
- Pickle (`read_pickle, to_pickle`) — Python pickle format; can use `serde_pickle` crate
- Stata (`read_stata, to_stata`) — DTA format; needs `stata` crate or custom impl
- HTML (`read_html, to_html`) — needs HTML parser (`scraper`/`html5ever`) + table writer
- XML (`read_xml, to_xml`) — needs `quick-xml` or similar
- LaTeX (`to_latex`) — pandas formatter; pure Rust string format
- Markdown (`to_markdown`) — same; pure formatter

**Phasing**:
- Easy formatters (LaTeX, Markdown): pure-Rust, no new deps. Ship first.
- Medium-complexity (XML, HTML): well-known crates exist.
- Hard (HDF5, Stata, ORC): heavyweight deps, may be optional features.
- Out-of-scope (BigQuery, Clipboard, SAS, SPSS): cloud / desktop / proprietary; document as non-goals.

Each format gets its own sub-bead. Methods on DataFrame and Series follow under frankenpandas-rjs51 / frankenpandas-m785r.

**Discoverer**: cc-pandas (claude-opus-4-7) reality-check 2026-05-03.
close_reason: Completed IO-format umbrella: child beads for HDF5, ORC, Pickle, Stata, HTML, XML, LaTeX, and Markdown are closed, and fp-io/root exports expose the corresponding read/write/DataFrame extension surfaces.

title: [MEDIUM] parity: MultiIndex .loc multi-axis slicing semantics
description:
Parity-gap audit: MultiIndex `.loc` multi-axis slicing.

**Scope.** pandas `df.loc[(level_0_key, level_1_key), columns]` indexes a row-MultiIndex DataFrame with a tuple. Supports:
- Exact tuple match: `df.loc[('A', 1)]` → Series.
- Partial tuple: `df.loc['A']` → sub-DataFrame (only outer-level filter).
- Slice in tuple: `df.loc[(slice(None), 1)]` → cross-section on inner level.
- pd.IndexSlice builder: `df.loc[pd.IndexSlice[:, 1], :]`.
- Slicing: `df.loc['A':'B', :]` requires sorted MultiIndex; raises `KeyError` otherwise.

**FrankenPandas state.** fp-index exposes `MultiIndex` struct and `DataFrame::xs` / `xs_level`. Check:

```
grep -n "pub fn loc\b\|loc_multi\|xs\|IndexSlice" crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs
grep -n "pub fn " crates/fp-index/src/lib.rs | head -20
```

DISC-006 documents scaffolded row-MultiIndex status. This bead is the concrete audit of `.loc` tuple semantics.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — MultiIndex is claimed in the README capability table but .loc edge cases are not proven at parity.

**Fix shape.**
1. Live-oracle tests in crates/fp-conformance/src/tests/conformance_multiindex_loc.rs covering 10 inputs: exact tuple match, partial tuple (outer), partial tuple (inner via slice), sorted-range slice, unsorted-range slice (expect error), pd.IndexSlice form, missing key KeyError, duplicate MultiIndex labels, NaN level values, empty frame.
2. Any divergence → child [HIGH] bead per failure case.

title: [LOW] community: no .github/FUNDING.yml — no Sponsor button / funding channels declared
description:
GitHub Sponsors / funding configuration audit.

**Observation.** `ls .github/FUNDING.yml` → No such file. No declared funding channels (GitHub Sponsors / Open Collective / Liberapay / etc.).

**Context.** For an open-source library targeting a sizable user base (pandas has tens of millions of users; frankenpandas targets their Rust-curious subset), a documented funding mechanism is a cheap way to let engaged users contribute financially. GitHub renders a "Sponsor" button on the repo header when `FUNDING.yml` is present; without it, the button is absent and engaged users have no path.

**Severity.** LOW — no operational impact. No release blocker. No code quality issue. Filing truthfully as LOW; user-visible polish, optional feature.

**Fix shape.** `.github/FUNDING.yml`:
```yaml
# Map the repo's "Sponsor" button to one or more funding platforms.
# Each line enables a platform. Remove entries that don't apply.

github: [<org-or-user>]           # GitHub Sponsors
# open_collective: frankenpandas
# liberapay: <name>
# ko_fi: <name>
# custom: ["https://frankenpandas.dev/sponsor"]
```

Requires a decision: does the maintainer have an active GitHub Sponsors account? If yes, one-line file. If not, skip this bead entirely.

Blast radius: 1 tiny file (< 10 lines), zero code changes.

Prerequisites: Maintainer decides which funding channel(s) to expose. Nothing else.

Related:
- br-8k1i (SECURITY.md), br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — same .github/ polish tier.
- No interlocks with other beads.
close_reason: .github/FUNDING.yml already shipped on main.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no STATEFUL fuzz target — 22 existing targets are all single-op; operation-chain state bugs uncovered
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill decision tree: "Protocol with state machine → STATEFUL (sequence of operations with shadow model oracle)".

**Observation.** All 22 existing fuzz targets are SINGLE-OPERATION fuzzers: each takes a byte input, projects it into ONE frame + ONE op, asserts invariants, exits. Examples:

  fuzz_groupby_sum_bytes       — one groupby(.).sum() call
  fuzz_rolling_window_bytes    — one series.rolling(w, mp).agg() call
  fuzz_series_add_bytes        — one left.add(&right)
  fuzz_format_cross_round_trip — one primary->secondary format hop

None exercises SEQUENCES of operations. In real pandas usage, users chain 3-10 ops per pipeline:
  df.query("col > 0").groupby("key").agg_named(...).reset_index().merge(other, ...).sort_values(...)

**Why that matters.** DataFrame / Series carry state that propagates through chained ops:
- `index` carries labels + optional row_multiindex (post br-1zzp.1)
- `column_multiindex` survives column-reshape ops
- `ValidityMask` propagates through arithmetic
- Column-order (Vec<String>) is preserved by some ops, reordered by others
- Categorical metadata should survive groupby but NOT arithmetic (per README)

A state-corruption bug in op N that's only observable after op N+2 or N+3 is invisible to the current single-op fuzz corpus. Concrete example: if `set_index_multi(&cols)` silently drops a row_multiindex from the result (regression of br-1zzp.1), a subsequent `reset_index()` fabricates the missing labels rather than pulling them from the MultiIndex. Single-op fuzz tests each call; chain fuzz would catch the silent drop.

**Skill rule invoked:** The skill's Decision Tree explicitly routes "Protocol with state machine" to STATEFUL archetype (Pattern 4 in HARNESS-CATALOG.md). Our DataFrame is effectively a state machine: index, columns, validity masks, multiindex metadata, categorical dtypes all evolve across operations.

**Severity.** HIGH — state-corruption bugs are the class most resistant to targeted unit tests (because the "bug" is an interaction, not a single-op violation) and most likely to surface as user-visible divergences from pandas. The current fuzz corpus has 0% coverage of the state-machine dimension.

**Fix shape:**

1. `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_dataframe_op_chain.rs` — wraps `fp_conformance::fuzz_dataframe_op_chain_bytes`.

2. `fp-conformance::fuzz_dataframe_op_chain_bytes(input: &[u8]) -> Result<(), String>`:
   - Bytes [0..N]: initial frame projection (N = ~32 bytes, produces small frame via existing fuzz_*_frame_from_bytes helpers).
   - Byte N: op count (clamp to 2..=8).
   - Bytes N+1..: per-op bytes, one byte tag picks an op from a fixed op alphabet:
       OP_SELECT   — df.select(&[col])
       OP_DROP_COL — df.drop_column(col)
       OP_FILTER   — df.query("col < const")
       OP_GROUPBY  — df.groupby(&[col]).sum()
       OP_MERGE    — df.merge(other_fixture_frame, how=inner)
       OP_SORT     — df.sort_values(col)
       OP_RESET_IX — df.reset_index()
       OP_SET_IX   — df.set_index(col) / set_index_multi(&[c1, c2])
       OP_STACK    — df.stack()
       OP_UNSTACK  — df.unstack()
       OP_ROUND_TRIP_CSV  — df.write_csv_string + read_csv_str
       (initial op alphabet: 12 ops; expandable)

   - Apply each op to the running frame. If any op returns Err, stop the chain without flagging — some op sequences are genuinely invalid.
   - After the chain: assert cross-op invariants:
     * `frame.index().len() == frame.columns.values().map(|c| c.len()).max()` (all columns aligned to the index)
     * `frame.column_order().len() == frame.columns().len()` (no orphan columns)
     * If row_multiindex is Some, its height matches index length (br-1zzp.1 invariant).
     * If any column's dtype is Categorical, the codes vector length matches the index length.

3. Shadow model variant (Phase 2): build a minimal reference implementation of the op alphabet using `BTreeMap<String, Vec<Scalar>>` (the most naive DataFrame). Run the chain against both the real DataFrame and the shadow. Assert same output frame → semantic_eq. Catches divergence bugs we couldn't otherwise distinguish from "this op sequence is invalid."

Seed corpus:
- empty (no ops)
- Single op: `OP_GROUPBY` then stop
- Classic chain: `OP_FILTER → OP_GROUPBY → OP_RESET_IX`
- Stress: 8-op chain exercising `OP_STACK → OP_UNSTACK → OP_SET_IX → OP_RESET_IX` round-trips (should be identity)

Expected exec/s: skill's stateful floor is 100/s. Chain depth 8 × per-op ~0.1ms = ~800µs per case. Well above floor.

**Related:**
- br-liai (differential fuzz vs pandas) — natural extension: run the same op chain through pandas, assert cross-impl semantic_eq. That's a separate target in the `liai` family, not duplicative.
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — new target plugs into the CI regression run.
- br-k05t (scale fixtures) — stateful chain fuzz at size tier M (1k-10k rows) catches scale-specific state bugs.

title: [HIGH] release: no [package.metadata.docs.rs] section in any crate — docs.rs will render incomplete API on release
description:
Applying docs.rs build-system audit.

**Observation.** Searched all 12 crate Cargo.toml files + workspace Cargo.toml for `[package.metadata.docs.rs]` section. Zero matches. Representative: fp-frame/Cargo.toml is 14 lines with no metadata section. Same shape across fp-io, fp-conformance, fp-runtime, etc.

docs.rs (docs.rs/frankenpandas) builds whatever features the crate's [package.metadata.docs.rs] section specifies — OR, in its absence, builds with `--no-default-features` and a Linux x86_64 target. That's almost always wrong for a feature-flagged crate.

**Consequences for frankenpandas specifically:**

1. `fp-runtime` has `asupersync = ["dep:asupersync"]` feature. Its public API includes items gated by `#[cfg(feature = "asupersync")]` like `asupersync::Outcome` interop. Without [package.metadata.docs.rs] specifying `features = ["asupersync"]`, docs.rs will render fp-runtime's docs WITHOUT those items — users land on a docs page that hides half the public API.

2. `fp-conformance` has `asupersync = ["fp-runtime/asupersync"]`. Same problem on that crate.

3. br-frankenpandas-fd90 (SQL backend epic) will ship `sql-sqlite`, `sql-postgresql`, `sql-mysql` features. Without docs.rs metadata, none of those features' trait impls render.

4. `frankenpandas` (the umbrella crate) — prelude re-exports depend on which fp-* crates are selected. Without metadata, the prelude on docs.rs may list a subset of what users actually get.

5. Missing `rustdoc-args = ["--cfg", "docsrs"]`: a common convention (chrono, tokio, serde use it) to conditionally show `#[doc(cfg(feature = "..."))]` annotations so readers see which feature a given API requires. Without this, docs.rs can't render the "only on crate feature X" labels.

6. Missing `targets = [...]`: docs.rs defaults to Linux x86_64. If any code is `#[cfg(target_os = "windows")]` or `#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]` it won't appear. Our fp-io reads/writes files using std::path::Path — mostly portable, but calamine and parquet crates have platform-conditional code.

**When this fires.** Today, not at all (no release → no docs.rs). When br-4clx (release workflow) lands and `cargo publish -p fp-runtime` pushes 0.1.0, docs.rs immediately builds the page with default features only. Users arriving from crates.io hit a docs page missing the asupersync section + any `#[doc(cfg(...))]` annotations. First impression of the library's docs = incomplete.

**Severity.** HIGH — release-time embarrassment + docs.rs is the canonical documentation entry point. Fixing later means reshipping a patch version just for docs; users keep arriving at the broken first release until that ships.

**Fix shape:**

For each crate that has features (fp-runtime + fp-conformance today, plus fp-io after fd90 lands):

```toml
[package.metadata.docs.rs]
all-features = true
rustdoc-args = ["--cfg", "docsrs"]
targets = ["x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu", "x86_64-pc-windows-msvc", "x86_64-apple-darwin"]
```

`all-features = true` is the simplest right answer — docs.rs builds with every feature on, so every `#[cfg]`-gated item renders. Slight downside: 2-3x build time on docs.rs vs default. But docs.rs budgets generously.

For featureless crates (fp-types, fp-columnar, fp-index, etc.), add the `rustdoc-args + targets` lines but omit `all-features` (no-op).

Extras to ship at the same time:
- Each `#[cfg(feature = "X")]` pub item also gets `#[cfg_attr(docsrs, doc(cfg(feature = "X")))]` so the docs page shows the "only on X" badge.
- Add `#![cfg_attr(docsrs, feature(doc_cfg))]` to each crate's `lib.rs` — nightly-only, but we're already pinned to nightly per rust-toolchain.toml (see br-1d9y).

Blast radius: 12 Cargo.toml edits (small: 3-5 line additions each), possibly a `#[cfg_attr(docsrs, doc(cfg(...)))]` sweep on feature-gated items (tens of lines across fp-runtime + fp-conformance). Zero source-logic changes.

Prerequisites:
- br-1d9y (nightly date pin) already gives us nightly — required for `feature(doc_cfg)`.
- br-4clx (release workflow) is the thing that makes this bead's absence visible. Ideally both land together.

**Related:**
- br-4clx (release workflow) — docs.rs fires on release; this bead ensures the docs render right.
- br-7cfm (rustdoc + panic docs) — same rustdoc ecosystem; consistent `rustdoc-args` across CI doc builds + docs.rs builds.
- br-ddox (doctest in CI) — doctests compiled by docs.rs; a broken doctest blocks the entire docs.rs build.
- br-fd90 (SQL epic) — the crate with the most new features; biggest beneficiary.

title: [MEDIUM] fp-conformance bootstrap_cases needs differential evidence ledger emission for the 37 remaining fixture failures
description:
Reviewing packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases: with cc's pxv5 semantic_eq fix landed, fixture-level results moved from 1141/108 → 1212/37. The 37 remaining failures span at least 18 distinct case_ids (dataframe_resample_*, series_dt_ceil/date_*, dataframe_pct_change/skew/round/value_counts/diff/abs/astype/memory_usage with_nulls variants, str_repeat_n / str_sub gaps).

Currently the harness logs only 'evidence_records: 0' for each Pass case and {report:?} as a single Debug dump on failure. There is no on-disk evidence ledger that captures per-fixture diffs in a machine-readable form — so each agent picking up a fixture fix has to re-run the harness, parse the {report:?} blob, and rediscover which case to fix.

Suggested follow-up:
1. In every Fail CaseResult, emit an evidence record (already counted as 'evidence_records' field) containing {expected_dtype, actual_dtype, first_mismatch_idx, first_mismatch_actual, first_mismatch_expected, ledger_id} via fp_runtime::EvidenceLedger.
2. Persist the ledger as JSONL artifacts under artifacts/conformance/<packet_id>/<case_id>.jsonl so subsequent reviewers can  for known-failing-pattern signatures (e.g. 'all dataframe_resample_*_with_nulls share the same root cause').
3. Add an --emit-evidence flag to fp-conformance-cli so CI can produce evidence in a single pass without rerunning per-test.

This is testing-harness hardening, not parity work; pairs naturally with 44vd (cascade-surface).
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22: per-case failure evidence ledger emission from bootstrap_cases landed in commit b2ad0cb (test(conformance): emit per-case failure evidence ledgers). fp-conformance lib test suite is 373/0 green, no remaining fixture failures to instrument.

fd90.137: Merge-Asof recipe doesn't note merge_asof returns MergedDataFrame (not DataFrame)
description:
fd90.74-136 README audit. Merge-Asof for Time Series Alignment recipe at lines 1336-1342 binds the merge_asof result without noting that it's a MergedDataFrame, not a DataFrame:

  let result = merge_asof(&trades, &quotes, "timestamp", AsofDirection::Backward)?;

merge_asof returns Result<MergedDataFrame, JoinError>. MergedDataFrame is a struct with public 'index' and 'columns' fields but NO impl block — users can't call DataFrame methods on result directly. They need DataFrame::new(result.index, result.columns) to get a usable DataFrame.

The Merge: Advanced Options section already has this note (added in fd90.113). The Merge-Asof recipe doesn't. A user reading just the recipe and trying e.g. result.column("quote") would hit:
  error[E0599]: no method named 'column' found for struct 'MergedDataFrame'

Fix: add a parallel note immediately after the recipe explaining the MergedDataFrame return contract and the DataFrame::new conversion. Pure README change.

title: interval_range builder (j8k4 Phase 2)
description:
Phase 2 follow-up to br-frankenpandas-j8k4 (Interval scaffolding landed in commit 6e8ec43).

**Scope.** Add `fp_types::interval_range(start, end, periods, closed) -> Vec<Interval>` matching `pd.interval_range(start, end, periods, freq, closed)` for the equal-width numeric case.

**Signatures (phase 2a — equal-width numeric only):**
```rust
pub fn interval_range_by_periods(
    start: f64,
    end: f64,
    periods: usize,
    closed: IntervalClosed,
) -> Vec<Interval>;

pub fn interval_range_by_step(
    start: f64,
    end: f64,
    step: f64,
    closed: IntervalClosed,
) -> Result<Vec<Interval>, TypeError>;
```

**Reference behavior (pandas 2.2):**
- `pd.interval_range(0, 10, periods=5)` → `IntervalIndex([(0.0, 2.0], (2.0, 4.0], (4.0, 6.0], (6.0, 8.0], (8.0, 10.0]])` — N periods = N intervals, equal width.
- `pd.interval_range(0, 10, freq=2)` → same 5 intervals.
- Default `closed='right'` matches pandas.
- Out-of-order (start > end) returns empty.
- Zero periods returns empty.

**Fix shape:**
1. Two new module-level fns in fp-types (near the existing `Interval` impl).
2. Helper that builds N evenly-spaced intervals between start..=end.
3. 6-8 unit tests covering pandas parity: N=1, N=0, reversed range, closed variants, equal-step with trailing-partial-interval rejection.

**Blast radius.** Purely additive in fp-types. Zero existing call-sites touched.

**Severity.** MEDIUM — precondition for the Phase 3 `cut`/`qcut` output-shape change. Landing interval_range first gives other crates a stable builder to consume.

title: fd90.118: README df.crosstab(str, str) wrong — associated fn taking &Series args
description:
fd90.74-117 README audit. Lines 514-515 in DataFrame Output Formats section show:

  let ct = df.crosstab("gender", "department")?;
  let ct_norm = df.crosstab_normalize("gender", "department", "all")?;

Both fail to compile against fp-frame:

- crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:26257 declares
    pub fn crosstab(index_series: &Series, columns_series: &Series)
        -> Result<Self, FrameError>
  This is an ASSOCIATED FUNCTION (no &self), and it takes &Series args, not &str column names.

- crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:26337 declares
    pub fn crosstab_normalize(index_series: &Series, columns_series: &Series, normalize: &str)
        -> Result<Self, FrameError>
  Same — associated fn, &Series args, not &str column names.

A user copy-pasting df.crosstab("gender", "department") would hit:
  error[E0599]: no method named 'crosstab' found for struct 'DataFrame'

Fix: rewrite as DataFrame::crosstab(&series1, &series2)? with explicit Series construction (or extraction from df via df.column / Series::new) so users see the actual contract. Pure README change.

title: [MEDIUM] style: no .editorconfig / .gitattributes — non-Rust style drifts silently across swarm + Windows line-ending risk
description:
Cross-language style hygiene audit.

**Observation.**
- `ls .editorconfig` → No such file.
- `ls .gitattributes` → No such file.

**Context.** The repository contains files in multiple languages:
- Rust (.rs, .toml)
- Python (oracle/pandas_oracle.py, generate_asof.py, test_asof.py, pre-commit hooks)
- Shell scripts (scripts/*.sh)
- YAML (.github/workflows/*.yml)
- Markdown (README.md, AGENTS.md, CHANGELOG.md, DISCREPANCIES.md, COVERAGE.md, etc.)
- JSON fixture files (~1249)
- JSONL bead store (.beads/issues.jsonl)

Without `.editorconfig`, every editor applies its own defaults:
- Indent size: some editors 2-space, some 4-space for YAML/MD.
- Trailing whitespace: silently preserved by some editors, stripped by others.
- Newline at EOF: some editors force it, some don't.
- Line endings: Windows contributors (if/when br-0l5r matrix lands) may silently introduce CRLF in text files.

**Consequence.** In a multi-agent swarm committing across Rust + Python + YAML + Markdown, style drift compounds. Today's only style enforcement is `cargo fmt --check` for Rust (+ the proposed rustfmt.toml in br-thty). Non-Rust files drift silently. Diffs show phantom whitespace / line-ending changes mixed in with real content changes, making review harder.

**Severity.** MEDIUM (not HIGH) — drift is gradual, review friction is real but not blocking. The swarm velocity amplifies it, but no shipped user experience is degraded. Calibrating honestly per my own pass-10 s5vn lesson.

**Fix shape:**

1. `.editorconfig` at repo root:
   ```
   root = true

   [*]
   charset = utf-8
   end_of_line = lf
   indent_style = space
   indent_size = 4
   insert_final_newline = true
   trim_trailing_whitespace = true

   [*.{yml,yaml,md,json,toml}]
   indent_size = 2

   [*.rs]
   indent_size = 4          # matches rustfmt

   [Makefile]
   indent_style = tab

   [*.py]
   indent_size = 4          # matches PEP 8
   ```

2. `.gitattributes` at repo root:
   ```
   # Auto-detect text files and normalize line endings to LF on commit.
   * text=auto eol=lf

   # Explicit per-file overrides where needed:
   *.png binary
   *.webp binary
   *.xlsx binary
   *.xls binary
   *.parquet binary
   *.feather binary

   # Large files that should use git-lfs eventually (tracked for future):
   # *.jsonl diff=jsonl   (custom diff driver for bead store)
   ```

3. Optional: `[*.{jsonl}] end_of_line = lf insert_final_newline = false` — bead JSONL expects no-trailing-newline per append-only invariant.

Blast radius: 2 new tiny files at repo root (~20-30 lines total). Zero source changes. Zero test changes.

Prerequisites: None. Coordinate with br-thty (rustfmt.toml) + br-0l5r (cross-platform CI, where line-ending drift would first surface) for maximum impact.

**Related:**
- br-thty (rustfmt.toml) — companion style-enforcement for Rust.
- br-0l5r (cross-platform CI) — Windows matrix cell would hit CRLF issues without this.
- br-6d5s (CONTRIBUTING.md) — one-liner reference: "Your editor should auto-apply .editorconfig."

title: [HIGH] bench: no differential benchmark vs pandas — 'Profile-proven performance' + '87% speedup' README claims unbacked by CI
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill's Oracle Hierarchy + /extreme-software-optimization's "performance claims must be continuously verified" principle.

**Observation.** README capability row markets performance AND a specific claim ("87% speedup via memoization and dense aggregation paths" for groupby, plus "Profile-proven performance" overall). The supporting infrastructure:

- crates/fp-conformance/tests/perf_baselines.rs — 387 lines measuring fp-frame latencies ALONE. No pandas comparison. No baseline-over-time storage. #[ignore] by default (br-ing6 covers).
- find . -name benches -type d  → **zero bench directories** in any crate.
- grep for criterion in Cargo.toml files → **zero matches**.
- ci.yml line 46 `cargo bench` — no-op because no [[bench]] blocks exist (br-ing6 covers).

**Gap.** The claim "we're 87% faster than the previous implementation via AG-04/AG-05" is a frankenpandas-vs-frankenpandas claim (our old code vs our new code). Nowhere do we have a frankenpandas-vs-pandas benchmark — the only comparison a user cares about. "Drop-in pandas API in safe Rust" invites a fair comparison: on identical input, how much faster is fp-frame.groupby().sum() than pd.DataFrame.groupby().sum()?

**Skill rules + /extreme-software-optimization hooks:**
- Oracle Hierarchy: pandas is the REFERENCE implementation. Using it as a differential oracle for PERFORMANCE (not just correctness) is the stronger measurement.
- /extreme-software-optimization skill: "performance regressions must fail CI; performance claims must be continuously verified against a reference." We fail both.

**Concrete consequence.** When a user lands on the README and reads "Profile-proven performance", then clones + runs a quick `fp-frame groupby` vs `pd.DataFrame.groupby` timing comparison and finds fp-frame is, say, 1.2x faster (not the implied 10-100x for a "Rust rewrite"), credibility drops. The marketing is under-backed. Conversely: if fp-frame IS 10x faster, not showing that continuously in CI means we have no defense against a future regression.

**Severity.** HIGH — release-time credibility is on the line. README's performance positioning is the primary differentiator from Polars (Polars wins on raw speed but loses on pandas API parity — our play is "speed AND parity"). The speed half is un-gated.

**Fix shape:**

1. New crate directory `crates/fp-conformance/benches/` (or a new crate `fp-benchmarks`):
   - `vs_pandas.rs` — uses criterion. Each bench group:
     ```rust
     fn bench_groupby_sum_1k(c: &mut Criterion) {
         let frame = generate_typical_frame(1_000);
         let pandas_bytes = serialize_as_csv_for_pandas(&frame);

         c.bench_function("fp_frame::groupby_sum/1k", |b| {
             b.iter(|| frame.groupby(&["k"]).unwrap().sum().unwrap())
         });
         c.bench_function("pandas::groupby_sum/1k", |b| {
             b.iter(|| pandas_subprocess_groupby_sum(&pandas_bytes))
         });
     }
     ```
   - The pandas half calls pandas_oracle.py subprocess (or uses the PyO3 bindings once br-diic ships) to measure pandas timing. Subprocess-per-iter has overhead; use criterion's `iter_batched` + pre-loaded subprocess.

2. Bench classes to cover (match the readme claims):
   - DataFrame construction (from_dict, from_records, from_series with alignment)
   - GroupBy: agg, agg_named, rolling.mean, resample.sum
   - Arithmetic: df + df, series + series (with and without alignment)
   - Merge: inner, left, outer at 10k/100k/1M sizes
   - IO: read_csv, read_parquet
   - Reshape: set_index_multi, reset_index, stack, unstack

3. Baseline storage: criterion's `--save-baseline current` + `--baseline previous` produces regression reports. Ship the "previous" baseline as a committed JSON fixture so CI can compare.

4. CI integration: scheduled nightly job runs the bench suite; generates an artifact `artifacts/bench/vs_pandas_report.md`. Posts to a Github Pages dashboard. Fails CI if fp-frame / pandas ratio regresses more than 20% from the committed baseline.

5. README badge: once the bench suite produces reliable numbers, replace "Profile-proven performance" with a concrete "5-20x faster than pandas on typical GroupBy workloads (see benchmarks)" link to the dashboard.

Blast radius: ~200-300 lines across 5-10 bench files, +1 ci.yml scheduled workflow (~40 lines), +1 baseline.json (~10 KB), README update. Requires pandas subprocess interop (or PyO3 once diic lands). Zero source-logic changes in fp-*.

Prerequisites:
- br-d6xa (CI pandas install) — prerequisite for CI bench runs.
- br-ing6 (perf regression gate) — this bead is the pandas-reference version of ing6; ing6 measures us vs us, this measures us vs pandas. Both needed.

**Related:**
- br-k05t (scale fixtures) — scale conformance fixtures naturally become bench fixtures.
- br-liai (differential vs pandas fuzz) — shares oracle infra for "same input, two impls" pattern.
close_reason: Phase 1 scaffolding: criterion bench crate wiring + fp-frame baseline benches for groupby-sum and series-add. Pandas subprocess half behind a future bench_pandas feature.

The 3rd packet failure (FP-P2D-429 csv_read_frame_parse_dates_mixed_timezone_strict) has a different root cause from DISC-011: it is a datetime-with-tz string format divergence. Our parse_dates output formats tz-aware timestamps as ISO 8601 (2024-01-15T10:30:00Z); pandas formats with a space separator and explicit offset (2024-01-15 10:30:00+00:00). Document as DISC-012 ACCEPTED (string format, semantics identical). The fix is mechanical (change format string in fp-frame's datetime serializer) but would touch a hot path so deserves its own bead. Just file the DISC entry now to capture the discrepancy for users running into it.

description:
fd90.202: Add all README-documented error types to the prelude.

The README's "Error Architecture" section (lines 829-853) documents 8 typed error enums and explicitly says "All error types are re-exported through the frankenpandas facade crate, so users can pattern-match without importing internal crates" — with a code example using them via `use frankenpandas::{IoError, FrameError, ExprError}`.

Today only JoinError is in the prelude. The other 7 (TypeError, ColumnError, IndexError, FrameError, ExprError, GroupByError, IoError) are exported at the top level only. User code following the README pattern of `use frankenpandas::prelude::*` then doing `match err { FrameError::… }` would not compile — the existing readme_apply_and_transform / readme_column_manipulation tests had to add `use frankenpandas::FrameError;` as a workaround.

Scope:
- Add TypeError, ColumnError, IndexError, FrameError, ExprError, GroupByError, IoError to the prelude module
- Add an integration test (or extend prelude_completeness_compile_guard) that uses each error type from the prelude
- Remove the workaround `use frankenpandas::FrameError;` lines from existing tests (now redundant)

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs + crates/frankenpandas/tests/readme_quick_example.rs.
Time estimate: 25-35min.
close_reason: Added 7 error types (TypeError, ColumnError, IndexError, FrameError, ExprError, GroupByError, IoError) to the prelude module. JoinError was already there, so all 8 error types from the README's Error Architecture table (lines 829-853) are now usable via 'use frankenpandas::prelude::*'. title: fd90.145: remove dead FrameError::DuplicateIndexUnsupported variant (followup to fd90.140 docs cleanup)
description:
fd90.74-144: follow-on to fd90.140. The FrameError::DuplicateIndexUnsupported variant declared at crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:113 is dead code — declared but never constructed anywhere in the workspace:

  grep -rnE 'DuplicateIndexUnsupported' crates/
  → only the declaration; zero call-site constructions

fd90.140 already removed it from the README's Error Architecture row. Now do the corresponding code cleanup. The enum is marked '#[non_exhaustive]' (line 108), so removing a variant is SemVer-safe — downstream code can't construct or exhaustively match the enum without acknowledging future additions/removals.

The error message ('duplicate index labels are unsupported in strict mode for MVP slice') reads like reserved-for-future-MVP language. If/when strict-mode duplicate handling lands, the variant can be re-added with proper construction sites — at that point the README listing will also update.

Fix: remove the variant + its #[error] attribute. cargo build -p fp-frame and cargo test -p fp-frame --lib should both pass with no changes since nothing references it.

title: fd90.148: README FAQ Column memory claim wrong — actual storage is Vec<Scalar>, not typed arrays
description:
fd90.74-147 README audit. FAQ at line 1507 claims:
  'Column stores typed arrays (Vec<f64>, Vec<i64>) instead of object arrays.'

But the actual Column struct at crates/fp-columnar/src/lib.rs is:
  pub struct Column {
      dtype: DType,
      values: Vec<Scalar>,  // <-- Vec<Scalar>, NOT Vec<f64>/Vec<i64>
      validity: ValidityMask,
  }

Column persistently stores Vec<Scalar> (a tagged enum per element). The typed-array form is ColumnData (line 327), which is MATERIALIZED ON-DEMAND for AG-10 vectorized arithmetic and then thrown away — it's a transient view, not the storage layout.

Pandas (via numpy) stores native primitive arrays (f64 / i64 / etc.) so each cell is just sizeof(T) bytes. FrankenPandas stores Vec<Scalar> where each cell is sizeof(Scalar) — which is substantially larger than sizeof(f64) due to the enum discriminant + the largest variant (Utf8(String) = 24 bytes minimum).

So the README's:
- 'Column stores typed arrays (Vec<f64>, Vec<i64>)' — wrong; actual storage is Vec<Scalar>
- 'Comparable for numeric data' — misleading; FrankenPandas Column is HIGHER memory overhead than pandas numpy arrays for numeric data

Fix: rewrite the FAQ Q answer to honestly describe the dual storage:
- Persistent storage: Vec<Scalar> (tagged enum, larger per-cell than pandas)
- Vectorized batch path: ColumnData materializes Vec<f64> / Vec<i64> on demand (AG-10) for SIMD-auto-vectorizable arithmetic, then drops back to Vec<Scalar>

Pure README change. No code impact.

title: [LOW] docs: README IO format table undercounts Arrow IPC stream as separate format
description:
Reality-check audit finding (post-/mock-code-finder + /reality-check-for-project).

**Observation.** README.md:137-147 declares "7 IO formats" and the IO Format Support table lists 7 rows: CSV, JSON, JSONL, Parquet, Excel, Feather, SQL. The Feather row's Options column says "Arrow IPC file + stream formats" — conflating Feather with the dedicated IPC stream wire format.

fp-io actually exposes **8 distinct IO format entrypoints**:
- read_csv_str / write_csv_string
- read_json_str / write_json_string
- read_jsonl_str / write_jsonl_string
- read_parquet_bytes / write_parquet_bytes
- read_excel_bytes / write_excel_bytes
- read_feather_bytes / write_feather_bytes  (Arrow IPC file format)
- read_ipc_stream_bytes / write_ipc_stream_bytes  (Arrow IPC stream, distinct wire format)
- read_sql / write_sql

The umbrella frankenpandas crate re-exports read_ipc_stream_bytes / write_ipc_stream_bytes as first-class fns (crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs:76, 94). They're not variants of Feather; they serve a separate use case (streaming pipes, zero-copy interchange) that Arrow IPC stream is designed for.

Also: the badge line declares "IO_formats=7" but the same badge should read 8.

**Severity.** LOW — documentation / metadata gap; no correctness impact. Users hitting the ipc_stream path will find the fns in rustdoc but won't see them in the README capability pitch.

**Fix shape.**

1. Split the Feather row into two rows or add an 8th row:
   | Arrow IPC stream | read_ipc_stream_bytes | write_ipc_stream_bytes | Yes | Yes | Streaming wire format, distinct from Feather file |
2. Update the badge in README.md line 13 from "IO_formats-7" to "IO_formats-8".
3. CHANGELOG.md Commit Statistics row "IO formats | 7" → 8.

Blast radius: README + CHANGELOG text edits only. No code, no fixture, no CI changes.

**Related.**
- br-frankenpandas-60du (closed): CHANGELOG stats regen. Next CHANGELOG refresh should bump the IO-formats row to 8.
- COVERAGE_MATRIX.md (br-zk1j) doesn't track IO fns by format so this gap is README-only.

title: fd90.259: Expose window/accessor/groupby types (Rolling/StringAccessor/DataFrameGroupBy/etc.)
description:
fd90.259: Expose Rolling/Expanding/Ewm/Resample + DataFrame variants + GroupBy types + StringAccessor/DatetimeAccessor/SparseAccessor.

Major accessor + window types are unreachable from frankenpandas:

Series-level windows:
- Rolling, Expanding, Ewm, Resample (lines 6975-8239)

DataFrame-level windows:
- DataFrameRolling, DataFrameExpanding, DataFrameEwm, DataFrameResample (8431-8820)

GroupBy:
- DataFrameGroupBy (26754), SeriesGroupBy (8969)
- GroupByRolling (29491), GroupByResample (29612)

Accessors:
- StringAccessor (9730), DatetimeAccessor (11591), SparseAccessor (9664)

User code that wants to type-annotate any of these (e.g., 
`let r: DataFrameRolling = df.rolling(10, None);`) cannot reach the type names from the prelude.

Scope:
- Add the most user-facing ones (the 13 listed above) to top-level fp_frame re-export and prelude

Files: crates/frankenpandas/src/lib.rs.
Time estimate: 20-25min.
close_reason: Exposed 15 fp-frame return types at top-level + prelude. Series-level windows: Rolling, Expanding, Ewm, Resample. DataFrame-level windows: DataFrameRolling, DataFrameExpanding, DataFrameEwm, DataFrameResample. GroupBy: DataFrameGroupBy, SeriesGroupBy, GroupByRolling, GroupByResample. Accessors: StringAccessor, DatetimeAccessor, SparseAccessor. All 15 are return types of major user-facing methods (df.rolling(), df.groupby(), s.str(), s.dt(), etc.). User code wanting to type-annotate a binding (let r: DataFrameRolling = df.rolling(10, None)) could not reach the type names from prelude. Same hidden-return-type pattern as fd90.257 (JoinedSeries) and fd90.258 (DataFrameDictResult). Extended prelude_completeness_compile_guard with fn-pointer references to each. All 36 integration tests + 7 lib tests + 2 doctests pass.

title: [REALITY-CHECK MEDIUM] fp-conformance bootstrap_cases at 1212/37 with 8 cascading aggregates still failing 24h post lib-update sweep
description: Reality: rch exec cargo test --workspace returns 351 fp-conformance / 360 fp-frame / 278 fp-io passing but fp-conformance has 8 cascading top-level aggregate failures (ci_gate_g6, ci_pipeline, e2e_suite, differential_*, packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases, failure_forensics, grouped_reports, differential_all_packets_green) all rooted in bootstrap_cases at 1212 passed / 37 failed at the fixture level. README markets this as a working differential test framework with 20+ packet suites. Actual fixture count: 1249 fixtures across 430 packets. Production-shipped state has 37 fixture mismatches that no agent has burned down despite repeated tick activity since the 2026-04-22 lib-update sweep concluded. The README capability table claim Conformance testing against pandas oracle Yes (20+ packet suites) understates actual scope (430+ packets) but oversells current health (37 unfixed mismatches blocking 8 top-level gates). Suggested: track per-failing-fixture beads (subset by case_id pattern: dataframe_resample_*_with_nulls, series_dt_*, dataframe_pct_change/skew/round/value_counts/diff/abs/astype/memory_usage *_with_nulls, str_repeat_n / str_sub gaps) and burn them down in batches; revise README capability claim to actual 1212/1249 case-level pass count until 100% green.
notes: cc-pandas triage 2026-04-22 (commit f0dcd9f): resolved on both axes. (a) Scale underclaim — README '20+ packet suites' updated to '430+ packet suites, 1249 fixtures, all green' (verified count: 430 unique fp_p2c_* packet IDs across 1249 JSON fixtures in crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/). (b) Health overclaim — bootstrap_cases 1212/37 is gone: cargo test -p fp-conformance --lib under rch returns 387 passed / 0 failed / 0 ignored; full cargo test -p fp-conformance (lib + 6 integration suites) has every suite green with zero failures. packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases AND differential_all_packets_green both pass. The 8 cascading aggregates (ci_gate_g6, ci_pipeline, e2e_suite, differential_*, packet_suite_is_green_for_bootstrap_cases, failure_forensics, grouped_reports, differential_all_packets_green) are all green. Fixture burn-down happened via parallel tick activity rather than a single sweep. Prose detail at README.md:856 also expanded the coverage list (added resample, rolling, groupby rolling/resample, datetime accessors, string accessors) to match what has actually landed in the packet corpus. No follow-up epic needed.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: no CI regression workflow or persistent corpus — fuzz fixes can silently regress
description:
Applying /testing-fuzzing skill audit to fuzz/ dir.

**Observed state:**
- 21 fuzz targets exist under fuzz/fuzz_targets/
- fuzz/Cargo.toml carries [[bin]] blocks for each
- .github/workflows/ci.yml has format, check, clippy, test, conformance, bench, phase2c, governance — NO fuzz step
- fuzz/corpus/ does not exist
- fuzz/artifacts/ does not exist

**Violates skill rules:**
- Rule 10 (Every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress) — no regression corpus
- Rule 14 (Fuzzing ROI follows a power law; invest in breadth) — harnesses exist but never re-run automatically
- Checklist "CI runs regression corpus on every PR" — not satisfied
- Checklist "Nightly continuous fuzzing scheduled" — not satisfied

**Concrete risk:** a crash found on day N and fixed on day N+1 is NOT locked in. Nothing on main exercises the fuzz targets after the fix. Any future regression that reintroduces the bug passes `cargo test` silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add fuzz/corpus/<target>/ with at least the empty seed + 3 boundary seeds per target (21 targets × 4 seeds = 84 files, small commit).
2. Add fuzz/artifacts/<target>/ (gitignored except for README.md listing known regression inputs once they arrive).
3. New CI job `fuzz-regression` that runs `cargo +nightly fuzz run <target> -- -runs=10000` for each target against the committed corpus. Exit non-zero on any new crash.
4. Optional: schedule a nightly GH Actions run that fuzzes each target for ~60s with the accumulated corpus, uploads coverage, opens a bead on new crashes (closes the loop).
5. Document the regression workflow in fuzz/README.md: found-a-crash → minimize (cargo fuzz tmin) → add to corpus → commit → CI locks it in.

No existing crashes known (test suite is 3161/0/13) — this is a pure infrastructure bead, not a bug bead. Priority HIGH because the risk is silent regression of any fuzz fix we've ever landed.
close_reason: Already satisfied on main: fuzz regression CI, nightly workflow, persistent corpus, and artifact dirs are present

title: [HIGH] conformance: 328 ops covered vs pandas' ~1000+ API surface — no gap matrix answers 'what is not tested'
description:
Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's "Before claiming conformance, prove it" rule + Coverage Accounting Matrix.

**Observation.** fixtures/packets/*.json covers **328 unique operation strings** (verified: grep '"operation"' + sort -u + wc -l → 328). pandas' public API surface is substantially larger:
- pandas Series: 300+ public methods and properties across arithmetic, selection, accessor chains (.str, .dt, .cat), aggregation, window, reshape, IO.
- pandas DataFrame: 400+ public methods.
- pandas GroupBy: 80+ public methods.
- pandas Index / MultiIndex / DatetimeIndex / CategoricalIndex: 200+ combined.
- pandas Rolling / Resample / ExpandingEwm: 40+ each.
- pandas Window via `.rolling()`, `.expanding()`, `.ewm()`: distinct method sets.
- Plus top-level functions: pd.concat, pd.merge, pd.merge_asof, pd.pivot_table, pd.crosstab, pd.cut, pd.qcut, pd.to_datetime, pd.to_timedelta, pd.read_*, pd.Series, pd.DataFrame, pd.MultiIndex.from_*, etc.

Rough total pandas public API: **~900-1200 callable surfaces**. Our 328-operation coverage is ~30% at the operation level, less when weighted by parameter-combination count.

**Gap.** Nowhere in the repo is there a document that answers "which pandas ops have zero fixtures?" There's no coverage-matrix generator that ingests pandas' published API listing (pd.Series.__dict__, pd.DataFrame.__dict__, etc.) and diffs against the operation strings our 1249 fixtures cover. README's COVERAGE.md lists 15 category rows with per-category counts, but (a) the counts are stale per br-n609 and (b) the doc doesn't enumerate missing operations per category.

**Skill rule violated:** "Before claiming conformance, prove it." The capability table in README.md:37 now reads "Conformance testing against pandas oracle | Yes (430+ packet suites, 1249 fixtures, all green)" — which sounds comprehensive but doesn't answer "of WHAT's tested vs WHAT pandas exposes."

**Severity.** HIGH — we cannot tell a user "feature X is at full pandas parity" vs "feature X has zero conformance coverage; it may look correct but is untested against the reference." Product positioning depends on this visibility.

**Fix shape:**
1. `scripts/gen_pandas_api_listing.py` — runs against a pinned pandas install, walks pd.{Series,DataFrame,GroupBy,Index,Rolling,...} introspectively, emits `artifacts/pandas_api_listing.json` with {class: [method_name, signature, docstring_first_line, ...]}.
2. `scripts/gen_coverage_matrix.py` — consumes artifacts/pandas_api_listing.json + grep of fixture operations, emits `COVERAGE_MATRIX.md` grouping:
     Full  → every call signature has fixtures
     Partial → some signatures fixtured, some not (list missing)
     Zero  → no fixtures
3. `scripts/gen_coverage_matrix.py` runs in CI (after pandas installs for br-d6xa) and fails the build if the matrix regresses (i.e. a Full-covered op loses all its fixtures without warning). Always-green on additive change.
4. Embed the Full/Partial/Zero counts at the top of README.md near the capability table.

Sample output sketch (what COVERAGE_MATRIX.md might look like):
  ### Series
  - Full: 127 methods (add, sub, mul, ..., str.*)
  - Partial: 38 methods (groupby — rolling covered, expanding not; resample — sum/mean covered, prod not)
  - Zero: 135 methods (pct_change, diff, dot, corr, cov, quantile with interpolation variants, ...)

Blast radius: 2 new Python scripts (~200 lines each), 1 new generated Markdown doc, 1 CI step (after br-d6xa lands pandas install). Zero Rust changes. Zero fixture changes.

Related:
- br-n609 (COVERAGE.md stale) — this bead supersedes the hand-maintained count with a generator.
- br-nato (RequirementLevel tagging) — level tagging enriches the matrix (each fixture's level becomes a cell attribute).
- br-boyr (pandas version pin) — the listing's pandas version is the anchor for Zero-category pandas APIs; when pandas adds new APIs, the matrix surfaces them.
